# Search
Search lets people enter a keyword or phrase to get relevant information

## Accessibility
Use cases {#isPasted}
---------------------
People should be able to use assistive technology to:

* Navigate to and focus on a search bar

* View the hinted search text or persistent label

* Input text and complete a search

* Interact with a list of search suggestions and results

* Clear the input text
Interaction \& style {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
### Autosuggest

When search suggestions and results appear, the screen reader must announce the change. This lets people know list items are available for selection.
Initial focus {#isPasted}
-------------------------
Initial focus lands on the first interactive element. This is often a leading icon button or text field. A leading icon button usually activates search directly or opens a navigation component.
Keyboard navigation {#isPasted}
-------------------------------
|            **Keys**            |               **Actions**                |
| **Tab** or **Shift** + **Tab** |  Navigate between interactive elements   |
|     **Space** or **Enter**     | Activate the search text field for input |
|           **Arrows**           |   Navigate between search result items   |
|--------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
Labeling elements
-----------------
The hinted search text should be used as the accessibility label describing the search bar.  

The role for the input field should be:

* Android: **Text field**

* iOS: **Search field**
Leading and trailing icon buttons should be labeled according to their [accessibility guidance](/google-material-3/pages/icon-buttons/accessibility).
Search suggestions and results use the list component. Screen readers automatically announce the results as a list.  

For accessibility labels, follow the [list accessibility guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/lists/accessibility).
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQs](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.{#isPasted}

[Apple's Human Interface Guidelines](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines) include additional iOS best practices and platform considerations.
Resources
---------
|    **Type**    |                                                           **Resource**                                                            |    **Contact**     | **Status** |
|     Design     | [iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=52172-50373&t=ZYYZaN4DZOR1BDw7-0) |   gm-design-kit@   | Available  |
| Implementation |   [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/OpenSearch/index.md?polyglot=objective-c&cl=head)   | material-ios-team@ | Available  |
|----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------|
Same as Android
---------------
This component is supported on iOS with the same specs, guidelines, and accessibility considerations as Android.

> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Search helps people find information quickly.  

Use search for products with many items to manage, such as files or messages.

<br />
### Different ways to search
The search entry point is dependent on a product's needs, and should be easy to find:

* Search bar: Use to search contents in a specific view, like **Search your messages**

* Search app bar: Use this app bar variant when search is the primary, global function

* Search icon button: Use when search is a secondary action or not the main focus
### Focused search

When a search entry point is selected, it opens focused search.

* Search suggestions can appear before text is entered

* Search results can show as someone is typing or after a search is executed

* Individual elements maintain their own interaction states when search is focused

[More on search states](/google-material-3/pages/search/specs#b0554e51-706d-4950-8655-7df3f7eb7c31)
If search is the primary action, focused search can be a standalone destination reached from a navigation bar.
### Search suggestions \& results

Search suggestions and results both appear in a list component by default.  

To help people find information quickly, consider adding variety and context, such as:

* Leading icons related to suggestions

* Category labels, like **Recent** , **Contacts** , or **Suggestions**

* Avatars or other high-priority items

* Filter chips to narrow down results
### Gaps

Use gaps to separate a list of suggestions or results into groups.  

[More on using gaps in lists](/google-material-3/pages/lists/guidelines#7c00002f-bf89-44d6-abd3-7163168abb0b)
Placement
---------
A search bar is typically placed at the top of a screen to remain prominent and accessible. Its location depends on whether search is the primary focus of a product or a secondary action.
### Focused search layouts

When focused, search suggestions and results appear in a list below the search bar.  

There are two layout options:

* **Docked** opens a list below the search bar, with a scrim covering main content

* **Full-screen** expands to fill the screen

[More on adaptive design](/google-material-3/pages/search/guidelines#30038a7e-08ba-43ba-a83c-ecfaa2989d7f)
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
### Search bar container

In the contained style, the search bar container remains the same shape in both the unfocused and focused states. Avoid changing the container behavior.   

The container's margins should be:

* Unfocused: 24dp

* Focused: 12dp

In the divided (baseline) style, a divider separates the search bar and results.
#### Container color

Search bars use the **surface container high** color role. This role applies when the screen background is white or a tonal **surface** color, ensuring the container has clear contrast.
Avoid using a **surface container high** color on a **surface container** background. This can cause the search bar to blend in, making it difficult for people to find.  

To ensure proper contrast, use surface container roles that are more than one step apart.
### Icons \& icon buttons

#### Leading icons

The leading side of a search bar should include either:

* A navigational icon button, such as a menu or arrow

* A non-functional search icon
#### Trailing icons

A search bar should have one or two trailing icons or icon buttons.  

Trailing actions can include:

* Additional modes of searching like voice search

* A separate high-level action such as current location or profile

* An overflow menu

* A decorative search icon
### Text

#### Hinted search text

Provide a short description of the information people can search, like **Search replies** or **Search your messages**.

#### Input text

When a person starts typing, the hinted text is replaced with the input text.
Adaptive design
---------------
The search bar position and alignment should scale with the layout, and stay close to the searchable content.  

In most cases, a search bar should:

* Stay in its pane and scale in width accordingly

* Internal elements anchor to the left and right as the parent container scales

[More on applying layout](/google-material-3/pages/applying-layout/window-size-classes/44b23a4c-e16f-491c-967c-31385f5c6960?edit=true#)
### Focused search

When focused, search can switch between showing suggestions or results in a:

* **Docked layout** : Best for medium and expanded windows

* **Full-screen layout** : Default for compact window sizes

[More on search layouts](/google-material-3/pages/search/specs#39c93eee-f5f5-4a04-8ff0-399b14d2f54b)
Search suggestions or results should swap from full-screen in compact windows to docked in larger window sizes.
Behavior
--------
### Focused search
When a search bar is selected, search becomes focused and can:

* Show historical suggestions before typing

* Show suggestions or results as someone is typing

* Wait to show suggestions or results until a search is queried

The **back** icon releases focus, dismisses any suggestions or results, and returns the search bar to its original state.
### Scroll

Depending on needs, a search bar can:

* Scroll away with content, then reappear when a person begins scrolling up

* Remain fixed at the top of the screen
### Search results

To execute a search, a person can:

* Type a query and press **Enter**

* Select a suggestion or result without querying a search

Search results appear in a list below the bar, and scroll beneath the bar.   

For accessibility, focused search needs a clear status indicator that it's searching content, like a search icon or **Results** label. [More on search accessibility](/google-material-3/pages/search/accessibility/db5d0029-49f5-4436-bd10-362930b6c361?edit=true)
When search results are queried, the input text should remain visible, but not in focus.
### Predictive back

On Android, [predictive back](https://github.com/material-components/material-components-android/blob/master/docs/foundations/PredictiveBack.md) allows a person to swipe left or right on search.

* Search detaches from the screen edge to signal the full-screen layout will minimize

* The previous screen is revealed in a preview

[More predictive back design guidance](https://developer.android.com/guide/navigation/custom-back/predictive-back-gesture)
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Use search for navigating a product with queries

* A search bar can include a leading search icon, hinted search text, and optional trailing icons

* Search can display suggested keywords or phrases as a person types

* A search bar displays search suggestions or results in a list

* Use a search app bar to provide an emphasized, global entry-point
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
Search has a new visual style, motion, and more flexibility for trailing icons. [More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview)
**February 2025**

Naming

* Search bar and search view are now collectively named **search**

Configurations

* Styles: Search can be contained (recommended) or divided

* Gaps can separate results into groups

Motion

* The search bar grows wider when focused

Supported platforms:

* Jetpack Compose
Differences from GM2 to GM3 baseline
------------------------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color

* Elevation: Lower elevation and no shadow by default

* Name: Search was formerly known as open search bar

* Variants: Two official variants of search components: search bar and search view

----

File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## Specs
Variants
--------
| Variant |    GM3    | GM3 Expressive |
|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Search  | Available | Available      |
Configurations
--------------
### Style
Search comes in two styles:

* Contained: Has an expressive look and feel. It uses a filled container to separate a search bar from a list of suggestions or results

* Divided (baseline): Doesn't have the latest visual style, motion, or flexibility
### Layout
Search suggestions and results appear in customizable lists, with two layout options: full-screen and docked. [More on search layouts](/google-material-3/pages/search/guidelines#f20ef6ca-0d6e-431e-84ba-4cfb9ed48c0e)
| Category |    Configuration    |    GM3    |         GM3 Expressive          |
|----------|---------------------|-----------|---------------------------------|
| Style    | Contained           | --        | Available                       |
|          | Divided             | Available | Not recommended.Use contained. |
| Layout   | Docked, full-screen | Available | Available                       |
Tokens \& specs
---------------

Use the table's menu to select a token set. The **search bar** set only contains tokens for the unfocused search bar. The **search view** set contains all other tokens when interacting with search, including all styles and layouts. [Learn more about design tokens](http://go/gm3-design-tokens)
Anatomy
-------
Search includes a search bar and a container for suggestions and results. The container is empty by default. Use the list component to add content. In the divided (baseline) style, a divider separates the search bar and results.
### Examples

1. With avatar

2. With one trailing icon button

3. With two trailing icon buttons

4. With trailing icon button and avatar
### Google-only examples

5. Product lockup with avatar  
6. Product lockup with one trailing icon button  
7. Product lockup with two trailing icon buttons  
8. Product lockup with trailing icon button and avatar
Color
-----
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For designers, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. In implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value.
### Full-screen layout
### Docked layout
States
------
States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or an interactive element. In [focused search](/google-material-3/pages/search/guidelines#13fd3349-f964-44c5-b210-fc1a23244212), individual elements maintain their own interaction states. [Learn more about interaction states](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/overview)
### Search bar
### Search suggestions \& results
Search includes a container for suggestions and results. The container is empty by default. Use the list component to add content.
Measurements
------------
### Search bar
In GM3 Expressive, the search bar expands when focused. The margins change from 24dp to 12dp.
|                      Element                      |           Attribute            |         Value          |
|                     Container                     |             Width              | Min: 360dp, max: 720dp |
|                     Container                     |
|                     Container                     |
|                     Container                     |
|                     Container                     |
|                     Container                     |
|                     Container                     |
|---------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------|
| Height                                            | 56dp                           |
| Label alignment                                   | Start-aligned                  |
| Leading padding                                   | Unfocused: 24dp, focused: 12dp |
| Trailing padding                                  | Unfocused: 24dp, focused: 12dp |
| Leading icon and label padding (from tap target)  | 4dp                            |
| Label and trailing icon padding (from tap target) | 4dp                            |
### Focused search
#### Contained style
|                     Element                      |               Attribute               |   Value    |
|              Full-screen container               |                 Width                 | Full width |
|              Full-screen container               |
|--------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Height                                           | Full height                           |

| Label alignment                                  | Start-aligned                         |

| Trailing padding                                 | 16dp                                  |

## Overview
* Two variants: Basic and rich banners

* Configurations for vibrant color and rounded shape

* Use banners for timely and actionable messages

* Place square basic banners above the body content

* Place round basic banners above or inline with the body content

* Place rich banners inline with the body content
Availability \& resources
-------------------------
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------
In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with new styling that includes glass and other visual changes. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
**Nov 2025**   

GM3 banners now support glass effect. [More details on iOS tab](/google-material-3/pages/banner/ios)
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
**May 2025**

Banners are more flexible: they can have images, text, buttons, or a close button, and can be placed above body content or inline. They have new vibrant color and round shape options to match other expressive components. Rich banners support longer content and images. [More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
Variants and naming:

* Original banner is now **basic** banner

* Added **rich** banner

Configurations:

* Color: Standard, vibrant

* Basic banner shapes: Square, round

Banners can now be placed inline with body pane content.
Differences from GM2
--------------------
* **Color** : New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Banners are meant to grab attention without interrupting someone's current task. Use banners to display important messages that are timely and actionable, but optional. A banner should remain on screen until either an action is taken, or it's dismissed.

Basic banners can be fixed on screen, scroll away, or be in-line with body content.

There are two variants of banners: **basic** and **rich**. They have standard and vibrant color schemes, and the elements can be customized.
### Basic banner
Basic banners are for smaller amounts of text, generally one or two lines. They come in two shapes: **square** and **round** . Round was introduced in the [expressive update](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true).{#isPasted}

**Square**

Square basic banners should always span the full width of the screen and be placed at the top of the screen, above the body pane.
star  
Note:  
Material recommends using round basic banners. Square banners are the baseline GM3 design.
**Round**{#isPasted}

Round basic banners can be placed either above all the content or within a specific body pane. When above the content, it can span the full width of the content or have a maximum width.

When it's within a body pane, it only appears above that content in two-pane layouts.

The round shape complements expressive features in other components.
### Rich banner
Rich banners are more flexible; they can have an image, icon, and more lines of text. They're larger than basic banners, so should be used sparingly. They're best used for contextual, more complicated actions.{#isPasted}

Only place rich banners within body panes, so they scroll with the body content.

Avoid placing rich banners above all the content in two- or three-pane layouts.
### Standard \& vibrant color
Banners come in two color styles: **standard** and **vibrant**. The vibrant color style is more prominent to better grab attention, while the standard style is more neutral.{#isPasted}

Use the color style that best fits the product's tone and level of emphasis needed.

Other color styles are also available:

* Customize banners to use other Material color roles, like the primary, secondary, and tertiary color sets. [View color mapping specs](/google-material-3/pages/banner/specs#ba7d6b80-4681-4a93-ae13-a4f3292f7fc2)
* Only apply semantic color when necessary (yellow for warnings, red for errors); it's not supported by Material color roles.
### Similar components {#isPasted}

[Snackbars](/google-material-3/pages/snackbar/overview) and [dialogs](/google-material-3/pages/dialogs/overview) are also designed to show important messages.{#isPasted}

Choose the right component based on the importance of the message.
{#isPasted}

| **Component** |      **Importance**      |                                                 **Action needed**                                                  |
|---------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Snackbar      | Low importance           | Optional: Snackbars may not have a button, and can disappear automatically                                         |
| Banner        | Low-to-medium importance | Optional: Banners remain on screen until acted on or dismissed, or if the state that caused the banner is resolved |
| Dialog        | High importance          | Required: Dialogs block the main content until an action is confirmed                                              |
Anatomy
-------
### Leading icon (optional)
The leading icon communicates the meaning of the banner. Avoid popular icons that could be confused as buttons, such as search or edit.
### Leading image (optional)
Rich banners can have an 80x80dp leading image to complement the message. Avoid changing the size of the image.
### Body text

The body text should be brief, timely, and actionable. If there's too much information to fit in the banner, consider using a dialog.{#isPasted}

Avoid hyperlinks in body text. Use the buttons instead.
### Buttons

Banners can contain up to two small buttons, and a close icon button. Place the secondary action on the left and the primary, confirming action on the right. Don't change the buttons to split buttons or button groups.{#isPasted}

The primary button should act on the banner's message. Most banners should have at least one button.

On mobile, buttons wrap underneath the body text.
**Close icon button**

Banners can include a close button to dismiss the banner completely. {#isPasted}

All banners need a way to be dismissed. Include either a dismiss button or the close icon, but not both.
Placement
---------
Basic square banners should always be placed at the top of the screen, above the main content. Only show one banner at a time.
Round basic banners and rich banners can be placed above all content, or within the body pane.
### Banners \& app bars

Square basic banners should be placed directly below the app bar, and above all body content.{#isPasted}

Round basic banners have extra margins.

The colors of the app bar and banner should be distinct.
### Banners \& search bars {#isPasted}

Place banners below persistent search bars. Try to make the colors visually distinct.
### Banners \& navigation rails

Square basic banners can be placed above the navigation rail. {#isPasted}

All other banners should appear within or above the body content, not above the nav rail.
Adaptive design
---------------
### Square basic banners

On desktop, square basic banners fill the window width with inline buttons. On mobile, the buttons wrap to a separate line.
### Round basic \& rich banners

Round basic banners and rich banners should fill the container they're placed in, whether above or inline with body content. On mobile, round basic banners should swap to rich banners.
In [two-pane layouts](/google-material-3/pages/applying-layout/pane-layouts#9f95ab7e-6d5c-4267-b8bf-81e7138f6fe0), round basic banners and rich banners should be placed within a body pane. This enables the banner to appear in context with the right content at any size.
On very small or zoomed-in screens with a width of 320 CSS pixels or less, switch to a scrollable banner, rather than increasing the banner height. This lets people navigate the banner without cutting off its contents or blocking the page underneath.
Behavior
--------
### Appearing {#isPasted}

Banners should appear as the page content loads, not afterwards. {#isPasted}

Square basic banners should appear from the top of the layout, while round basic and rich banners should animate on screen from within the page content.

Don't push content downwards as banners load in.
### Dismissing banners

When dismissing a banner, the main content should push up into the empty space.
### Scrolling

On desktop, square basic banners should remain fixed at the top of the screen, at an elevation level above the main content. They shouldn't scroll with the content.{#isPasted}

On mobile they can remain fixed, or can scroll with the body content.

All other banners should scroll away with the page content.
### Panning

Banners should remain on screen when scrolling content that can pan around, like a map.

Use round and rich banners with caution on panned backgrounds.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Variants
--------
|   Variant    |    GM3    | GM3 Expressive |
|--------------|-----------|----------------|
| Basic banner | Available | Available      |
| Rich banner  | --        | Available      |
Configurations
--------------
By default, basic banners look like the baseline GM3 design: full-width, with square corners, and a standard color scheme. View color configurations in the [color specs](/google-material-3/pages/banner/specs#ba7d6b80-4681-4a93-ae13-a4f3292f7fc2). [View baseline tokens](/google-material-3/pages/banner/specs#43eec874-8584-4842-8b7b-016ab3e56306)
|      Category      |   Configuration    |    GM3    | GM3 Expressive |
|--------------------|--------------------|-----------|----------------|
| Color              | Standard (default) | Available | Available      |
| Color              | Vibrant            | --        | Available      |
| Basic banner shape | Square (default)   | Available | Available      |
| Basic banner shape | Round              | --        | Available      |
Tokens \& specs
---------------
Browse the component elements, attributes, tokens, and their values. [More about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Anatomy
-------
Nested components include small [buttons](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons) and a small [icon button](/google-material-3/pages/icon-buttons).
### Basic banner
### Rich banner
Common layouts
--------------
Basic banners have an icon, label text, buttons, and a close icon button. The body text can wrap to a second line.{#isPasted}

Always include at least one button to dismiss the banner.
Rich banners can have an icon or an image, label text, supporting text, buttons, and a close icon button. {#isPasted}

Banners can contain 1--3 lines of text.
Color
-----
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview/6fcbeebf-42ab-4d55-a5c5-9a20a8bee1bd?edit=true)
### Standard
### Vibrant
Other color mappings
--------------------
Banners can use other color mappings, like the [primary](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/undefined#e0a84fdf-9b3b-498d-8b44-7ef9fca3c3c0), [secondary](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/undefined#d49dcc1a-c489-4675-ab2d-4047c7d1a3ed), and [tertiary](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/undefined#93ad5625-61a4-4ab6-b965-193cc995e6f4) color sets. Map the color roles consistently to ensure proper contrast. Semantic color mappings, like yellow for warnings and red for errors, aren't available using tokens; apply them sparingly if needed.
Measurements
------------
### Basic banner
### Rich banner
### Margins and shape {#isPasted}
Rich banners and round basic banners should have margins of at least 16dp from the edge of the window. The corner shape should be 28dp. Basic banners have no margins.
Baseline tokens
---------------
These tokens are used for the baseline GM3 banner. They're no longer recommended for GM3 Expressive.

File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------
People should be able to do the following with assistive technology:{#isPasted}

* Navigate into and out of the banner and move between its elements
* Interact with banner actions
* Exit the banner without taking any action and continue interacting with screen content
Interaction \& style
--------------------
### Basic banner {#isPasted}

Banners should usually appear **when the page loads**, and should remain on the page until dismissed.{#isPasted}

If a banner appears **after the page loads**, it doesn't trap or steal focus, but it does need to be announced to screen readers when it appears.

* Low-importance, like subscribe or sign in: Use the **polite** banner announcement
* Medium-importance, like refresh a stale page: Use the **assertive** banner announcement
* High-importance: Don't use a banner. Instead, use a more disruptive component like a dialog.
People should be able to freely enter and exit the banner content. Once a person takes an action, dismisses the banner, or exits the banner, focus should return to where it was on the page before the banner appeared.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
{#isPasted}

|          Keys          |                                                     Actions                                                      |
|------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Tab**                | Focus lands on the next action contained in the banner, or outside the banner to the next item on screen         |
| **Shift** + **Tab**    | Focus lands on the previous action contained in the banner, or outside the banner to the previous item on screen |
| **Space** or **Enter** | Activates the focused action                                                                                     |
Labeling elements
-----------------
On web, don't use the **banner** role, as it's only meant for the topmost page header. Banners should have the **complementary** role.
All elements in a banner, like buttons and icons, should be labeled according to their accessibility guidelines.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.
Resources
---------
|    **Type**    |                                                                                  **Resource**                                                                                   |            **Contact**            |  **Status**  |
|----------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|--------------|
| Design         | [GM3 iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=97988-32585&p=f&t=md2FZli2owzElBfG-0) (Figma)                               | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug) | Available    |
| Design         | [Material Labs: iOS 26 Liquid Glass Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/KCk1VpuDP9n0tgRxQ9Ubwe/Material-Labs--iOS-Liquid-Glass-Kit?node-id=331-5518&t=sImcHitTCAlxJuUf-0) (Figma) | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug) | Early access |
| Design         | [Apple Human Interface Guidelines](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines)                                                                               |                                   | Available    |
| Implementation | [GM3 iOS dev docs: Banners](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Banner/index.md?polyglot=objective-c&cl=head)                                 | material-ios-team@                | Available    |
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------
star  
Note:  
The iOS 26 code and Design Kit are in early access, and the code has not yet undergone final accessibility review. Designs and guidelines may change when code is complete.
In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with new styling that includes glass and other visual changes. When this component appears in the functional layer, rather than the content layer, glass effects are applied. [More on the iOS 26 update](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-26)

* Layout: All iOS banners use an inset and rounded, rather than square, layout

* Button configurations: Secondary actions in the iOS banner use **outlined** buttons instead of **text** buttons

* Glass: iOS banners use Material Glass when they are placed in the functional layer
**Use of glass effect depends on behavior:**

* **When banners remain floating** while content scrolls behind, they exist in the [functional layer](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-26#ebe247f4-15f6-456b-9702-467158fd16af) and take the glass effect.

* **When banners scroll inline** along with content, they exist in the [content layer](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-26#53b3b378-83fc-44c3-bb88-f2dc8f45b22b) and use a surface color as a result.
Differences from GM3 baseline
-----------------------------
The expressive update is unavailable. However, significant differences include:

* XS buttons are used in a one-line banner

* The close button is not supported yet
Guidelines
----------
### Action button configurations
* To match iOS patterns, use the outline button for secondary actions inside the banner

* Don't use text buttons, which aren't typical for actions within similar iOS contexts like dialogs and alerts

* Use one-line banners in wider windows, like tablet
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Toolbars
Toolbars display frequently used actions relevant to the current page

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.
Resources
---------
|                                                                                       **Type**                                                                                        |                                                                   **Resource**                                                                    |            **Contact**            | **Status** |
|                                                                                        Design                                                                                         | [GM3 iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=98008-33296&p=f&t=FTkOPvJvR3YiLb4C-0) (Figma) | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug) | Available  |
|                                                                                        Design                                                                                         |
|                                                                                        Design                                                                                         |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|------------|
| [Material Labs: iOS 26 Liquid Glass Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/KCk1VpuDP9n0tgRxQ9Ubwe/Material-Labs--iOS-Liquid-Glass-Kit?m=auto&node-id=96-1322&t=SkIMPxurQEAcsE6L-1) (Figma) | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug)                                                                                                                 | Early access                      |
| [Apple Human Interface Guidelines: Toolbars](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/toolbars)                                                                  |                                                                                                                                                   | Available                         |
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------
star  
Note:  
The iOS 26 code and Design Kit are in early access, and the code has not yet undergone final accessibility review. Designs and guidelines may change when code is complete.
In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with new styling that includes glass and other visual changes. As a functional layer component, glass effects are always applied. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
The content beneath the toolbar remains partially visible due to the glass effect.
Same as Android
---------------
This component is supported on iOS with the same specs, guidelines, and accessibility considerations as Android.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage {#isPasted}
-----------------
Use a toolbar to provide actions related to the current page. {#isPasted}

Toolbars can contain many actions and can scale to show more actions in larger windows.
There are two variants of toolbars:

* **Docked toolbar**  
  Spans the full width of the window. It's best used for global actions that remain the same across multiple pages.

<!-- -->

* **Floating toolbar**  
  Floats above the body content. It's best used for contextual actions relevant to the body content or the specific page.

The baseline **bottom app bar**is no longer recommended, but is still supported.
When actions don't fit in a toolbar, add a menu.
There are two color configurations:  
{#isPasted}

* **Standard**  
  A low-emphasis color scheme best used for focusing attention on the body content.  
  {#isPasted}

<!-- -->

* **Vibrant**  
  A high-emphasis color scheme that draws attention to the controls. It can also indicate a temporary change in the page behavior, such as entering edit mode.{#isPasted}

Consider using alternative color roles to create greater or lesser emphasis depending on the needs of the app. Experiment with different color roles to achieve different effects.
### Toolbars \& navigation bars {#isPasted}
The toolbar and navigation bar are both placed at the bottom of the window, so should **not** be shown at the same time. Show the navigation bar on primary pages, and toolbars on subsequent pages with actions.
Floating toolbars can be used as tabs between related subsequent pages in the product hierarchy. {#isPasted}

This helps group similar pages together, and shows that the selection affects the body content underneath.
Consider the existing app hierarchy when using a toolbar for local navigation.  

Avoid redundant or confusing navigation combinations in the same view.
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
### Container {#isPasted}

The docked toolbar's container spans the full width of the window.   

Avoid applying rounded corners to the container. This can imply the container expands or changes upon interaction.
As long as there's a minimum of 16dp padding on the leading and trailing edge, arrange controls inside however you see fit. The 32dp padding between items is just the default. {#isPasted}

All elements need a minimum 48x48dp target area to be accessible.  

Be cautious of including too many controls as it can be overwhelming.
The floating toolbar's container should be fully visible on screen. If more actions are needed, use an overflow menu.
#### Elevation

Floating toolbars have elevation by default.

If the content beneath the toolbar is visually distinct, elevation can be removed.
### Flexibility \& slots {#isPasted}

When configuring a toolbar, think of it as a container with several slots.  

These slots can be populated by buttons, icon buttons, images, text fields, or any kind of custom component. {#isPasted}

Icon buttons provide an even hierarchy of controls. Mixing in a filled icon button can help add emphasis to a single action.
Visually emphasizing a single action more than others is an effective way to create hierarchy and guide people to controls they use most often. Avoid emphasizing more than one action at a time.  

Some common ways to add emphasis to toolbar actions:  
{#isPasted}

* Use different icon button color styles, such as filled, tonal, and standard  
  {#isPasted}
* Customize the color roles of a single action, such as a primary or secondary palette  
  {#isPasted}
* Use wide and narrow icon buttons   
  {#isPasted}
* Pair the toolbar with a FAB{#isPasted}
Avoid using square icon buttons in floating toolbars. Their square shape conflicts with the fully-rounded shape of the floating toolbar container.{#isPasted}

Square buttons can be used in the docked toolbar.
### Floating toolbar with FAB {#isPasted}

A FAB can be placed next to a floating toolbar to present one high-priority action alongside a unified set of toolbar actions. {#isPasted}

Use a FAB for the highest-priority action in the view, or to complement the controls.
Position \& orientation
-----------------------
Only place docked toolbars at the bottom of the window. {#isPasted}

If using other bottom-aligned elements, such as a navigation bar, don't use a docked toolbar.
Floating toolbars can be horizontal or vertical.   

Horizontal toolbars should have a minimum 16dp margin from the edge of the window.
In larger window sizes, floating toolbars can be vertical and placed on either side of the screen.{#isPasted}

Vertical toolbars should have a minimum 24dp margin.
To keep vertical toolbars compact, don't use wide icon buttons. {#isPasted}

Use narrow or default icon buttons instead.
Vertical toolbars should be positioned opposite the navigation rail to balance out the screen and keep actions easy to access.{#isPasted}

When showing a navigation rail and vertical floating toolbar at once, use the centered configuration of the navigation rail.
Adaptive design
---------------
Adaptive design allows an interface to respond or change based on context, such as the user, device, and usage. [More on adaptive design](/google-material-3/pages/adaptive-design?s=google-material-3)
### Resizing
#### Docked {#isPasted}

The docked toolbar should always span 100% of the screen width.{#isPasted}

In compact window sizes, elements in the toolbar should be evenly spaced.{#isPasted}

In medium window sizes and larger, adjust the padding between controls to create a comfortable layout. This can be achieved by:   
{#isPasted}

* Centering all elements  
  {#isPasted}
* Customizing to center a key action, and aligning other elements to the edges{#isPasted}
On [Material Wiz (web)](https://gm-catalog.corp.google.com/material3/toolbar), the docked toolbar is rounded by default, and can be placed in different parts of the page. Dividers can be used to organize large amounts of items. Only shrink the height and use extra small buttons if vertical space is limited.
#### Floating

The container should only be as big as needed to hold the items inside before reaching the 16dp margin.{#isPasted}

If there's not enough space for all items, put them in an overflow menu in the trailing slot. As the window size expands, more actions can be revealed.

The floating toolbar width can also be capped to keep it smaller and hide more elements.
Vertical toolbars aren't recommended for compact windows.   

They take up a significant area of the screen and may feel visually overwhelming, especially on screens with complex layouts.{#isPasted}

Only use them when the screen is simple or when the toolbar has a few controls.
On web, the docked toolbar is rounded by default, and can be placed in different parts of the page. Dividers can be used to organize large amounts of items. Only shrink the height and use extra small buttons if vertical space is limited.
### Presentation
In larger window sizes, floating toolbars can be aligned to opposite edges of the screen so they're easy to reach and group similar actions. For example, consider placing the undo and redo actions in one toolbar, and editing controls like highlight, erase, and select in another. Stylistic differences can help emphasize each toolbar's purpose and clarify hierarchy.
Don't use multiple toolbars in compact windows. There typically isn't enough room on screen. {#isPasted}

Instead, use one toolbar for all actions.
Actions at the trailing edge of the toolbar can collapse into an overflow menu at smaller window sizes, and become visible again at larger sizes.
### Right-to-left languages

In right-to-left (RTL) languages, mirror individual items that need it, like icons and text direction. If the order of actions is important, flip the order of the actions as well.
Behavior
--------
### Scrolling {#isPasted}

Docked toolbars can either remain on the screen during scroll, or animate offscreen.
Floating toolbars can remain on the screen, animate offscreen, or collapse into a single, high-emphasis action on scroll.
On Jetpack Compose, the floating toolbar can collapse to a FAB or key action on scroll.
Don't collapse actions and scroll at the same time.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------

People should be able to do the following with assistive technology:

* Navigate and activate any actions in the toolbar

* Select a destination from a menu

* Activate a back button

* Maintain access to toolbar controls when the content is scrolled or collapsed
Interaction \& style {#isPasted}
--------------------------------

The toolbar has no interactions by default. All interactions are with the elements placed inside.
**Touch**{#isPasted}

* When tapping on an icon button in the toolbar, a touch ripple appears, indicating interaction feedback.{#isPasted}
**Cursor**{#isPasted}

* When hovered, the hover state provides a visual cue to the user that the element is interactive.   
  {#isPasted}
* When clicked (in both active and inactive states), a ripple appears, showing the user feedback.{#isPasted}
### Initial focus {#isPasted}

Focus lands on the first interactive element. {#isPasted}

Use **Tab** to navigate through all other actions.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------

{#isPasted}{#isPasted}

|      Keys      |                Actions                |
| Tab or Arrows  | Navigate between interactive elements |
| Space or Enter |     Activate the focused element      |
|----------------|---------------------------------------|
### Labeling elements {#isPasted}

On web, the toolbar container should have the **toolbar** role. {#isPasted}

On mobile, it can be a generic container.

All actions inside the toolbar should follow their respective accessibility guidelines.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Two expressive variants: docked toolbar and floating toolbar

* Use the vibrant color style for greater emphasis

* Can display a wide variety of control types, like buttons, icon buttons, and text fields

* Can be paired with FABs to emphasize certain actions

* Don't show at the same time as a navigation bar
Availability \& resources
-------------------------
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------
In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with new styling that includes glass and other visual changes. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
**Nov 2025**   

GM3 toolbars now support glass effects. [More details on iOS tab](/google-material-3/pages/toolbars/ios)
GM3 Expressive update {#isPasted}
---------------------------------
The **bottom app bar** is no longer recommended and should be replaced with the **docked toolbar** , which functions similarly, but is shorter and has more flexibility. The **floating toolbar** was created for more versatility, greater amounts of actions, and more variety in where it's placed. [More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
Variants and naming:

* Added **docked toolbar** to replace **bottom app bar**

  * Size: Shorter height

  * Color: Standard or vibrant

  * Flexibility: More layout and element options

* Added **floating toolbar** with the following configurations:

  * Layout: Horizontal or vertical

  * Color: Standard or vibrant

  * Flexibility: Can hold many elements and components. Can be paired with FAB.

* **Bottom app bar**is still available, but not recommended
Differences from GM2
--------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Elevation: No shadow
* Layout: Container height is taller and the FAB is now contained within the app bar container
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## XR
star  
Note:  
This is a rapidly changing space. Guidelines are primarily intended for designers at this time. Find what's implemented in code in the [design kit](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/branch/cVjQvQ0moD8wkPWH2rn2c6/GM3-Design-Kit?m=auto&node-id=255618-55642&t=YXTHxmkArej6ABxW-1%20%203P:%20https://www.figma.com/community/file/1035203688168086460%20%20XR%20developer%20documentation%E2%80%A81P%20+%203P:%20https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/foundations%20%20orbiter%201P%20+%203P:%20https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/spatial-ui#orbiters%20%20floating%20toolbar%201P:%20/google-material-3/pages/toolbars/overview%20%203P:%20https://carbon.googleplex.com/m3/pages/toolbars/overview).
Extended reality (XR) interfaces have special design requirements, like showing apps in 3D space. Material has an XR-specific toolbar with custom specs and guidance. Read [XR developer documentation](https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/foundations) for more details.
Variants
--------

There is one toolbar orbiter. It closely aligns with the floating toolbar. It can be configured to be horizontal or vertical.
Anatomy
-------
Color \& elevation
------------------

XR uses color to communicate the elevation of UI elements and orbiters. With [spatial elevation](https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/spatial-ui#spatial-elevation), the toolbar displays above the spatial panel on the Z-axis. Elevated toolbars can use any of these color options:
Measurements
------------
Usage
-----

A toolbar can appear in an orbiter for a more immersive experience. Currently, this spatial capability is only available in full space. In home space, use a regular toolbar on the same plane as the body content to mimic a 2D experience.
Behavior
--------
### Local context (recommended)

When placed in local context, the toolbar orbiter is centered at the bottom of the spatial panel it controls. {#isPasted}

It repositions in response to layout or content changes.
### Global context

When placed in global context, the toolbar orbiter is centered at the bottom of the app. {#isPasted}

It stays anchored to the app during layout or content changes.

<br />
### Expand \& collapse

Toolbar orbiters with more than five items can expand and collapse to reveal or hide additional content.{#isPasted}

When a toolbar orbiter expands, it stays within the bounds of the adjacent spatial panel.

Alternatively, more complex toolbars can be split into multiple toolbars.
### Additional toolbars

In some cases, full space apps can have more than one toolbar orbiter, placed in either global or local context.
Placement
---------
### Offset \& inset positioning

In full space, a toolbar orbiter can be positioned adjacent to or overlap a spatial panel.
### Horizontal alignment
### Vertical alignment
### Spatial panel alignment

By default, toolbar orbiters are center-aligned to the spatial panel. Their placement can be adjusted to accommodate specific user needs, such as improved ergonomics or [right-to-left (RTL) languages](/google-material-3/pages/understanding-layout/bidirectionality-rtl).
Avoid placing a vertical toolbar orbiter between spatial panels.   

This negatively affects the interface structure and can make it difficult to find.
Accessibility considerations
----------------------------

[XR accessibility](https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/get-started#make-app) guidelines are still evolving. XR toolbars should follow applicable Material [toolbar accessibility standards](/google-material-3/pages/toolbars/accessibility).

## Specs
Variants
--------
### Baseline variant
The baseline bottom app bar is no longer recommended. It should be replaced with the docked toolbar, which is very similar and more flexible.
|     Variant      |    GM3    |                  GM3 Expressive                  |
|------------------|-----------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Docked toolbar   | --        | Available                                        |
| Floating toolbar | --        | Available                                        |
| Bottom app bar   | Available | Not recommended. Use **docked toolbar** instead. |
star  
Note:  
Implementation differs per platform. On Jetpack Compose, the floating toolbar is a separate component from the docked toolbar and bottom app bar.
Configurations {#isPasted}
--------------------------
|        Category         |    Configuration     |             GM3             | GM3 Expressive |
|-------------------------|----------------------|-----------------------------|----------------|
| Color                   | Standard (default)   | Available as bottom app bar | Available      |
| Color                   | Vibrant              | --                          | Available      |
| Floating toolbar layout | Horizontal (default) | --                          | Available      |
| Floating toolbar layout | Vertical             | --                          | Available      |
| Other elements          | With FAB             | Available as bottom app bar | Available\*    |
star  
Note:  
\*Implementation differs per platform. On Jetpack Compose, floating toolbar with FAB is [fully supported](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/components/floatingtoolbar.md?cl=head). On other platforms, each component needs to be added separately.
Tokens \& specs
---------------

Browse the component elements, attributes, tokens, and their values. [Jump to baseline bottom app bar specs](/google-material-3/pages/toolbars/specs#6fb748db-1e9c-4263-8f4b-4baba54d9486)
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
### Flexibility \& slots {#isPasted}
When configuring a toolbar, think of it as a container with several slots. {#isPasted}

Each slot can be a different element. The most common elements are icon buttons, buttons, and text fields.
Color {#isPasted}
-----------------
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. Learn more about design tokens
### Standard {#isPasted}
### Vibrant {#isPasted}
Measurements {#isPasted}
------------------------
By default all toolbars are 64dp high, center-aligned, have equal padding between items, and have a minimum outside padding of 16dp.
### Docked toolbar {#isPasted}
### Floating toolbar {#isPasted}
Bottom app bar (baseline)
-------------------------
### Tokens \& specs
Bottom app bar tokens are in one token set.
### Color
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For designers, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. In implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. Learn more about design tokens
### Measurements
### Common layouts
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Google Symbols
Google Symbols is the icon font used for all system icons.

## Symbols in Figma
The Google Symbols library can be added directly to Figma files. Here's how to turn it on, accept updates, and use Google Symbols in your Figma designs.
Using Google Symbols in the GM3 Design Kit {#isPasted}
------------------------------------------------------
The GDP Systems Design team created this walkthrough about how to use Symbols in Figma. Instructions are also written below.
How to get Google Symbols in your GM3 components {#isPasted}
------------------------------------------------------------
### Add Google Symbols library {#isPasted}

Select "Libraries" from the Figma main menu button.   

Search for "Google Symbols library." Select "Add to file."
### Accept GM3 Design Kit updates {#isPasted}

Select the library icon with the blue notification badge in the toolbar.  

Select "Update all" to update all instances in your file.  

To review each change, select the specific change from the Updates list, and it will show a side-by-side comparison. Once you've reviewed it, update the instance.
### How to fix overrides {#isPasted}

Updating the GM3 Design Kit library won't affect component overrides. This means you'll still have the old icons, not Google Symbols.
Reset the icon property from the instance options menu. The icon should start using Google Symbols at the same size as your previous icon.  

You can now easily swap between symbols from the Google Symbols library.
How to customize variable fonts {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------------
With the symbol selected, in the text panel select the 3-horizontal-dot icon for type settings.  

In the modal that appears, go to the "Variable" tab.  

There you will find sliders for weight, fill, and grade, which you can adjust to change the appearance of the symbol.
How to insert symbols {#isPasted}
---------------------------------
### Drag and drop {#isPasted}

Open the assets panel on the left side. Shortcut: Alt/Option+2  

Use the search function to quickly find the symbols you're looking for and narrow down your search.  

Drag the icon you'd like to use and drop it into your working file.
### Copy and paste {#isPasted}

Open the library file as a new tab.  

Browse and select the icons fromthe Google Symbols page.  

Copy the icons and paste them into your working file.
How to resize symbols {#isPasted}
---------------------------------
Access the scale tool using the shortcut "K" on your keyboard.  

Adjust the size of the bounding box of the symbol.
Make sure "Set optical size automatically" is selected in the type settings.  

Optical sizing for symbols is only supported for sizes between 20dp and 48dp.
How to swap symbols {#isPasted}
-------------------------------
Individual symbols can be swapped, or static icons can be updated to symbols. If you're converting an icon to a symbol, the size will reset to 24dp. There are three ways to swap symbols into your Figma file.
### Select symbol instance directly and swap {#isPasted}

Select the symbol instance and open the instance swap menu.  

Search for a new symbol from the Google Symbols library, and select it to replace the previous one.
### Drag symbol from the asset panel to swap icon {#isPasted}

Open the asset panel, and search for a symbol in the Google Symbols library.  

Drag the symbol onto the canvas and hover over the target symbol while holding the **option/alt** key or **option/alt** **+ cmd/control** for nested icons. This will replace the previous one.
### Icon properties selection in components {#isPasted}

Select a component. If the icon properties have been enabled, an option to replace a nested icon will be available.  

Select the icon name to search for a new symbol from the Google Symbols library. Select the new symbol to replace it.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Applying icons
Customizing Symbols
-------------------

Google Symbols have four adjustable stylistic variable font attributes called **axes**. An axis is a typographic term referring to the attribute of a symbol that can be altered to create visual variations.

Each style symbol contains four axes: **weight, fill, grade,** and**optical size.**
### Weight {#isPasted}

Weight defines the symbol's stroke weight, with a range of weights between thin (100) and bold (700). Weight can also affect the overall size of the symbol.
### Use the safe weight range for better legibility {#isPasted}

The 300 to 500 range is generally safe for baseline components, with 400 being the best default weight. If you need to choose a lighter or heavier weight for your icons, be sure you use that weight consistently across your product for visual cohesion.
### Fill {#isPasted}

Fill gives you the ability to transition from a more outlined style to a reversed or more filled style. {#isPasted}

A fill attribute can be used to convey a state of transition, such as unfilled and filled states. Values range from 0 to 1, with 1 being completely filled. Along with weight, fill is a primary attribute that impacts the overall look of a symbol.
### Grade {#isPasted}

Weight and grade affect a symbol's thickness. Adjustments to grade are more granular than adjustments to weight and have a smaller impact on the size of the symbol.{#isPasted}

Grade is also available in Google Sans Flex. Grade levels between Google Sans Flex and symbols can be matched for a harmonious visual effect. For example, if the text font has a -25 grade value, the symbols can match it with a suitable value of -25.
Grade can also compensate for **visual bleed**, which is when images can look bigger or smaller depending on the color contrast. To match the apparent icon size, the default grade for a dark icon on a light background is 0, and -25 for a light icon on a dark background.
To make strokes heavier and more emphasized, use positive value grade, such as when representing an active icon state.
### Optical sizes {#isPasted}

Optical sizes range from 20dp to 48dp.

For the image to look the same at different sizes, the stroke weight (thickness) changes as the icon size scales. Optical size offers a way to automatically adjust the stroke weight when you increase or decrease the symbol size.
Traditionally, icons are resized from a 24dp source vector, resulting in a large scaled icon that's too heavy compared to the original. With the optical size axis, you can maintain the stroke weight (thickness) as the icon size grows.
### Round

The round axis in Google Symbols can be used to change forms from sharp and angular to fully rounded. Values run from 0 to 100.
By default, Google Symbols start with a roundness value of 50. This differs from the variable typeface GSF, which starts with a roundness value of zero. These different values are intended, and still produce a matched icon and text.  

Generally, check that optical weight, rather than numerical value, is aligned between icon and text.
Using Google Symbols with typography
------------------------------------

Google Symbols are designed with similar considerations to typefaces, and often appear alongside text. Choosing the right icon set can tie the content of an interface together, enhancing the cohesive branded feel of your product.
Accessibility {#isPasted}
-------------------------
### Icons with a label text {#isPasted}

Label text provides short, meaningful descriptions that are helpful when symbols are more abstract.   

Navigation items must be paired with labels to ensure that they remain clear and accessible to all users.
### Small icons {#isPasted}

Google Symbols can scale up or down in size without a loss of fidelity. Simple symbols, like stars for ratings or spark icons for AI-generated responses, can be used on their own at any size, as long as they remain identifiable.{#isPasted}

Other symbols should have an accompanying text label below 20dp to ensure their meaning is clear and to maintain accessibility. These symbols include:  
{#isPasted}

* Complex icons, which are highly detailed or have multiple parts  
  {#isPasted}
* Icons with a key action, which are essential to using the product{#isPasted}
### Target size {#isPasted}

Adequate space should surround icons to allow legibility and interaction.
Symbols of 24dp should have a target size of 48dp by default.
When a mouse and keyboard are the primary input methods, measurements may be condensed to accommodate denser layouts.

A 20dp size symbol can use a target size of 40dp.
Localizing icons
----------------
To make sure iconography translates effectively in local markets, test it across age groups, cultures, and languages, and follow these best practices:

* Use labels when icons and symbols are more abstract

* Remember that navigation items must have labels for clarity and accessibility

* Consider tech knowledge: people who use the internet a lot may have different understandings of icons than people who use the internet less

Take the quiz at [go/icon-quiz](http://go/icon-quiz) to test your icon knowledge.
### Cultural influence of colors and symbols {#isPasted}

Color carries cultural significance and can convey different emotions in different cultures. White is commonly associated with purity in western cultures but symbolizes mourning in some eastern cultures.  

Consider cultural interpretations of symbols. In many western cultures, owls represent wisdom, while some eastern cultures view them as a negative omen. When using or creating symbols, be mindful that their meanings can vary significantly across cultures.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Google Symbols has replaced Google Material Icons. Get Google Symbols at [go/icons](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons).  
  {#isPasted}
* Use the Google Symbols variable font to enable dynamic styling in product ([download](https://fonts.corp.google.com/download?family=Google+Symbols))  
  {#isPasted}
* You can change the weight, fill, optical size, grade, and roundedness of variable font icons  
  {#isPasted}
* Static exports of Google Symbols are still available for environments that temporarily require static icons  
  {#isPasted}
* Request new system icons at [go/newicons](https://b.corp.google.com/issues/new?component=1489343&template=1923678){#isPasted}
* Product icons are available at [go/product-icons](https://logos.teams.x20web.corp.google.com/#/). Google Symbols doesn't contain product icons. [Learn more about](/google-material-3/pages/product-icons/overview)[product icons](/google-material-3/pages/product-icons/overview){#isPasted}
Availability \& resources
-------------------------

|                                                                                                    Design                                                                                                    | [go/icons](http://go/icons) | fonts-team@ | Available |
|                                                                                                    Design                                                                                                    |
|                                                                                                    Design                                                                                                    |
|                                                                                                    Design                                                                                                    |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------|-------------|-----------|
| [Google Symbols variable font](https://fonts.corp.google.com/download?family=Google+Symbols) (direct download)                                                                                               | fonts-team@                 | Available   |
| [Google Symbols library](https://www.google.com/url?q=https://www.figma.com/file/6iUAbBfL2Taf0QHvgn679m/Google-Symbols-library?node-id%3D22106%253A9&sa=D&source=buganizer&usg=AOvVaw2dDhwZiyrlVOMnyiB32UTj) | fonts-team@                 | Available   |
| [Icon keyline template (ZIP)](https://storage.googleapis.com/material-io-design/downloads/gm_icon_template.ai.zip)                                                                                           | fonts-team@                 | Available   |
| [Android](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/g3doc/icons/android.md?cl=head)                                                                                           | material-android-team@      | Available   |
| [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/foundations/styles/fundamentals/Icons/index.md?cl=head&polyglot=objective-c)                                                                | material-iOS-team@          | Available   |
| [Flutter](http://go/gm3-flutter#icons)                                                                                                                                                                       | material-flutter-team@      | In Progress |
