<br />
What's new {#isPasted}
----------------------
### Copy \& paste customized Google Symbols {#isPasted}

You can now copy and paste icons from [go/icons](http://go/icons) into workspace products. Once you search for and select the desired icon, options will appear in the right-hand panel to resize, recolor, and copy the customized icon to clipboard.
### Google Symbols has replaced Google Material Icons {#isPasted}

Google Material Icons have been deprecated. Google Symbols is now the standard. We recommend updating all icons to Google Symbols.  

Rather than static instances, like the previous icons, Google Symbols is a variable font, allowing for change and fluidity in shape and styling. Variable fonts have technical advantages, such as reducing font family file size.  

Google products should access the latest Google Symbols at [go/icons](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons) or from [within the Figma library.](http://goto.google.com/googlesymbols-library)
### Adjustable axes {#isPasted}

Google Symbols have four adjustable stylistic variable font attributes called axes. These axes are: weight, fill, grade, optical size. An additional axis for roundness is available on Figma and through the API.{#isPasted}

<br />
### Pair with the brand type {#isPasted}

Google Symbols are designed to be paired with the Google Sans font family as the icon counterpart to the branded typeface.{#isPasted}

<br />
### Variable fonts and symbols {#isPasted}

Google Sans is now available as a variable font. Google Sans Flex is a more customizable version of Google Sans, currently available on a limited basis.  

Google Symbols are offered as a variable font, just like Google Sans and Google Sans Flex. They can be customized using the same values, creating consistent new looks and feels between icons and text.{#isPasted}

<br />
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Selecting icons
Selecting icons
---------------
Find and get all icons directly from the [Google Symbols](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons) library. You can browse all icons in Figma or at [go/icons](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons). Here are some common recommended icons, and some best practices for searching the Google Symbols library.
### Common icons
Icons are listed by most popular on [go/icons](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons). This generally overlaps with the most common icons used across all Google product areas.
### Commonly misused icons
### Icons only for iOS
The following iOS icons need to be used instead of Google Symbols.  
More information can be found in the [Material iOS icons guidance](http://goto.google.com/material-ios-icons#which-icons-should-you-use).
How to search for icons
-----------------------
Search for icons by their intended action, like "add," "edit," or "search." This ensures you're using the best icon for your situation. Searching by the icon's appearance, like "magnifying glass," can work, but is not recommended.{#isPasted}

Find icons in this order:  
{#isPasted}

1. Search your design system in Carbon  
   {#isPasted}
2. Browse by icon popularity on go/icons  
   {#isPasted}
3. Search for individual icon by action in go/icons or Figma{#isPasted}
### Start with your team's design system {#isPasted}

Start with your team's design system. Here you may find a sticker sheet containing relevant or approved icons.  

These pages may also offer product-specific guidance that differs from general icon guidance.
It's possible that within a product group's Carbon page an interaction or feature is mentioned that has no icon. If your team's icon is not available in Google Symbols, [request for the icon to be added](https://b.corp.google.com/issues/new?component=1489343&template=1923678).
### Browse by icon popularity on go/icons {#isPasted}

Icons are initially sorted by popularity, so browse the collection to find an icon that matches your use case. The most popular icons tend to be basic navigation actions, like Home or Menu.
When possible, use the same icon for the same action, regardless of product. The Home icon for Ads should be the same Home icon as for Google Play, for example.
### Search for individual icon by action {#isPasted}

When looking for an individual icon, the best way to search is by desired action. For example "Close," rather than "X," or "Logout," instead of "arrow."
You may still find icons through visual description, due to icon metadata. For example, "Delete" can be found by searching "garbage" which describes its appearance. However, this can be unpredictable, and is not recommended.
Use the same icon to represent an action everywhere it appears in your product. For example, if "Bookmark" is used to save an item for later, "Bookmark" should consistently perform this action.
Accessibility
-------------
### Alt text for icons
Figma has a Greenlines plugin that can suggest alt text labels for icons in buttons.   

You can learn more about Greenlines at [go/greenlines-figma-plugin](http://goto.google.com/greenlines-figma-plugin).  
Install Greenlines for Figma at [go/greenlines-figma-v1](http://goto.google.com/greenlines-figma-v1).
A screen recording of the label attachment process is viewable [here](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1ssTQPsCEyO_Kc3kSt6YgUIkptcBIObLyhym9ThyNaKs/edit?pli=1&resourcekey=0-YUpmrRnaE_GktQ0fO207NQ#slide=id.g2a1fd2a5535_0_57).
[Labelizer](https://labelizer.googleplex.com/) contains alt text for many icons in Google Symbols, but not every icon. This alt text describes the purpose and function of an icon. Labelizer information is planned to be merged with symbol information on [go/icons](https://labelizer.googleplex.com/) in 2024. View labels at [go/labels](https://labelizer.googleplex.com/products).
### Product icons
Branded product icons are available at [go/product-icons](https://logos.teams.x20web.corp.google.com/#/). They're not included in Google Symbols. [Learn about product icons](/google-material-3/pages/product-icons/designing-icons)
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Designing icons
Designing new icons
-------------------
Before designing a new icon, first check [go/icons](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons) for a suitable alternative. To design a new icon, follow the principles and instructions on this page, then make a new request at [go/newicons](https://b.corp.google.com/issues/new?component=1489343&template=1923678). View the [icon program](https://fonts.corp.google.com/documentation/icon-program#overview) for more details, including information on implementation.  

New icons take about 4 weeks. The Fonts team will decide if an existing icon could work, or if a new icon is needed. If a new icon is needed, the Fonts team will add it to the Google Symbols library.
Design principles {#isPasted}
-----------------------------

Icons are an essential element of any interface, packing an informative punch into a small form factor. They're designed to be simple, modern, friendly, and sometimes quirky. To ensure consistency and readability, their limited size means that each icon must strictly adhere to guidance while still expressing essential characteristics.
Icon sizes and layout {#isPasted}
---------------------------------
### Standard (Baseline) icon size {#isPasted}

Standard icons are displayed as 24dp x 24dp. For pixel-perfect accuracy, create icons for viewing at 100% scale.
### Additional optical icon sizes {#isPasted}

Icons support additional sizes: 20dp, 40dp, and 48dp, with 20dp primarily for desktop, dense layouts, and small scale visuals, and 40dp and 48dp optimized for display or headline type, plus larger screen sizes.
### Standard (Baseline) icon layout {#isPasted}

Icon content should remain inside of the **live area**, which is the region of an image that is unlikely to be hidden from view (such as an area where sidebars appear upon scrolling).

If additional visual weight is needed, content may extend into the padding between the live area and the **trim area** (the complete size of a graphic). No parts of the icon should extend outside of the trim area.
Grid and keyline shapes {#isPasted}
-----------------------------------
### Icon design template

If your design requires an icon that isn't covered by the over 2,000 variations in [Google Font's icon library](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons), you may want to create your own. [Download this 24dp keyline template](https://storage.googleapis.com/material-io-design/downloads/gm_icon_template.ai.zip)\* (ZIP file) to design custom icons in Adobe Illustrator.

*\*This template is available under* [*Apache 2.0*](https://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.html)*. By downloading this file, you agree to the* [*Google Terms of Service*](https://policies.google.com/terms)*. The* [*Google Privacy Policy*](https://policies.google.com/privacy)*describes how data is handled in this service.*
### Icon grid and keyline {#isPasted}

The icon grid establishes clear rules for the consistent, but flexible, positioning of graphic elements.

Keyline shapes are the foundation of the grid. By using these core shapes as guidelines, you can maintain consistent visual proportions across system icons.
Icon metrics {#isPasted}
------------------------
### Anatomy {#isPasted}
### Corners {#isPasted}

Corner radii are 2dp by default. For the outlined style symbols, interior corners are square, not rounded. For shapes 2dp wide or less, stroke corners shouldn't be rounded.

For the rounded style symbols, both exterior and interior corner radii are rounded and for the sharp style symbols, both exterior and interior corners radii reduce from 2dp to 0dp.
### Weight and stroke {#isPasted}

The recommended stroke weight for icons is 2dp or the regular weight (400), which includes curves, angles, and both interior and exterior strokes. Material Symbols can provide a range of weights between thin (100) and bold (700).
### Complex icon shapes {#isPasted}

If an icon requires complex details, subtle adjustments can be made to improve its legibility. These adjustments are referred to as optical corrections. Any optical correction should use the geometric forms on which all other icons are based, without skewing or distorting those shapes.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# AI overview
Use these recommended generative AI colors, icons, components, and writing patterns in all Google products

## Overview
Upcoming brand update {#isPasted}
---------------------------------

The Gemini brand is updating soon company-wide. Gemini product will begin launching updates on August 1, 2025. Other 1P teams can start using the new brand August 8, 2025.

Launching before August 8? See [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook). Launching after August 8? See [go/geminiplaybook2.0](http://go/geminiplaybook2.0). Get support at [go/gemini-brand-update-faq](http://go/gemini-brand-update-faq).
Every AI experience is built upon a specific combination of styles and design elements showcasing Google's AI-powered features. These visual cues help build user trust, understanding, and expectations of actions performed by artificial intelligence, machine learning, or intelligent automation. Google AI encompasses Gemini, which has its own branding and color palette, generative AI, and other AI experiences, models, and platforms.{#isPasted}

AI guidance was developed through an ongoing partnership across Google Design Platform, Creative Lab, and product teams across Google. Reach out to [go/ai-brand-inquiry](http://go/ai-brand-inquiry) with questions.
Resources {#isPasted}
---------------------
|                                   **Type**                                    |                           **Resource**                           |                  **Contact**                  |              **Status**               |
|                                    Design                                     | Pre-August 8th [Gemini brand playbook](http://go/geminiplaybook) | [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook) | Available for pre-August 8th launches |
|                                    Design                                     |
|                                    Design                                     |
|                                    Design                                     |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| [AI icons](http://go/genai-icons) (Fonts)                                     | [go/xpa-ai-review](http://go/xpa-ai-review)                      | Available                                     |
| Post-August 8th [Gemini brand visual guidelines](http://go/geminiplaybook2.0) | [go/geminiplaybook2.0](http://go/geminiplaybook2.0)              | Available for post-August 8th launches        |
| [Gemini Brand Update FAQ](http://go/gemini-brand-update-faq)                  | [go/gemini-brand-update-faq](http://go/gemini-brand-update-faq)  | Available                                     |
What's new {#isPasted}
----------------------

**May 2025**{#isPasted}

* Upcoming Gemini brand update announced
* For launches before August 8, see [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook). For launches on or after August 8, see [go/geminiplaybook2.0](http://go/geminiplaybook2.0). Get support at [go/gemini-brand-update-faq](http://go/gemini-brand-update-faq).

**May 2024**{#isPasted}

* Launch of Gemini branding and guidance at [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook)
* Updates to AI color guidance, including GenAI baseline and GenAI dynamic color
* Deprecation of AI Color Workbook
Gemini vs Google AI
-------------------
Gemini has evolved into an ecosystem of models, products, and platforms that represent our most capable AI. Gemini represents our top 10-20% most advanced generative AI experiences across Google only made possible by a Gemini model.{#isPasted}

Google AI encompasses more than just Gemini or generative AI, and will continue to coexist with Gemini. Not all products utilizing Gemini models are branded as Gemini, and not all products specify their model or relationship to Gemini.

Use [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook) for the latest on Gemini branding, colors, icons, messaging, and marketing needs.
AI branding
-----------
Google's AI brand is communicated through the specific colors, gradients, motion patterns, and icons described in this guidance. For the latest on Gemini AI branding, refer to [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook).{#isPasted}

For information on how AI products fit into Google's overall brand architecture, reach out to [go/ai-brand-inquiry](http://go/ai-brand-inquiry).
Aspects of AI UX {#isPasted}
----------------------------
### Icons {#isPasted}

Feature icons represent actions powered by generative AI while product icons represent standalone AI-first products. [See AI icon guidance](/google-material-3/pages/genai-icons)

Additional updates to AI feature icons that reflect the new spark silhouette are coming soon, and will automatically update when using the Google Symbols font.
### Writing

Writing for AI products is complicated and nuanced. It's crucial to be transparent with users, explain and guide in context, and empower and educate users. [Get started with AI writing guidance](/google-material-3/pages/genai-writing)
### Components {#isPasted}

#### Buttons

Action components like buttons can signal AI-powered outcomes with a single-spark feature icon and descriptive label text.
#### Chips {#isPasted}

Use chips to suggest AI prompts in a chat or initiate AI-powered actions.
### Color \& gradient {#isPasted}

When users see a UI element with a color gradient, they should immediately recognize it as an active, AI-powered generative process. AI color is only applied to active states, not enabled states. {#isPasted}

GenAI baseline color theming for in-product use is based on Gemini branding. For the latest on Gemini AI color and gradient, refer to [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook). [For more on Material's AI color guidance, see the color tab](/google-material-3/pages/genai-color-gradient).
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://goto.google.com/gm3-bug)

## Branding
The AI landscape is rapidly changing. Having Google **AI features show up the same way** across products amplifies user trust and understanding by setting consistent user expectations across the product ecosystem.
Build with official AI styles and guidelines {#isPasted}
--------------------------------------------------------
Google AI encompasses more than just Gemini or generative AI. Ensure that your product supports Google's bigger AI story by using the established generative AI colors, gradients, icons, and other guidance.{#isPasted}

For Gemini-specific branding, naming, and messaging, see [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook), which details the branding and color system created to articulate the Gemini product ecosystem. Submit additional questions to [go/ai-brand-inquiry](http://go/ai-brand-inquiry).
Only highlight generative AI experiences initiated by users {#isPasted}
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Not every feature that uses AI and ML needs to show it. Generative AI styles and guidelines were designed for experiences where the AI needs to be invoked by a user to create something, like a magic erase feature used to edit the background of a photo. If you're not sure whether your feature should use generative AI styles, reach out to [go/ai-brand-inquiry](http://go/ai-brand-inquiry).
### Example features that **should**use generative AI styles {#isPasted}
### Example features that **shouldn't**use generative AI styles {#isPasted}
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug){#isPasted}


# Static color schemes
Static color schemes are ideal for branded products that should have a consistent, uniform design

## GM3 baseline
**GM3 baseline** is the default static color scheme for Google products. It uses accessible color pairings and includes colors for both light and dark themes.

**With the baseline color scheme, end-users see**

* An accessible UI with static colors

* A product that "looks like Google"
Baseline colors
---------------

Get baseline colors in Figma using the Material Theme Builder.
Baseline color tokens
---------------------
Design with baseline
--------------------
### Use the GM3 Design Kits and baseline colors in new design files

1. Open your Figma file. [Enable the GM3 Design Kit](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-design-kit/tab-1#33c6f0cd-776c-4d24-a406-5ee98d1b87f9) for [Android](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Android-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=jLHSBhOAsN80st9Y-7), [iOS](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=jLHSBhOAsN80st9Y-7), or [Web](https://www.figma.com/design/lcRw8MOK5FMHmNl820IMAo/GM3-Web-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=jLHSBhOAsN80st9Y-7) in your Assets panel.

2. Compose screens and layouts using Material Components from the design kit.

3. Apply GM3 baseline color roles to custom components and UI elements by hovering on the element's color property in the Design panel on the right of the screen and selecting the **Style** icon (four dots). This opens a selection dialog.

4. Search for "Baseline Blue" to see the baseline color roles.

5. Select the baseline color role that most closely matches the use case and intent (see [Color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles) for more information on what color to use where).

6. Repeat until all custom elements are using GM3 baseline color roles.

<br />

### Apply baseline colors to an existing file

**First, get the GM3 baseline blue colors into your file**

1. Open your Figma design file. Select the Actions menu (or Ctrl/Command+K). ([screenshot](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/7t3GGj4a29m9gXp))

2. Find the [Material Theme Builder 1P plugin](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1020820278641129628/Material-Theme-Builder-1P) and select **Run**. This will open a plugin dialog showing the default color scheme, including Core colors and Extended colors.

3. Open the plugin's **Settings** (gear icon at lower right of dialog) and select the checkbox for **Generate State Layers** . This makes sure there are color for the state layers needed to design interactions. [Learn more about state layers](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/state-layers)

4. Navigate out of settings.

5. With the Current Theme dropdown at the top of the dialog, select **Baseline Blue.**

6. Select the frames or components in your file and then hit **Swap** in the bottom right of the dialog. This will automatically update the colors for any GM3 Design Kit components.

**Then, update any remaining non-GM3 color styles**

1. Manually change any hex values or non-GM3 color styles by selecting all and looking through the Selection colors in the Design panel on the right of the screen ([screenshot](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/BeqKaCnMMoGgbqB)).

2. Any colors that don't start with "Baseline Blue" need to be replaced.

3. Hover on a non-GM3 color row in the Design panel and select the **Style** icon (four dots). This opens a selection dialog.

4. Search for "Baseline Blue" to see the baseline color roles.

5. Select the baseline color role that most closely matches that color's use case (see [Color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles) for more information on what color to use where) and select **Use style** to apply it to the selected objects.

6. Repeat until all non-GM3 colors in the file have been replaced with GM3 baseline color roles.

**Need to make adjustments to the scheme? Check out** [**Advanced customizations**](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/overview)
Develop with baseline
---------------------
* [Android Views](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/g3doc/components/color.md?cl=head#design-api-documentation)
* [Flutter](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/mobile/flutter/google_material/g3doc/material3.md?cl=head#color)
* [Jetpack Compose](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/styles/color.md?cl=head)
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Custom brand
In a brand-based static scheme, the colors are hand-picked by your team to align with your product's brand color. Brand-based schemes are entirely created and maintained by your team, so this approach requires a larger investment of time and effort. Only a limited number of Google apps will require a brand-based color scheme.

**With a brand color scheme, end-users see**{#isPasted}

* An accessible UI with static colors
* A product that "looks like its brand"
Create a custom brand color scheme
----------------------------------
1. Open your Figma file. Select the **Actions** menu (or Ctrl/Command+K). ([screenshot](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/7t3GGj4a29m9gXp))

2. Find the [Material Theme Builder 1P plugin](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1020820278641129628/Material-Theme-Builder-1P) and select **Run**. This will open a plugin dialog showing the default color scheme, including Core colors and Extended colors.

3. Open the plugin's **Settings** (gear icon at lower right of dialog) and select the checkboxes for both **New theme color diagram** and **Generate State Layers** . This will create a handy visualization of your branded color scheme and also generate state layers essential for designing interactions. [Learn more about state layers](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/state-layers)

4. Navigate out of settings

5. Open the Current Theme dropdown at the top of the dialog and select **+ ADD NEW THEME**

6. Give your theme a short name (this name will become the prefix of your color roles in Figma)

7. Select **ADD THEME**

8. With **Custom** selected, select **Primary**. This opens a dialog prompting you to select a custom source color.

9. Enter the Hex value for your brand color and hit **Apply**.

This will generate a full custom color scheme. You can use the scheme as-is or repeat steps 5 and 6 to set custom sources for the Secondary, Tertiary, Error, Neutral, and Neutral Variant colors.

**Want to further adjust your brand color scheme? Check out** [**Advanced customizations**](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/overview)
Design with brand colors
------------------------
### Use brand colors in new design files {#isPasted}

1. Create your Figma file. [Enable the GM3 Design Kit](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-design-kit/tab-1#33c6f0cd-776c-4d24-a406-5ee98d1b87f9) in your Assets panel.
2. Copy your scheme's color diagram and paste it into the file (this makes the color roles available in the Design panel on the right of the screen as part of your local styles)
3. Apply your brand color roles to custom components and UI elements by hovering on the element's color property in the Design panel on the right of the screen and selecting the **Style** icon (four dots). This opens a selection dialog.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
4. Search for your theme's name to see your brand color roles
5. Select the brand color role that most closely matches the use case and intent (see [Color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles) for more information on what color to use where)
6. Repeat until all custom elements are using your brand color roles

<br />

### Apply brand colors to an existing file or GM3 Design Kit components

**First, get your brand colors into your file**

1. Copy your scheme's color diagram and paste it into the file (this makes the color roles available in the Design panel on the right of the screen as part of your local styles)

**Swap colors in GM3 Design Kit components for your brand colors**

1. Find the [Material Theme Builder 1P plugin](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1020820278641129628/Material-Theme-Builder-1P) and select **Run**. This will open a plugin dialog showing the default color scheme, including Core colors and Extended colors.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. In the Current Theme dropdown at the top of the dialog, select your scheme{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
3. Select the frames or GM3 Design Kit components in your file that need a color update and then hit **Swap** in the bottom right of the dialog. This will automatically update their colors from baseline colors to your brand colors

**Then, update** **any remaining non-brand color styles**{#isPasted}

1. Manually change any hex values or non-brand color styles by selecting all and looking through the Selection colors in the Design panel on the right of the screen ([screenshot](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/BeqKaCnMMoGgbqB))
2. Any colors that don't start with your theme name need to be replaced with a corresponding brand color
3. Hover on a non-brand color row in the Design panel and select the **Style** icon (four dots). This opens a selection dialog.
4. Search for your theme name to see the brand color roles
5. Select the brand color role that most closely matches that color's use case (see [Color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles) for more information on what color to use where) and select **Use style** to apply it to the selected objects
6. Repeat until all non-brand colors in the file have been replaced with brand color roles

<br />

**Need to make adjustments to the scheme? Check out [Advanced customizations](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/overview)**
Develop with brand colors
-------------------------
* [Export your branded color scheme](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/AmknR77JqzeBhKX) from the Material Theme Builder (Available for Jetpack Compose, Android Views, Flutter, Web, or as a JSON file)
* Android: [Customize the default theme](https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/views/theming/themes#CustomizeTheme)
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Divider
Dividers are thin lines that group content in lists or other containers

## Specs
Tokens \& specs
---------------

Browse the component elements, attributes, tokens, and their values.
Color {#isPasted}
-----------------

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview/){#isPasted}
Measurements
------------
|                Attribute                 | Value |
|            Divider full-width            | 100%  |
|        Divider inset left margin         | 16dp  |
|        Divider inset right margin        |  0dp  |
|     Divider middle-inset left margin     | 16dp  |
|    Divider middle-inset right margin     | 16dp  |
| Space between divider \& supporting-text |  4dp  |
|           Divider right margin           |  8dp  |
|          Divider bottom margin           |  8dp  |
|------------------------------------------|-------|
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Dividers are one way to visually group components and create hierarchy. They can also be used to imply nested parent/child relationships.  

The divider can be used in two ways:

1. Full width

2. Inset
Anatomy
-------
A divider is a simple line.
Full-width dividers
-------------------
Use full-width dividers to separate larger sections of unrelated content. They can be used directly on a surface or inside other components like cards or lists.  

Full-width dividers can also separate interactive areas from non-interactive areas.   

They are used to group visual elements together, and indicate when elements are related to each other from an interaction perspective.
Inset dividers
--------------
Use inset dividers to separate related content within a section.  

Inset dividers are equally indented from both sides of the screen by default.
### Using dividers both ways on the same screen

If dividers are used both ways in a UI, they must reinforce the hierarchy of information within different sections.

1. To separate a different kind of content, use a full-width divider

2. To separate nested content items, use inset dividers
List items with repetitive formats may not require an inset divider, in which using only the margin between items is acceptable.
Vertical divider
----------------
A vertical divider can be used to arrange content on a larger screen, such as separating paragraph text from video or imagery media.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS. [Apple's Human Interface Guidelines](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/components/) include additional iOS best practices and platform considerations.
Not supported on iOS
--------------------
The divider is not supported on iOS.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Dividers are decorative elements, which have no contrast minimums.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Make dividers visible but not bold{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Only use dividers if items can't be grouped with open space
* Use dividers to group things, not separate individual items
Resources {#isPasted}
---------------------
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Configurations: Ability to have vertical dividers
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)


# Buttons
Buttons prompt most actions in a UI

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.{#isPasted}

[Apple's Human Interface Guidelines for buttons](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/components/menus-and-actions/buttons) include additional iOS best practices and platform considerations.
Resources
---------
|                                                     **Type**                                                     |                                                           **Resource**                                                            |    **Contact**     | **Status** |
|                                                      Design                                                      | [iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=52172-48193&t=FpluCNTyTBnoqrkm-0) |   gm-design-kit@   | Available  |
|                                                  Implementation                                                  |                   [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Buttons/index.md)                   | material-ios-team@ | Available  |
|                                                  Implementation                                                  |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------|
| [iOS: Expressive update](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Buttons/index.md) | material-ios-team@                                                                                                                | Available          |
Spec differences
----------------
The default small button height on iOS is 44pt, in contrast to Android's 40dp height. This is to account for the iOS target sizes and align with the dimensions of actions and list cells. [Learn more about iOS and Android target sizes](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/company/teams/gar/criteria/mobile-criteria.md?cl=head#target_size){#isPasted}

Hover and focused states are not available on iOS.
Guidelines
----------
Consider using full-width buttons to draw additional focus to actions. This can be useful for actions or information presented on sheets, such as onboarding steps or feature promos.{#isPasted}

<br />
For actions within a list, use a list cell instead of a button.  

[Lists](/google-material-3/pages/lists/ios) can contain cells that navigate to a different page or perform actions. These cells function the same way as buttons, and trigger an action on press.
A text button should be used in app bars for actions including Back, Done, or Cancel, and in modal sheets for Done or Cancel.
Button layout
-------------

In iOS, the extra small button size is not recommended, and the small size is 44dp tall instead of 40dp. All other specs are the same.
Target size
-----------
The extra small button target sizes have heights of 44pt to meet the recommended iOS target sizes.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage {#isPasted}
-----------------
Buttons communicate actions that people can take. They are typically placed throughout the UI, in places like:  
{#isPasted}

* Dialogs  
* Modal windows  
* Forms  
* Cards  
* Toolbars

They can also be placed within standard button groups.
Buttons are just one option for representing actions in a product and shouldn't be overused. Too many buttons on a screen can disrupt the visual hierarchy.  

Consider placing additional actions in a navigation rail, set of chips, text links, or icon buttons.
A button group is a collection of buttons that relate to each other and can respond to one another. Both buttons and icon buttons can be used inside a button group.  

In some cases, there are primary and secondary actions within a button group. Buttons with primary actions should have a higher visual emphasis through size, color, or shape.{#isPasted}

[More on button groups](/google-material-3/pages/button-groups/overview)
Toggle buttons {#isPasted}
--------------------------
Toggle buttons should be used for binary selections, such as **Save** or **Favorite**. When toggle buttons are pressed, they can change color, shape, and labels.{#isPasted}

Toggle buttons should use an outlined icon when unselected, and a filled version of the icon when selected. If a filled version doesn't exist, increase the weight instead.

By default, toggle buttons change from round to square when selected.
If the label changes on selected or unselected states, be mindful of the character count. Changing the label significantly is disruptive to the user and the page layout.
Anatomy
-------
### Label text {#isPasted}

Label text is the most important element of a button. It describes the action that will occur if someone taps a button. It should be very brief, ideally 1--3 words.

Use sentence case, which only capitalizes the first word and proper nouns. This allows the text to distinguish proper nouns, for example: **Book with Flights** , not **BOOK WITH FLIGHTS**.

Don't truncate or wrap label text. It should always be fully visible on a single line.
Buttons with the **outlined** and **text** color style depend on the colors to be recognizable from other text and elements. Use caution when putting these buttons next to visually similar elements, such as chips or large text.
### Container

Button containers hold the label text and optional icon. Buttons with the **text** color style have a visible container only when hovered, focused, or pressed.

Buttons with a round shape have containers with fully rounded corners.
Buttons with a square shape have containers with more subtle rounding that changes based on button size.
### Icon (optional) {#isPasted}

Icons visually communicate the button's action and help draw attention. They should be placed on the leading side of the button, before the label text.
Color styles
------------
### Elevated style
The **elevated** button style is the same as the tonal button, but with a shadow.

To avoid overusing shadows, use the elevated style only when absolutely necessary, such as when the button requires visual separation from a visually prominent background.
Buttons at higher elevations typically have more emphasis in a design, and should be used sparingly. For high emphasis, consider the filled style instead.
### Filled style
The **filled** button style has the most visual impact after the FAB, and should be used for important, final actions that complete a flow, like **Save** , **Join now** , or **Confirm**.
Since they have such strong emphasis, the filled style should be used sparingly, ideally for only one action on a page.{#isPasted}

In some cases, filled buttons can use tertiary colors.
### Tonal style
The **tonal** button style is useful in contexts where a lower-priority button requires slightly more emphasis than an outline would give, such as **Next** in an onboarding flow. Tonal buttons use the secondary color mapping.
### Outlined style
The **outlined** style is ideal for medium-emphasis buttons which contain actions that are important, but aren't the primary action in a product.{#isPasted}

Outlined buttons pair well with filled buttons to indicate alternative, secondary actions.
Outlined buttons display a stroke around the button container, and have no fill by default.   

They should be placed on simple backgrounds, not visually prominent backgrounds such as images or videos.
### Text style
The text button style should be used for the lowest priority actions, especially when presenting multiple options.{#isPasted}

They should be placed on simple backgrounds, not visually prominent backgrounds such as images or videos. The container isn't visible until someone interacts with the button.{#isPasted}

Don't underline the text button. Use hyperlinked body text instead to emphasize links. [More on hyperlinks](/google-material-3/pages/applying-typography/using-the-type-scale/latest#c877b62a-d117-421f-ba8f-9cf68de14f95)
Text buttons are often placed within components such as cards, dialogs, and snackbars. Since text buttons don't have a visible container in their default state, they don't distract from nearby content.{#isPasted}

However, since there's no container, the label text color must always be recognizable from non-button text and elements.
In cards, text buttons help maintain an emphasis on card content.
Dialogs use text buttons because the absence of a container helps unify the action with the dialog text.{#isPasted}

Align text buttons to the trailing edge of dialogs, on the right for left-to-right languages and on the left for right-to-left languages.
Adaptive design {#isPasted}
---------------------------
### Resizing
When scaling layouts for large screen devices, buttons can adapt their visual presentation, size, alignment, and arrangement to fit different contexts and user needs.
Choose the best button position based on screen size.
The icon and label text in a button stay centered and grouped as the button's width changes.
Buttons can be customized to change size and scaling behavior across different window sizes.

To avoid creating very long buttons in large windows, constrain button width or place buttons beside other elements.
### Placement
The size and placement of buttons can change as parent containers, such as cards, adapt for larger screens.

Keep items, including buttons, in the same order between large and small screens to provide a consistent experience for screen readers and keyboard navigation.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Variants
--------
|      Variant       |    GM3    | GM3 Expressive |
|--------------------|-----------|----------------|
| Default            | Available | Available      |
| Toggle (selection) | --        | Available      |
Configurations
--------------
|       Category       |                   Configuration                   |    GM3    |      GM3 Expressive       |
|----------------------|---------------------------------------------------|-----------|---------------------------|
| Size                 | Small (default)                                   | Available | Available                 |
| Size                 | XS, M, L, XL                                      | --        | Available                 |
| Shape                | Round (default)                                   | Available | Available                 |
| Shape                | Square                                            | --        | Available                 |
| Color                | Elevated, filled (default), tonal, outlined, text | Available | Available                 |
| Small button padding | 24dp                                              | Available | Not recommended. Use 16dp |
| Small button padding | 16dp                                              | --        | Available                 |
Tokens \& specs
---------------

Use the table's menu to select a token set. Button token sets are separated into common tokens, color, and size. [View baseline tokens](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/specs#a89b82c2-d44e-4ccb-b519-2c62ed8d6ae4)
Anatomy
-------
Color
-----
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For designers, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. In implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value.

* There are five built-in button color styles: elevated, filled, tonal, outlined, and text

* The default and toggle buttons use different colors

* Toggle buttons don't use the text style
star  
Note:  
These color roles were chosen to create design coherence and familiarity. Other color roles can be used as long as the container and text have a 3:1 contrast ratio. For example, tertiary and on tertiary.
|                  <br />                   |                  1. Default                  |             2. Toggle unselected             |         3. Toggle selected         |
|-------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Elevated container Elevated icon \& label | Surface container low Primary                | Surface container low Primary                | Primary On primary                 |
| Filled container Filled icon \& label     | PrimaryOn primary                           | Surface container On surface variant         | Primary On primary                 |
| Tonal container Tonal icon \& label       | Secondary container On secondary container   | Secondary container On secondary container   | Secondary On secondary             |
| Outlined container Outlined icon \& label | Outline variant (outline) On surface variant | Outline variant (outline) On surface variant | Inverse surface Inverse on surface |
| Text icon \& label                        | Primary                                      | --                                           | --                                 |
### High contrast
High contrast mode is an accessibility feature that aims to maximize legibility by using a limited color palette, like black and white. Focus rings are shown when buttons are focused.
States
------
States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element.
### Elevated button states

The elevated button style has an elevation of 1 by default and 0 when disabled.
#### Default
#### Toggle
### Filled button states
#### Default
#### Toggle
### Tonal button states {#isPasted}
#### Default
#### Toggle
### Outlined button states {#isPasted}
The outlined button's container fill is invisible at rest, but the opacity and state layers behave the same as other button styles when disabled, hovered, focused, or pressed.
#### Default
#### Toggle
### Text button style states {#isPasted}
The text button's container is invisible at rest, but the opacity and state layers behave the same as other button styles when disabled, hovered, focused, or pressed. There is no toggle text button.
Shape morph
-----------
### Pressed state

When pressed, buttons can morph to become more square. Both round and square buttons should have the same pressed shape.{#isPasted}

The corner radius value differs for each button size. [See full button corner measurements](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/specs#62d39080-7bc2-4d50-a306-231bb9e366cd)
### When selected

In addition to changing shape when pressed, toggle buttons also change the resting shape from round (unselected) to square (selected). {#isPasted}

If the resting unselected shape is square, the selected shape should be round.
Measurements
------------
### Target areas
Extra small and small icon buttons must have a target size of 48x48dp or larger to be accessible.

iOS touch target sizes for small buttons need to be at least 44x44dp. Refer to the [iOS tab](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/ios/) for specific touch target details.
### Corner sizes
|                  |  XS  |  S   |  M   |  L   |  XL  |
|------------------|------|------|------|------|------|
| A. Round button  | Full | Full | Full | Full | Full |
| B. Square button | 12dp | 12dp | 16dp | 28dp | 28dp |
| C. Pressed state | 8dp  | 8dp  | 12dp | 16dp | 16dp |
Baseline tokens
---------------
Use the table's menu to switch token sets. The baseline button token sets are organized by color.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases {#isPasted}
---------------------

People should be able to do the following with assistive technology:

* Use a button to perform an action
* Navigate to and activate a button
Interaction \& style {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
### Color contrast

Enabled buttons need a 3:1 contrast ratio with the background to meet accessibility best practices. {#isPasted}

This is measured from the container for elevated, filled, and tonal button styles, and the label text for outlined and text button styles.
### 200% text size

Avoid excessive text wrapping or truncation by choosing concise strings. {#isPasted}

On Android, button labels should be kept concise enough to fit within two lines after the text size is increased to 200%. If a button label exceeds this limit and gets truncated, provide an alternative way to access the full content in a single tap.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
|      Keys      |       Actions        |
|      Tab       | Navigate to a button |
| Space or Enter |  Activate a button   |
|----------------|----------------------|
Labeling elements {#isPasted}
-----------------------------
The accessibility label for a button should match the visible label text on the button such as **Done** , **Send** , or **Reply**. {#isPasted}

It can contain extra contextual information if necessary.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Two variants: default and toggle

* Can contain an optional leading icon

* Five color options: elevated, filled, tonal, outlined, and text

* Five size recommendations: extra small, small, medium, large, and extra large

* Two shape options: round and square

* Keep labels concise and use sentence case
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
**May 2025**{#isPasted}

Buttons now have a wider variety of shapes and sizes, toggle functionality, and can change shape when selected. [More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
Variants and naming:

* Default and toggle (selection)

* Color styles are now configurations (elevated, filled, tonal, outlined, text)

Shapes:

* Round and square

* Shape morphs when pressed

* Shape morphs when selected

Sizes:

* Extra small

* Small (existing, default)

* Medium

* Large

* Extra large

New padding for **small** buttons:

* 16dp (recommended to match padding of new sizes)

* 24dp (no longer recommended)
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color. Icons and labels now share the same color. Neutral text button is no longer recommended.

* Icons: Standard size for leading and trailing icons is now 20dp

* Shape: Fully-rounded corner radius and additional height options
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Icon buttons
Icon buttons help people take actions with a single tap

## Guidelines
Usage {#isPasted}
-----------------
Use icon buttons to display common actions. There are two variants: **default** and **toggle**.

* Default icon buttons can open other elements, such as a menu or search.

* Toggle icon buttons can represent binary actions that can be toggled on and off, such as **favorite** or **bookmark**.

Icon buttons can be placed directly on the background or in most container components, such as cards, app bars, and toolbars.  

Multiple icon buttons can be placed in a standard button group to add interaction and motion between the buttons when pressed. [More about standard button groups](/google-material-3/pages/button-groups/overview)
### Color

There are four icon button color styles, in order of emphasis:

1. Filled{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Tonal{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
3. Outlined{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
4. Standard{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}

For the highest emphasis, use the filled style. For the lowest emphasis, use standard.
Use a filled, tonal, or outlined icon button when the button needs more visual separation from the background.  

Choose the right style and emphasis for the situation.
Use the **filled** style for visual impact and key actions that require high emphasis.  

Avoid overusing the filled style on a screen. Use them sparingly.
Use the **tonal** style as a middle ground between filled and outlined icon buttons. It's useful for secondary actions paired with a high emphasis action.  

For example, use the tonal style for actions like **Raise hand** in a video meeting. When selected, its visual emphasis is greater than the outlined menu button, but less than the filled **End call** button.
Use the **outlined** style for medium-emphasis buttons. It's useful when the button isn't the main focus of the interaction, such as browsing through sets of cards.  

Use the **standard** style for low-emphasis buttons, or when placing buttons on a colorful surface.
### Size \& width

Icon buttons are available in five different sizes:

* Extra small - 32dp{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Small - 40dp (default)
* Medium - 56dp
* Large - 96dp
* Extra large - 136dp

And three widths:

* Default
* Narrow
* Wide

Use size and width to provide emphasis and visual hierarchy in a page with multiple buttons. The main action should be the most visually prominent, whether through color or size.
Not all icon buttons will need to emphasize a primary and secondary action.

When buttons have a similar importance, they should be the same size.
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
### Icon {#isPasted}

Icons visually communicate the button's action. Their meaning should be clear and unambiguous. [Browse popular icons](http://go/icons)  

Default icon buttons should use filled icons.{#isPasted}

Toggle buttons should use an outlined icon when unselected, and a filled version of the icon when selected.
#### Icon accessibility requirements

For selected toggle buttons, if a filled version of an icon doesn't exist, increase the icon weight to semibold. If semibold doesn't provide enough visual change, use bold.{#isPasted}

This is to ensure that selection is communicated through at least two properties, rather than just color. This requirement doesn't apply to default non-toggle buttons.
### Container {#isPasted}

The container provides increased contrast and hierarchy in places that need more visual separation from the background or other elements.
Placement
---------
Icon buttons are commonly used in other components, such as app bars and cards.

These buttons should be used for common, easily understandable actions.

Only use a few icon buttons at once.
In dense layouts, group popular actions by placing many icon buttons next to each other in components like a toolbar or button group.

These components draw attention or add interaction between buttons.
Behavior
--------
### Hover {#isPasted}

On hover, the icon button displays a tooltip describing its action, rather than the name of the icon itself.
### Selection {#isPasted}

Toggle icon buttons allow a single choice to be selected or deselected, such as adding or removing something from favorites.   

When placed in a button group, icon buttons change shape to help the selected button stand out.  

[More on button groups](/google-material-3/pages/button-groups/overview)
The icon should become filled to represent selection.{#isPasted}

If a filled version of the icon doesn't exist, use semibold weight instead.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------
People should be able to do the following using assistive technology:

* Understand meaning of the icon
* Navigate to and activate an icon button
* When applicable, a tooltip should be available to help describe the icon button's purpose
Interaction \& style
--------------------
Ensure the icon has contrast of at least 3:1 with the surface or background.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
{#isPasted}{#isPasted}

|    **Keys**    |                **Actions**                |
|      Tab       | Focus lands on (non-disabled) icon button |
| Space or Enter | Activates the (non-disabled) icon button  |
|----------------|-------------------------------------------|
Labeling elements
-----------------
The accessibility label for icon buttons describes the action the button is executing, such as **Add to favorites** , **Bookmark** , or **Send message**.
Layout \& density
-----------------
Groups of similar components can be nested together inside a component, or they can stand alone.  

The target size of each icon button should be at least 48dp, even when nested.
### Avoid applying density by default {#isPasted}

Don't apply density to icon buttons by default. This lowers their targets below the required 48x48 CSS pixels minimum size. {#isPasted}

Provide density options that allow people to choose a higher density, such as selecting a denser layout or changing the theme. Controls for adjusting density must maintain a target size of at least 48x48 CSS pixels.
Hover
-----
On web, icon buttons should display a tooltip with an accessibility label.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://goto.google.com/gm3-bug){#isPasted}

## Overview
* Icon buttons must use a system icon with a clear meaning

* Two variants: default and toggle

* Many configurations: Color, size, width, and shape

* On web, display a tooltip describing the action while hovering

* In toggle buttons, use the outlined style of an icon for the unselected state, and the filled style for the selected state
Availability \& resources
-------------------------
GM3 Expressive update {#isPasted}
---------------------------------
Icon buttons now have a wider variety of shapes and sizes, changing shape when selected. When placed in button groups, icon buttons interact with each other when pressed. [More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
**Aug 2024**

Variants and naming:

* Default and toggle (selection)

* Color styles are now configurations (filled, tonal, outlined, standard)

Shapes:

* Round and square options

* Shape morphs when pressed

* Shape morphs when selected

Sizes:

* Extra small

* Small (default)

* Medium

* Large

* Extra large

Widths:

* Narrow

* Default

* Wide
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* **Color:**New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color

* **Variants and naming:** Icon buttons were called toggle buttons. There are now two variants of icon buttons: default and toggle.
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## Specs
Variants
--------
|      Variant       |    GM3    | GM3 Expressive |
|--------------------|-----------|----------------|
| Default            | Available | Available      |
| Toggle (selection) | Available | Available      |
Configurations {#isPasted}
--------------------------
| Category |                   Options                   |    GM3    | GM3 Expressive |
|----------|---------------------------------------------|-----------|----------------|
| Size     | Small (default)                             | Available | Available      |
| Size     | XS, M, L, XL                                | --        | Available      |
| Shape    | Round (default)                             | Available | Available      |
| Shape    | Square                                      | --        | Available      |
| Color    | Filled (default), tonal, outlined, standard | Available | Available      |
| Width    | Default                                     | Available | Available      |
| Width    | Narrow, wide                                | --        | Available      |
Tokens \& specs {#isPasted}
---------------------------

Icon button token sets are organized by common tokens, color, and size. Select the token set from the table's menu. [Learn about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/tokens/)
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
Color {#isPasted}
-----------------
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For designers, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens; in implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value.{#isPasted}

There are four built-in color styles: filled, tonal, outlined, and standard. Default and toggle buttons use different color roles per style.
star  
Note:  
These color roles were chosen to create design coherence and familiarity. Other color roles can be used as long as the container and text have a 3:1 contrast ratio. For example, tertiary and on tertiary.
|              <br />              |                  1. Default                  |            2. Toggle, unselected             |        3. Toggle, selected         |
|----------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| Filled container Filled icon     | Primary On primary                          | Surface container On surface variant         | Primary On primary                 |
| Tonal container Tonal icon       | Secondary container On secondary container   | Secondary container On secondary container   | Secondary On secondary             |
| Outlined container Outlined icon | Outline variant (outline) On surface variant | Outline variant (outline) On surface variant | Inverse surface Inverse on surface |
| Standard icon                    | On surface variant                           | On surface variant                           | Primary                            |
High contrast: default icon button
----------------------------------

High contrast mode is an accessibility feature that aims to maximize legibility by using a limited color palette, like black and white. {#isPasted}

Focus rings are shown when buttons are focused.

Note: Filled, tonal, and outlined icon buttons look the same in high contrast mode.
High contrast: toggle icon button
---------------------------------

Toggle icon buttons invert the colors when selected in high contrast mode.
States {#isPasted}
------------------
States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element. State layers slightly change button color. Disabled states have different base colors. [View tokens for details](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
### Filled button states

#### Default
#### Toggle {#isPasted}
### Tonal button states {#isPasted}

#### Default
#### Toggle {#isPasted}
### Outlined button states {#isPasted}

#### Default {#isPasted}
#### Toggle
### Standard icon button states
The standard icon button's container is invisible at rest, but visible when the state layer is applied.
#### Default
#### Toggle
Shape morph
-----------
### Pressed state

While pressed, icon buttons can morph to become more square.

Both round and square icon buttons should have the same pressed shape radius.  

The corner radius value differs for each button size. [See full icon button corner measurements](/google-material-3/pages/icon-buttons/specs#6a57c215-c58c-4df4-8938-c33103342eaa)
### When selected

In addition to changing shape when pressed, toggle icon buttons also change the resting shape from round (unselected) to square (selected) by default.  

If the resting shape is square, the selected shape should be round.
Measurements {#isPasted}
------------------------
### Target sizes

Extra small and small icon buttons must have a target size of 48x48dp or larger to be accessible.
### Button corner radius {#isPasted}
{#isPasted}{#isPasted}{#isPasted}

|                  |  XS  |  S   |  M   |  L   |  XL  |
|------------------|------|------|------|------|------|
| A. Round button  | Full | Full | Full | Full | Full |
| B. Square button | 12dp | 12dp | 16dp | 28dp | 28dp |
| C. Pressed state | 8dp  | 8dp  | 12dp | 16dp | 16dp |
Baseline tokens
---------------
Use the table's menu to select a token set. Filled, tonal, and outlined icon button tokens are no longer recommended. Standard tokens are still available in the module at the top of the page.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS. {#isPasted}

[Apple's Human Interface Guidelines](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines) include additional iOS best practices and platform considerations.
Resources
---------
|                                                    **Type**                                                     |                                                           **Resource**                                                            |    **Contact**     | **Status** |
|                                                     Design                                                      | [iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=85905-18512&t=FpluCNTyTBnoqrkm-0) |   gm-design-kit@   | Available  |
|                                                 Implementation                                                  |                   [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Buttons/index.md)                   | material-ios-team@ | Available  |
|                                                 Implementation                                                  |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------|
| [iOS Expressive update](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Buttons/index.md) | material-ios-team@                                                                                                                | Available          |
Spec differences {#isPasted}
----------------------------
### Small size difference

The default small icon button has different widths, and a height of 44pt, to meet iOS recommended target sizes. These sizes also align with the dimensions of actions and list cells.  
{#isPasted}

* Default: 44x44pt  
  {#isPasted}
* Narrow: 34x44pt  
  {#isPasted}
* Wide: 58x44pt{#isPasted}
### Extra small size difference {#isPasted}

The target size for extra small icon buttons should be at least 44x44pt on iOS.
On Android, pressed icons have a square state layer. On iOS, a round state layer is used.
