# Snackbar
Snackbars show short updates about app processes at the bottom of the screen

## Overview
* Snackbars shouldn't interrupt a person's experience{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Usually appear at the bottom of the UI
* Can disappear on their own or remain on screen until a person takes action
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color
* Behavior: Clarified that snackbars can either appear temporarily (dismissive) or persist until the user takes an action (non-dismissive)
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Snackbars inform users of a process that an app has performed or will perform. They appear temporarily, towards the bottom of the screen.{#isPasted}

They shouldn't interrupt the user experience. People can browse the page content without being required to interact with the snackbar.

**Frequency**   
Only one snackbar may be displayed at a time.

**Actions**   
A snackbar can contain a single action. "Dismiss" or "cancel" actions are optional.
### Similar components {#isPasted}

Banners and dialogs are also designed to show important messages.

Choose the right component based on the importance of the message. This component messaging strategy can help avoid overusing snackbars.
{#isPasted}

| **Component** |  **Importance**   |                                                 **Action needed**                                                  |
|---------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Snackbar      | Low importance    | Optional: Snackbars may not have a button, and can disappear automatically                                         |
| Banner        | Medium importance | Optional: Banners remain on screen until acted on or dismissed, or if the state that caused the banner is resolved |
| Dialog        | High importance   | Required: Dialogs block the main content until an action is confirmed                                              |
### Accessibility requirements for web {#isPasted}

On web, auto-dismissing snackbars are inaccessible for people with low vision or who require additional time to perceive information. This can be solved in 2 ways:

#### 1. Add inline feedback

Information in auto-dismissing snackbars must also be communicated using another accessible method inline or near the action that triggered the snackbar.

For example, update the label on a "Save" button to "Saved", and trigger an auto-dismissing snackbar that communicates the same message.

#### 2. Make the snackbar actionable

Alternatively, add actions to the snackbar so it doesn't dismiss until acted on.
Anatomy
-------
### Text label

Snackbars contain a text label that directly relates to the process being performed. In compact window sizes, the text label can contain up to two lines of text.
### Container

Snackbars are displayed in rectangular containers with a grey background. Containers should be completely opaque, so that text labels remain legible.
### Action

Snackbars can display a single text button that lets users take action on a process performed by the app. Snackbars shouldn't be the only way to access a core use case, to make an app usable.
Placement
---------
### At the bottom of a UI {#isPasted}

Snackbars should be placed at the bottom of a UI, in front of the main content. In some cases, snackbars can be nudged upwards to avoid overlapping with other UI elements near the bottom, such as FABs or toolbars.

Avoid placing a snackbar in front of frequently used touch targets or navigation.
To ensure accessibility for keyboard users on the web, avoid positioning the snackbar in a way that completely obscures actionable elements. Blocking elements makes it difficult to know what is being focused and selected.
Snackbars can span the entire width of the screen only when a UI does not use persistent navigation components like app bars or navigation bars.{#isPasted}

Snackbars that span the entire width of a UI can push up FABs when they appear.
**Snackbars and floating action buttons (FABs)**   

Snackbars should appear above FABs.
Responsive layout
-----------------
### Compact window size

In compact window sizes, snackbars should expand vertically from 48dp to 64dp to accommodate one or two lines of text, while maintaining a fixed distance from the leading, trailing, and bottom edges of the screen.
### Medium \& expanded window sizes

On medium and expanded window sizes, like tablet and desktop, snackbars should scale horizontally to accommodate longer text strings, keeping in mind that the ideal line length for text is typically between 40-60 characters.

Snackbars use a flexible distance from the trailing edge of the screen. Whenever possible, snackbars on medium and large displays should aim for a single line of text with an optional button.
In wider layouts, snackbars can be left-aligned or center-aligned if they are consistently placed on the same spot at the bottom of the screen.
Behavior
--------
### Appearing and disappearing

Snackbars appear without warning, but they don't block users from interacting with page content.{#isPasted}

Snackbars without actions can auto-dismiss after 4--10 seconds, depending on platform. Avoid using auto-dismissing snackbars on web unless there's also inline feedback.
Snackbars with actions must remain on the screen until the user takes an action on the snackbar, or dismisses it.
### Consecutive snackbars

Consecutive snackbars must appear one at a time. {#isPasted}

Snackbars without actions appear and disappear automatically, while those with actions remain on screen until dismissed. However, a snackbar with updated information can immediately replace an outdated snackbar.

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.{#isPasted}

[Apple's Human Interface Guidelines](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines) include additional iOS best practices and platform considerations.
Resources
---------
|    **Type**    |                                                           **Resource**                                                            |    **Contact**     | **Status** |
|     Design     | [iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=52172-50689&t=ZYYZaN4DZOR1BDw7-0) |   gm-design-kit@   | Available  |
| Implementation |                  [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Snackbar/index.md)                   | material-ios-team@ | Available  |
|----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------|
Same as Android
---------------
This component is supported on iOS with the same specs, guidelines, and accessibility considerations as Android.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs){#isPasted}  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Tokens and specs
----------------

Browse the component elements, attributes, tokens, and their values. [++Learn more about design tokens++](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Color {#isPasted}
-----------------

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value.
Measurements
------------
Configurations
--------------
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Accessibility
-------------
### Use cases

Users should be able to:

* Be alerted, but not disrupted, when a snackbar appears{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Move focus to an actionable snackbar
* Take action on a snackbar using assistive technology
### Interaction \& style

Snackbars with actions shouldn't auto-dismiss. This way, users can read and interact with it at their own pace.{#isPasted}

Snackbars without actions can auto-dismiss after a sufficient amount of time, however this can still present difficulties on web without additional feedback.

Each platform has its own requirements for auto-dismiss durations, however common acceptable durations are 4--10 seconds.
Snackbars use a color intended to stand out against UI elements. Use the default color mapping to avoid color conflict issues.
### Accessibility requirements on web

On web, auto-dismissing snackbars can be difficult to navigate for people with low vision or who require additional time to perceive information. This information can be made clearer for all users in two ways:{#isPasted}

#### 1. Add inline feedback

Information in auto-dismissing snackbars must also be communicated inline or near the action that triggered the snackbar.

For example, update the label on a "Save" button to "Saved", and trigger an auto-dismissing snackbar that communicates the same message.

#### 2. Make the snackbar actionable

Alternatively, add actions to the snackbar so it doesn't dismiss until acted on. Actionable snackbars shouldn't auto-dismiss.
### Focus

Snackbars have the following focus requirements:{#isPasted}

* When a snackbar appears, announce the message but don't move focus.
* Don't automatically move focus.
* Don't trap focus in the snackbar. Users should be able to freely navigate in and out.
* On web, a shortcut should exist for users to move focus to snackbars with actions (like Alt+G). Ensure that this shortcut is clearly documented, like in a help article.
Focus exits the snackbar differently per platform:{#isPasted}

* Ideally, focus should either return to the element that triggered the snackbar, or go to the next most logical element on the page.
* On Android and iOS, focus may move to the nearest visible element, or to the first actionable item on the page.
### Keyboard navigation

| Keys |                 Actions                  |
|------|------------------------------------------|
| Tab  | Moves focus between interactive elements |
| Esc  | Dismisses the snackbar when in focus     |
### Labeling elements

Snackbars should be announced once they appear on the screen, but shouldn't grab focus or prevent people from completing their current task. {#isPasted}

* On Android and web, use a live region with a polite (queued) announcement instead of an assertive announcement.
* On iOS 17+, snackbars use polite announcements by default.

If a snackbar appears when the app is launched, it should be announced after the page's title, but not receive focus.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Motion physics system
The motion physics system makes a UI expressive and easy to use

## Specs
Cross-platform experiences
--------------------------

The motion physics system is available on Jetpack Compose and Android Views, and can be easily adapted to other platforms.
|    Platform     |                 Status                  |                                                                                                                How to apply                                                                                                                 |
| Jetpack Compose |                Available                |                                                                                                 Use built-in components and spring tokens.                                                                                                  |
|  Android Views  | Available. Not yet added to components. |                                                                                                         Use built-in spring tokens.                                                                                                         |
|       Web       |               Compatible                | Use springs when possible, otherwise use curves that mimic the springs for animations without interruptions or gestures. [View web conversion table](/google-material-3/pages/motion/specs#bebce58a-3cd6-40c0-a248-56d2570d4857){#isPasted} |
|       iOS       |              Not available              |                              Use the recommended native platform springs for a close match. [View iOS conversion table](/google-material-3/pages/motion/specs#e7d41993-be10-4a16-8263-3f3d2ec5106c){#isPasted}                              |
|-----------------|-----------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
Tokens and specs
----------------

The **spring composite** tokens are used in the motion physics system. These composites combine two **spring** tokens (damping and stiffness) into a single token for ease of use. The **easing** , **duration** , and **path** tokens are used by the legacy system. [View in token catalog](/google-material-3/tokens/designSystems/20543ce18892f7d9/tokenSets/09cfe5568544b8f7){#isPasted}
Web: Convert springs to curves
------------------------------
|           Spring           |                  Curve                   |
|----------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Expressive fast spatial    | 0.42, 1.67, 0.21, 0.90. Duration = 350ms |
| Expressive default spatial | 0.38, 1.21, 0.22, 1.00. Duration = 500ms |
| Expressive slow spatial    | 0.39, 1.29, 0.35, 0.98. Duration = 650ms |
| Expressive fast effects    | 0.31, 0.94, 0.34, 1.00. Duration = 150ms |
| Expressive default effects | 0.34, 0.80, 0.34, 1.00. Duration = 200ms |
| Expressive slow effects    | 0.34, 0.88, 0.34, 1.00. Duration = 300ms |
| Standard fast spatial      | 0.27, 1.06, 0.18, 1.00. Duration = 350ms |
| Standard default spatial   | 0.27, 1.06, 0.18, 1.00. Duration = 500ms |
| Standard slow spatial      | 0.27, 1.06, 0.18, 1.00. Duration = 750ms |
| Standard fast effects      | 0.31, 0.94, 0.34, 1.00. Duration = 150ms |
| Standard default effects   | 0.34, 0.80, 0.34, 1.00. Duration = 200ms |
| Standard slow effects      | 0.34, 0.88, 0.34, 1.00. Duration = 300ms |
iOS: Select the matching iOS default
------------------------------------
|             Springs             | Native iOS default |
|---------------------------------|--------------------|
| Expressive spatial (all speeds) | Bouncy             |
| Expressive effects (all speeds) | Snappy             |
| Standard spatial (all speeds)   | Smooth             |
| Standard effects (all speeds)   | Snappy             |
Easing and duration
-------------------

The original easing and duration tokens are still available to use as a fallback, and are currently used for animating transitions. [View easing and duration system](/google-material-3/pages/easing-and-duration/applying-easing-and-duration/)
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## How it works
A motion system designed for expression
---------------------------------------

Material introduced the **motion physics system** with GM3 Expressive. This new physics-based system makes interactions and transitions feel more alive, fluid, and natural. It represents a new motion language for Google products, and is easier to implement and customize than ever before. {#isPasted}

The physics system is replacing the previous system based on [easing and duration](/google-material-3/pages/easing-and-duration/applying-easing-and-duration/).  

[More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------

|                                                              **Type**                                                              |                        **Link**                        |                                                                 **Contact**                                                                  | **Status** |
|                                                               Design                                                               | [Android Motion Tools](http://go/android-motion-tools) |                                                               android-motion@                                                                | Available  |
|                                                               Design                                                               |
|                                                               Design                                                               |
|                                                               Design                                                               |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------|
| [Material Motion Figma plugin](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1397759704974764283/material-motion)                         | android-motion@                                        | Available. [How to use](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1JiENTw5TaiAVq46zG5Ffzu8HEcY4VmAa3BwfVZvWfRQ/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.oumz2ok0c3hc) |
| [After Effects Sample](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1zYEcy5i-YKFFUwsUlXzSlTStrDmfrUk-?usp=drive_link)                    | material-ux@                                           | Available                                                                                                                                    |
| [Guidelines motion assets](https://drive.google.com/corp/drive/folders/1ocLLJIANvDM9PymGYzUCyLOPMsUnrxwX)                          | gdp-structured-content@                                | Available (limited)                                                                                                                          |
| Flutter                                                                                                                            | material-flutter-team@                                 | Unavailable                                                                                                                                  |
| iOS                                                                                                                                | material-ios-team@                                     | Available. [See specs](/google-material-3/pages/motion/specs/)                                                                               |
| [Jetpack Compose](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/styles/motion.md?cl=head) | material-compose-team@                                 | Available                                                                                                                                    |
| Wiz                                                                                                                                | material-web-team@                                     | Compatible with Compose springs. [See specs](/google-material-3/pages/motion/specs/)                                                         |
The basics: Motion schemes
--------------------------

The physics system has two preset motion schemes: **expressive** and **standard** . The motion scheme you choose defines how your product feels. While most motion in a product should use the same scheme, products can make [advanced customizations](/google-material-3/pages/motion/overview/latest#b51b3963-613e-458a-a9f5-7fbaed425154) to swap the scheme to emphasize key moments.
**Expressive** is Material's opinionated motion scheme, and should be used for most situations, particularly hero moments and key interactions.
**Standard** feels more functional with minimal bounce, and should be used for utilitarian products.
Need something other than the preset schemes? [Create your own!](/google-material-3/pages/motion/how-it-works/latest#988f4b0e-ec60-4019-ab81-9a5d97533829) The physics system makes it easy to create custom motion schemes beyond expressive and standard, while still leveraging theming. Schemes can be easily switched between expressive, standard, or custom as needed.
How it works: Springs
---------------------

Motion schemes use **springs** . A spring is a combination of three attributes which control all motion behavior: stiffness, damping, and initial velocity.
**Springs are versatile**. One spring can apply to many situations, such as transitions, button effects, or gestures. This makes the motion and expression feel consistent throughout the product.{#isPasted}

**Springs feel natural**. Springs are designed to be predictable, like how objects move and bounce. They handle gestures, interruptions, and retargeting animations seamlessly.
Spring tokens
-------------

On Jetpack Compose and Android Views, these springs are available as [spring tokens](/google-material-3/pages/motion/specs/)**.** Use tokens to easily apply motion to any element, making all motion feel predictable and consistent across multiple platforms. See [specs](/google-material-3/pages/motion/specs) for how to convert springs to other platforms like Wiz and iOS.
There are tokens for **spatial** movement and **effects** , with three durations each: **default** , **fast** , and **slow**.{#isPasted}

For example, to apply fast, spatial, expressive motion, call the "expressive" motion scheme, then use the token: md.sys.motion.spring.fast.spatial.

Notice that the "expressive" scheme isn't part of the token itself. Rather, it's called at the product level and applied to all tokens. This makes it easier to swap schemes without changing assigned tokens.
### Style

**Spatial** spring tokens are used for animations that move something on screen, for example the x and y position, rotation, size, rounded corners. This spring overshoots the final value and bounces into place.
**Effects** spring tokens are used to animate properties such as color and opacity animations, where there shouldn't be any overshoot.
### Speed

Spatial and effect spring tokens come in three speeds: **default** , **fast** , and **slow**. Most motion should use the default speed, while smaller elements may use fast and larger elements may use slow.
|  Speed  |                                        Spatial example                                        |                 Effects example                 |
| Default | Animations that partially cover the screen, such as bottom sheet and expanded navigation rail | Opacity of the content within a navigation rail |
|  Fast   |                        Small components, such as switches and buttons                         |        Color change of the switch handle        |
|  Slow   |                                    Full-screen animations                                     |           Full-screen content refresh           |
|---------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
Spring tokens work across devices. For example, the spatial fast token will always be faster than default or slow, but the exact values of each token differ depending on if the device is a wearable, phone, or tablet. This ensures the movement feels fast in the context of the device.
Application
-----------
### Components

On Jetpack Compose, 21 Material components use the motion physics system by default. Android Views support is coming soon. To add the motion physics system to other components, including custom-built components, use spring tokens. [View full specs](/google-material-3/pages/motion/specs/)
Advanced customizations
-----------------------

There are a few different levels for applying motion. Choose the level that applies best to your product or specific component.
### Level 1: Use a default motion scheme {#isPasted}

The expressive and standard schemes should be sufficient for all motion needs. On Jetpack Compose, components use these schemes by default.
### Level 2: Create a custom motion scheme {#isPasted}

On Jetpack Compose, to change the default motion scheme that all components and transitions use, create a custom MotionScheme object, and return different AnimationSpec for each property of the motion scheme.
### Level 3: Swap the default motion scheme per element {#isPasted}

Why use just one scheme when you can use multiple? On Jetpack Compose, to use one scheme for most of the product, such as **expressive** , but on certain elements swap it for another scheme, like **standard**, override the CompositionLocal for that particular composable, screen, or element.
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Progress indicators
Progress indicators show the status of a process in real time

## Overview
* Two variants: linear and circular

* Use the same configuration for all instances of a process (like loading)

* They capture attention through motion

* Option to apply a wave to the active track for use cases that would benefit from increased expressiveness
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
**Aug 2024**{#isPasted}

The progress indicators have configurations for height and wavy shape. Choose the visual style that best fits your product. [More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
* Track height: Configurable
* Shape: Wavy
Previous updates
----------------
**Dec 2023: Non-text contrast (NTC)**{#isPasted}

* Anatomy: Added an end stop indicator to improve accessibility
* Contrast: Higher contrast between track and active indicator to enhance the perception of progress
* Motion: New motion behavior
* Shape: Rounded corners
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
**July 2022: Added to Material 3**{#isPasted}

* **Color:** New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage {#isPasted}
-----------------
Use progress indicators to show the status of ongoing processes, such as loading an app, submitting a form, or saving updates.{#isPasted}

When multiple items are loading, use a single progress indicator to show progress for the group. Don't add progress indicators to every activity.
Choose a loading or progress indicator that corresponds to the expected wait time and kind of process.

If the wait is very long, consider allowing people to navigate away from the page while the process finishes up.
|    **Expected wait time**    | **Recommendation** |
|------------------------------|--------------------|
| Instant (under 200ms)        | No indicator       |
| Short (between 200ms and 5s) | Loading indicator  |
| Long (Over 5s)               | Progress indicator |
There are two variants of progress indicators:

1. Linear

2. Circular

**Linear** indicators are best when placed on the edge of a container.

**Circular** indicators are best when centered in an element.

A process should be represented by the same variant of progress indicator throughout the product. For example, if refreshing uses a circular indicator in one place, it should use circular indicators everywhere.
Progress indicators behave differently based on the time of progress being tracked:{#isPasted}

* **Determinate**: Known progress and wait time
* **Indeterminate**: Unknown progress and wait time

When using a **determinate** indicator, the indicator must accurately represent the progress of what it's measuring.

Use **indeterminate** indicators to show that a process is happening, but the wait time is unknown.
As more information about a process becomes available, a progress indicator should change from **indeterminate** to **determinate**.
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
### Active indicator

The active indicator shows the progress that has been made so far. {#isPasted}

In indeterminate processes, it grows and shrinks along the track repeatedly.
The active indicator appears as soon as progress begins. At low percentages where space is limited, this should appear as a dot to help people understand that there's progress underway.
The active indicator has two shape options: **flat** and **wavy**. Use the shape that best fits the product's tone.{#isPasted}

The wavy shape can make longer processes feel less static and is best used when a more expressive style is appropriate.

When using the wavy shape, the overall height of the component changes. At very small sizes, the wavy shape may not be as visible.
### Stop indicator

The stop indicator is a 4dp circle that marks the end of a linear determinate progress indicator to meet Material's accessibility standards.{#isPasted}

It's not used for indeterminate or circular progress indicators.

The stop indicator is required if the track has a contrast below 3:1 with its container or the surface behind the container.
Placement
---------
Place a linear progress indicator along the edge of a container that's loading. If the container changes shape, place it on the edge that animates. It can also be placed in the middle of a container.{#isPasted}

Use a single progress indicator at the top of a page to show progress of the whole group. Don't add one for every element unless they're activated independently.
Circular progress indicators should be centered directly on the container or page that's loading, such as a button or card.{#isPasted}

When loading more items on a page, place the circular progress indicator in the empty space where the new content will appear, not overlapping existing content.

However, if the content does not take long to load, consider using a loading indicator instead.
### Progress indicators in buttons {#isPasted}

A circular indicator can be placed in a button to show that the button's action is currently in progress.{#isPasted}

In very small buttons, use the flat shape since the wavy shape is not as visible at that size.

To ensure a minimum 3:1 contrast ratio, change the active indicator color to be the same color as the button's icon or label text, and remove the track.
Responsive layout
-----------------
### Right-to-left languages

Linear progress indicators should be mirrored horizontally for products using right-to-left (RTL) languages. {#isPasted}

Circular progress indicators don't need to be mirrored.
### Large screens

Circular progress indicators have flexible sizes. They can range from 24dp to 240dp, depending on the placement and the window size. Avoid exceeding the minimum and maximum sizes.{#isPasted}

Reserve very large progress indicators for large and extra-large windows, such as desktop.
Linear progress indicators dynamically adjust to fit the width of the window or element they're placed within, such as a card. They shouldn't be used in any elements smaller than 40dp.{#isPasted}

The padding on each end should be 4dp minimum, but can be modified.
Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------
People should be able to do the following using the assistive technology:{#isPasted}

* Navigate to the progress indicator
* Understand what progress the indicator is communicating
Interaction \& style
--------------------
The active indicator, which displays progress, provides visual contrast of at least 3:1 against most background colors.
When integrated into another component, such as a button, make sure that the active indicator provides visual contrast of at least 3:1 against the other component.

For the active indicator, use the same color as the label text or icon. The track should be removed.
For linear progress indicators, the stop indicator is required if the track has a contrast below 3:1 with its container or the surface behind the container.{#isPasted}

Essentially, the end of the track must be easy to identify.
Labeling elements
-----------------
Since the progress indicator is a visual cue, it needs an accessibility label to describe the kind and amount of progress made.

Use the **progress bar**accessibility role, and write an accessibility label that describes the purpose of the progress indicator. The label should include the process, such as "loading," and the affected content, such as a page, article, or episode. For example: "Loading news article" or "Refreshing page."
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

Variants
--------
|           Variant           |    GM3    | GM3 Expressive |
|-----------------------------|-----------|----------------|

| Circular progress indicator | Available | Available      |
Configurations
--------------
|    Category     |            Configuration             |    GM3    | GM3 Expressive |
|-----------------|--------------------------------------|-----------|----------------|
| Behavior        | Determinate (default), Indeterminate | Available | Available      |

Anatomy
-------
Color
-----
Measurements {#isPasted}
------------------------
Wavy indicators use **amplitude** and **wavelength** to determine the shape of the wave. The height is the overall container height.
Baseline tokens
---------------
The circular and linear progress indicator had separate token sets. These are no longer recommended.

# Building for all
Building for everyone with everyone

## Co-design
Co-design supports innovation by involving the people who use a product or service in the creation process and goes beyond traditional user research. Prioritize engaging with people of varied backgrounds and experiences and remember that building with everyone is an ongoing process. By actively engaging with communities that are often overlooked, you can build products that are more beneficial for everyone. Validate your assumptions and discover new opportunities by conducting market research with a broad range of people.
Questions to consider
---------------------
* What are some opportunities to address user needs keeping in mind attributes of people and their contexts?
  * Who else can benefit from the product?
  * What are their needs?
  * How are you making sure overlooked communities are included in ongoing testing?
  {#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* How are you engaging community members and experts early and often throughout the development process?
  * How will their feedback shape key decisions about feature priorities, functionality, and messaging?
  * How can you continue to apply these insights as you move from the ideation phase to other stages of product development?
  * What are some opportunities to gain even more insights?
* What are the risks and potential harms if you exclude people from overlooked communities?
  * How can you address them?
* If your tool uses ML or AI, what can you do to mitigate the risk of bias? This [collection of resources](https://design.google/library/people-ai-research) can help.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## User needs
Product teams can't always represent all of their users, but they can better understand different people's experiences and needs by working with communities, organizations, experts, and researchers. Including perspectives of those who are often overlooked leads to building more helpful products for everyone.
Dimensions of experience
------------------------
Considering people's attributes and environments can help:{#isPasted}

* Ensure you take into account a variety of lived experiences
* Discover insights into who to collaborate with to gather different perspectives
* Expand product benefits to more people
Keep this evolving list in mind when you conduct research and design, comprehensive testing, and marketing:

* Age
* Culture
* Disability
* Education and literacy
* Ethnicity
* Gender
* Geography and global relevance
* Physical attributes
* Race
* Religion
* Sexual orientation
* Socioeconomic status
* Technology proficiency
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Bottom sheets
Bottom sheets show secondary content anchored to the bottom of the screen

## Specs
Modal bottom sheets are above a scrim while standard bottom sheets don't have a scrim. Besides this, both variants of bottom sheets have the same specs.
Tokens and specs
----------------

Browse the component elements, attributes, tokens, and their values. [++Learn more about design tokens++](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Color {#isPasted}
-----------------

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview){#isPasted}
Measurements
------------
Bottom sheets span the full window width up to 640dp. When the window width exceeds 640dp, bottom sheets adjust to have a top margin of 56dp and side margins of 56dp. {#isPasted}  

|                Attribute                 |               Value               |
|    Drag handle alignment (horizontal)    |              Center               |
|      Drag handle padding top/bottom      |               16dp                |
|                Top margin                |               72dp                |
|    Top margin (window width \> 640dp)    |               56dp                |
| Start/end margin (window width \> 640dp) |               56dp                |
|                  Width                   | Full width, up to max-width 640dp |
|                  Height                  |             Variable              |
|------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Bottom sheets display supplementary content and actions on a mobile screen.
Bottom sheets are a versatile component that can contain a wide variety of information and layouts, including menu items (in list or grid layouts), actions, and supplemental content.
Anatomy
-------
A container is the only required element of a bottom sheet. Bottom sheet layouts can vary widely to support the kinds of content they contain.
### Container {#isPasted}

Bottom sheet containers hold all bottom sheet elements. Their size is determined by the space those elements occupy.{#isPasted}

The container is the only required element of a bottom sheet. All other elements are optional.

<br />
### List items (optional) {#isPasted}

Lists are a continuous group of text or images. List items can include label text, icons, and text buttons, among other elements.
### Dividers (optional) {#isPasted}

Dividers can be used to separate related content in bottom sheets.
### Media (optional) {#isPasted}

**Thumbnail**   
Bottom sheets can include thumbnails for an avatar or logo.{#isPasted}

**Image**   
Bottom sheets can include photos, illustrations, and other graphics, such as weather icons.

**Video**   
Bottom sheets can include video.
Standard bottom sheets
----------------------
Standard bottom sheets co-exist with the screen's main UI region and allow for simultaneously viewing and interacting with both regions, especially when the main UI region is frequently scrolled or panned.{#isPasted}

Use a standard bottom sheet to display content that complements the screen's primary content, such as an audio player in a music app.
At full-screen height, standard bottom sheets contain a collapse icon in an app bar to return to their initial position.{#isPasted}

Standard bottom sheets can contain supplementary content that continues below the screen, such as location information over a map.
Modal bottom sheets
-------------------
Like dialogs, modal bottom sheets appear in front of app content, disabling all other app functionality when they appear, and remaining on screen until confirmed, dismissed, or a required action has been taken.
Use a modal bottom sheet as an alternative to inline menus or simple dialogs on mobile, especially when offering a long list of action items, or when items require longer descriptions and icons.{#isPasted}

Modal bottom sheets are used in mobile apps only.
### Visibility {#isPasted}

To provide access to its top actions, the initial vertical position of modal bottom sheets is capped at 50% of the screen height.{#isPasted}

Modal bottom sheets whose contents exceed 50% of the screen height can then be pulled across the full screen and scrolled internally to access their remaining items.
Modal bottom sheets appear when triggered by a user action, such as tapping a button or an overflow icon. They can be dismissed by:{#isPasted}

* Tapping a menu item or action within the bottom sheet
* Tapping the scrim
* Swiping the sheet down
* Using a close affordance within the bottom sheet's app bars, if available

Display a close affordance in a full-screen modal bottom sheet.
Responsive layout
-----------------
### Compact window size

In compact window sizes, like mobile devices, bottom sheets extend across the width of a screen and are elevated above the primary content.
### Medium and expanded window sizes

For larger screens with medium and expanded window sizes, bottom sheets have a default max-width to prevent undesired layouts and awkward spacing. However, this can be overridden if needed. For more complex tasks and flows, consider using a non-transient surface such as a floating sheet.
On larger expanded window sizes, like desktop, a bottom sheet can be swapped for a side sheet that shows similar content.
Behavior
--------
Bottom sheets can offer an expansion option where the sheet is fully raised and toggled between a collapsed and expanded state. This provides a more predictable footprint of the sheet, and can be set by the system or toggled by the user.
### Custom positioning {#isPasted}

The drag handle can be dragged or selected to change the bottom sheet height.   

Sheets should be able to cycle through preset heights and close completely without dragging. Selecting the drag handle should toggle through preset heights or close the sheet, while selecting the scrim should always close the bottom sheet.  

If the bottom sheet has multiple preset heights but can't use a drag handle, Material requires the inclusion of a single-pointer alternative to change height.
### Scrolling {#isPasted}

Bottom sheets can be horizontally scrolled, independent of the rest of the screen's content.
### Back

On Android, a gesture called [predictive back](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/third_party/java_src/android_libs/material_components/g3doc/foundations/PredictiveBack.md?cl=head) allows a user to swipe left or right on the bottom sheet.

* Bottom sheet detaches from the left and right edges of the screen to signal it will close

* Previous screen is revealed in a preview

A list of compatible components is available in the [gestures article](/google-material-3/pages/gestures/tab-1/latest#4ac4c989-2425-4f75-9053-e8b00f571bf6).
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs){#isPasted}  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Use bottom sheets in compact and medium window sizes

* Two variants: standard and modal

* Content should be additional or secondary (not the app's main content)

* Bottom sheets can be dismissed in order to interact with the main content
Resources {#isPasted}
---------------------
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color
* Shape: Bottom sheets have a 28dp top corner radius
* Layout: New max-width of 640dp and an optional drag handle with an accessible 48dp hit target
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------
Users should be able to:

* Resize bottom sheets without having to rely on touch gestures
Interaction \& style
--------------------
### Touch target area

The top 48dp portion of the bottom sheet is interactive when user-initiated resizing is available and the drag handle is present.
### Initial focus

The optional drag handle can be focused in the tab order and interacted with using non-touch inputs, such as keyboard or switch controls.
### Dragging {#isPasted}

Include a single-pointer alternative for any action that can be completed by dragging.  

Drag handles should cycle the bottom sheet through available heights when selected. If a drag handle can't be used, add a button to do this action.
Keyboard navigation {#isPasted}
-------------------------------
|     Keys      |              Actions              |
|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| Tab           | Focus lands on drag handle        |
| Space / Enter | Toggles between available heights |
Labeling
--------
Label only the drag handle. The accessibility role for the drag handle is "button."
*** ** * ** ***

> > Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> > File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.{#isPasted}

[Apple's Human Interface Guidelines](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines) include additional iOS best practices and platform considerations.
Resources
---------
|    **Type**    |                                                           **Resource**                                                            |    **Contact**     | **Status** |
|     Design     | [iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=52172-50561&t=ZYYZaN4DZOR1BDw7-0) |   gm-design-kit@   | Available  |
| Implementation |                [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/BottomSheets/index.md)                 | material-ios-team@ | Available  |
|----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------|
Same as Android
---------------
This component is supported on iOS with the same specs, guidelines, and accessibility considerations as Android.
Interaction {#isPasted}
-----------------------
To meet GAR 2025 requirements, bottom sheets must be able to close completely without dragging. On Android, users can select outside the bottom sheet to dismiss it.  

When the bottom sheet starts in a partially expanded state but supports both fully expanded and fully dismissed states, a single-pointer alternative to dismiss the sheet is required. Adding a close button can solve this.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs){#isPasted}  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Google prefabs
Prefabs are full-stack UI for implementing common Google-wide functionality. Built by product teams. Recommended by Material.

What are prefabs?
-----------------
* Common components and patterns built by other Google teams and recommended by Material{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Built-in logic, webhooks, interaction, layout, color, and more{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Fully accessible
* Low-effort, high-reward design \& implementation
* Creates consistency with other Google products
* Can leverage GM3-exclusive features like dynamic color
* **Pre** tty **fab**ulous{#isPasted}
Background
----------
GM3 features popular prefabs from the [Product Primitives team](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/primitives/home?authuser=0) and other Google teams to bring more visibility to recommended cross-Google tools, components, and functionality. Prefabs in GM3 were formerly known as "product primitives.", however the term "primitives" is being reassessed for inclusivity. Some design systems may still use the term "primitives."

The name "prefab" was defined internally by the [Search Design System](https://carbon.googleplex.com/search-design-system/) (SDS). [Check out SDS prefabs](https://carbon.googleplex.com/search-design-system/pages/building-blocks/prefabs) for more details, and to view their catalog of Search-specific prefabs.
All recommended prefabs
-----------------------

These prefabs are **recommended by Material**. Use them rather than building your own version. Some may not fully use GM3 yet. Guidelines and documentation are on their team's sites.

* [Google Feedback](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview/tab-1/latest#e1df0556-9b03-429c-b33f-a5d2e8caef85)

* [Google Notifications Platform (GNP)](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview/tab-1/latest#e4f99402-61f1-474a-ad01-aff0a16f2ec5)

* [HaTS](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview/tab-1/latest#fe8a7e62-2010-4aa7-9377-0e7071dc5d4a)

* [In-product help](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview/tab-1/latest#9356f414-4cfb-404e-8347-8b80e6fff735)

* [OneGoogle](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview/tab-1/latest#e0175ac6-b6c4-485c-b70e-a9a96ceb7066)

* [Payments platform](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview/tab-1/latest#079000bc-137e-404a-b914-52dc5b9819e3)

* [People ShareKit](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview/tab-1/latest#31616d46-a5eb-4c98-8320-c36191cf7749)

* [Text-to-speech (TTS) voice framework](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview/tab-1/latest#a3c4b3a9-eec4-4ccf-9b0a-3263c2bae777)
### Google Feedback {#isPasted}

Google Feedback gathers feedback with screenshots from a product.{#isPasted}

**Guidelines:** [Feedback Site](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/feedback/getting-started/enable-feedback)  
**Sticker sheet:** Not available  
**Eng docs:** [Implementation guides](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/company/teams/uservoice/feedback/index.md?cl=head)

**All docs:** [Stacks](https://stacks-v4.corp.google.com/product/feedback?productPhase=ga&productPhase=sa&productPhase=pre)  
**Owner:** [User Voice](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/1387095371111?hq=type%3Ateams&q=help%20experience)  
**File a bug:** [go/feedback-bug](https://go/feedback-bug)  
**Contact:** product-uplift-aloha@
### Google Notifications platform (GNP) {#isPasted}

[GNP](http://go/gnp) is a self-service, multi-channel notifications platform for managing and sending many types of notifications, such as in-app, push, SMS, email, and desktop.{#isPasted}

GNP combines 3 notification services from the Google Notifications Team (GaNT):

* **Chime**   
  Push notifications to Android, iOS, Web Push; Email, SMS, and more.
* **Gamma**   
  Advanced email campaigns, also supports SMS and push notifications.
* **Growth Platform (GrowthKit)** In-app campaigns for feature discovery, rating prompts, cross-app promos, permission prompts, and custom prompts.

**Guidelines:** [Push notification best practices](http://go/noti-bp)  
**Sticker sheets:** [GrowthKit Figma](http://go/growthkit-gm3) • Chime (unavailable) • Gamma (unavailable)  
**Eng docs:** [GNP docs](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/notifications/platform/g3doc/index.md?cl=head) • [GNP knowledge base](http://go/gnp-kb)

**All docs:** [Stacks](https://stacks-v4.corp.google.com/product/gnp?productPhase=ga&productPhase=sa&productPhase=pre)  
****Product site:**** [Google Notifications Platform](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/gant-platforms/gnp)**Owner:** [GNP team](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/gant-platforms/gnp)  
**Questions:** [YAQs](http://go/gnp-yaqs)  
**Contact:** gnp-leads@
### HaTS {#isPasted}

Happiness Tracking Surveys (HaTS) are the Google standard for in-product surveys to measure user attitude.

**Guidelines:** [Carbon](https://carbon.googleplex.com/ha-ts-ui-kit/pages/introduction)  
**Design kit:** [Figma](https://www.figma.com/file/wLxaXTPcCvjd8a4uC0tb1s/%F0%9F%A7%A9-HaTS-Design-Kit?type=design&node-id=0-1&mode=design)  
**Eng docs:** [go/hats](http://go/hats)

**All docs:** [Stacks](https://stacks-v4.corp.google.com/product/hats)  
**Owner:** [User Voice](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/1387095371111?hq=type%3Ateams&q=help%20experience)**Contact:** [go/ask-hats](http://go/ask-hats)
### In-product help {#isPasted}

Provides access to Help Center resources from within the product.

**Guidelines:** Temporarily unavailable  
**Design kit:** [Figma](https://www.figma.com/file/ENbY6icKHOi8uwR9v38iL3/HeX-M3-Library?type=design&node-id=15037%3A12925&mode=design&t=oFVcyHgAVaayCMce-1)  
**Eng docs:** [g3docs](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/company/teams/googlehelp/index.md?cl=head)

**All docs:** [Stacks](https://stacks-v4.corp.google.com/product/googlehelp)  
**Owner:** [Help Experience Team](https://carbon.googleplex.com/help-experience-ux/pages/intro){#isPasted}**Contact:** iph-team@
### OneGoogle {#isPasted}

The OneGoogle bar, account particle, and account menu are the essential prefabs for providing a consistent identity and branding experience across 200+ Google products. {#isPasted}

**Guidelines:** [go/og](http://go/og)  
**Sticker sheet:** [Mobile](https://www.figma.com/file/nQIsi5G1bKq4sroD4auoOw/OneGoogle-GM3---Mobile-Stickersheets?type=design&node-id=0-1&mode=design&t=0Seh84ZSmoJYSALh-0), [Web](https://www.figma.com/file/ZvDGePLmuDHoQ2GdGLPkIP/OneGoogle-GM3---Web-Stickersheets?type=design&node-id=1357-14795&mode=design&t=DWF8MsZCuBDtpTzE-0)  
**Eng docs:** [UX and Eng Resources](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/ogsite/resources/ux-engineering)

**All docs:** [Stacks](https://stacks-v4.corp.google.com/product/onegoogle)  
**Owner:** [OneGoogle](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/9362720912)  
**Contact:**og-hello@
### Payments platform {#isPasted}

Google Payments Platform is a set of UIs, APIs, billing systems, and infrastructure that powers monetization for over 100 first-party Alphabet products.{#isPasted}

**Guidelines:** [Carbon](https://carbon.googleplex.com/payments-platform)  
**Sticker sheet:** [Figma](https://go/pp-stickersheet-gm)  
**Eng docs:** [Development overview](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/company/teams/payments-platform/integrator-corner/development-overview.md?cl=head)

**All docs:** [Stacks](https://stacks-v4.corp.google.com/product/payments)  
**Owner:** [Payments Platform](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/6696330588)  
**Contact:** payments-platform-docs@
### People ShareKit

People ShareKit is a multi-platform library (Android, iOS, and web) designed for sharing flows with a visual preview.

It includes full-screen preview layouts, secondary actions, share sheets, and more.

**Learn more:** [go/sharekit](http://go/sharekit)  
**Sticker sheet:** Not available  
**Eng docs:** [Android](http://go/sharekit-android), [iOS](http://go/sharekit-ios), [web](http://go/sharekit-web)

**All docs:** [Stacks](https://stacks-v6.corp.google.com/home/product/sendkit)  
**Owner:** [Sharing \& Connections](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/7827795111?q=Sharing%20%26%20Connections)  
**Contact:** [go/peoplestack-onboarding](http://go/peoplestack-onboarding)
### Text-to-speech (TTS) voice framework

A standardized set of design principles, 14 components, and guidelines for applying TTS experiences across Google products, including how to choose voice names and attributes.

Note: Full implementation is not yet available.

**Guidelines:** Temporarily unavailable
