## Overview
* Use a **menu** to show a temporary set of actions. To show actions on screen at all times, use a **toolbar** instead{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Menus can open from many components, including icon buttons, split buttons, and text fields
* **Context menus** provide actions for a specific element, like an image or highlighted text, and usually open with a secondary click
Availability \& resources
-------------------------
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------

In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with Liquid Glass styling or other visual changes. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
**Nov 2025**

In iOS 26, GM3-branded iOS menus are restyled to use glass effects.

[More details on iOS tab](/google-material-3/pages/menus/ios)
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
**Vertical menus** were introduced with new shapes, color styles, selection states, and refined submenu motion. Gaps can be used for a more flexible layout on Android. [More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
Variants:{#isPasted}

* Added **vertical menus**, recommended for new designs
* Baseline **menu** is still available 

Color styles:

* Standard
* Vibrant

Platforms:{#isPasted}

* Jetpack Compose
* Wiz
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* **Color** : New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* **Variants**: Dropdown menu and exposed dropdown menu are now both referred to as menu, since they differ only in the element which opens the menu surface
{#isPasted}
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Variants
--------
### Vertical menus
Use vertical menus for a more expressive look and feel, including rounded corners, standard and vibrant color styles, more selection states, and submenu motion.
### Baseline variant
In GM3 Expressive, baseline menu is still available to use, but doesn't have the latest shapes, color styles, selection states, and motion. [See baseline menu specs](/google-material-3/pages/menus/specs#eef930b2-a82e-4471-8c8f-f57c54970f0a)
|   **Variant**   |  **GM3**  | **GM3 Expressive** |
|-----------------|-----------|--------------------|
| Vertical menus  | --        | Available          |
| Menu (baseline) | Available | Available          |
Configurations
--------------
### Vertical menus layout
{#isPasted}{#isPasted}

| **Category** | **Configuration** |  **GM3**  | **GM3 Expressive** |
|--------------|-------------------|-----------|--------------------|
| Color        | Standard          | Available | Available          |
| Color        | Vibrant           | --        | Available          |
| Layout       | Standard          | Available | Available          |
| Layout       | Grouped           | --        | Available          |
Tokens \& specs
---------------
Select a component variant below to see its elements, attributes, tokens, and their values. [Learn about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Anatomy
-------
### Vertical menus
Color
-----
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For designers, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. In implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Menus have two color mappings:{#isPasted}

* Standard: Surface-based
* Vibrant: Tertiary-based

These mappings provide options for lower or higher visual emphasis. Vibrant menus are more prominent so should be used sparingly.
### Standard colors
### Vibrant colors
### High contrast
High contrast mode is an accessibility feature that aims to maximize legibility by using a limited color palette, like black and white. Focus rings are shown when buttons are focused. [Learn more about high contrast](/google-material-3/pages/color/how-the-system-works#a360c176-b99e-4804-8034-9884833009c8)
States {#isPasted}
------------------

States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or an interactive element. [More on interaction states](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/overview/)  

Shape morphing in vertical menus creates an expressive active state. As focus moves between submenus, the corner shape changes to highlight the active menu. [More on menu focus](/google-material-3/pages/menus/guidelines#0c30b7f2-06c2-4717-a37f-63f139a0fdcf)
Measurements
------------
Menu (baseline)
---------------
The baseline menu variant is available and continues to work in existing products. However, GM3 expressive vertical menus are recommended for new designs.
### Baseline tokens \& specs
Select a component variant below to see its elements, attributes, tokens, and their values. [Learn about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
### Anatomy
### Color {#isPasted}
### States {#isPasted}
#### Default menu items
#### Selected menu items
[State specs are in the token module above](/google-material-3/pages/menus/specs#f11a89ef-e542-484b-921d-cbadfb2c1bfe)
### Measurements
### Configurations
A baseline menu appears when a person interacts with a button, action, or other control.

A few examples:

1. Button

2. Text field

3. Icon button

4. Selected text
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases {#isPasted}
---------------------
People should be able to do the following using assistive technology:{#isPasted}

* Navigate to, open, and close a menu
* Navigate between and select menu items
Interaction \& style
--------------------
Menu items need certain cues to clearly show when they're selected: {#isPasted}

* By default, menu items change shape and color when selected
* The default color contrast is 3:1 between selected and unselected menu items
* It's recommended to include another visual cue, like a checkmark

<br />

<br />

<br />
star  
Note:  
Google products must apply all cues: shape, color, and a visual cue like a checkmark
Flexibility \& slots
--------------------
Use caution when adding slots to menus:{#isPasted}

* Make sure the menu remains accessible
* Elements must follow the rules and interaction patterns of the menu component
* Keep the same menu item padding
* Targets should be 48x48dp or larger

Don't add buttons, switches, or other direct actions into the menu item. Nested elements should only perform one action. Adding multiple actions can break keyboard navigation and screen reader functionality.

[More on slots in menus](/google-material-3/pages/menus/guidelines#0523c3b7-0388-4a3c-a610-7afc6a713433)
Focus
-----
**Initial focus**{#isPasted}

When a menu opens, focus should be placed on the first menu item. This allows people using a keyboard or other assistive technologies to begin navigating the menu immediately.

**Exiting a menu**

People expect to exit a menu by:

* Selecting an option
* Tapping **Escape** or outside of the menu
* Using the system back button

Where focus is placed after closing the menu depends on the app.
iOS has different navigation methods.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
|             Keys              |                                        Actions                                         |
|            **Tab**            |                                  Focus lands on menu                                   |
|     **Space** or**Enter**     | For closed menus: Opens menu or submenu{#isPasted} For open menus: Selects a menu item |
|  **Up** and **Down** arrows   |       For closed menus: Opens menu For open menus: Moves focus to the next item        |
| **Left** and **Right** arrows |                               Opens or closes a submenu                                |
|            Letters            |                 Focus moves to the next menu item starting with letter                 |
|          **Escape**           |                                      Closes menu                                       |
|-------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
star  
Note:  
Keyboard conventions differ slightly by platform. For example, Space or Enter opens a menu on Android and web, while only Space opens a menu on iOS.
Interactability
---------------
Disabled menu items can receive focus but aren't selectable.  

Dividers and gaps can't receive focus.
Navigating to a submenu
-----------------------
On web, submenus have a **magic triangle** behavior for more intuitive mouse navigation.{#isPasted}

This keeps the submenu open when the mouse moves within a triangular area between the triggering item and the submenu.

[More on submenus](/google-material-3/pages/menus/guidelines#ee242da2-4b01-4004-a6ca-aec902985e9f)
Labeling elements
-----------------
Accessibility labels are used with assistive technology devices like screen readers.   

The accessibility label should be the same as the menu item text.

The role is [dependent on platform](/google-material-3/pages/menus/accessibility#b69ce8e6-fcea-4c26-adb5-355901a429dc).

<br />
|----------------|----------------|----------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|---------|
| **Element**    | **A11y label** | **Role (Web)** | **Role (Android Views)**   | **Role (Jetpack Compose)** | **iOS** |
| Menu item text | Preview        | Menu item      | Generic actionable element | Generic actionable element | Button  |
For menu items with text and an icon, the icon's accessibility label should be marked as **decorative** to avoid redundant verbalizations.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://goto.google.com/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage {#isPasted}
-----------------
Use a menu to show a temporary set of actions. To show actions on screen at all times, use a toolbar instead.{#isPasted}

A menu takes up less space than a set of radio buttons or chips.

### Color options

Menus have two color mappings:

* Standard: Surface-based, lower visual emphasis
* Vibrant: Tertiary-based, higher visual emphasis

Vibrant menus are more prominent, and should be used sparingly.
### Opening menus

Menus temporarily appear in front of all other permanent UI elements.

A menu should open when a person:

* Selects an element, such as an icon, button, or text field

* Performs a specific action to trigger the menu, like right-click or press-and-hold

Use menus in situations that need extra actions, like:

* Overflow menus

* Text field dropdown menus

* Select menus

* Context menus
### Menu groups

Vertical menu items can be grouped by adding a divider or small gap. Use groups to bundle similar actions together.{#isPasted}

[Gaps and dividers guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/menus/guidelines#f4c40e5e-c23c-45f9-9907-b97f50a91c55)
### Context menus
Context menus provide a list of additional actions a person can take on an item. A secondary click, like a right-click on a mouse or a two-finger tap on a trackpad, opens a context menu.
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
### Menu items
Menu items can include label text, leading icons, trailing icons, and keyboard commands. {#isPasted}

When a menu item can only be used under specific conditions, it should appear disabled rather than be removed.
### Gaps \& dividers (optional) {#isPasted}

Gaps and dividers can be used to separate and group menu items.{#isPasted}

**Gaps**

Use a gap to visually divide menu items into distinct groups. Gaps are more expressive than dividers and make the relationship between items clear.

* Avoid changing the size of the gap
* Limit the number of gaps in a menu to one or two
* Don't use gaps in scrollable menus
star  
Note:  
Gaps are not currently available on web
**Dividers** {#isPasted}

Dividers create a more subtle separation between items. Use a divider for:

* Scrollable menus
* Text fields with a dropdown menu, where a grouped treatment isn't appropriate

On web, use a divider to separate menu items.
Flexibility \& slots
--------------------
Menus have custom slots that support more flexible item layouts.{#isPasted}

When creating a complicated menu, think of the menu item as a container with a swappable slot.

Slots work best with simple content such as:

* Images
* Progress indicators
* Color swatches
**Slot accessibility**   

Use caution when adding slots to menus:{#isPasted}

* Make sure the menu remains accessible
* Elements must follow the rules and interaction patterns of the menu component
* Keep the same menu item padding
* Targets should be 48x48dp or larger

Don't add buttons, switches, or other direct actions into the menu item. Nested elements should only perform one action. Adding multiple actions can break keyboard navigation and screen reader functionality.

[More on required accessibility guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/menus/accessibility)
Placement
---------
A menu is positioned relative to the window edge. It typically appears below, next to, or in front of the element that generates it.{#isPasted}

If a menu is in a position to be cut off, it should automatically reposition to appear to the left, right, or above the element that generates it.
### Submenus

Submenus should open next to the parent menu item without overlapping it.

Submenus are best used on large screens where there's space. [See adaptive guidance](/google-material-3/pages/menus/guidelines#550e848c-5ad6-4a8a-a23c-c42d919fb7f2) for alternatives on mobile.

[More on submenus accessibility](/google-material-3/pages/menus/accessibility#e93cc5b9-43ff-4e7c-bff0-c64c5c6de6c1)
star  
Note:  
Submenus are not currently available on Jetpack Compose
Adaptive design
---------------
### Compact window sizes

Consider adapting menus into bottom sheets on small screens. They have more space to display additional items and longer labels.
### Other window sizes

On medium and expanded windows, menus are most effective as they appear in context with the content. On larger screens, menus can also display more items, and can use submenus to organize complex sets of options.
Behavior
--------
### Appearing {#isPasted}

A menu can appear when a person interacts with an element on the page, like a button, text field, filter chip, or highlighted text.{#isPasted}

A menu's position on screen affects where and how it appears. If opened at the top of the screen, it expands downwards to avoid being cropped.
**Motion**{#isPasted}

Menus use an enter and exit transition. This animation creates a relationship between the menu and the element that generates it.

When a menu expands, the trigger element becomes pressed. When an item is selected, a ripple appears on touch.
In dense products, such as on desktop, menus can open instantly to reduce motion.
### Filtering

A menu can include a text field to filter options. This pattern is also known as autocomplete.

As someone types, the list of menu options filters to show relevant results. This helps people quickly find the right option from a long list.

Menu items ease into their new position as the menu is filtered.

[See Wiz autocomplete developer docs](https://gm-catalog.corp.google.com/material3/autocomplete)
### Scrolling {#isPasted}

Menus can scroll when all menu items can't display at once. In this state, menus show a persistent scrollbar.{#isPasted}

Don't use gaps if a menu scrolls; this is currently unsupported.
### Selecting {#isPasted}

When a menu is opened, the corresponding button or icon button should remain the same visually, with the addition of a pressed state.{#isPasted}

This should happen even when opening from a keyboard shortcut.

<br />
### Single- and multi-select menus

Menus can allow either single-select or multi-select actions:{#isPasted}

* **Single-select** menus can have one item selected at a time. When a new item is selected, the previously selected item is automatically unselected.
* **Multi-select** menus can have many selected items. They stay open until the person dismisses the menu.

[More on selection accessibility requirements](/google-material-3/pages/menus/accessibility#f205207f-4f85-4d09-a591-7fdff501de6f)
Focus
-----

When a menu has multiple submenus, focus follows the current hovered or focused submenu. {#isPasted}

**Shape morphing**

As a person moves from one submenu to the next, the corners of the focused submenu become more rounded, while the unfocused submenu becomes less rounded. This adds a dynamic quality to menu interactions.
Density
-------
On web only, density levels control the spacing between elements. Increasing density decreases the top and bottom padding. [More on layout density](/google-material-3/pages/understanding-layout/density)
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.
Resources
---------
|                                                                                                                                                                             Type                                                                                                                                                                             |                                                                   Resource                                                                    |              Contact              |  Status   |
|                                                                                                                                                                            Design                                                                                                                                                                            | [GM3 iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=98745-30598&t=tooAEgycbK3jXX5y-0) (Figma) | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug) | Available |
|                                                                                                                                                                            Design                                                                                                                                                                            |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|-----------|
| Material Labs: iOS Liquid Glass Kit: Menus: [Context menu](https://www.figma.com/design/KCk1VpuDP9n0tgRxQ9Ubwe/Material-Labs--iOS-Liquid-Glass-Kit?m=auto&node-id=528-5757&t=4YjFNpc395IiZnu5-1), [action sheet](https://www.figma.com/design/KCk1VpuDP9n0tgRxQ9Ubwe/Material-Labs--iOS-Liquid-Glass-Kit?m=auto&node-id=61-163&t=4YjFNpc395IiZnu5-1) (Figma) | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug)                                                                                                             | Early access                      |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | [Apple Human Interface Guidelines: Menus](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/menus)                                | --                                | Available |
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------
star  
Note:  
The iOS 26 code and Design Kit are in early access, and the code has not yet undergone final accessibility review. Designs and guidelines may change when code is complete.
In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with Liquid Glass styling and other visual changes.

iOS 26 styling is automatically applied to this component. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
Branded iOS component available
-------------------------------
The Material menu is not implemented on iOS. Instead, use the default menu styling provided by Apple's APIs, with the addition of Google Symbols for icons.

Use action sheets to confirm actions or show secondary actions. Use menus in all other cases.

Unlike default Android styling, destructive actions on Apple platforms have red styling. When someone selects a destructive action, prompt them to confirm the action using a system action sheet.
iOS can surface a [context menu](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/context-menus) for quick actions on images, lists, links, and more. Context menus can contain common actions and destructive actions.
Use [Google Symbols](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons) so icons can adjust to a person's text preferences, like scaling. See [go/material-ios-icons](https://goto.google.com/material-ios-icons) for developer guidance.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Switch
Switches toggle the selection of an item on and off

## Guidelines
Usage {#isPasted}
-----------------
Switches are best used to adjust settings and other standalone options.

They make a binary selection:

* On and off

* True and false

The effects of a switch should start immediately, without needing to save.
Use switches to:{#isPasted}

* Toggle a single item on or off
* Immediately activate or deactivate something
Switches should be used instead of radio buttons if each item in a set can be independently controlled.
Switches control binary options, not opposing ones. A binary option represents a single selection that's either on or off.

Opposing options are when only one option in a set can be selected at a time, like a list or map view. Use a connected button group instead.
### Alternate selection controls

Checkboxes, radio buttons, and switches are the three main kinds of selection controls. They help people make choices, like selecting options or turning settings on and off.

Use checkboxes to select multiple related options in a list.

Use radio buttons to select a single option in a list.

Use switches to select standalone or more verbose options in a list, like settings.
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
### Icon (optional) {#isPasted}

The switch handle can contain an optional icon.
Icons can be used to visually emphasize the switch's selection. The icon's meaning should be clear and unambiguous to help people understand whether switch is on or off.
### Label text {#isPasted}

Switches should always be paired with an inline label describing what the switch controls when selected.
Placement {#isPasted}
---------------------
Switches are often arranged in stacked layouts.
Behavior
--------
A switch is successfully toggled when the handle slides to the other side of the track after an interaction.
When a person toggles a switch, its handle size changes and the corresponding action takes effect immediately.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Tokens \& specs
---------------
Browse the component elements, attributes, tokens, and their values. [++Learn more about design tokens++](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Color
-----

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview/)
### Adjacent text label color

Use the color role **on surface** for adjacent text labels. This remains the same even if interacting with the label or component.
States
------

States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element. [++Learn more about interaction states++](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states)
[State specs are in the token module above](/google-material-3/pages/switch/specs#e33deefa-8bbe-4adb-89a9-0864bae415d3)
Measurements
------------
|      Element       |                                                      Attribute                                                       | Value |
|       Track        |                                                        Height                                                        | 32dp  |
|       Track        |
|       Track        |
|       Track        |
|--------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------|
| Width              | 52dp                                                                                                                 |
| Outline width      | 2dp                                                                                                                  |
| Shape              | [md.sys.shape.corner.full](/google-material-3/pages/shape/shape-scale-&-tokens#b050acfd-ddc9-44a0-8c28-a51fd3f420fb) |
| Height - with icon | 24dp                                                                                                                 |
| Height (selected)  | 24dp                                                                                                                 |
| Height (pressed)   | 28dp                                                                                                                 |
| Width (unselected) | 16dp                                                                                                                 |
| Width - with icon  | 24dp                                                                                                                 |
| Width (selected)   | 24dp                                                                                                                 |
| Width (pressed)    | 28dp                                                                                                                 |
| Shape              | [md.sys.shape.corner.full](/google-material-3/pages/shape/shape-scale-&-tokens#b050acfd-ddc9-44a0-8c28-a51fd3f420fb) |
| Shape              | [md.sys.shape.corner.full](/google-material-3/pages/shape/shape-scale-&-tokens#b050acfd-ddc9-44a0-8c28-a51fd3f420fb) |
Configurations {#isPasted}
--------------------------
1. Without icons
2. Icon on selected switch
3. Icon on selected and unselected switch

Note:  
The iOS 26 code and Design Kit are in early access, and the code has not yet undergone final accessibility review. Designs and guidelines may change when code is complete.
In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with Liquid Glass styling and other visual changes. iOS 26 styling is automatically applied to this component. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
Branded iOS component available
-------------------------------
Switches on iOS are only used in lists, so they have a different size and style optimized for lists.

* Use the iOS switch with GM3 branding

* Only use iOS switches in list rows. iOS switches are 28pt tall, smaller than the 44pt GAR minimum tap area. When in list rows, the whole row is tappable, meeting GAR requirements.

* Outside of lists, use toggle icon buttons or checkboxes instead of switches.

Switches support Apple's system accessibility features including increase contrast and on/off labels.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug){#isPasted}

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------

People should be able to do the following with assistive technology:

* Navigate to a switch with a keyboard or switch input

* Toggle the switch on and off

* Get appropriate feedback based on input type documented under [Interaction \& style](/google-material-3/pages/switch/accessibility#c85e7d47-cf08-4553-b80f-284185330118)
Interaction \& style
--------------------
The switch handle increases in size to indicate interactivity for both touch and cursor control interactions.{#isPasted}

**Touch**   
When tapped or dragged, the handle size grows, providing interaction feedback.

**Cursor**   
When hovered (in both on and off states), the hover area grows, providing a visual cue that the handle is interactive. When clicked, the handle size grows.
### Avoid applying density by default {#isPasted}

Don't apply density to switches by default --- this lowers their targets below our best practice of 48x48 CSS pixels. Instead, give people a way to choose a higher density, like selecting a denser layout or changing the theme.

To ensure that this density setting can easily be reverted when it's active, keep all targets to change it at a minimum 48x48 CSS pixels each.
Initial focus {#isPasted}
-------------------------
Initial focus lands directly on the switch's handle, since it's the primary interactive element of the component.
Keyboard navigation {#isPasted}
-------------------------------

|          Keys          |             Actions              |
|        **Tab**         | Focus lands on the switch handle |
| **Space** or **Enter** |  Toggles the handle on and off   |
|------------------------|----------------------------------|
Labeling elements {#isPasted}
-----------------------------
The accessibility label for a switch uses the adjacent label text if implemented correctly. {#isPasted}

Assistive tech such as a screen reader will read the UI text followed by the component's role.
When the visible UI text is ambiguous, accessibility labels need to be more descriptive. For example, a switch visibly labelled **Photo album** would benefit from additional information to clarify the switch's function.

Consider making the adjacent label text more descriptive when possible. This reduces the need for different accessibility text.

## Overview
* Use switches (not radio buttons) if the items in a list can be independently controlled

* Switches are the best way to let people adjust settings

* Make sure the switch's selection (on or off) is visible at a glance
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------

In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with Liquid Glass styling and other visual changes. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
**Nov 2025**

In iOS 26, switches are a new size and are optimized to be used in lists.

[More details on iOS tab](/google-material-3/pages/switch/ios)
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* Accessibility: Visual presentation is more accessible{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Color: New color mappings meet Material's non-text-contrast requirements in addition to compatibility with dynamic color
* Icons: Ability to have an optional icon within the switch handle
* Layout: Track is taller and wider
{#isPasted}
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)


# Time pickers
Time pickers help users select and set a specific time

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------
People should be able to use assistive technology to: {#isPasted}

* Select or enter hours/minutes, and in some cases, seconds/milliseconds
* Choose from multiple time formats, including 24-hour clock view and AM/PM
* Enter time selection manually using input fields
Interaction \& style
--------------------
Time pickers should allow manual time entry through text input, rather than exclusively through the dial selector. This makes it easier for those using keyboard inputs rather than touchscreens. {#isPasted}

If a screen is not large enough to display the dial selector, consider displaying the input selector alone. Currently for Android Views, the dial selector is always visible.

The input selector should be accessible from the dial selector via the keyboard icon. This interaction allows multiple input methods and makes the time picker accessible for assistive technology users.
### Targets

Targets for dial selectors should be 48x48dp.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
|          Keys          |                 Actions                 |
|        **Tab**         | Focus lands on (non-disabled) time slot |
| **Space** or **Enter** | Activates the (non-disabled) time slot  |
|------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
Labeling elements
-----------------
If the input text is correctly linked, assistive tech like a screenreader will read the component's role first, then the UI text.
The dial selector will read a selection of total hours, such as **Hour 7 of 12**.
### Dial selector {#isPasted}

|                  Element                  |         Accessibility label         | Role (Wiz and Jetpack Compose) | Role (Android Views) |
|         Hour input (input picker)         |                Hour                 |           Text input           |          -           |
|               Minutes input               |               Minute                |           Text input           |          -           |
|              AM/PM selection              |              AM or PM               |     Radio button (in list)     |  Checkbox (in list)  |
|              Keyboard button              |         Toggle input picker         |             Button             |        Button        |
|               Cancel button               |               Cancel                |             Button             |        Button        |
|                 OK button                 |                 OK                  |             Button             |        Button        |
| Clock dial time selection (dial selector) | {Value} Hours or minutes of {Total} |             Button             |          -           |
|-------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|--------------------------------|----------------------|
### Input selector {#isPasted}

|          Element          | Accessibility label | Role (Wiz and Jetpack Compose) | Role (Android Views) |
| Hour input (input picker) |        Hour         |           Text input           |          -           |
|       Minutes input       |       Minute        |           Text input           |          -           |
|       Clock button        | Toggle dial picker  |             Button             |        Button        |
|       Cancel button       |       Cancel        |             Button             |        Button        |
|         OK button         |         OK          |             Button             |        Button        |
|---------------------------|---------------------|--------------------------------|----------------------|
*** ** * ** ***

File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Time pickers are modal and cover the main content

* Two variants: dial and input

* People can select hours, minutes, or periods of time

* Make sure time can easily be selected by hand on a mobile device
Availability \& resources
-------------------------
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------

In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with Liquid Glass styling or other visual changes. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
**Nov 2025**

Material iOS uses a GM3-branded iOS date picker configured for time.

[More details on iOS tab](/google-material-3/pages/time-pickers/i-os)
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Time pickers allow people to enter a specific time value. They're displayed in dialogs and can be used to select hours, minutes, or periods of time.{#isPasted}

They can be used for a wide range of scenarios. Common use cases include:

* Setting an alarm
* Scheduling a meeting

Time pickers are not ideal for nuanced or granular time selection, such as milliseconds for a stopwatch application.
### Time input picker

Time input pickers allow people to specify a time using keyboard numbers. This input option should be accessible from any other mobile time picker interface by tapping the keyboard icon.
### 24-hour time selection

The dial view can be changed to reflect time selection across 24 hours. This option is set outside of the time picker component, typically through system settings.
Anatomy
-------
### Container

Like dialogs, the container should appear above other screen elements. To focus attention, surfaces behind the container have a temporary scrim overlay to make them less prominent.
### Input selector

The input selector is a unique kind of text field input. It differs from typical text field inputs in that it has:

* An added highlight to call attention to the selected field

* A larger shape, size, and font

* A label below the field

Hours and minutes should have separate inputs. For people using a 12-hour clock, an AM/PM selector appears to the right of minutes. For people using a 24-hour clock, the AM/PM selector shouldn't appear.
### Dial selector

Dial selectors always mimic a round watch face. Hours and minutes can be selected by tapping a number or dragging the dial selector track.

When representing a 12-hour dial, all numbers appear in the outer ring. When representing a 24-hour dial, even numbers appear in an inner ring, and odd numbers appear in an outer ring.
### Text \& icon buttons {#isPasted}

Icon buttons are used to switch between the input selector, represented by a keyboard, and the dial selector, represented by a clock.{#isPasted}

Text buttons are used to exit the dialog (**Cancel** ) and save the selector input (**OK**).
### Landscape orientation

The clock dial interface adapts to a device's orientation. In landscape mode, the stacked input and selection options are positioned side-by-side.
Placement
---------
Time pickers shouldn't be obscured by other elements.

Time pickers should change orientation or variant to ensure they aren't cropped by the edge of the screen.

Time pickers are modal windows above a scrim. This puts the time pickers at the forefront of a person's view, calling attention to make a selection of time.
Adaptive design
---------------
Time pickers can swap between orientation or variant depending on device orientation and viewport constraints.

For example, the time picker can change to landscape orientation on larger breakpoints or when viewport height is limited, to avoid scrolling the dial presentation.

Time pickers can fallback to the input time picker when there isn't enough vertical real estate to present the landscape orientation without scrolling.
### Density {#isPasted}

Don't apply density to the time picker dial when the viewport is constrained. Instead, use an input picker.
Behavior
--------
There are two primary methods for selecting time with the mobile time picker. People can:{#isPasted}

* Type in a specific value in the hour and minute fields
* Select the hour or minute field from the text input and adjust the clock dial to simultaneously change the corresponding time field above
### Appearing \& disappearing

Like other kinds of dialogs, time pickers use an enter and exit transition pattern to appear on the screen.{#isPasted}

To exit a time picker, the input can either be confirmed (**OK** ) or dismissed (**Cancel**). Interacting outside of the dialog will also dismiss the time picker. Unless one of these actions is taken, a time picker will continue to retain focus.
### Toggle between dial \& input

Tapping the keyboard icon on a mobile time picker switches the view to the input picker.
### Scrolling

Time pickers should avoid scrolling, and swap component orientation or variant based on device orientation or viewport size.

Time pickers don't scroll with elements outside of the modal window, such as the background.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: YAQs  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://goto.google.com/gm3-bug)


# Checkbox
Checkboxes let users select one or more items from a list, or turn an item on or off

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.
Resources
---------
|----------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|--------------|
| **Type**       | **Resource**                                                                                                                                                                                      | **Contact**                       | **Status**   |
| Design         | [GM3 iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=98008-32832) (Figma)                                                                          | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug) | Available    |
| Design         | [Material Labs: iOS Liquid Glass Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/KCk1VpuDP9n0tgRxQ9Ubwe/Material-Labs--iOS-Liquid-Glass-Kit?node-id=528-5742&t=NDqdoks6xqfWTzQL-4) (Figma)                      | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug) | Early access |
| Design         | [Apple Human Interface Guidelines: Lists and tables](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/lists-and-tables)                                                              | --                                | Available    |
| Implementation | [GM3 iOS dev docs: Radio group / checkmark group cells](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Lists/cell-catalog.md?polyglot=objective-c&cl=head#checkmark-cells) | material-ios-team@                | Available    |

--------------------------

The iOS 26 code and Design Kit are in early access, and the code has not yet undergone final accessibility review. Designs and guidelines may change when code is complete.
In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with Liquid Glass styling and other visual changes. iOS 26 styling is automatically applied to this component. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)

-------------------------------

*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs){#isPasted}  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Use checkboxes (instead of switches or radio buttons) if multiple options can be selected from a list
* Label should be scannable
* Selected items are more prominent than unselected items
Availability \& resources
-------------------------
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------

In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with Liquid Glass styling or other visual changes. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
**Nov 2025**

Material iOS uses a GM3-branded [list with checkmark cells](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Lists/cell-catalog.md?polyglot=objective-c&cl=head#checkmark-cells).

[More details on iOS tab](/google-material-3/pages/checkbox/ios)
Differences from GM2
--------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color
* States: New indeterminate states as well as error states for unselected, selected, and indeterminate{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------
People should be able to use assistive technology to:{#isPasted}

* Navigate to a checkbox
* Toggle the checkbox on and off
* Get appropriate feedback based on input type documented under [Interaction \& style](/google-material-3/pages/checkbox/accessibility#0478ed50-ee49-4750-97fb-2b4f95b801a8)
Interaction \& style
--------------------
Users should be able to select either the text label or the checkbox to select an option.
The parent checkbox has three states: selected, unselected, and indeterminate. {#isPasted}

Checkboxes can be selected or unselected regardless of the state of the other checkboxes in a group.

If some, but not all, child checkboxes are checked, the parent checkbox becomes indeterminate. Selecting an indeterminate parent checkbox will check all of its child checkboxes.
Avoid applying density by default {#isPasted}
---------------------------------------------
Don't apply density to checkboxes by default --- this lowers their targets below our best practice of 48x48 CSS pixels. Instead, give people a way to choose a higher density, like selecting a denser layout or changing the theme.{#isPasted}

To ensure that this density setting can be easily reverted when it's active, keep all the targets to change it at minimum 48x48 CSS pixels each.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
| **Keys**  |                         **Action**                         |
|-----------|------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Tab**   | Moves to the next element in a group                       |
| **Space** | Toggles a focused checkbox between selected and unselected |

{#isPasted}
Labeling elements
-----------------
If the UI text is correctly linked to the checkbox, assistive tech (such as a screen reader) will read the UI text followed by the component's role.{#isPasted}

The accessibility label for an individual checkbox is typically the same as its adjacent text label.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Use checkboxes to:{#isPasted}

* Select one or more options from a list
* Present a list containing sub-selections
* Turn an item on or off in a desktop environment
* Visually group similar options together
Checkboxes should be used instead of switches if multiple, related options can be selected from a list. Checkboxes visually group similar items effectively and take up less space than switches.
### Alternate selection controls

Checkboxes, radio buttons, and switches are the three main selection controls. They all help people make choices, like selecting options or switching settings on or off.

* Use checkboxes to select multiple related options in a list.

* Use radio buttons to select a single option in a list.

* Use switches to select standalone or more verbose options in a list, like settings.
Anatomy
-------
Responsive layout
-----------------
In expanded window sizes, placing checkboxes within a contained region such as a side sheet can help group related controls and available actions.
Behavior
--------
Multiple checkboxes in a list can be selected.
Checkboxes can have a parent-child relationship with other checkboxes.{#isPasted}

* When the parent checkbox is checked, all child checkboxes are checked
* If a parent checkbox is unchecked, all child checkboxes are unchecked
* If some, but not all, child checkboxes are checked, the parent checkbox becomes an indeterminate checkbox. Checking an indeterminate checkbox checks all child items.
When selected, a checkbox clearly and instantly communicates its selected state.{#isPasted}

If used to turn something on or off, the action should be immediately executed.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Tokens \& specs
---------------

Browse the component elements, attributes, tokens, and their values.
Anatomy
-------
Color
-----
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
### Adjacent text label color

Use the color role **on surface** for adjacent text labels. This remains the same even if interacting with the label or component.
States
------
States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element. [Learn more about interaction states](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states)
Measurements
------------
|       Attribute        |     Value      |
|     Container size     |      18dp      |
| Container corner shape |      2dp       |
|       Icon size        |      18dp      |
|     Icon alignment     | Center-aligned |
|      Target size       |      48dp      |
|    State-layer size    |      40dp      |
|------------------------|----------------|
*** ** * ** ***

# Date pickers
Date pickers let people select a date, or a range of dates

## Specs
Tokens \& specs {#isPasted}
---------------------------
Select a component variant below to see its elements, attributes, tokens, and their values.
Docked date picker {#isPasted}
------------------------------
### Docked date picker color {#isPasted}
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview){#isPasted}
### Docked date picker measurements {#isPasted}
### Docked date picker configurations {#isPasted}
Modal date picker {#isPasted}
-----------------------------
### Modal date picker color {#isPasted}

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
### Modal date picker measurements {#isPasted}
### Modal date picker configurations {#isPasted}
Modal date input {#isPasted}
----------------------------
### Modal date input color {#isPasted}

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
### Modal date input measurements {#isPasted}
### Modal date input configurations {#isPasted}
Element states {#isPasted}
--------------------------
[State specs are available in the token module above](/google-material-3/pages/date-pickers/specs#8b86bd31-7435-4274-aa77-f3bc8b226bdd)
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------
People should be able to:

* Enter dates manually by inputting text, without using the picker
* Use multiple input methods, making it accessible to those using assistive technology  

On the docked date picker, the text field can be used for input.  
On the modal date picker, the date input option should be available using the edit icon.
Interaction \& style
--------------------
The edit icon indicates the ability to switch to the modal date input.  

Interactive targets for all elements meet Material's 48x48dp minimum touch target requirement. Increasing density would negatively impact accessibility by limiting tappable/clickable targets.
Date entry methods
------------------
The date entry component offers two ways to enter a date:{#isPasted}

* Direct text entry into a text field
* Through the date picker

The calendar icon is the exclusive entry point for the date picker.

This improves efficiency for a screen reader and other keyboard users, as it makes interaction with the date picker optional and reduces the amount of key presses required to input a date.

Each input is a separate tab stop, which improves discoverability of the control.
Accessible date input
---------------------
Automatically format the date after the user hits "Enter" or navigates out of the text field. Don't automatically format the date by adding slashes or other special characters while the user is typing (also known as input masks). This can cause confusion for people using screen readers because it changes what they typed.{#isPasted}

To reduce errors, accept a range of formats including dashes, spaces, slashes, dots, and 0 to the left of a single digit month/day. This is especially helpful for assistive technology users who might be more prone to errors when interacting with complex inputs.
Optional **Clear** button
-------------------------
If it's not needed for your use case, remove the **Clear** button from the screen to reduce the number of tab stops for keyboard users.
Affordance for keyboard shortcuts
---------------------------------
Ensure keyboard shortcuts are readily available for keyboard and screen reader users by providing the shortcut key in the tooltip. It should be included in the hint description to be read out by the screen reader.  

As shown here, the previous year button is interactive and can therefore be focused via the keyboard. Upon focus, the tooltip explains the behavior of the button and shows the shortcut key.
Truncated labels \& tooltips
----------------------------
Truncating labels isn't ideal, but tooltips allow the full text to be shown on hover or keyboard focus.  

Days of the week are not interactive and are therefore not focusable via keyboard, yet the tooltip is available on hover. The date picker relies on the conventionality of these abbreviations for some assistive technology users.
Color contrast between dates
----------------------------
Dates should have contrast of at least 4.5:1 between the link text colors and the background.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
|       **Keys**       |                   **Actions**                    |
|----------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Enter/return         | Enter/return                                     |
| Enter/return         | Closes the calendar and saves the selected date  |
| Page up/down         | Move to the same date on next/previous month     |
| Home/End             | Move to the first day of the month               |
| Shift + Page up/down | Moves to the same date in the next/previous year |
| Shift + M            | Moves to the month list dropdown                 |
| Shift + Y            | Moves to the year list dropdown                  |
Labeling elements
-----------------
The text field's accessibility label should clearly state the purpose of the input (for example, event date or reservation date) and should match the placeholder text when the field is empty.  

The helper text (below the text field) should specify the date format (for example, MM/DD/YYYY or YYYY/MM/DD) and act as a description for the text field. The default helper text is "MM/DD/YYYY," but this can be customized.
|          **Element**           | **A11y label** | **Role** |
|--------------------------------|----------------|----------|
| Previous / next month and year | "{label}"      | Button   |
| Month and year dropdowns       | "{label}"      | Button   |
| Days of the week               | Column header  |          |
| Month grid                     | Grid           |          |
Screen reader verbalizations
----------------------------
To support screen reader users, labels are used to enumerate the complete date. This allows screen reader users to hear the full context of "Monday, August 17" instead of just part of the date.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)[](http://go/gm3-yaqs)File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://goto.google.com/gm3-bug){#isPasted}

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.
Resources
---------
|                                                                                    **Type**                                                                                    |                                                                 **Resource**                                                                 |                                      **Contact**                                      | **Status** |
|                                                                                     Design                                                                                     | [GM3 iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=98738-7555&t=Kjez3OFChGuLEBgb-0) (Figma) | [File a bug](https://b.corp.google.com/issues/new?component=1240212&template=1729813) | Available  |
|                                                                                     Design                                                                                     |
|                                                                                     Design                                                                                     |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------|
| [Material Labs: iOS Liquid Glass Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/KCk1VpuDP9n0tgRxQ9Ubwe/Material-Labs--iOS-Liquid-Glass-Kit?node-id=61-215&p=f&t=uKeNqwcbSTe9dXgP-0) (Figma) | [File a bug](https://b.corp.google.com/issues/new?component=1240212&template=1729813)                                                        | Early access                                                                          |
| [Apple Human Interface Guidelines: Pickers](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/pickers)                                                             | --                                                                                                                                           | Available                                                                             |
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------
star  
Note:  
The iOS 26 code and Design Kit are in early access, and the code has not yet undergone final accessibility review. Designs and guidelines may change when code is complete.
In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with Liquid Glass styling and other visual changes. iOS 26 styling is automatically applied to this component. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
Branded iOS component available
-------------------------------
Material iOS uses a GM3-branded iOS date picker.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs){#isPasted}  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Date pickers let people select a date or range of dates. They should be suitable for the context in which they appear.  

Date pickers can be embedded into:

1. Dialogs on compact [window sizes](/google-material-3/pages/applying-layout/window-size-classes#ab8d335c-8b02-42f4-95d2-b1095d507891) like mobile
2. Text field drop-downs on medium and expanded window sizes like tablet and desktop
There are three variants of date pickers:

1. Docked date picker

2. Modal date picker

3. Modal date input
Anatomy
-------
### Docked date picker
### Modal date picker
### Modal date input
### Full-screen date picker
Docked date picker
------------------
### Usage

Docked date pickers allow the selection of a specific date and year. The docked date picker displays a date input field by default, and a dropdown calendar appears when the user taps on the input field. Either form of date entry can be interacted with.  

Docked date pickers are ideal for navigating dates in both the near future or past and the distant future or past, as they provide multiple ways to select dates.
### Behavior

Dates can be added by using a keyboard or by navigating the calendar UI; both options are immediately available when the docked date picker is accessed.
### Month selection {#isPasted}

Month selection can be navigated with the corresponding back and next arrows or by tapping the dropdown menu.
### Year selection {#isPasted}

Year selection can be navigated with the corresponding back and next arrows or by tapping the dropdown menu.
Modal date picker
-----------------
### Behavior {#isPasted}

Modal date pickers navigate across dates in several ways:

* To navigate across months, swipe horizontally
* To navigate across years, scroll vertically
* To access the year picker, tap the year

Don't use a modal date picker to prompt for dates in the distant past or future, such as a date of birth. In these cases, use a modal input picker or a docked date picker instead.
### Date range selection

Date range selection provides a start and end date. Common use cases include:{#isPasted}

* Booking a flight
* Reserving a hotel

Modal date pickers navigate across date ranges in several ways:

* To select a range of dates, tap the start and end dates on the calendar
* To navigate across months, scroll vertically
Modal date input
----------------
### Date range selection {#isPasted}

Modal date inputs allow the manual entry of dates using the numbers on a keyboard. People can input a date or a range of dates in a dialog.
### Behavior {#isPasted}

You can swap between the modal date picker and modal date input using the edit or calendar icon.
### Compact window size

On compact [window sizes](/google-material-3/pages/applying-layout/window-size-classes#ab8d335c-8b02-42f4-95d2-b1095d507891), such as mobile, a full-screen modal date picker is recommended to increase readability and touch target size. It can cover the entire screen.
### Medium and expanded window sizes {#isPasted}

The docked date picker works best for medium and expanded window sizes. It displays a date input field by default, and a dropdown calendar appears when a person taps on the input field. A person can interact with either form of date entry.  

Docked date pickers are ideal for navigating dates in both the near future or past, and in the distant future or past, as they provide multiple ways to select dates.
### Selection {#isPasted}

Selection is indicated through color, drawing visual attention. In date ranges, start and end dates are selected, while dates in-between appear connected with a subtle highlight.
### Appearing and disappearing {#isPasted}

Like other kinds of dialogs, modal date pickers use an enter and exit transition pattern to appear on the screen.  

To exit a date picker, the input can either be confirmed (**OK** ) or dismissed (**Cancel** ). Interacting outside of the dialog will also dismiss the time picker. Unless one of these actions is taken, a time picker will continue to retain focus. Mobile full-screen pickers also have an additional close affordance (x) icon button and **Save** confirmation.  

Docked date pickers appear just below the input field.
### Responsive layout {#isPasted}

The sizing of the docked and modal date picker components don't scale responsively to different window sizes.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Date pickers can display past, present, or future dates

* Three variants: docked, modal, modal input

* Clearly indicate important dates, such as current and selected days

* Follow common patterns, like a calendar view
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
iOS 26 Liquid Glass update
--------------------------

In iOS 26, Material iOS components are updated with Liquid Glass styling or other visual changes. [More on the iOS 26 update](http://goto.google.com/gm3-ios)
**Nov 2025**

Material iOS uses a GM3-branded iOS date picker. [More details on iOS tab](/google-material-3/pages/date-pickers/ios)
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* Typography and spacing: Titles and labels are larger and have increased spacing to accommodate 48dp target size

* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color

* Variants: The three variants of date pickers have been renamed to not be device-dependent. The former desktop date picker is now known as the docked date picker. The former mobile date picker and date input are now known as modal date picker and modal date input to reinforce that the user must take an action.
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)


# Data tables
Data tables display information in columns and rows

## Overview
* Data tables help people see a lot of information in a dense format

* The rows and columns can be interactive

* Content can be edited, filtered, reordered, and rearranged
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Tokens \& specs
---------------

Browse the component elements, attributes, tokens, and their values. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Color
-----
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For designers, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. In implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
States
------
States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element.
### Interactive header states {#isPasted}
### Interactive row states
[State specs are in the token module above](/google-material-3/pages/cards/specs/latest#d889840f-9a4f-4871-b609-ad7d7455d7e9)
Measurements
------------
|                    Attribute                     |  Value   |
|                 Container width                  | Dynamic  |
|                 Container height                 | Dynamic  |
|                Container outline                 |   1dp    |
|                  Header height                   |   56dp   |
|--------------------------------------------------|----------|
| Header vertical alignment                        | Centered |
| Header component - Left/right padding            | 8dp      |
| Header headline - Left/right padding             | 16dp     |
| Header padding between headline and sorting icon | 4dp      |
| Row height                                       | 52dp     |
| Row divider                                      | 1dp      |
| Row vertical alignment                           | Centered |
| Row component - Left/right padding               | 8dp      |
| Row label - Left/right padding                   | 16dp     |
| Footer height                                    | 52dp     |
| Footer vertical alignment                        | Centered |
| Footer padding between elements                  | 8dp      |
| Footer supporting text - Left/right padding      | 40dp     |
### Alignment
Common configurations
---------------------
### Non-interactive data table
### Interactive data table
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage {#isPasted}
-----------------
Non-interactive data tables display information in a grid-like format of rows and columns, but have no interactive elements.
Interactive data tables display information in a grid-like format of rows and columns, with one or more interactive elements.

Interactive elements can include components such as checkboxes, chips, badges, buttons, text fields, and dropdown menus.
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
### Header row

Column header labels describe the kind of content displayed in each column.

Headers can contain the following elements:

* Checkbox

* Sorting icon button
### Rows {#isPasted}

Rows contain the data associated with the header row. Rows can be selected or unselected. Selected rows display a selected checkbox and a background fill color on the entire row.{#isPasted}

Rows can contain the following elements:

* Checkbox  
* Icon buttons
Allow the width and height of table containers to be flexible for different kinds of data.

When a table is too wide for a window, it becomes horizontally scrollable.
Table containers with a fixed maximum width truncate text that exceeds the width.
### Footer row (optional) {#isPasted}

A footer row can be interactive or non-interactive.

Footers can contain the following elements:

* Menu
* Supporting text
* Icon buttons for pagination
Adaptive design
---------------
Behavior
--------
### Hover {#isPasted}

When a person hovers over a row, that row displays a background color.
### Column hover {#isPasted}

When a person hovers over a column header:{#isPasted}

* A tooltip can display the full column name if it's truncated, or a more detailed description of the column
* If sorting is enabled, an arrow icon button can appear next to the column header
### Selected {#isPasted}

Rows that are selected display these two visual indicators:{#isPasted}

* A selected checkbox
* A background fill color on the entire row
### Sorting {#isPasted}

Sorting icons can appear in the header.

When a sorting icon is clicked, a linear progress indicator appears and the data is sorted.
### Pagination

Pagination allows people to navigate between different pages of a data table.

When a next or previous pagination icon is clicked, a progress bar appears, then the data table is updated with the new page's data.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------
People should be able to use assistive technology to:

* Navigate to the data table container

* Navigate between different row and column items

* Activate cells

* Select an action or destination

* Maintain access to primary actions when the content is scrolled out of view
Interaction \& style {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
### Touch

When tapping on an icon button or checkbox, a touch ripple appears on the element, indicating interaction feedback.  

There's no touch ripple feedback on the table cells.
When the data table can't fit on screen, people can swipe left or right to navigate to the remaining data.
Instead of scrolling, people can also use **Tab** or **Arrows** to navigate to the remaining data, which brings it into the viewport.
### Cursor

When interactive rows are hovered, a background fill color appears over the entire row. The row itself is not interactive.  

When interactive elements like checkboxes or icon buttons are hovered, that element switches to its hover state, providing a visual cue that the element is interactive.
### Keyboard

When tabbing to a data table, a focus indicator appears, providing a visual cue that the destination is now selected.  

People can navigate through actionable cells, headers, and footer actions using **Tab** . They can activate a selected cell or button using **Space** or **Enter**.{#isPasted}

<br />
Initial focus
-------------

When navigating to a data table using assistive technology, **Tab** should place initial focus on the first actionable cell or button, not on the entire data table.  

If there are no interactive elements in the table, keyboard focus will skip the table entirely. The table will still receive screen reader focus.   

When loading a new page of table rows, the screen reader announces the rows being displayed. For example, "Showing rows 4 to 6 of 18 rows".
Keyboard navigation
-------------------

|          Keys          |                               Actions                               |
|------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Tab**                | Moves focus between interactive cells and buttons in the data table |
| **Space** or **Enter** | Activates the data table or the focused destination                 |
| **Arrows**             | Navigate to each individual cell                                    |
Labeling elements
-----------------
The data table container has the **table** role. Caption the table based on the information it's showing, like **Grocery items**.
Assign proper aria-roles to the different areas of the table, like **columnheader** , **rowgroup** , **row** , and **cell** .   

Screen readers can automatically read out the total items and the current item in focus (like "cell 2 of 4"), so this information isn't needed in the label.


# App bars
App bars are placed at the top of the screen to help people navigate through a product

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS. {#isPasted}

[Apple's Human Interface Guidelines for navigation bars](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/components/navigation-and-search/navigation-bars) include additional iOS best practices and platform considerations.
Resources
---------
{#isPasted}{#isPasted}{#isPasted}

|                                                    **Type**                                                     |                                                           **Resource**                                                            |    **Contact**     | **Status** |
|                                                     Design                                                      | [iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=86169-20096&t=FpluCNTyTBnoqrkm-0) |   gm-design-kit@   | Available  |
|                                                 Implementation                                                  |                   [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/AppBar/index.md)                    | material-ios-team@ | Available  |
|                                                 Implementation                                                  |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------|
| [iOS: Expressive update](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/AppBar/index.md) | material-ios-team@                                                                                                                | Available          |
Spec differences
----------------
The Material iOS app bar is built using the [iOS navigation bar](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/navigation-bars) so navigation behaviors and transitions are consistent. The following attributes were modified to align with Material's theme:{#isPasted}

* Icon buttons: GM3 icon buttons used for trailing actions
* Typography: Google Sans used in all buttons and titles
* Color: Surface container color role for background on scroll

The **search app bar** is not available on iOS.
Guidelines
----------
### Motion

The [forward and backward transition pattern](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/transition-patterns#87b30c4c-3350-4daf-8150-4045916e7d02) is used for navigating between screens at consecutive levels of hierarchy, like navigating from an inbox to a message thread.  

Use built-in app bar navigation animations to align with platform norms. iOS uses a parallax effect, meaning the background slides slower than the foreground. This also reduces the amount of motion.{#isPasted}

<br />
### With modal sheets

Use an app bar on modal sheets to navigate, close, and label the sheet. This keeps navigation and page identification consistent between views. {#isPasted}

In a modal sheet, the back button should have the Material **primary** color role.
### Back button {#isPasted}

Keep the iOS-styled **Back** button for visual consistency. This also supports press-and-hold navigation between pages.
### Typography

The small app bar title and **Back** button label text are Google Sans 17pt to align with iOS navigation bar text sizes on a compact screen.
### Height

The small app bar height is 44pt to align with the iOS navigation bar on a compact screen.
### Scroll behavior

Use the large flexible app bar when first landing on a page to provide a greater visual impact. On scroll, the large flexible app bar should transform to a small, center-aligned app bar.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQs](http://go/gm3-yaqs){#isPasted}  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage {#isPasted}
-----------------
Use an app bar to provide content and actions related to the current page, such as page navigation actions, headlines, images, and 1--2 essential actions.{#isPasted}

The information and actions in the app bar should be contextual and specific to a page, but can also include global product controls, such as search or notifications.
App bars should only have one action, two if necessary. {#isPasted}

The primary action should alter or exit the entire page, like **Send** , **Save** , or **Edit**.

If the product has many actions, place those in a toolbar. Avoid placing an overflow menu in the app bar when possible.
To boost visibility of a primary action, change the style of the icon button to filled or tonal, and consider using a wide icon button.

Avoid using multiple filled or tonal buttons.
The four variants of app bars are:

1. **Search app bar**   
   Use on home pages when search is key to the product.   

2. **Small**   
   Use in dense layouts or when a page is scrolled.  

3. **Medium flexible**   
   Use to display a larger headline. It can collapse into a small app bar on scroll.  

4. **Large flexible**   
   Use to emphasize the headline of the page.
### Baseline app bars

There are two baseline app bars that are no longer recommended:

1. **Medium**   
   Replace with medium flexible.  

2. **Large**   
   Replace with large flexible.

<br />
Search app bar
--------------
Use a search app bar to provide an emphasized entry-point to open the search view.
Search bars should always include the word **Search**. They can use various capitalization styles depending on the product.{#isPasted}

1. **Search**
2. Searching a specific area  
   Example: **Search inbox**
3. Search \[Product\]   
   Example: **Search Photos**

For more on product name capitalization, see [product names](/google-material-3/pages/voice-and-tone/product-names/5bab8bcb-3cc3-4a66-9c9f-f86a5c2b62f4#2ca11723-75dd-47b2-a6be-e3875ac28bb3).
### Buttons in search app bar

In addition to a trailing avatar, search app bars can have up to two trailing icons on mobile. {#isPasted}

Trailing icons can be placed inside or outside the search bar.
