The leading element of a search app bar can be used for a product's logo to brand the app's overall experience.   

This logo can be purely cosmetic, or can trigger an action like returning to the home screen or refreshing it. {#isPasted}

Avoid using a logo to open an expanded navigation rail.
Don't use more than two trailing icon buttons with an avatar.   

If more actions are needed, place them in a toolbar instead.
### Large screens

The search app bar dynamically adapts to available width. There should be up to four trailing icons on larger screens. The [OneGoogle](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview#e0175ac6-b6c4-485c-b70e-a9a96ceb7066) bar can also be used in expanded window sizes that need more actions. [More on app bar adaptive design](/google-material-3/pages/app-bars/guidelines#aff13df1-4f87-4cc0-9a5f-4a456d1c1406)
### Alternate color options

By default, search containers in app bars use the **surface container** color to distinguish it from the app background. If the background is darker, use a lighter container color on the search bar, like **surface bright**.

When choosing alternate colors, make sure the search text and container have at least 3:1 contrast for readability.
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
### Container {#isPasted}

The app bar container holds all information and actions at the top of a screen, including navigation icons, headlines, and buttons. {#isPasted}

Avoid changing the position or shape of the container.

<br />
Always use the default height of the app bar, and make it span the full width of the window.
### Adding logos

Image logos can be used in app bars to bolster brand identity or visual appeal.

The image should be high quality and pertinent, and shouldn't disrupt the app bar's functionality.
### Leading button {#isPasted}

The leading button should be used for navigating the product. {#isPasted}

It typically is one of the following:

* A menu icon, which opens a modal expanded navigation rail
* A back arrow, which returns to the previous screen
### Headline {#isPasted}

The headline can describe:{#isPasted}

* The current page
* The current section
* The product

Headline text should be brief enough to easily fit in the app bar.

In medium flexible and large flexible app bars, the headline can wrap to a second line.

Don't truncate the headline text.
Headlines can be aligned to the leading edge or centered.

The headline's typography size and style change depending on the app bar variant.
### Subtitle

Subtitles can add additional context to a page.

These can be leading-aligned or center-aligned with the headline text.
### Trailing icon buttons {#isPasted}

Up to two icon buttons can be placed after the headline, aligned to the trailing edge of the app bar. Place most-used actions closest to the leading edge.{#isPasted}

Avoid using these buttons to open a menu with more actions. If more actions are needed, place them in a toolbar instead.

If changing the icon button color style to filled or tonal, only use one icon button.
Use filled icons when possible for the best visibility. Outlined icons can also be used, particularly for unselected toggle buttons.
Adaptive design
---------------
Adaptive design allows an interface to respond or change based on context, such as the user, device, and usage. [More on adaptive design](/google-material-3/pages/adaptive-design/)
### Resizing

The width of the app bar container responds to the view or device width.

It should always span 100% of the window width.
Resizing may cause actions at the trailing edge of the app bar to collapse into an overflow menu at smaller window sizes.

These actions become visible again at larger sizes.
The search container of the search app bar should fill 100% of the space between leading and trailing app bar elements until it reaches 312dp. Then, it should only grow further to fill 50% of that space.
### Presentation

The app bar automatically supports right-to-left (RTL) languages by aligning the layout of elements to the leading and trailing edges of the container.

This means that in RTL languages, the layout of the app bar is mirrored.
Behavior
--------
### Scrolling {#isPasted}

App bars should initially be the same color as the background, then fill with a contrasting color on scroll to provide visual separation from the background.{#isPasted}

The app bar can remain on a page at all times, or can hide and reappear when scrolling.
To focus more on body content, consider setting the app bar container to be transparent on scroll. This allows the buttons to float above the content. {#isPasted}

Make sure icon buttons have a container fill.

Consider using narrow-width icon buttons for actions, like **Back**, to reduce the amount of space they take up.
Selecting the search bar should open the search view component.
When scrolled, **medium flexible** and **large flexible** app bars can transform into **small** app bars. They should remain small until the page is scrolled back to the top. Don't transform app bars into a **search app bar**.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## XR
star  
Note:  
This is a rapidly changing space. Guidelines are primarily intended for designers at this time. Find what's implemented in code in the [design kit](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/branch/cVjQvQ0moD8wkPWH2rn2c6/GM3-Design-Kit?m=auto&node-id=229457-13769&t=KUWffrena21nxWJl-1).
Extended reality (XR) interfaces have special design requirements, like showing apps in 3D space. Material has an XR-specific app bar with custom specs and guidance. See [XR developer documentation](https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/foundations) for more details.
Types \& configurations {#isPasted}
-----------------------------------
There is one app bar orbiter. It closely aligns with the small app bar. It can be configured to be center-aligned or left-aligned.
Anatomy {#isPasted}
-------------------
Color \& elevation {#isPasted}
------------------------------
XR uses color to communicate the elevation of UI elements and orbiters. With [spatial elevation](https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/spatial-ui#spatial-elevation){#isPasted}, the app bar displays above the spatial panel on the Z-axis. Elevated app bars can use any of these color options:
Measurements {#isPasted}
------------------------
Usage {#isPasted}
-----------------
An app bar can appear in an orbiter for a more immersive experience. Currently, this spatial capability is only available in full space. In home space, use a regular app bar on the same plane as the body content to mimic a 2D experience.
Behavior {#isPasted}
--------------------
### Global context {#isPasted}

When placed in global context, the orbiter is centered at the top of the app it controls.

It stays anchored to the app during layout or content changes.

This ensures navigation elements are always easy to find and use.
### Local context {#isPasted}

When placed in local context, the orbiter is centered at the top of the spatial panel it controls.

It repositions in response to layout or content changes.
### Additional app bars {#isPasted}

In most cases, apps should only have one app bar orbiter, placed in global context.
Placement {#isPasted}
---------------------
### Offset and inset positioning {#isPasted}
### Horizontal alignment {#isPasted}
### Spatial panel alignment {#isPasted}

By default, app bar orbiters are center-aligned to the spatial panel. Their width and placement can be adjusted to accommodate specific user needs, such as improved ergonomics or right-to-left (RTL) languages.
### Width boundaries {#isPasted}

An app bar orbiter's width should adjust to stay in a person's [field of view](https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/spatial-ui#where-place).

This makes crucial navigation elements easy to find.
It's not recommended to increase the width of an app bar orbiter beyond a person's natural [field of view](https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/spatial-ui#where-place). {#isPasted}

This creates a visual imbalance and makes it difficult to find navigation elements.
### Adaptable width {#isPasted}

When placed in a local context, an app bar orbiter can expand to the width of its adjacent spatial panel. 

Be sure the orbiter stays in a person's field of view, and test for usability.
Accessibility considerations {#isPasted}
----------------------------------------
[XR accessibility](https://developer.android.com/design/ui/xr/guides/get-started#make-app){#isPasted} guidelines are still evolving. XR app bars should follow applicable Material [app bar accessibility standards](/google-material-3/pages/app-bars/accessibility).
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases {#isPasted}
---------------------

People should be able to do the following using assistive technology:{#isPasted}

* Understand what page they're currently visiting
* Take actions or navigate to a new page destination
* Maintain access to app bar actions when the content is scrolled
Interaction \& style {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
### Touch

When tapping on an icon button, a touch ripple appears, indicating interaction feedback.
### Cursor

When hovered, the hover state provides a visual cue to the user that the element is interactive.

When clicked (in both active and inactive states), a ripple appears to indicate feedback.
### Keyboard or switch {#isPasted}

When navigating to an interactive element, a focus indicator appears to show that action can be taken.

When the element is selected, an action is then performed.

<br />
### Initial focus

Focus should initially land on the leading button, since it's the first interactive element of the app bar.
Color
-----
On search app bars, use the default color roles when possible.{#isPasted}

* Search container: **surface container**
* Search label: **on surface variant**

On darker backgrounds, search bar containers can use the **surface bright** role to maintain strong visual contrast.

If mapping to other color roles, make sure the text and container have 3:1 contrast to ensure readability.
Keyboard navigation {#isPasted}
-------------------------------

|      Keys      |                  Actions                   |
|      Tab       | Move focus to the next interactive element |
| Space or Enter |        Activate the focused element        |
|----------------|--------------------------------------------|
Labeling elements
-----------------
The accessibility label for a title should be the same as the content within the title. If needed, add additional context to the accessibility label to ensure users understand what page they're on or what content is being shown.{#isPasted}

Screen readers will read the UI text followed by the component's role.
Label icon buttons according to their [accessibility guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/icon-buttons/accessibility).
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Variants
--------
### Baseline variants

The baseline GM3 **medium** and **large** app bars are no longer recommended in GM3 Expressive, and should be replaced with **medium flexible** and **large flexible** app bars, which are similar visually, but have multi-line support, a shorter height, and can contain a wide variety of elements, like images. [Jump to baseline app bar specs](/google-material-3/pages/app-bars/specs#6cca5071-c397-42a5-b71d-5f1cfc1aadea)
|      Variant      |    GM3    |                     GM3 Expressive                      |
|-------------------|-----------|---------------------------------------------------------|
| Search app bar    | --        | Available                                               |
| Small             | Available | Available                                               |
| Center-aligned    | Available | Merged into **small**. Use centered text configuration. |
| Medium (baseline) | Available | Not recommended. Use **medium flexible**                |
| Medium flexible   | --        | Available                                               |
| Large (baseline)  | Available | Not recommended. Use **large flexible**                 |
| Large flexible    | --        | Available                                               |
Configurations
--------------
### Text alignment
|    Category    |     Configuration      |    GM3    | GM3 Expressive |
|----------------|------------------------|-----------|----------------|
| Text alignment | Leading edge (default) | Available | Available      |
| Text alignment | Centered               | --        | Available      |
Tokens \& specs
---------------

Select a token set to view in the table's menu. App bar token sets are organized into a common token set, and size-specific tokens. [Learn about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/tokens/)
### Search component tokens \& specs

The default search component tokens are used in the search app bar.
Anatomy
-------
App bars can be customized to include:{#isPasted}

* An image or logo
* A subtitle
* A filled icon button

Avoid customizing the size of the heading and subtitle, or adding too many actions.
### Search
The search app bar can include trailing actions inside and outside the search bar. When the search bar is selected, it should open the search view component.
### Image

An image can be placed in the app bar. In small app bars, this can replace the label text.
### Filled trailing icon button

The app bar's trailing icon buttons can be replaced with a single, primary, or tonal filled icon button in default or wide sizes.
### Subtitle
Color
-----

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/tokens/){#isPasted}

All app bars share the same color roles. On scroll, the container changes color to **surface container**.
### Scroll states
Measurements
------------
### Search app bar
### Small app bar
### Medium flexible app bar
### Large flexible app bar
*** ** * ** ***

Baseline app bars
-----------------
The **medium** and **large** app bars are no longer recommended in GM3 Expressive. Use the **medium flexible** and **large flexible** app bars in their place.
### Tokens \& specs
Select a token set to view in the table's menu. Baseline app bar token sets are organized into medium, large, and older baseline token sets. [Learn about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/tokens/)
### Color
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For designers, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. In implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens)
### Measurements
#### Medium app bar
#### Large app bar
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Focus on describing the current page and provide 1--2 essential actions

* Displays labels and page navigation controls at the top of the page. (Use a toolbar to display page actions)

* Four variants: Search app bar, small, medium flexible, large flexible

* On scroll, apply a fill color to separate from body content

* Can animate on and off screen with another bar of controls, like a row of chips
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
**Mar 2025**{#isPasted}

### Search app bar

The new **search app bar** supports icons inside and outside the search bar, and centered text. It opens the search view when selected.

Currently only available on Android Views.

[More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
**Sep 2024**

### More flexible app bars

The new **medium flexible** and **large flexible** app bars come with significant improvements, like flexible heights, multi-line support, centered text, and they can contain a wider variety of elements, like images. They should replace **medium** and **large** app bars, which are no longer recommended in GM3 Expressive. The **small** app bar has been updated with the same flexible improvements.
Variants and naming:

* Renamed component from **top app bar** to **app bar**

* Added **search app bar**

* M**edium** and **large** app bars are no longer recommended

* Added **medium flexible** and **large flexible**app bars with:

  * Reduced overall height

  * Larger title text

  * Subtitle

  * Left- and center-aligned text options

  * Text wrapping

  * More flexible elements for imagery and filled buttons

* Added features to **small**app bar:

  * Subtitle

  * Center-aligned text option

  * More flexible elements for imagery and filled buttons
Differences from GM2
--------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color
* On scroll: No drop shadow, instead a color fill creates separation from content
* Typography: Larger default text
* Layout: Smaller default height
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Typography
Use typography to make writing legible and beautiful

## Type scale & tokens
Type scale {#isPasted}
----------------------

A **type scale** is a selection of type styles used across a product to ensure consistency.
GM3 type scale
--------------
### 30 type styles {#isPasted}

GM3 has one type scale containing two sets of **type styles** : **15 baseline** and **15 emphasized**. Both of these styles follow the same scale from Display Large to Label Small.

The **emphasized** styles were added in the expressive update. They have a higher weight and roundness than the baseline styles, and are best applied to bold, selection, and other areas of emphasis. Baseline and emphasized styles are meant to be used together.
### 2 font themes {#isPasted}

The GM3 type scale can use either static fonts or variable fonts across a product, defined by the **font theme**.

* **Variable font theme (opt-in upgrade)**: Added in the expressive update. All styles use Google Sans Flex (GSF) by default.
* **Static font theme (required minimum)** : Default GM3 font theme. Larger styles use Google Sans and smaller styles use Google Sans Text by default.{#isPasted}

Material recommends that products opt in to the variable font theme to use variable font axes and increase readability. All 30 type styles work with both static and variable font themes.
### Font theme differences {#isPasted}

GSF is very visually similar to other Google Sans fonts. However, due to legibility improvements, it's not a perfect match, so updating to the variable font theme may impact the product:{#isPasted}

* Below 18sp, updating to the variable font theme may slightly increase line length  
* Above 18sp, updating to the variable font theme may slightly decrease line length  
* If keeping line length is the product's highest priority, beyond other benefits of GSF, consider using the static theme  
* GSF is likely to increase loading latency. Material recommends that all products interested in GSF first conduct a performance test. If performance is undesirable, products are recommended to use the static theme.
Type scale tokens
-----------------

Each of the 30 styles has a single token that captures the default properties. Tokens are separated into the **baseline** and **emphasized** sets. These tokens should be used in most situations. Each axis and property, such as font, line height, size, tracking, and weight, also has an individual token for greater customization. [Learn more about design tokens](https://carbon.googleplex.com/m3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
### Baseline type style tokens
Emphasized type styles
----------------------
The GM3 type scale has 15 emphasized type styles. Products should use both baseline and emphasized type styles together to achieve expressive experiences. Material recommends using emphasized styles for selection, actions, headlines, and other editorial use cases.{#isPasted}

Like baseline, the emphasized type styles are available in two themes: **variable** using GSF, and **static** using GS and GS Text, by default.
### Emphasized type style tokens
### Components

When used in components, the emphasized type styles can communicate hierarchy or importance, such as an active or selected component, or an unread message. The emphasized styles work well with:{#isPasted}

* Badges
* Buttons (for primary actions)
* Extended FAB
* Selected list items
* Selected menu items

Material components don't use emphasized type styles by default. To use an emphasized type style, swap the baseline token for the emphasized token of the same style. For example:

* Baseline: md.sys.typescale.display-large
* Emphasized: md.sys.typescale.**emphasized**.display-large
Where emphasized styles can be used
-----------------------------------
### Weight

Use the emphasized styles on text that already uses weight (such as medium, bold) to add more expression and further communicate hierarchy.

### Context

Use emphasized styles to draw attention to specifics aspects of components, such as selected states, unread messages, or key interactions.{#isPasted}

Emphasized context and weight can be used simultaneously.
### Where emphasized styles shouldn't be used
Don't apply emphasized styles broadly across all UI elements as this can diminish hierarchy and confuse focus.
Customizing the type scale
--------------------------
Sometimes, products need font sizes that aren't available on the type scale, such as when designing for different devices.{#isPasted}

In this case, update an existing style such as display medium with a value that works well for the product. Make sure not to use sizes that are too similar to existing sizes, otherwise they lose visual hierarchy and are hard to tell apart.
The M3 static font theme references different typefaces at different sizes. A **brand** typeface is used for larger type styles, like Headline and Display, to focus on expression. A **plain** typeface is used for smaller type styles, like Body and Label, to focus on readability.
### Sizes outside the type scale

Designers may want to use a specific style without an available position in the type scale. For example, text that is larger than display, such as display extra large, or a fourth label size. In this case, create a custom token to ensure consistent usage throughout the design system.
Font size units {#isPasted}
---------------------------

The following units are used to express font size on Android and the web.
|     Platform     | Android |  Web   |
|  Font size unit  |   sp    |  rem   |
| Conversion ratio |   1.0   | 0.0625 |
|------------------|---------|--------|

{#isPasted}

Web browsers calculate the rem (the root em size) based on the root element size. The default for modern web browsers is 16px, so the conversion is SP_SIZE/16 = rem.
#### Example conversions {#isPasted}

| Android |   Web    |
|  10sp   | 0.625rem |
|  12sp   | 0.75rem  |
|  24sp   |  1.5rem  |
|  60sp   | 3.75rem  |
|---------|----------|
### Letter spacing units {#isPasted}

The following units are for spacing letters in a UI.
|      Platform       |                          Android                          |                            Web                            |
| Letter spacing unit |                            em                             |                            rem                            |
|  Conversion ratio   | (Tracking value in px / font size in sp) = letter spacing | (Tracking value in px / font size in sp) = letter spacing |
|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|

{#isPasted}
#### Letter spacing examples {#isPasted}

|                  Android                   |                     Web                     |
| (.2 tracking / 16sp font size) = 0.0125 em | (.2 tracking / 16px font size) = 0.0125 rem |
|--------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Fonts
Static fonts \& variable fonts
------------------------------
Google has a collection of default static and dynamic fonts used in most products. Static fonts are currently applied by default in Material 3 to all components, however variable fonts such as Google Sans Flex (GSF) are preferred and will eventually become the default in GM3 and Google products.{#isPasted}

Static versions of variable fonts are available, and even necessary, in some environments.
|        Font        |                                           Supported Environment                                            |  Status   |
|--------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------|
| GS Flex            | Figma, 1P Fonts Site, Pixel system                                                                         | Available |
| GS variable        | 1P Fonts Site, Pixel system                                                                                | Available |
| GS static          | Figma, Workspace Editors                                                                                   | Available |
| GS Text            | Figma, Workspace Editors, 1P Fonts Site                                                                    | Available |
| GS Mono variable   | Figma, 1p Fonts Site, Pixel system (pending)                                                               | Available |
| GS Mono static     | Workspace Editors, 1P Fonts Site                                                                           | Available |
| Noto Sans static   | Workspace Editors, 1P Fonts Site, [Android Open Source Project](https://source.android.com/), Pixel system | Available |
| Noto Sans variable | 1P Fonts Site, [Android Open Source Project](https://source.android.com/), Pixel system                    | Available |
Google Sans Flex (GSF)
----------------------
In the expressive update, the GM3 type scale was updated to include GSF. To update to GSF, first adopt the GM3 type tokens. Then, select the **variable font theme** to opt in.
[GSF](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Google+Sans+Flex) is a proprietary variable typeface created exclusively for Google. All product teams should use GSF. {#isPasted}

[GSF](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Google+Sans+Flex) goes beyond the variable version of GS in possible customization. Rather than a weight range of 400--700, for example, GSF ranges from 1--1000. Rather than 3 axes, GSF offers 6. This allows legible variations at different scales, fitting small watch faces or large headlines.
Expressive qualities such as roundness and weight can also be altered, changing feel and sensibility. Through different combinations of values, the same underlying GS font can now produce numerous distinct styles. [More on rounded](/google-material-3/pages/typography/variable-axes#9a14d892-4857-4841-97ef-46922cede765){#isPasted}

With a wide range of possible sizes and styles, refer to [readability best practices](https://fonts.corp.google.com/knowledge/readability_and_accessibility/introducing_accessibility_in_typography) to avoid inaccessible outcomes.
Google Sans (GS)
----------------
### Variable {#isPasted}

[Google Sans variable](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Google+Sans) is a proprietary typeface created exclusively for Google, and offers fallback support for languages that GSF does not support.{#isPasted}

GS variable offers adjustable axes for weight, italic, grade, and optical size. All internal product teams should use [GSF](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Google+Sans+Flex).

Preset styles such as **regular** or **bold** still exist in both GSF and GS variable, if customization is not desired.
### Static (display) {#isPasted}

Google Sans offers [static versions](https://carbon.googleplex.com/google-sans/pages/how-to-use) for environments where variable fonts are not yet available. When a static version of Google Sans is required, use it for **display** , **headline** , and **title text** (size 18 and up) typography roles.{#isPasted}

<br />
### Static (text) {#isPasted}

For smaller text that requires a static version, use [Google Sans Text](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Google+Sans+Text?query=google+sans+text) (GST), an optimized version of GS. Use GST at font sizes 17 and below, as well as in longer blocks of text, to increase comprehension. {#isPasted}

On [iOS](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-foundations-styles#451ceb20-9faf-42b4-b1bc-753620f0e5ef), SF Pro is used for most body text instead of GST.

GST is used for **body** , **label** , and some **title** typography roles.
Google Sans Mono
----------------
[Google Sans Mono](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Google+Sans+Mono) is a fixed-width typeface. All characters are optically adjusted to take up the same amount of space.{#isPasted}

While the use cases for a monospace typeface are more limited than other styles in the Google Sans family, there are situations in which a fixed-width font is preferable, such as for tabular data.

The variable version of Mono has a single adjustable axis for weight. Mono also has a static version.
Noto Sans
---------
[Noto Sans](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Noto+Sans) is a global font collection for writing in all modern and ancient languages. {#isPasted}

Noto Sans is compatible with Google Sans and the collection supports more than 150 scripts and 1,000 languages. It's primarily used as fallback support for languages or characters not supported by GSF, GS Variable, or similar fonts. This prevents missing letter forms, represented by empty boxes known as [tofu](https://fonts.corp.google.com/knowledge/glossary/tofu).

Noto has different variable versions with different numbers of axes, as well as different static versions, depending on language. [Find a full list on the 1P fonts site](https://fonts.corp.google.com/?query=Noto+Sans)

[More about typography language considerations](https://m2.material.io/design/typography/language-support.html#language-considerations)
|      **Font**      |                **Available axes**                |
|--------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| GS Flex            | Slant, Width, Weight, Grade, Round, Optical Size |
| GS variable        | Weight, Italic, Grade, Optical Size              |
| GS Mono variable   | Weight, Italic                                   |
| Noto Sans variable | Width, Weight, Italic                            |
Fallback protection with variable fonts
---------------------------------------
Products should have font fallback support. This is when a similar font is used as a replacement when the current font doesn't support the text's characters. GSF is only available in [Extended Latin](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Google+Sans+Flex/glyphs). In the meantime, languages not yet supported by GSF can fall back and [gracefully degrade](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fault_tolerance) to Google Sans, and ultimately Noto Sans.
Products should apply font fallback in the following order:{#isPasted}

* Google Sans Flex
* Variable version of Google Sans
* Noto Sans font collection

This ensures that text will have a consistent visual style regardless of font support.

Designers should connect with their product and engineering partners to confirm that font fallback is available.
Lack of fallback support can mean that individual letters or entire words in an unsupported language will render in unpredictable ways. {#isPasted}

For example, Hindi is not supported within GSF. So, without GS variable or Noto Sans in the fallback stack, a Hindi name would not show up in an English text string using GSF. Instead, the Hindi would show up as an unpredictable font choice, or in worst case, [tofu](https://fonts.corp.google.com/knowledge/glossary/tofu) blocks representing missing characters.

## Overview
* Download Google Sans Flex (GSF) at [go/getgsf](http://go/getgsf)
* GM3 type scale has two sets of type styles: 15 baseline and 15 emphasized
* GM3 has two font themes: static and variable. Opt in to use the variable font theme to use GSF.
* Variable fonts such as GSF offer more control of axes for expression
* Use tokens to define font, line height, size, tracking, weight, and more
Availability \& resources
-------------------------

This shows where the type scale is available and implemented into Material components.{#isPasted}  
{#isPasted}{#isPasted}{#isPasted}

|                                                                                         Type                                                                                         |                                          Link                                          |    Contact     |  Status   |
|                                                                                        Design                                                                                        | [Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/file/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit) (Figma) | gm-design-kit@ | Available |
|                                                                                        Design                                                                                        |
|                                                                                        Design                                                                                        |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------|-----------|
| [Google Sans Flex](https://carbon.googleplex.com/google-sans/pages/how-to-use)                                                                                                       | material-content@                                                                      | Available      |
| [go/fonts](https://fonts.corp.google.com/){#isPasted}                                                                                                                                | fonts@                                                                                 | Available      |
| [Android Views: Expressive update](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/g3doc/components/typography.md#google-material-3-next-typographic-scale) | material-android-team@                                                                 | Available      |
| [Flutter](http://go/gm3-flutter#typography)                                                                                                                                          | material-flutter-team@                                                                 | Available      |
| [iOS](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-foundations-styles#451ceb20-9faf-42b4-b1bc-753620f0e5ef)                                                                      | material-ios-team@                                                                     | Available      |
| [iOS: Expressive update](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-foundations-styles#451ceb20-9faf-42b4-b1bc-753620f0e5ef){#isPasted}                                        | material-ios-team@                                                                     | Available      |
| [Jetpack Compose](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/styles/typography.md?cl=head)                                               | material-compose-team@                                                                 | Available      |
| [Jetpack Compose: Expressive update](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/styles/typography.md?cl=head)                            | material-compose-team@                                                                 | Unavailable    |
| [Wiz](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/javascript/materialdesign/gm3/g3doc/reference/typography.md?cl=head)                                                                             | material-web-team@                                                                     | Available      |
| Wiz: Expressive update                                                                                                                                                               | material-web-team@                                                                     | Unavailable    |
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
### Google Sans Flex (GSF) {#isPasted}

GSF is the new standard for Google product typography. It's available in all GM3 components. {#isPasted}

GSF adds advanced customization options not available in the variable font version of Google Sans (GS). Rather than three limited axes, GSF has six full-range axes with more options than GS: {#isPasted}

* Roundness
* Weight
* Width
* Grade
* Optical size
* Slant

GSF is available to download from [go/fonts](http://go/fonts) and is built into Figma as a standalone style, and as part of the [GM3 Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/file/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit).

[More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
### Benefits of GSF

GSF is a variable font that offers endless options for appearance and style using axes of width, weight, and more.  

The benefits of GSF include:{#isPasted}

* Improved readability through nuanced optical sizing  
* Wide degree of expressive, emotional potential
warning  
Caution:  
Currently, switching to GSF will likely impact performance. Products should run performance tests on GSF to determine impact.
### Updated GM3 type scale with new styles \& variable fonts

Material's type scale includes fifteen **baseline** type styles, the same as before, and fifteen new **emphasized** type styles. {#isPasted}

The emphasized type styles employ stylistic adjustments in weight and roundness to create more expressive moments.

All styles support both the existing static font theme (default) and the new variable font theme. The variable font theme is opt-in, and uses GSF.

[More on how to use emphasized styles](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens#6b35be2c-df20-46fd-a402-f84b014f5e66)
### Emphasized type style tokens {#isPasted}

Design tokens offer an improved way to define typography in products by assigning an element's type style by a configurable value, rather than a set value.

Emphasized tokens allow for clearer hierarchies and prioritized components within a layout.
Differences from GM2 {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
### Variable fonts {#isPasted}

Variable fonts are a key feature of GM3. A variable font allows adjustment along different style axes.

Precise values can be selected along these axes. Unlike a static font, which may limit use to set increments---for example, weights of 100, 200, and so on, variable fonts allow extremely specific values---for example, weights of 253, 361, and so on.
### Google Sans as a variable font {#isPasted}

Google Sans (GS) is available as a variable font. It was previously only a static font. The variable font has weight, italic, optical size, and grade axes.
### Type scale updates
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Applying type
Applying type {#isPasted}
-------------------------
Hierarchy is communicated through differences in font weight, size, line height, and letter spacing. The updated type scale organizes styles into five roles that are named to describe their purposes: display, headline, title, label, body. The new roles are device-agnostic, allowing easier application across a variety of use cases.
Typefaces {#isPasted}
---------------------

### Google Sans family

Google-branded products use three proprietary typefaces: Google Sans, GS Text, and Google Sans Mono (GS Mono).

Use [Google Sans](https://carbon.googleplex.com/google-sans/pages/google-sans) for display and headline text (size 18 and up) across all platforms.

Use [GS Text](https://carbon.googleplex.com/google-sans/pages/google-sans-text) for subhead, body, caption, and overline text (size 17 and below).

Use [GS Mono](https://carbon.googleplex.com/google-sans/pages/google-sans-mono) for code blocks, as well as monospaced numbers in tables or instances where values may change often.

Currently Google Sans covers Latin-extended, Greek, and Cyrillic-extended languages. To follow the ongoing development of the typeface and internationalization efforts, see [go/googlesans](http://go/googlesans).
Roles {#isPasted}
-----------------
### Display {#isPasted}

There are three display styles in the default type scale: large, medium, and small. As the largest text on the screen, display styles are reserved for short, important text or numerals. They work best on large screens.
### Headline {#isPasted}

Headlines are best-suited for short, high-emphasis text. These styles can be good for marking primary passages of text or important regions of content.
### Title {#isPasted}

Titles are smaller than headline styles, and should be used for medium-emphasis text that remains relatively short. For example, consider using title styles to divide secondary passages of text or secondary regions of content.{#isPasted}

<br />
### Body {#isPasted}

Body styles are used for longer passages of text in your app.{#isPasted}

GS Text is optimized for readability at smaller sizes and longer blocks of text, opening up a wider range of uses for Google-branded typography.

<br />
### Label

Label styles are smaller, utilitarian styles, used for things like the text inside components or for very small text in the content body, such as captions. {#isPasted}

Buttons, for example, use the label large style.
Typesetting {#isPasted}
-----------------------

Vertical typesetting relies on padding, bounding boxes, and baselines to ensure text legibility at any size.

Take engineering considerations and the conventions of your platform into account when making decisions for typesetting, text resizing, density, and using text in adaptive layouts.
### Using padding and bounding boxes {#isPasted}

Use this method for web products, as well as iOS products, where applicable. Note that some design tools also use bounding boxes for typesetting, but their methods vary and will need to be reconciled with the engineering implementation.
In web UIs, the line height and bounding box height are the same. Text is vertically centered within the bounding box, following the "[half-leading](https://www.w3.org/TR/css-inline-3/#leading-trim)" behavior established by CSS.{#isPasted}

The vertical position of the text isn't controlled directly, but through the combination of the bounding box and font metrics.
Padding is the space between UI elements, such as between an image and a bounding box, or between the inner edge of the bounding box and the text.
Specify the distance of UI elements from fixed reference points, such as the container edge. For the web, automate this calculation using Sass or CSS.
Vertical alignment using padding and bounding boxes:{#isPasted}

1. **Line height**   
   Measure the height of the bounding box
2. **Centering**   
   Ensure equal top and bottom padding around the inner edge of the bounding box by using center align
3. **Spacing**   
   Use the height of the bounding box, and top and bottom padding to determine spacing

<br />
### Using the baseline {#isPasted}

Use this method for Android products or platform-agnostic specs.
The baseline is the invisible line upon which a line of text rests. In Material Design, the baseline is an important specification in measuring the vertical distance between text and an element.
For Android, specifying distances relative to baseline enables accurate implementation.{#isPasted}

The baseline can also be used to communicate text position between designers in a way that's agnostic to the platform or design tool.
Vertical alignment using the baseline:{#isPasted}

1. **Line height**   
   Measure distance from the text baseline of one line to the text baseline of the next line
2. **Centering**   
   Specify center alignment as a reference instead of measuring the distance to the text baseline
3. **Spacing**   
   Use the distance from a reference point to the text baseline
Ensuring readability {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
**Line height**{#isPasted}

Line height is the space between each line of text and is directly connected to type size.

Material's type tokens are optimized for intended size and use.
**Tabular numbers**{#isPasted}

Use tabular figures (also known as monospaced numbers) rather than proportional digits in tables or places where values may change often, such as clocks.

Use monospaced tabular numbers to keep values optically aligned for better scanning.
Using Material Symbols with typography {#isPasted}
--------------------------------------------------

Material Symbols are designed with similar considerations to typefaces, and often appear alongside text. Choosing the right icon set can tie the content of an interface together, enhancing the cohesive branded feel of your product.
Accessibility {#isPasted}
-------------------------
### Color \& contrast {#isPasted}

Support visual accessibility by choosing the appropriate color contrast between your product's text and background. Contrast is the perceived difference between the lightness or darkness of two colors, and is quantified by a contrast ratio. Key contrast ratios indicate levels of contrast that are sufficient for accessibility.

[Learn more about contrast and accessibility](/google-material-3/pages/designing/color-contrast#8ef2bac2-6175-4312-8772-7d034212429b)
Text should achieve sufficient contrast between its color and that of its background. Sufficient contrast depends on the size of the text and the level of accessibility required. {#isPasted}

[Learn more about contrast requirements for text](/google-material-3/pages/designing/color-contrast#ccf80af1-c775-4fac-8d26-0e10766c074d)
The default color for typography is **on surface** , but **on surface variant** can be used for less-emphasized text.
For hyperlinked text appearing on top of a **surface color** , use **primary** or **tertiary** .  

Hyperlinked text must also be underlined.
(removed per b/304969883)

**Line length**{#isPasted}

Line length is how many characters are on a single line of text.

For longer body text, the recommended line length is between 40 to 60 characters.

For shorter lines of text, the ideal length is 20 to 40 characters.

## Google Sans Flex
Using Google Sans Flex (GSF)
----------------------------
GSF is a variable font with the largest degree of customization among Google fonts. Variable fonts have style axes that can be customized to produce distinct styles and expressive treatments. GSF's style axes are:{#isPasted}

* Roundness
* Weight
* Width
* Grade
* Optical size
* Slant

Other variable fonts, such as the variable version of Google Sans, can be modified similarly, though with fewer options. Switching to a variable font, such as GSF, has multiple benefits, including:

* Reducing overall size of font files for complete typeface families
* Improving load time on web pages
* Increasing readability
* Enhanced possibility for expression
GSF axes
--------
### Roundness

Roundness determines the amount of curve on letter stroke endings. {#isPasted}

The use of round is informed by Google's hardware form factor, and is an integral part of the GM3 component and shape language. Roundness in typography can infuse characters with a playful, optimistic, or approachable sensibility.
Use round sparingly. Don't use for long-form text or complete sentences. A few words or phrase is ideal.{#isPasted}

<br />
### Weight

Weight defines the thickness of a typeface's strokes. The most common weights are regular and bold, but weights can cover extremes of light and heavy.{#isPasted}

GSF's font weight can be any value from 1--1000, making the number of weights effectively unlimited.

[Learn more about weight on Google Fonts](https://fonts.corp.google.com/knowledge/choosing_type/exploring_typefaces_with_multiple_weights_or_grades)
Use weight to establish visual hierarchy and to communicate interactive states in user interfaces.  

Heavier weights, like bold, can be used to emphasize titles, selected states, and key interactions.  

Lighter weights, like regular and medium, can apply to any element. They should be used for body text to ensure readability.
### Combining roundness and weight
Roundness is informed by our hardware form factor, and is an integral part of the Material 3 component and shape language. Using round and weight in typography infuses characters with a sense of confidence, familiarity, warmth, and play.
Round and weight combinations form the core of the emphasized styles. Emphasized styles can draw attention to specific layout elements, improve hierarchy, and add a sense of approachability.{#isPasted}

<br />

<br />
### Width

Width is the horizontal space taken up by a typeface's characters. It can be set to any value from 25--151. Width can be adjusted for stylistic effect, or to allow text to fit a narrow or wide space.{#isPasted}

[Learn more about width on Google Fonts](https://fonts.corp.google.com/knowledge/glossary/width)
Width can compress words that grow longer during localization, preventing collisions or line breaks. Use when improving readability or preventing truncation. Only adjust width for functional reasons, not as part of a style.{#isPasted}

<br />

<br />
Shifting width in combination with weight can indicate different states of completion, such as percentage of a goal reached or degree of brightness. Only adjust width to communicate functional information.
Avoid compressing width more than 15 points below the baseline width to ensure that the adjustment is as imperceptible as possible. {#isPasted}

To ensure width adjustment does not interfere with readability, follow [internationalization best practices](https://carbon.googleplex.com/search-design-system/pages/internationalization/guidelines).

<br />

<br />
### Grade

Grade subtly adjusts a typeface's apparent weight, independent of the weight axis. Both weight and grade axes affect a letter's thickness, but adjustments with grade are much more granular and don't change any letter widths or line breaks, unlike weight.{#isPasted}

This can be useful when hovering or changing other states. For example, apply grade when hovering over a button to make the text thicker without changing the width of the button.

Both grade and weight axes use the same unit system, so transitioning between weight and grade by adjusting values is simplified.

[Learn more about grade on Google Fonts](https://fonts.corp.google.com/knowledge/choosing_type/exploring_typefaces_with_multiple_weights_or_grades)
### Optical size

Optical size adapts a typeface to different text sizes, by changing many parameters at once, such as spacing, stroke thicknesses, x-height, and other subtle details.{#isPasted}

Small optical sizes enhance legibility and readability, while large optical sizes increase expressivity with even more weights and widths.

GSF offers a comprehensive system of optical sizes, adjusting to every text size from the smallest at 6sp to the largest at 144sp.

[Learn about choosing typefaces that have optical sizes](https://fonts.corp.google.com/knowledge/choosing_type/choosing_typefaces_that_have_optical_sizes)
When using variable fonts in Figma, the option to change optical size automatically based on text size is set by default.
### Slant

Slant allows text to lean until it resembles an italicized state. Select full or zero slant to create italic or upright states.{#isPasted}

While it's less common to use degrees of slant in between full and zero, it can produce expressive qualities, especially when applied with other axes.
Animate from GSF with caution {#isPasted}
-----------------------------------------
It's possible to animate GSF, including along one particular axes, such as weight. However, due to potential performance impacts, designers are advised to test before choosing this option over another way to animate text.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQs](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


## Accessibility
Use cases {#isPasted}
---------------------
People should be able to do the following using assistive technology:

* Navigate to and activate the FAB

* Perform an action with the FAB

* Expand and minimize an extended FAB
Interaction \& style
--------------------
Don't disable the FAB. If the action represented in the FAB is unavailable, the FAB shouldn't appear.  
{#isPasted}

Ensure the icon has a minimum 3:1 contrast ratio with the container.{#isPasted}

<br />
Focus
-----
Ensure the FAB is prioritized in the overall focus order to create an efficient experience for people who navigate UIs with assistive tech.   

On mobile, the focus order may start with the app bar, move to the navigation bar, and then skip past any other content on the page to land on the FAB.  

Consider displaying a tooltip when the FAB is focused. This is supported on web.
Layout \& position
------------------
To make it easier for users of screen readers to reach a primary action such as a FAB on expanded window sizes, consider placing the FAB in the upper left region.   

However, it's critical to test placement options with users to see if the upper left region is the best position in all browser windows. For compact and medium window sizes, the best place for the FAB is the lower right corner of a screen.
To ensure accessibility for keyboard users on the web, avoid positioning the FAB in a way that completely obscures the focus indicator of an actionable element.   

It's okay to partially cover the desired element, as long as the focus indicators are still visible.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
|        **Keys**        |              **Actions**              |
|        **Tab**         |        Focus lands on the FAB         |
| **Space** or **Enter** | Perform the default action on an item |
|------------------------|---------------------------------------|
Labeling elements
-----------------
The accessibility label should describe the action that the button is performing, such as **Compose a new message**.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://goto.google.com/gm3-bug){#isPasted}

## Overview
* Use a FAB for the most common or important action on a screen

* Make sure the icon in a FAB is clear and understandable

* FABs persist on the screen when content is scrolling

* Three variants: FAB, medium FAB, large FAB
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
**Aug 2024**

The FAB has new sizes to match the extended FAB and more color options. The small FAB is no longer recommended. [More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
Variants and naming:

* Added **medium** FAB size

* **Small** FAB size is no longer recommended

* FAB and large FAB sizes are unchanged

* FAB variants are based on size, not color

Color:

* Added tone color styles:

  * Primary

  * Secondary

  * Tertiary

* Renamed existing tonal color styles to match their token names:

  * **Primary** to **Primary container**

  * **Secondary** to **Secondary container**

  * **Tertiary** to **Tertiary container**

  * The values haven't changed

* Surface color FABs and branded color FABs are no longer recommended
Differences from GM2
--------------------
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Variants
--------
### Baseline variants

The small FAB is still available, but no longer recommended. [Jump to baseline specs](/google-material-3/pages/fab/specs#b3fedee2-09d8-48bd-a1fc-653826d40cb5)
|  Variant   |    GM3    |           GM3 Expressive            |
|------------|-----------|-------------------------------------|
| FAB        | Available | Available                           |
| Medium FAB | --        | Available                           |
| Large FAB  | Available | Available                           |
| Small FAB  | Available | Not recommended. Use a larger size. |
Configurations
--------------
In the expressive update, the **primary** , **secondary** , and **tertiary** set colors were renamed to **primary container** , **secondary container** , and **tertiary container** to match the actual color roles used. New primary, secondary, and tertiary color styles were created to match the corresponding color roles. [View details in the color styles section](/google-material-3/pages/fab/specs#f82e4d4f-6e7e-4731-a8aa-26b8d788bb4e)
| Category |                       Configuration                        |                    GM3                    | GM3 Expressive |
|----------|------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|----------------|
| Color    | Primary container, secondary container, tertiary container | Available as primary,secondary, tertiary | Available      |
| Color    | Primary, secondary, tertiary                               | --                                        | Available      |
Tokens \& specs
---------------

Use the table's menu to select a token set. FAB tokens are organized by size and color. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens)
Anatomy
-------
Color
-----

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. In implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens)
### Color styles {#isPasted}

FABs can use several combinations of **color** and **on-color** styles, such as **primary** and **on-primary**. The following color mappings provide the same legibility and functionality, so the color mapping you use depends on style alone.
### Baseline color styles
Surface and branded FAB color styles are still available, but no longer recommended.
States
------

States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element.  

When using a non-default color mapping for FABs, make sure the state layer color is the same as the icon color. For example, the state layer color for the **primary** color style should be md.sys.color.primary.
Measurements
------------
### FAB
### Medium FAB {#isPasted}
### Large FAB {#isPasted}
Baseline tokens \& specs
------------------------
Use the table's menu to select a token set. This only includes baseline tokens, including small FAB, surface FAB, and branded FAB. It doesn't include large or regular FAB, since those are still currently used.
### Small FAB measurements
*** ** * ** ***
