# Applying typography
Use typography styles, adjust variable axes, and emphasize text for special editorial moments

## Using the type scale
Using the type scale
--------------------
The Material 3 type scale organizes styles into five roles that are named to describe their purposes: display, headline, title, label, body. Each role has three sizes: large, medium, and small. These roles and sizes create clear page hierarchy and work across many devices.
Roles
-----
### Display

There are three display styles in the default type scale: Large, medium, and small. As the largest text on the screen, display styles are reserved for short, important text or numerals. They work best on large screens.
### Headline

Headlines are useful for short, high-emphasis text on smaller screens. These styles can be good for marking primary passages of text or important regions of content.
### Title {#isPasted}

Titles are smaller than headline styles, and should be used for medium-emphasis text that remains relatively short. For example, consider using title styles to divide secondary passages of text or secondary regions of content.{#isPasted}

<br />
### Body

Body styles are used for longer passages of text.

GSF includes optical sizing to optimize text for readability at smaller sizes and longer blocks of text, opening up a wider range of uses for Google-branded typography.

<br />
### Label

Label styles are smaller, utilitarian styles, used for things such as the text inside components or for very small text in the content body, such as captions. Buttons, for example, use the label large style.
Typesetting
-----------

Vertical typesetting has two main approaches to ensure text readability at any size. One relies on padding and bounding boxes. The other relies on baselines. Take engineering considerations and the conventions of your platform into account when making decisions for typesetting, text resizing, density, and using text in adaptive layouts.
### Using padding and bounding boxes

Use this method for web products, and iOS products, where applicable. Note that some design tools also use bounding boxes for typesetting, but their methods vary and will need to be reconciled with the engineering implementation.
In web UIs, the line height and bounding box height are the same. Text is vertically centered within the bounding box, following the "[half-leading](https://www.w3.org/TR/css-inline-3/#leading-trim)" behavior established by CSS.{#isPasted}

The vertical position of the text isn't controlled directly, but through the combination of the bounding box and font metrics.
Padding is the space between UI elements in a container, such as between an image and a bounding box, or between the inner edge of the bounding box and the text.
Specify the distance of UI elements from fixed reference points, such as the container edge. For the web, automate this calculation using Sass or CSS.
Vertical alignment using padding and bounding boxes:{#isPasted}

1. **Line height**   
   Measure the height of the bounding box
2. **Centering**   
   Ensure equal top and bottom padding around the inner edge of the bounding box by using center align
3. **Spacing**   
   Use the height of the bounding box, and top and bottom padding to determine spacing

<br />
### Using the baseline {#isPasted}

Use this method for Android products or platform-agnostic specs.
The baseline is the invisible line upon which a line of text rests. In Material Design, the baseline is an important specification in measuring the vertical distance between the text and an element.
For Android, specifying distances relative to baseline enables accurate implementation.

The baseline can also be used to communicate text position between designers in a way that's platform agnostic or design tool agnostic.
Vertical alignment using the baseline:{#isPasted}

1. **Line height**   
   Measure distance from the text baseline of one line to the text baseline of the next line
2. **Centering**   
   Specify center alignment as a reference instead of measuring the distance to the text baseline
3. **Spacing**   
   Use the distance from a reference point to the text baseline
Ensuring readability {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
**Line height**{#isPasted}

Line height is the space between each line of text and is directly connected to type size.

Material's type tokens are optimized for intended size and use.
**Tabular numbers**

Use tabular figures (also known as monospaced numbers) rather than proportional digits in tables or places where values may change often, such as clocks.

Use monospaced tabular numbers to keep values optically aligned for better scanning.
Using Google Symbols with typography {#isPasted}
------------------------------------------------
Properly aligning typography with Google Symbols can improve cohesion and unity in your product. Use of [emphasis tokens](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens#6b35be2c-df20-46fd-a402-f84b014f5e66) also requires consistent pairing of icons and text across weight and roundness. [Learn more about matching icons and text](/google-material-3/pages/icons/applying-icons#0352c8a6-1471-44eb-bc42-03d2af3578a0)
Accessibility {#isPasted}
-------------------------
### Color \& contrast

Support visual accessibility by choosing the appropriate color contrast between your product's text and background. Contrast is the perceived difference between the lightness or darkness of two colors, and is quantified by a contrast ratio. Key contrast ratios indicate levels of contrast that are sufficient for accessibility.

[Learn more about contrast and accessibility](/google-material-3/pages/designing/color-contrast)
Text needs sufficient contrast with its background to be clearly visible. To achieve sufficient contrast at all sizes, use:

* 3:1 minimum contrast for large text

* 4.5:1 minimum contrast for small text

[Learn more about contrast requirements for text](/google-material-3/pages/designing/color-contrast#ccf80af1-c775-4fac-8d26-0e10766c074d)
The default color for typography is **on surface** , but **on surface variant** can be used for less-emphasized text.
For hyperlinked text appearing on top of a **surface color**, use **primary**. However, **tertiary** can be used to make links less prominent.

Hyperlinked text must also be underlined.

<br />

## Overview
### Using the type scale in UI: Best practices

Using the baseline type scale, with or without tokens, requires best practices in layout, accessibility, contrast, and other elements to generate consistent and readable type.

[Learn about using the type scale](/google-material-3/pages/applying-typography/using-the-type-scale/)
### Editorial treatments: Customizing typography

Several curated configurations, and guidelines on how to adapt them, ensures appropriate and powerful uses of editorial treatments.

[Learn about creating editorial treatments](/google-material-3/pages/applying-typography/editorial-treatments/)
Resources

| Type  |                                                          Resource                                                          |
|-------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|

| Fonts | [Android for Google Fonts](https://developers.google.com/fonts/docs/android)                                               |
| Fonts | [Android downloadable fonts](https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/views/text-and-emoji/downloadable-fonts)             |

## Editorial treatments
Editorial treatments
--------------------

Editorial treatments are type-driven, stand-alone departures from other purely functional areas of the user flow. They involve dynamic, attention-grabbing use of custom sizes, which can involve larger display type or a blockier look and feel. In the expressive system, they can be combined with elements such as motion, shape, or color, to create product-wide hero moments.
### How to use editorial treatments {#isPasted}

Editorial treatments operate as distinct expressive choices outside of the baseline and emphasized type styles, but can be enhanced by them. These treatments can express tone or intent through manipulation of variable font axes, such as round, weight, width, and slant. Editorial treatments shouldn't be used in repeatable labels or purely informational capacities.

The following are a collection of recommended combinations of axes. Use editorial treatments in three key ways: to celebrate content, to highlight the voice of the user, or to draw attention to bespoke functionality within the product.
### Celebrating content

Editorial treatments can dramatically take over the screen to mark a particular user action, memory, or preference.{#isPasted}

Content can express different tones and emotions by altering text using different combinations of the variable axes.

Tailor to different audiences and moods by switching between seriousness and irreverence, or calmness and energy.
### Voice of the user

Express the voice of the user by letting them personalize the appearance of typography.{#isPasted}

Use customization selectively to frame a user's mood and make it stand out.
### Bespoke functionality

Editorial treatments can also be employed to help express moments of bespoke and novel functionality within the product experience.
Combinations for baseline and emphasized
----------------------------------------
For editorial treatments intended to be stylistically consistent with baseline and emphasized tokens, use the combinations shown here. For all combinations of axes, automatic optical sizing should be enabled. Be mindful of letter-spacing with display sizes.
Combinations for celebrating content
------------------------------------
For editorial treatments intending to celebrate and enrich content (e.g. photo album cover) use either of the two previous recommended combinations or one of these additional recommended combinations. Be mindful to use appropriate letter-spacing with extremely large display sizes.
Combinations for personalization
--------------------------------
For personalization options intended to celebrate the voice of the user, use any of the previous recommended combinations, as well as these additional combinations, which offer a range of seriousness and irreverence, or calmness and energy. Be mindful to use appropriate letter-spacing with extremely large display sizes.
Editorial treatment best practices
----------------------------------
As there is intentionally wide latitude in developing editorial treatments, there are a few guard rails that will prevent inconsistency, unreadability, or distracting design.{#isPasted}

These best practices include:

* Using less weight with the rounded axis at display sizes
* Ensuring consistency between similar-looking editorial moments
* Not mixing multiple or clashing styles in the same layout
* Not mimicking personalization theming
* Matching the emotional tone of text to the task at hand
Preserve customizations with tokens
-----------------------------------
To ensure stylistic choices are consistent, you can create tokens for each custom type style. [More on customizing the type scale](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens#b0283d6a-3a0e-440a-be94-434d6afdf092)
Experimenting with axes in real time
------------------------------------
Google Sans Flex (GSF) is available in Figma. When it's selected, under **type settings** , select **variable**, and play around with the different axes. {#isPasted}

Test out the impact of different axes on the possible styles of GSF in real time using the [GSF type tester](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Google+Sans+Flex/tester). Additional Google-exclusive brand fonts are found on [go/fonts](https://fonts.corp.google.com/). Other fonts are available on [fonts.google.com](https://fonts.google.com/).
*** ** * ** ***

File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


## Overview
* Use lists to help people find a specific item and act on it

* Order list items in logical ways, like alphabetical or numerical

* Keep items short and easy to scan

* Show icons, text, and actions in a consistent format

* Choose between standard and segmented styles
Availability \& resources
-------------------------
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
Lists have a new segmented visual style, improved selection treatment, and slot functionality. [More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview)
**December 2025**

Variants:

* Added **expressive** list

  * Recommended for new designs

<!-- -->

* List (baseline) is still available

New visual styles:

* Standard or segmented

* Highlighted selection states

* Flexible slots

Supported platforms:

* [Android Views](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/g3doc/components/listitem.md?cl=head)

* [Jetpack Compose](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/components/list.md?cl=head)

* [Wiz](https://gm-catalog.corp.google.com/material3/list)
Differences from GM2 to GM3 baseline
------------------------------------
* **Color:** New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color

* **Layout:** Padding and spacing rules are updated to be more consistent

* **Height:** The tallest element within a list item determines the list item's height - either 56dp, 72dp, or 88dp

* **Alignment:**

  * In most cases, elements in a list item are middle-aligned

  * If a list is 88dp or larger, or contains three or more lines of text, elements are top-aligned

----

File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Lists are vertical groups of text, icons, images, and other elements, optimized for reading comprehension.

<br />

List items can contain multiple actions at once, like selection, icon buttons, overflow menus, and more.
Use lists for communicating or selecting discrete items, such as choosing from a set of colors.
A list should be easy to scan. Any element can be used to anchor and align list item content.

Place supporting visuals and primary text in the same position in each list item.

Don't vary the position of elements within a list.

<br />
List items can adapt to different lengths of text:

<br />

**Label text only**A list item can contain a single line of label text. If the text doesn't fit on one line, it can wrap or be truncated.

<br />

**Label text with supporting text**A list item can include supporting text below the label text. Both the label and supporting text can wrap or be truncated.
Anatomy
-------
### Container

List containers hold all list items and their elements. List item size is determined by the tallest element within the list item. [See layout measurements](/google-material-3/pages/lists/specs#072bb8fb-7cda-4b46-b495-e4feb31ea76b)

When a list item features an image, consider customizing the container color to use a content-based color scheme. This should be applied to either the enabled state or for an interaction.
### Label \& supporting text

Keep label text brief.   

To ensure list items are scannable:

* Limit supporting text to one to three lines

* Truncate supporting text, depending on screen size

[See adaptive guidance](/google-material-3/pages/lists/guidelines#f89e52fe-1aec-4fe4-a49c-d4a1abab76de)
### Icons

**Leading icon**   
A leading icon should provide a quick visual cue that relates to the item's label text, helping people scan the list.

**Trailing icon**   
A trailing icon is often used to communicate status or indicate an action, like **Show more**.
### Leading media

List items can contain a leading avatar, image, or video. Anchor visuals to the leading edge of the list to improve scannability.

Leading video thumbnails can open a video player or even play within the list.

<br />
**Avatars**   
List items can include images in circular or expressive shapes to represent a person or entity.

Use square or rectangular images for other content, such as products or videos.
**Primary \& secondary actions**

Use spacing to draw attention to the most important aspect of the list item, usually the primary action area or key content.
### Trailing text

Trailing text can provide additional meta-information about a list item, such as a price, count, or other details.
### Selection controls

Selection controls display list item actions. Position controls at the leading or trailing end of a list item:

* Use checkboxes to select multiple items

* Use switches to toggle settings on or off

* Use radio buttons to select a single item
### Gaps \& dividers

Gaps or dividers can separate lists into items and groups:

* Use **gaps** for contained lists. Gaps leverage expressive shape and containment tactics.

* Limit **dividers** to uncontained or complex lists, only when a stronger visual separation is necessary.
Adaptive design
---------------
### Line length

In fluid layouts, avoid excessively long lines of text when expanding containers and text-heavy components. This often means changing margins and typography properties as the container scales.
Adapt the width of the list container based on a line's length, or by switching to a multi-column layout.
The ideal line length for text is typically between 40 to 60 characters, but large-screen devices can accommodate up to 120 characters per line. If a line of text is close to 120 characters in length, consider increasing the line height to improve readability .
A list in a compact window can become part of a two-column layout in an expanded window, adjusting the amount of information shown in each list item.
### Adapt list elements \& layout

Lists can change their layout to adapt to different window sizes. This affects the size and placement of content.

For example, a list in a compact window can adjust margins, spacing, or density to better fit an expanded window.
### Swap components

Lists are just a compact composition of images, text, and actions. Other components, like cards and carousels, use the same elements but take up more space. 

On large screens, consider swapping a list to a component with a similar purpose to take advantage of available space.
### Compact window size

Lists should extend edge-to-edge in compact windows. Selecting a list item should open a page with the details.
### Medium \& expanded window sizes

Medium and expanded window sizes, such as tablet and desktop screens, can display primary and secondary content in the same view.

For example, a list and the detailed information can appear side-by-side.
On a larger window size, a list may transform into a carousel.
Lists can also show more or less content as they scale up and down in size.

For example, a list item can reveal more content when the component expands.
Behavior
--------
### List selection modes

The selected state applies to the entire list item. For example, when an item with a checkbox is selected, both the list item and the checkbox show a selected state.
**Single-select**

Lists can feature a single-selection component such as a radio button.

Single-select list items:

* Don't support multi-actions

* Can't have secondary nested actions

* Shouldn't use checkboxes

<br />
**Multi-select**

Multi-select lists allow for multiple list items to be toggled on.

Multi-select list items:

* Pair well with checkboxes and switches

* Can't have secondary nested actions

* Shouldn't use radio buttons
**Single-action**

In a single-action list, the entire list item performs one action, such as navigating to a new page.

Single-action list items:

* Can't have secondary nested actions

* Can't be toggled into a persistent selected state
**Multi-action**

Multi-action lists can support multiple nested actions within a list item.

The primary action should take up the majority of the space in the leading and content positions.

Place supplementary actions, like a bookmark or menu, in the trailing position.

[More on multi-action accessibility](/google-material-3/pages/lists/accessibility#e13d7fe0-3dc1-43c0-ac08-6c18eaba96b4)
**Non-interactive**

Non-interactive lists can organize information in a scannable way. They don't perform any actions and can't be selected.
### List interactions
**Expand \& collapse**

List items containing other list items can expand and collapse in a folder-like manner, to reveal or hide content.

Tapping a list item expands it vertically across the entire screen using a container transform transition pattern.
### Swiping

Swiping a list item (either left or right) can perform an action.
### Dragging

Items can be dragged to reorder a list.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Variants
--------
### Expressive lists

Use the expressive list variant for more flexible styling, highlighted selection states, and customizable slots.
### Baseline lists

In GM3 Expressive, baseline lists are still available to use, but don't have the latest visual style, selection treatment, and slot functionality.

[See baseline list specs](/google-material-3/pages/lists/specs#2244e695-a113-4b62-97d9-d24f9f2f7ed8)
|   **Variants**    |  **GM3**  | **GM3 Expressive** |
|-------------------|-----------|--------------------|
| List (expressive) | --        | Available          |
| List (baseline)   | Available | Available          |
Configurations
--------------
### Styles

The standard and segmented styles are a visual choice, and don't affect a list's behavior.
### List selection modes

A list can have only one selection mode at a time. For example, a single-action list can change to a multi-select list, but can't be both at once.
### List interactions
#### Expand

On Android, lists can [expand and collapse](/google-material-3/pages/lists/guidelines#db30ef5c-77f0-4e44-89ec-f681e446a63f).
|  **Category**   |                    **Configuration**                     |  **GM3**  | **GM3 Expressive** |
|-----------------|----------------------------------------------------------|-----------|--------------------|
| Styles          | Standard                                                 | Available | Available          |
| Styles          | Segmented                                                | --        | Available          |
| Selection modes | Single-action, multi-action, single-select, multi-select | Available | Available          |
| Interactions    | Expand                                                   | Available | Available          |
Tokens \& specs
---------------

Use the table's menu to select a token set. The **common** set combines baseline tokens with new expressive shapes and sizes. The **expand** set has tokens for the expand interaction. [Learn about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Anatomy
-------
### Flexibility \& slots

The [GM3 Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Android-Design-Kit?node-id=50205-26723&t=ukK8QfwshshycQx8-0) includes lists with custom slots for designing flexible item layouts. Think of a custom list as a container with three different slots: leading, content, and trailing. Each slot can hold a different element.

#### **Slot accessibility**

Slots are not accessible by default. Consider the following:

* Elements must follow the rules, structure, and interaction patterns for lists

* Use standard list item padding

* Targets must be at least 48x48dp

* Don't add interactive elements that make the list item difficult to navigate, especially for people using screen readers

[More on required accessibility guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/lists/accessibility/c9bf6a45-e3ff-413a-87e5-7b01cec04eea)
warning  
Caution:  
Slots require custom code implementation that you must create and maintain
The **leading** and **trailing** slot positions must be a smaller width than the **content** section.

1. **Leading slots** can contain:

* Visual elements: Avatar, icon, image, or video thumbnail

* Selection controls: Checkbox, radio button, or switch

* Customizations: Badge or larger image

2. **Content slots** must be the largest-width slot and can contain:

* Default content: Label text, supporting text

* Optional add-ons: Badge, icon, in-line label, or more text elements

* Avoid long lines of text to preserve readability

<br />

3. **Trailing slots** can contain:

* Action elements or text: Icon, icon button, or trailing text

* Selection controls: Checkbox, radio button, or switch
#### Selection lists

For selection lists, use only one selection interaction per list item.
Color
-----
Color values are implemented through design tokens. For designers, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. In implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
States
------
States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or an interactive element. [Learn more about interaction states](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/overview)
### Default list items
### Selected list items
Measurements
------------
List (baseline)
---------------
The baseline list variant is available and continues to work in existing products. However, the [expressive list](/google-material-3/pages/lists/specs#7b0d8155-5afb-40b4-bf74-4cb983098195) variant is recommended for new designs.
### Tokens \& specs

Baseline list tokens are in the **common** token set. Note: This set also includes several expressive tokens.
### Color

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For designers, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. In implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
### States

States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element.
### Layout

#### One-line lists
#### Two-line lists
#### Three-line lists
|                              Attribute                              | Value  |
|                           Label alignment                           | Center |
|            Label alignment when height is 88dp or taller            |  Top   |
|                         Label left padding                          |  16dp  |
|                Leading element alignment (vertical)                 | Center |
| Leading element alignment (vertical) when height is 88dp or taller  |  Top   |
|                    Leading element left padding                     |  16dp  |
|                  Leading icon alignment (vertical)                  |  Top   |
|                      Leading icon top padding                       |  8dp   |
|       Leading icon top padding when height is 88dp or taller        |  12dp  |
|                Trailing element alignment (vertical)                | Center |
| Trailing element alignment (vertical) when height is 88dp or taller |  Top   |
|                    Trailing element left padding                    |  16dp  |
|                   Trailing element right padding                    |  24dp  |
|                     Padding above/below divider                     |  0dp   |
|                               Targets                               |  48dp  |
|                         Divider full-width                          |  100%  |
|                     Divider inset left padding                      |  16dp  |
|                     Divider inset right padding                     |  24dp  |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------|--------|
### Configurations
#### Leading avatar
#### Leading image or thumbnail
#### Leading video
#### Leading icon
#### Text-only
#### Leading checkbox
#### Leading radio button
#### Trailing switch
*** ** * ** ***

File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS.{#isPasted}

[Apple's Human Interface Guidelines for lists](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/components/layout-and-organization/lists-and-tables) include additional iOS best practices and platform considerations
Resources
---------
|    **Type**    |                                                           **Resource**                                                            |    **Contact**     | **Status** |
|     Design     | [iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=62618-47498&t=ZYYZaN4DZOR1BDw7-0) |   gm-design-kit@   | Available  |
| Implementation |                [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Lists/index.md?cl=head)                | material-ios-team@ | Available  |
|----------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------|------------|
Spec differences
----------------
iOS includes a robust list component that can be customized. Lists can be used to display items, act as a navigation tree, and function as text fields.
### Lists have 3 different styles

* Inset grouped{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Grouped
* Plain

The inset grouped style use tonal surface colors as a background. The inset grouped list style makes groups more easily scannable by full containing the list cells with outer margins on every side. Use this style for long lists with lots of groups. Each style has optional headers and footers.
The grouped list style also uses tonal surface colors to distinguish between groups, but the list cells run full width to the edge of the screen. The headers of each group stick to the top of the view until the contents of their group scroll off screen. This is useful for contexts with lots of scrolling and searching like a list of contacts.
The plain list style uses no tonal surface colors and the list cells run full width to the edge of the screen. This style works best with cells that have a lot of information, or contexts where the information in the list is very dense.
### Text in list cells can have different configurations
### Cells can contain a leading and trailing item in addition to text

Cells can navigate to subsequent pages, function as a text field or perform an action. These actions can range from turning a switch on or off, to triggering a modal sheet. Use leading and trailing items to indicate a cell's functionality.{#isPasted}

Leading items can include avatars, icons or other visuals to help distinguish list items from one another and indicate their functionality.

Trailing items can include a chevron icon to indicate navigation, a switch, checkmark, progress indicator or other icons.
Guidelines
----------
List cells can be used as text fields for forms and long-form editing contexts. Tapping anywhere on the cell should enter the editing state.
Use text labels with chevron navigation icons to indicate the current state of the next screen or to provide additional context as to what is on the next page.
Use checkmarks instead of square checkboxes inside list cells. Tapping anywhere in the list cell checks or unchecks the cell, just as tapping in a list cell with a switch toggles the switch on or off.
If an un-toggled selection state is required, consider using a trailing icon button. This can indicate that the list cell is selectable.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug){#isPasted}

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------
People should be able to do the following with assistive technology:

* Navigate to a list item

* Select a list item
Indicate selection with more than color
---------------------------------------
To make selected items clear for everyone, don't rely on color as the only visual cue. 

Use an additional indicator that an item is selected such as:

* Radio buttons or checkboxes

* Leading or trailing icons

* A visual style not related to color, like underlined text
Interaction \& style
--------------------
### Touch

When a person taps on a list item, a touch ripple appears, indicating interaction feedback.
### Cursor

When hovered, the hover state provides a visual cue that a list item is interactive.
### Keyboard \& switch

When a person tabs to a single-action list, a focus indicator appears, providing a visual cue that the first list item is now focused and action can be taken.

When a person interacts with the focused list item via **Space** or **Enter**, the action is performed.
Focus
-----
### Single-action lists

The first element in a list should always receive focus, unless the list has a selected element. In that case, focus should go to the selected list item instead.  

After an element is focused, a person should be able to navigate within the list using arrow keys.
All list items must be able to be activated using the **Space** or **Enter** key.  

[More on single-action lists](/google-material-3/pages/lists/guidelines#fdd61904-0430-42e1-8c67-fda114e08b0a)
### Multi-action lists

Multi-action list items contain a primary action and at least one supplementary action.  

The list item as a whole isn't selectable; only the individual actions are.

A person should be able to use a keyboard to:

* **Tab** to the list item, which focuses the first element

* Move between between all focusable elements in the list using the **Up** , **Down** , **Left** , and **Right** arrow keys

* Activate a focused element using **Space** or **Enter** 

[More on multi-action lists](/google-material-3/pages/lists/guidelines#ff135382-3ba2-4e7b-903e-2916245d9914)
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
|         **Keys**          |                                                             **Actions**                                                             |
|          **Tab**          |                       To move focus to the first list item, last list item, or outside of the list component                        |
| Down and right arrow keys |     Moves to the next element in the list; if the focused element is the last in the list, it wraps back to the top of the list     |
|  Up and left arrow keys   | Moves to the previous element in the list; if the focused element is the first in the list, it wraps back to the bottom of the list |
|  **Space** or **Enter**   |                                               To select a list item not yet selected                                                |
|---------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
Labeling elements
-----------------
Accessibility labels are used with assistive devices like screen readers.

The accessibility label for a list item is typically the same as the **label text** and **supporting text**.

Some labels, roles, and states are [dependent on platform](/google-material-3/pages/lists/accessibility#a4f80bb6-fa92-4fc0-8322-a0e3a6956ab5).
### Platform-specific labels
#### Single-select lists

| **Trait**  |                                            **Web**                                             |               **Android Views**                |              **Jetpack Compose**               |                    **iOS**                     |
|------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Aria label | Container label: Should describe selection type List item: Should match the visible label text | List item: Should match the visible label text | List item: Should match the visible label text | List item: Should match the visible label text |
| Role       | Container: List boxList item: Option                                                          | List item: Radio button                        | List item: Radio button                        | List item: Button                              |
| State      | Selected or Not-selected                                                                       | Checked or Not-checked                         | Checked or Not-checked                         | Selected or Not-selected                       |
#### Multi-select lists

| **Trait**  |                                            **Web**                                             |               **Android Views**                |              **Jetpack Compose**               |                    **iOS**                     |
|------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Aria label | Container label: Should describe selection type List item: Should match the visible label text | List item: Should match the visible label text | List item: Should match the visible label text | List item: Should match the visible label text |
| Role       | Container: List boxList item: Option                                                          | List item: Checkbox                            | List item: Checkbox                            | List item: Button                              |
| State      | Selected or Not-selected                                                                       | Checked or Not-checked                         | Checked or Not-checked                         | Selected or Not-selected                       |
On web, a list container's accessibility label describes the type of selection that can be made, and the role is **List box**.
On Jetpack Compose and iOS, the role applies to the list item as a whole.

If a list isn't selectable, the label text is read out without a role.
On Android Views, components contained within the list should be labeled according to that component's specific guidelines:

* [Checkbox](/google-material-3/pages/checkbox/accessibility)

* [Radio button](/google-material-3/pages/radio-button/accessibility)

<br />
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: YAQs

File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://goto.google.com/gm3-bug)


# GM3 for iOS
iOS-specific resources, guidance, and required features

## iOS foundations & styles
Foundations
-----------
### Accessibility

Material components automatically adapt to the following iOS system accessibility features:

* Increase contrast: Components darken or lighten to increase overall contrast  
* Dynamic type: Type and icons on screen scale larger or smaller  
* Bold text: Components add weight to icons and typography for greater emphasis and legibility
### Design tokens {#isPasted}

All iOS tokens are part of the md. system to ensure consistency between engineering and design. iOS tokens were formerly in the gmapple. system, which is now being deprecated.{#isPasted}

Use the "Platform: iOS" context to design and build with the iOS-specific values.
star  
Note:  
The Material iOS platform is still migrating from gmapple. to md., so it doesn't yet generate code from md.
### Interaction states

Use iOS system touch feedback animations over custom animations like the Android ripple.
### Layout {#isPasted}

Use iOS layout defaults for UIKit components. Don't override default margins and spacing unless it is explicitly called out at the component level.
Color
-----
* iOS uses the GM3 baseline color scheme

* Tonal color is used to define surfaces

* There is no system-level dynamic color on iOS.

  * Content-based dynamic color is currently under investigation

* **iOS dark mode** supports the elevated surface theme

* **iOS increase contrast mode** is supported in all components, and uses Material's medium contrast colors

* GM3 baseline color scheme is used to tint Material's version of Liquid Glass
### Use the GM3 baseline color scheme {#isPasted}

The GM3 baseline color scheme and tonal surfaces should be used to theme UIKit components where possible. {#isPasted}

Use tonal surfaces to communicate elevation, and use shadows only when required to create additional protection against a background or to encourage interaction.
### iOS elevated dark mode

In dark mode, iOS has an elevated surface theme that brightens surfaces.

Material iOS tokens support this by mapping to one level brighter.
|        Base token         |      Elevated value       |
|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Surface container lowest  | Surface container low     |
| Surface container low     | Surface container         |
| Surface container         | Surface container high    |
| Surface container high    | Surface container highest |
| Surface container highest | Surface bright            |
### Increase contrast is supported by all iOS components {#isPasted}

Support for the **increase contrast** accessibility feature on iOS is included with all components that use the provided iOS color tokens. Colors will automatically lighten and darken when this system feature is enabled to provide additional contrast to UI. {#isPasted}

**iOS increase contrast mode** uses Material's medium contrast token values. iOS doesn't use high contrast tokens.
### iOS color tokens
Elevation
---------
Use color to communicate elevation where possible. If shadows are required to create additional protection against a background or encourage interaction, use the Material shadow styles tailored to iOS.

iOS uses Material's [elevation model](/google-material-3/pages/elevation/overview/), but applies a simplified one-drop-shadow model to define the elevation effect. This can be seen in the [iOS Design Kit](http://go/gm3-ios-design-kit). This greatly improves app performance and creates visual consistency with how system shadows are used on iOS.

Note: This is not optimized for iOS 26 Liquid Glass. Consider using the functional layer for elevation.
Icons
-----
Use [Google Symbols](http://go/icons) icons except in places where standard iOS icons are the norm. {#isPasted}

Example of iOS icons to use instead:

* Horizontal dot **More** icon instead of vertical icon
* iOS **Share** icon
* Checkmarks in lists instead of checkboxes
* iOS **Back** arrow in top app bars
Typography
----------
iOS uses Material's font themes (static and variable) and type styles (baseline and emphasized), with adjustments. Use the "Platform: iOS" context to get the proper token values.

iOS also has an "iOS bold" font theme when the system setting for bold fonts is turned on. This is separate from emphasized type styles, as the bold theme can still be further emphasized.
### Static font theme (Google Sans \& SF Pro Text)

iOS ships with the default system font [San Francisco](https://developer.apple.com/fonts/#:%7E:text=SF%20Pro,%2C%20Greek%2C%20and%20Cyrillic%20scripts), used for all body and label content except for buttons. For larger fonts, such as display, headline, or title styles, use [Google Sans](https://carbon.googleplex.com/google-sans/pages/google-sans) to make products feel distinctly Google.
### Variable theme (Google Sans Flex)
Material iOS also provides type styles using [Google Sans Flex](/google-material-3/pages/typography/google-sans-flex), a variable font derived from Google Sans offering unprecedented customization possibilities.
### iOS typography tokens
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs){#isPasted}  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Requirements
*GM3 requirements for iOS products have not changed since January 2023. iOS 26 Liquid Glass requirements are not documented yet.*
Planning \& process
-------------------

1. Identify your team's cross-functional owners, including PM, PgM, UX, and Eng

2. Walk through the required features as a cross-functional group

3. Conduct an audit to determine which features apply to your product

4. Complete engineering prerequisites

5. Create a design proposal and set up a [Material UX Review](http://goto.google.com/gdp-ux-review)

6. Create an implementation roadmap with feature release milestones

7. Make it happen
Actionable migrations
---------------------

**Component migrations** became actionable in 2021. These migrations involve moving from custom Material components to equivalent Branded iOS system components. For example, migrating from the Material date picker to iOS' system date picker.

**Branding migrations** became available in 2023. Migrations involve moving from GM2 Branding/Tokens APIs to GM3 Branding/Tokens APIs.

**iOS 26 Liquid Glass migrations** are not documented yet.
Required features
-----------------
##### Color
> [Baseline color scheme](/google-material-3/pages/static/gm3-baseline)
> The static baseline (blue) scheme is the default color scheme for GM3.
##### Components
> [App bar](/google-material-3/pages/app-bars/ios){#isPasted}
> [Badge](/google-material-3/pages/badges/overview){#isPasted}
> [Buttons](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/ios){#isPasted}
> [Cards](/google-material-3/pages/cards/ios){#isPasted}
> [Dialogs](/google-material-3/pages/dialogs/ios){#isPasted}
> [FAB](/google-material-3/pages/fab/ios) + [Extended FAB](/google-material-3/pages/extended-fab/ios)
> [Lists](/google-material-3/pages/lists/ios){#isPasted}
> [Menus](/google-material-3/pages/menus/ios)
> [Navigation bar](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-bar/ios){#isPasted}
> [Switch](/google-material-3/pages/switch/ios){#isPasted}
> The requirements only apply to the original GM3 designs. The GM3 Expressive updates are not yet required.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## iOS 26
What's new in iOS 26?
---------------------
In iOS 26, Apple introduced **Liquid Glass** : a dynamic, translucent style that makes navigation and control elements appear to float above the rest of the app. To make this effect at scale, iOS 26 also introduced a UI layer system: a **functional layer** on top of a **content layer**.
### Liquid Glass
Liquid Glass applies translucent glass and shine effects on components primarily in the functional layer. It emphasizes how apps are structured, visually separating the functional layer components---toolbars, navigation, and more---from the content layer, which contains everything else. This makes it easier for people to identify key actions and understand app hierarchy.
### Functional layer
The functional layer is the topmost, floating layer of the interface.

Material iOS components in this layer include the app bar, navigation bar, toolbar, and FAB. To establish a clear visual hierarchy, glass effects are applied to these functional elements, so the underlying content can peek through. This creates a dynamic, layered look with stronger perceived depth.

Place navigational and control components in the functional layer. When deciding on components to add, consider components that float above the main content already, or function independently of the main content.

<br />
### Content layer
The content layer is the primary focus of the user experience and is where the content and primary tasks live.

Material iOS components in this layer are not glassy, so they can more easily leverage Google's brand and expressive design tactics. Some native UI components, such as the switch, still have a glass bubble effect when pressed.
Liquid Glass for GM3: Material Glass
------------------------------------
To adapt Liquid Glass for Material components, the iOS team created **Material Glass** --- a customized version of Apple's existing glass materials to be specifically engineered for GM3 Expressive.

Material Glass is tuned for increased opacity to enhance legibility and Material's distinctive color palette.
### Components
Material Glass is built into Material components that are typically on the functional layer by default, like the app bar and FAB.

Components such as buttons and button groups, which can be placed on either the functional layer or content layer, will have both solid and glass versions to choose from.
#### Component release timeline

Material Glass components are rolling out over Q1 and Q2 2026. Mandatory compliance components are first, followed by strategic innovation components, so 1P partners can begin adopting quickly.

For a detailed, version-specific list of which components are available in which rollouts, view the internal delivery schedule: [go/iOS-LG-delivery-schedule](http://goto.google.com/iOS-LG-delivery-schedule).
### Liquid Glass for GM3 Design Kit (Figma)
The Material Labs [Liquid Glass for GM3 Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/KCk1VpuDP9n0tgRxQ9Ubwe/Material-Labs--iOS-Liquid-Glass-Kit?node-id=1594-21145) is an early access Figma Design Kit to start designing with spec-accurate iOS 26 components before they fully launch. The components follow Liquid Glass guidelines.

Note: There's a small chance designs may change when in early access.
### Guidelines
**Avoid stacking glass effects**

To prevent excessive visual noise and distractions, Apple advises to avoid layering glass components on top of other glass materials. Use solid components on top of glass surfaces.
**Use contextual transformations**

Context menus, action sheets, and modal sheets can now morph from their initiating action buttons. This transition provides a clear visual connection between the user's action and the resulting interface.
### Accessibility
Material Glass should be used as specified to enhance contrast and accessibility in components. Material is working closely with the GAR standards team to test for GAR compliance regarding contrast. More guidance will be available as components roll out.
### App icons
To create glass icons for your app, follow Material Design's [Liquid Glass product icon guidelines](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/foundations/styles/fundamentals/ProductIcons/design.md?cl=head) and use Apple's [Icon Composer tool](https://developer.apple.com/icon-composer/). This will recreate your app icon using the layered structure and dynamic effects of Liquid Glass, while ensuring brand consistency and alignment with other Google iOS apps. Product teams need to create these icons themselves.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](https://go/gm3-yaqs)File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](https://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
Use Material Design with [Apple's Human Interface Guidelines](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/guidelines/overview/) to craft distinct experiences attuned to Apple's ecosystem and users. GM3 for Apple platforms balances behavioral expectations on iOS while maintaining the strengths of Google's design system.

---------

| **Design**         | [Liquid Glass for GM3 Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/KCk1VpuDP9n0tgRxQ9Ubwe/Material-Labs--iOS-Liquid-Glass-Kit) (Figma)               | [material-ios-team@](https://mail.google.com/mail/u/0/?fs=1&tf=cm&source=mailto&to=material-ios-team@google.com) | Early access |
| **Implementation** | [GM3 iOS developer docs](http://go/material-iOS)                                                                                                 | [material-ios-team@](https://mail.google.com/mail/u/0/?fs=1&tf=cm&source=mailto&to=material-ios-team@google.com) | Available    |
| **Implementation** | [Component catalog](http://go/material-ios-components#download-the-google-material-catalog)                                                      | [material-ios-team@](https://mail.google.com/mail/u/0/?fs=1&tf=cm&source=mailto&to=material-ios-team@google.com) | Available    |
| **Implementation** | [Component comparison: Android vs iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/concepts/gm3/component-translation.md?cl=head) | [material-ios-team@](https://mail.google.com/mail/u/0/?fs=1&tf=cm&source=mailto&to=material-ios-team@google.com) | Available    |

What's new
----------
### iOS 26 Liquid Glass for GM3 available (early access)
**Dec 2025**

Added [iOS 26 foundational guidance](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-26) and [GM3 for iOS principles](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/principles).

Added the [Liquid Glass Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/KCk1VpuDP9n0tgRxQ9Ubwe/Material-Labs--iOS-Liquid-Glass-Kit?node-id=1594-21145)(Figma).

iOS 26 guidelines have been published for the following components:

* [Checkboxes](/google-material-3/pages/checkbox/ios)

* [Date picker](/google-material-3/pages/date-pickers/ios)

* [Time picker](/google-material-3/pages/time-pickers/i-os)

* [Menus (context menu \& action sheet)](/google-material-3/pages/menus/ios)

* [Switch](/google-material-3/pages/switch/ios)

Note: Component guidelines will be updated on a rolling basis as they become available
### iOS tokens available in Material Design as "iOS" contexts

**Mar 2025**

gmapple. tokens are now incorporated into the main md. system. The gmapple. tokens still exist for legacy usage.
The following token contexts were added:

* Platform: iOS
  * All iOS token values are available using this context
* Font: iOS Bold
  * iOS values also added for Font: Static \& Variable
* Theme: Dark iOS elevated, Dark iOS elevated medium contrast
  * iOS values also added for Theme: Light, Dark, Light medium contrast, \& Dark medium contrast.

Note: Material iOS hasn't yet migrated their codegen to using md., so they still generate token code from gmapple.
### Variable font theme \& emphasized styles
**Mar 2025**

The GM3 type scale for iOS has expanded in two key ways:

New font themes in tokens to easily swap between static and variable fonts:

* The **static** font theme uses Google Sans \& SF Pro Text.

* The **variable** font theme uses Google Sans Flex.

15 new emphasized type styles for key editorial moments.

* Swap in the equivalent emphasized token as needed

## Principles
Material Design Principles for iOS
----------------------------------
### Embrace the platform, express the brand

Material Design continues to be the foundation for Google's iOS apps. The strategy is to integrate essential iOS patterns into our branded elements so that Google apps harmoniously blend with the platform while upholding Material Design principles and feeling distinctly Google. GM3 makes it easy to build unique, premium designs expected of top apps, while helping people quickly identify the Google brand and connect with our products.
### Apply Expressive tactics
Google Material 3 Expressive builds on the foundations of Material to address user and market demands with fresh app design, cutting-edge technology, and cross-device experiences.
Expressive tactics are design techniques for creating an impactful user experience using GM3 Expressive components, subsystems, and themes. When applied properly, these tactics greatly improve usability and make apps more desirable.

1. Apply rich and nuanced colors

2. Diversify use of shapes

3. Compose refined typography

4. Create powerful hierarchy

5. Enhance content with containment

6. Leverage component flexibility

7. Celebrate hero moments

[Learn more about GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview)  
[Learn more about expressive tactics](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/applying-expressive)
### Fonts \& icons
To create a distinctly Google app experience, apply Material Design's [iOS type scale](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-foundations-styles#451ceb20-9faf-42b4-b1bc-753620f0e5ef).

This uses a mix of SF Pro, Google Sans, and Google Sans Flex; and Google Symbols for icons, which visually complement GM3 Expressive designs.
### Colors
Material iOS uses Google's [signature GM3 baseline color scheme](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-foundations-styles#911504d8-c34d-4c7d-85c3-9914ea0f6d3b) for immediate and consistent Google brand recognition.
### GM3 iOS components
GM3 components for iOS are designed to be HIG-compliant. Apple expects apps on their platforms to align and integrate with operating system features and patterns, and so do users. GM3 does this in a few ways:

* Adjusting attributes, like size and touch targets, to align with iOS components \& guidelines

* Applying GM3 branding, like colors and icons, to iOS components

* Applying iOS patterns, like glass effects, to GM3 components
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](https://go/gm3-yaqs)File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](https://go/gm3-bug)


# Color system
Create accessible, personal color schemes communicating your product's hierarchy, state, and brand

## How the system works
It's like paint-by-number
-------------------------
Imagine your product screen as a paint-by-number canvas:

* Each element on the screen has a number
* Each number is assigned a color
You can hand-pick a color for every "number" to create a static color scheme.
But now, you can also use Material's dynamic color system to automatically generate an entire palette of accessible colors for each "number" from a single source.

This source can be a user's wallpaper, or in-app content like imagery. If the source changes, the product colors update to match.
You can customize how dynamic color appears in your product by:

* Setting the color source
* Adding static or harmonized colors
* Changing which "numbers" are assigned to which elements

[Learn about advanced customizations](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/overview)
Essential terms
---------------
### Color role

Like the "numbers" on a paint-by-number canvas, color roles are assigned to specific UI elements. They have semantic names like **primary** , **on primary** , and **primary container,** and matching color tokens. [See all color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles)

### Dynamic color

Dynamic color takes a single color from a user's wallpaper or in-app content and creates an accessible color scheme assigned to elements in the UI. If the user's wallpaper or the in-app content changes, the colors in the UI will change to match.

### Static color

UI colors that don't change based on the user's wallpaper or in-app content. Static colors can be hand-picked or generated in design tools like the Material Theme Builder. Once assigned to their respective color roles and UX elements, the colors remain constant.

#### **Baseline static color**

The default static color scheme for Material products. [See the baseline color scheme](/google-material-3/pages/static/gm3-baseline)
How dynamic color generates color schemes
-----------------------------------------
### 1. It starts with a source color

There are three ways to get a source color.
#### **A. Generate it from a wallpaper**

User-generated color is sourced from a user's personal wallpaper. The wallpaper is digitally analyzed through a process called quantization, and a single color is selected as the source color.
#### **B. Generate it from in-app content** {#isPasted}

Content-based color is sources from in-app content, like an album thumbnail image, logo, or video preview.

Like user-generated color, the image is digitally analyzed through quantization, and a single color selected as the source color.
#### **C. Pick it by hand** {#isPasted}

A hand-picked source color is deliberately selected by a designer.{#isPasted}

**Did you know?** The baseline static color scheme uses a hand-picked source color and was then further customized to make it easier for products to migrate from GM2 to GM3.
### 2. Feed the source color into an algorithm {#isPasted}

Dynamic color is powered by the [Material Color Utilities](https://github.com/material-foundation/material-color-utilities) (MCU), a set of color libraries containing algorithms and utilities that develop color themes and schemes in your app.

There are many color algorithms, but the most common ones are:

* ****User-generated color algorithm**** Uses personal wallpaper to identify source color. Maps colors of specific tones (lighter or darker) into the scheme according to a combination of system design choices and user preferences.  

  {#isPasted}
* **Content-based color algorithm**   
  Uses image for source color. Tones are adjusted to match the appearance of the source image, while maintaining accessible contrast.   

* **Custom colors**  
  Colors closely match the chosen input colors, such as those representing brand or semantic meaning.
### 3. The algorithm generates key colors
Material's color algorithms manipulate the source color's hue and chroma to generate **five complimentary key colors**.

1. Primary
2. Secondary
3. Tertiary
4. Neutral
5. Neutral variant
### 4. The algorithm creates tonal palettes

The system then manipulates tone and chroma values to create a **tonal palette** for each key color. Colors in these palettes are given a number from 0 to 100 in increments of 10, as well as 95, 98, and 99. Some palettes include more values.
### 5. The algorithm assigns tones to color roles

The algorithm uses accessible color relationships to **assign specific tones to the 26** **color roles** in both light and dark theme.
For example, the algorithm assigns the color tone primary40 to the **p** **rimary** role and the tone primary100 to the **o** **n primary** role.

[See all color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles)
Dark theme colors are also automatically assigned so that apps receive both light and dark themes through a single set of color roles.
### 6. The new colors are applied to the UI

The 26 standard color roles are already assigned to elements of the UI. When a new source color is picked, the UI dynamically changes color.
Color roles support three levels of contrast
--------------------------------------------
In addition to light and dark theme, color roles also support three levels of contrast. This helps people select the contrast setting that best suits their vision needs:{#isPasted}

* Standard (default)
* Medium
* High

The standard contrast emphasizes visual hierarchy using high and low contrast elements. People with vision disabilities may choose medium or high contrast options for better support.
star  
Note:  
Material iOS "increase contrast" mode uses medium contrast. In iOS dark theme, "elevated mode" modifies surface colors for improved contrast. [More on iOS elevated dark theme](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-foundations-styles/latest#37d7117c-a36e-48ac-b2b4-6547ba3019a5)
The contrast settings are automatically applied to both light and dark themes.
Custom components can support contrast levels by using Material's appropriate color roles. For example, use **primary container** and **on primary container**.

Use design tokens to apply color roles to custom components.
Pairing accessible tones
------------------------
The system manipulates hue, chroma, and tone (HCT) values to **create a tonal palette for each color** with tones ranging from 0 to 100.

Color has physical limitations---whether it's actual physics, our own biological visual limitations, or the limitations of on-screen color rendering. For example, some hues cannot exist with certain chroma or tones. Color limitations are the reason colors such as bright light blue or bright light red are not quite possible. This is why the chroma value may increase or decrease for some tones in a tonal palette.
Material's color algorithms use these palettes to find and **pair contrasting tones,**creating accessible color combinations.
Because tone can describe the lightness or darkness of a color, it's used to define accessible color relationships. Those relationships are built into Material's color algorithms.{#isPasted}

For example, the algorithms assign a dark tone to a button's container color and a light tone to its label color, ensuring that the colors have a 3:1 contrast.
For even more contrast, the algorithms assign tones even farther apart, achieving a 7:1 contrast.

This is the concept powering **u** **ser-controlled contrast** features.
Defining colors with hue, chroma, and tone (HCT)
------------------------------------------------
**The system uses a color space called HCT**, which defines all colors using three dimensions: hue, chroma, and tone.{#isPasted}

Changing HCT values lets you manipulate colors in flexible but predictable ways. Unlike other color spaces (like HSL or RGB), HCT allows the manipulation of a color's hue and chroma without affecting its tone. Watch to learn more:
### Hue

Hue is the perception of a color as red, orange, yellow, green, blue, violet, and so on. Hue is quantified by a number ranging from 0-360 and is a circular spectrum (values 0 and 360 are the same hue).
### Chroma

Chroma is how colorful or neutral (grey, black or white) a color appears. Chroma is quantified by a number ranging from 0 (completely grey, black or white) to infinity (most vibrant), though Chroma values in HCT top out at roughly 120.

Because of biological and screen rendering limitations, different hues and different tones will have different maximal chroma values.
### Tone {#isPasted}

Tone is how light or dark a color appears. Tone is sometimes also referred to as luminance. Tone is quantified by a number ranging from 0 (pure black, no luminance) to 100 (pure white, complete luminance).

Tone is crucial for visual accessibility because it determines contrast. Colors with a greater difference in tone create higher contrast, while those with a smaller difference create lower contrast.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
**The Material color system includes:**

* Built-in set of accessible color relationships
* 26+ color roles mapped to Material components
* Built-in dark theme colors
* Static baseline color scheme with default colors assigned to each color role
* Dynamic color features including user-generated and content-based color

[Learn how the system works](/google-material-3/pages/color/how-the-system-works)
For products migrating from GM2 to GM3, start by mapping the baseline color scheme to your existing product. It can easily switch to dynamic color when ready.
Play with dynamic color
-----------------------
Products with dynamic color can automatically generate and assign colors to each element in the UI.{#isPasted}

This provides:

* Personalized UI
* Accessible contrast
* User-controlled contrast
* Automatic dark theme

To play with dynamic color, create a custom color scheme using the [Material Theme Builder](https://serve-dot-zipline.googleplex.com/asset/28fea625-b752-5869-9bdb-3728c187418e/) embedded below.
Resources {#isPasted}
---------------------

|                                                               Type                                                                |                                                         Link                                                          |              Contact              |
|                                                              Design                                                               | [Android Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Android-Design-Kit?node-id=229513-14923) | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug) |
|                                                              Design                                                               |
|                                                              Design                                                               |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| [iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?node-id=34773-7738)                       | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug){#isPasted}                                                                          |
| [Web Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/lcRw8MOK5FMHmNl820IMAo/GM3-Web-Design-Kit?node-id=24-2695)                          | [File a bug](http://go/m3-dk-bug){#isPasted}                                                                          |
| [Jetpack Compose](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/styles/color.md?cl=head) | material-compose-team@                                                                                                |
| [Flutter](http://go/gm3-flutter#color)                                                                                            | material-flutter-team@                                                                                                |

What's new {#isPasted}
----------------------
May 2025

### Three levels of contrast

Color roles support three levels of contrast so people can select the one that best suits their vision needs. Contrasts also are tokenized.
August 2024{#isPasted}

### More colorful text and icons

The following color roles are updated in light theme to be more colorful while still having accessible color contrast:

* On primary container
* On secondary container
* On tertiary container
* On error container

Affected components:

* Badges
* Buttons
  * Buttons
  * Extended FAB
  * FAB
  * Icon buttons
  * Segmented buttons
* Chips
* Lists
* Menus
* Navigation bar
* Navigation drawer
* Navigation rail
* Switches
* Toolbars{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
Oct 2023

### Reorganized guidelines

Same color system, explained in a new way. Updated sections include:

* [How the system works](/google-material-3/pages/color/how-the-system-works)
* [Advanced customizations](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/overview)
* [Color resources](/google-material-3/pages/color-resources)
Feb 2023

### Tone-based surface colors {#isPasted}

[Tone-based surface color roles](https://material.io/blog/tone-based-surface-color-m3) have replaced the previous approach of surfaces at +1 to +5 elevation. The new color roles are not tied to elevation and offer more flexibility and support for color features, such as user-controlled contrast.
Technical changes were made to align the color system with Android SysUI:{#isPasted}

* Updated the default light theme surface from tone 99 to tone 98
* Updated the chroma for the neutral palette, increasing it from 4 to 6
* Slightly darkened surface roles in dark theme
Feb 2023

### Additional accent colors {#isPasted}

Additional accent colors in the scheme provide more flexibility and choice for color application. In particular, a new set of fixed colors for the **primary** , **secondary** , and **tertiary** accent groups provide colors which stay the same across light and dark themes.
YAQS {#isPasted}
----------------

Can't find what you're looking for? Search or ask your question on YAQs.
