File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Usability
Usability focuses on making products intuitive and easy to understand for everyone

## Applying M3 Expressive
Aura: An example app showcasing usability with M3 Expressive
------------------------------------------------------------
Aura is a conceptual breathing app that illustrates how [Material 3 (M3 Expressive design tactics](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/applying-expressive) can make an app more usable and draw the user's attention to the most important actions. It's used with a smart watch to measure heart rate. It was created based on the [eye tracking and focus group research](https://design.google/library/expressive-material-design-google-research) that played a key role in the creation of M3 Expressive. Research showed that participants were able to spot key UI elements up to **four times faster** in the M3 Expressive designs compared to other designs.

Examples include:{#isPasted}

* Using scale, color, and containment to guide people to start a breathing session
* Using shape, color, and empty space to guide people to breathe slowly and with intention
* Minimizing cognitive load by using empty space and fewer actions, so people can stay focused on their breath
* Balancing primary tasks with supportive data to show progress and impact of a session
Example 1: Start a breathing session
------------------------------------
Starting the session is the primary goal when opening the app. Size, placement, color, and contrast guide the user to the **Start breathing** button. The button's large size and low placement makes it easy to reach when holding a phone.{#isPasted}

The **settings** use a secondary color to draw attention, but they're not as emphasized as the button.

The **daily** **message** is the least emphasized, but uses large containment and type to draw attention.

Expressive components used:

* [Extra large button](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/overview)
* [Button groups](/google-material-3/pages/button-groups/overview)
* [Switch](/google-material-3/pages/switch/overview)
* [Navigation bar](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-bar/overview)
### Usability design tactics
|-----------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Color \& contrast           | The prominent dark purple **Start breathing** button (1) on a soft light purple background uses [Material primary color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles?s=google-material-3) to create high contrast, making the button easy to find and remember.                                   |
| Hierarchy                   | The main goal is to tap the large **Start breathing** button (1). The daily message (3) and settings (2) are in lighter colors and in subtle containers because they are supportive actions, but not required.                                                                                   |
| Placement                   | The button (1) is close to the bottom so it's easy to reach. It's the final, most prominent element in the vertical flow, naturally guiding the eye down the screen without competing with other content.                                                                                        |
| Shape                       | The rounded button form reinforces it as a distinct, touchable control.                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
| Size                        | The button (1) is extra large to make it the most emphasized element on the screen.                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| Spacing                     | Generous spacing separates the button (1) from the message (3) and settings (2).                                                                                                                                                                                                                 |
| Typography                  | The button (1) has larger text to emphasize the primary action.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| Visual harmony \& hierarchy | The daily message is placed at the top in a soft blue container with medium sized text, setting a reflective tone without drawing too much focus. {#isPasted} <br /> The hierarchy guides the user from the daily message (3) to settings (2) and finally to the **Start breathing** button (1). |
Example 2: Breathing session (inhale \& exhale)
-----------------------------------------------
The guided breathing exercise is the [hero moment](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/applying-expressive){#isPasted}. A large central flower expands and contracts, serving as the visual guide for each breath, while a countdown shows remaining seconds for inhaling, exhaling, and holding the breath. {#isPasted}

The **pause** and **stop** buttons are less prominent than the flower to encourage people to focus on the session. The buttons are placed at the bottom so they're easy to reach.

Expressive components and styles used:

* [Large buttons](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/overview) in a [button group](/google-material-3/pages/button-groups/overview)
* [Material shape library](/google-material-3/pages/shape/overview-principles#76fb0225-57af-4497-83d0-2b8827505fba) ("flower" and "sunny")
* [Emphasized typography](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens#a91f1194-a47d-42d4-a9ae-301a3ce16ca4)
### Usability design tactics
|-------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Color \& contrast | The vibrant yellow appears when inhaling to contrast the soft purple background and be obvious.                                                                                                                                               |
| Motion            | The flower expands and contracts to guide the pace of the breath. The motion uses[Material Spring Motion Tokens](http://figma.com/community/plugin/1397759704974764283/material-motion).                                                      |
| Placement         | The **pause** and **stop** buttons are at the bottom, spaced away from the flower, but easy to reach.{#isPasted} The navigation bar hides during the breathing journey.                                                                       |
| Shape             | The flower uses the "flower" and "sunny" [Material shapes](/google-material-3/pages/shape/overview-principles#76fb0225-57af-4497-83d0-2b8827505fba) to draw attention and clearly stand apart from the simple **pause** and **stop** buttons. |
| Size              | The size of the animating, breathing flower dominates the screen to draw attention.                                                                                                                                                           |
| Typography        | The countdown numbers are very large in comparison to the inhale, hold, and exhale text to focus attention on the exercise. This provides strong visual contrast, while still keeping the instructions associated with the countdown.         |
Example 3: Breathing report
---------------------------
The breathing report comes after the breathing exercise. It's the secondary goal in the app, not the primary hero moment, so it uses fewer design tactics to reduce cognitive load.{#isPasted}

It draws attention to each data point on the page using shapes and decreasing size. The button is less emphasized than the **Start breathing** button on the landing page.

Expressive components and elements used:

* [Medium button](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/overview)
* [Material shape library](/google-material-3/pages/shape/overview-principles#76fb0225-57af-4497-83d0-2b8827505fba)
* [Emphasized typography](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens#a91f1194-a47d-42d4-a9ae-301a3ce16ca4)
### Usability design tactics
|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Color \& contrast   | Dark text on the light flower shapes creates a strong contrast, making each metric easy to read at a glance. {#isPasted} The solid, dark purple **Finish** button stands out clearly against the light purple background, guiding users to the next step. |
| Placement           | The metrics are spread out across the screen in a loose cluster, guiding the user from one to the next. {#isPasted} The **Finish** button is centered at the bottom so it's easy to reach.                                                                |
| Shape               | The metrics are inside Material flower shapes, making achievements stand out. The use of flower shapes make the design look consistent.                                                                                                                   |
| Size \& typography  | Key numbers like **18** and **3min** are larger and use emphasized styles, making it easy to scan the most important data.                                                                                                                                |
| Spacing \& grouping | Ample spacing around each metric avoids clutter, while making the layout tide any scannable. {#isPasted} Playful Material shapes serve as clear containers, making the grouping feel lively.                                                              |
Example 4: Check progress
-------------------------
The progress report is a tertiary goal in the app to track statistics and see progress over a monthly view. Since it's not a primary goal or hero moment, it uses more subtle design tactics to make the app usable and draw more attention.{#isPasted}

The key data is dark on a light background to draw attention, while the yellow shapes on the calendar highlight completed sessions.

Expressive components and elements used:

* [App bar](/google-material-3/pages/app-bars)
* [Progress indicator](/google-material-3/pages/progress-indicators/overview)
* [Medium button](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/overview)
* [Navigation bar](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-bar/overview)
* [Material shape library](/google-material-3/pages/shape/overview-principles#76fb0225-57af-4497-83d0-2b8827505fba)
* [Emphasized typography](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens#a91f1194-a47d-42d4-a9ae-301a3ce16ca4)
### Usability design tactics
|---------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Color \& contrast   | The data uses the **primary** role to be darker than all other elements and draw attention.{#isPasted} The yellow accents on the daily goal bar and calendar use the **secondary** roles to highlight progress. |
| Grouping \& spacing | The metrics (days, breaths, and minutes) are grouped together. The equal vertical spacing between elements makes it easy to scan data.                                                                          |
| Shape               | The rounded Material flower shape on the highlighted days in the calendar makes data more expressive. {#isPasted} The flower shapes remind the user of the breathing exercise visualization.                    |
| Size                | Custom-scaled numbers for key statistics such as days, breaths, and minutes are large enough to scan, but they don't dominate the screen.                                                                       |
Testing \& iteration improves the experience
--------------------------------------------
By testing the experience with users, it's easy to identify usability issues and address them. From version 1 to 2, the design shifts from cluttered to calm and simplified.
Version 1 loaded the screen with settings for duration, sound, and haptics all fighting for attention, creating unnecessary complexity. In Version 2, these options are neatly grouped as list items above the main action. The focus stays on starting the breathing session.{#isPasted}

By carefully using hierarchy, containment, shape, and color, the final design is easier to follow and more intuitive. Exploring different design refinements can result in an experience that feels intuitive and easy for the user to follow.
Best practices for applying usability design tactics
----------------------------------------------------
### Use clear scale \& placement

Avoid crowding the screen with too many large or equally prominent elements. Scale and placement create a clear focal point.
### Reinforce with consistent color roles

Use different color roles for actions and data to create a visual hierarchy that makes it simple for users to identify what they can do.
### Create calm, balanced layouts

Use uniform shapes and sizes. Add space between shapes and data to make it simple to compare data. Create gentle visual rhythm by aligning elements in a consistent flow to support a serene, focused experience.
### Guide with expressive motion and shape {#isPasted}

Use motion to guide the user. Avoid overanimating with too many elements moving at the same time.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
Key takeaways
-------------
* Emphasize key actions to create effective visual hierarchy{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Leverage [expressive design tactics](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/applying-expressive) to improve usability
* Don't overwhelm the user with too much visual information
* Test and iterate to validate designs
Usability helps create digital products that are easy to use and engaging. By leveraging M3 Expressive [design tactics](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/applying-expressive) like containment, size, shape, color, and typography, designers can guide users through experiences and emphasize key actions to create intuitive, usable products.
What is usability?
------------------
The [Nielsen Norman Group](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/usability-101-introduction-to-usability/) defines usability by how effectively users interact with a product through five aspects:
|    Aspect    |                                                 Definition                                                 |
|--------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Efficiency   | Users can efficiently complete tasks and goals                                                             |
| Errors       | Proper design reduces the likelihood of mistakes, and users can easily correct any errors that do occur    |
| Learnability | New users learn to use the product and complete tasks easily, even if it's the first time they're using it |
| Memorability | When users come back to a product, they remember how to use it                                             |
| Satisfaction | Users are satisfied with the designed experience                                                           |
### How is usability different from accessibility?
**Accessibility** focuses on making products accessible for people with disabilities. Accessible experiences are perceivable, operable, understandable and robust, and support people who use assistive technology. [More on accessibility](/google-material-3/pages/accessibility/principles)

**Usability** focuses on making products intuitive and easy to understand for everyone.
Usability design tactics
------------------------
There are many ways to design for usability; not all design tactics need to be combined at the same time. An effective combination of design tactics can: {#isPasted}

* Make a product easily understood, learnable, and memorable
* Help people quickly identify what do to next
* Minimize distractions to focus on the task

Start by creating a strong visual hierarchy to emphasize important information by using color, size, spacing, placement, containment, and other [tactics](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/applying-expressive). Then add unique emphasis to celebrate success or progress, by adding illustrations, scale, shapes, and shape morph.
### [Color](https://m3.material.io/styles/color/system/overview?utm_source=homepage&utm_medium=referral&utm_campaign=IO25) \& [color contrast](https://m3.material.io/foundations/designing/color-contrast) {#isPasted}

Use eye-catching primary and secondary colors to create hierarchy and emphasize key actions. Instead of similar colors, try using contrasting colors, like purple and green. Colors should always follow [basic accessibility guidelines](https://m3.material.io/foundations/designing/color-contrast). {#isPasted}

Tip: Material Design's dynamic [color roles](https://m3.material.io/styles/color/roles) automatically create color palettes with proper emphasis and accessible contrast ratios.

<br />
### Containment \& grouping content

Group related elements in subtle containers to make them easier to understand. Break content into manageable sections using containment, [spacing](https://m3.material.io/foundations/layout/understanding-layout/spacing), and headings.
### [Motion](https://m3.material.io/styles/motion/overview/how-it-works) {#isPasted}

Motion emphasizes key moments or unique experiences. However, use it sparingly since motion can be distracting.
### [Shape](https://m3.material.io/styles/shape/overview-principles#579dd4ba-39f3-4e60-bd9b-1d97ed6ef1bf) \& [shape morph](https://m3.material.io/styles/shape/shape-morph)

The [Material shape library](https://m3.material.io/styles/shape/overview-principles#579dd4ba-39f3-4e60-bd9b-1d97ed6ef1bf) has 35 shapes to apply to designs. Shapes are often used to mask imagery or fill empty space.{#isPasted}

Shape:

* Adds emphasis and delight
* Guides focus
* Differentiates containers, buttons and animations
* Signals interaction
* Sets emotional tone

Every shape can morph into another in the library. Shape morph is also applied when interacting with components like [buttons](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/specs#9dd7333b-741c-4098-b349-4e06846267e3). Shape morph:

* Communicates interaction states, like selected, tap, swipe, scroll, release, long press
* Emphasize actions in progress
### Size {#isPasted}

The size and scale of elements shows their level of importance. The most important action or the main call to action (CTA) should be the largest element.

Using larger sizes for key actions dramatically increases usability and makes products more efficient. Users are satisfied, they make fewer errors, and find the products to be more learnable.
### [Typography](https://m3.material.io/styles/typography/overview) {#isPasted}

Type can separate different hierarchies of information. More important information might use one font and less important or supplemental content might use another.

* The largest, most legible text on the screen could signal a primary action
* Smaller text signals secondary or tertiary action
* Group similar content by using the same font style
Design based on primary goals
-----------------------------
Primary goals are the main tasks to use a product successfully, like starting a process or making decisions, so they need the strongest emphasis. Secondary and tertiary goals add to the experience of the product but aren't required, like viewing statuses or settings. Identify primary, secondary, and tertiary goals by considering product needs and user satisfaction.{#isPasted}

To guide people to the primary goal while ensuring that other goals are still discoverable, try:

* Creating a strong visual hierarchy with size, color, and other design tactics
* Simplifying to one primary task on each page, leveraging empty space to focus attention
* Making core actions recognizable and easily reachable, like using large buttons for the most frequent actions
* Designing a harmonious experience that is aesthetically pleasing and easy to understand
* Not using too many expressive tactics at the same time as they can be distracting
Iterate
-------
Test, iterate, and gather feedback early and often from a range of users and contexts to validate design choices and minimize errors. User testing offers valuable insights into how users perceive and interact with the usability of the product experience.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# GM3 for Web
Web-specific resources, guidance, and required features

## Overview
Web-specific guidelines
-----------------------
* GM3 guidelines generally apply to both Android and the web{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Large screen guidelines are under **Adaptive design** or **Responsive design** for each component
* Specs and availability are only listed for [GM3 Wiz](https://gm-catalog.corp.google.com/material3), and may differ on Angular and ACX
* Useful guidelines for web:
  * [Expanded window size class](/google-material-3/pages/applying-layout/expanded): Layouts for screen widths larger than 840dp
  * [Canonical layouts](/google-material-3/pages/canonical-layouts/overview): Ready-to-use compositions that show how layouts adapt for common use cases and screen sizes
  * [Accessible keyboard shortcuts](/google-material-3/pages/accessibility/principles#711ae9c4-be12-49f6-887c-6a8ef630d8f9): Help users access menus and app functions without using a mouse
  * [Keyboard focus indicator](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/applying-states#f4f7268f-f732-4ed8-8b51-fea7b9976306): Ring-like indicator when web users navigate a page with a keyboard
Developer resources
-------------------
GM3 Wiz is made in-house by Material, and has the latest web implementation including GM3 Expressive. Angular Material and ACX are made by their respective teams.
{#isPasted}{#isPasted}

|             **Type**              |                                                     **Resource**                                                      |                                   **Contact**                                    |              **Status**              |
|-----------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **GM3 Wiz**                       | [Developer docs](http://go/gm3-wiz){#isPasted}                                                                        | [material-web-team@](mailto:material-web-team@google.com)                        | GM3 Expressive available             |
| **GM3 Wiz**                       | [Component catalog](http://go/gm3-web-catalog)                                                                        | [material-web-team@](mailto:material-web-team@google.com)                        | GM3 Expressive available             |
| **GM3 Wiz**                       | [Roadmap](http://go/gm3-web-roadmap)                                                                                  | [material-web-team@](mailto:material-web-team@google.com)                        | GM3 Expressive available             |
| **Angular Material**              | [Developer docs](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/javascript/angular2/components/g3doc/index.md?cl=head)                 | [angular-components-team@](https://yaqs.corp.google.com/team/acx-web-users)      | GM3 available                        |
| **ACX Web Components**            | [Component catalog](https://acx-gallery.googleplex.com/)                                                              | [YAQS](https://yaqs.corp.google.com/team/acx-web-users)                          | GM3 available                        |
| **ACX Web Components**            | [Developer docs](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ads/acx2/g3doc/index.md?cl=head)                                       | [YAQS](https://yaqs.corp.google.com/team/acx-web-users)                          | GM3 available                        |
| **Material Web Components (MWC)** | [Developer docs](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/third_party/javascript/material/web/g3doc/index.md?cl=head){#isPasted} | [material-web-components@](mailto:material-web-components@google.com){#isPasted} | **Maintenance mode.** GM3 available. |

{#isPasted}
GM3 Wiz
-------
[GM3 Wiz](http://go/gm3-wiz) is the latest web implementation of GM3 Expressive, built by Material.{#isPasted}

* GM3 baseline components are the standard for web. To ease migration, they're based on GM2 GMDC-Wiz components, with improved color mappings and accessibility features.
* [GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview) adds redesigned variants with more features and introduces new components.
### Migrating from GM2
* The **Theming API**, backed by cross-platform design tokens, is now required for styling instead of mixins. Mixins remain available and recommended for GM2.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Browser support no longer includes IE11. Reach out to [material-web-team@](mailto:material-web-team@google.com) for options if you need support for IE11.
### GM3 Expressive components
### GM3 components
The following GM3 Expressive components are available in GM3 Wiz:{#isPasted}

* [Banner](/google-material-3/pages/banner/tab-1)
* Buttons
  * [Extended FABs](/google-material-3/pages/extended-fab)
  * [Floating action buttons (FABs)](/google-material-3/pages/fab/overview)
  * [Icon buttons](/google-material-3/pages/icon-buttons/overview)
  * [Split buttons](/google-material-3/pages/split-buttons/overview)
* [Navigation rail](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-rail/overview)
* [Progress indicators](/google-material-3/pages/progress-indicators/overview)
* [Toolbars](/google-material-3/pages/toolbars/overview)
All other components in GM3 Wiz use the baseline GM3 variants, or have no expressive update. [View full component catalog](http://go/gm3-web-catalog)

* [Badges](/google-material-3/pages/badges/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Buttons
  * [Common buttons](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/overview)
  * [Segmented buttons](/google-material-3/pages/segmented-buttons/overview)
* [Cards](/google-material-3/pages/cards/overview)
* [Checkbox](/google-material-3/pages/checkbox/overview)
* [Chips](/google-material-3/pages/chips/overview)
* [Data tables](/google-material-3/pages/data-tables/overview)
* [Date pickers](/google-material-3/pages/date-pickers/overview)
* [Dialogs](/google-material-3/pages/dialogs/overview)
* [Lists](/google-material-3/pages/lists/overview) (expressive coming soon){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Menus](/google-material-3/pages/menus/overview) (expressive coming soon)
* Navigation
  * [Bar](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-bar/overview)
  * [Drawer](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-drawer/overview)
* [Radio button](/google-material-3/pages/radio-button/overview)
* [Sliders](/google-material-3/pages/sliders/overview)
* [Snackbar](/google-material-3/pages/snackbar/overview)
* [Switch](/google-material-3/pages/switch/overview)
* [Tabs](/google-material-3/pages/tabs/overview)
* [Text fields](/google-material-3/pages/text-fields/overview)
* [Tooltips](/google-material-3/pages/tooltips/overview)
Other implementations
---------------------
[**Angular Material**](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/javascript/angular2/components/g3doc/index.md?cl=head) is owned by the [Angular Components team](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/2159957392764?prev_event_id=RTGnaMmYEMKWpMYP99uqGQ&hq=type%253Apeople&q=angular). It supports GM3 styles using Material's design tokens and provides override APIs to easily customize component-level tokens. GM3 Expressive is not available. [Learn how to set up Angular Material's theming](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/javascript/angular2/components/g3doc/theming.md?cl=head)

[**ACX**](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ads/acx2/g3doc/components.md?cl=head) is owned by the [ACX Web team](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/11682182911?hq=type%253Apeople&q=acx). It contains components and styles built with Dart and Angular Dart. The site contains best practices, usage guidelines, built-in styles, colors, sass variables, components, and more. GM3 Expressive is not available. [View the ACX gallery](https://acx-gallery.googleplex.com/)

[**Material Web Components (MWC)**](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/third_party/javascript/material/web/g3doc/index.md?cl=head) is an external-facing web implementation owned by Material, but is **currently in maintenance mode.**MWC has custom HTML elements with encapsulated styles and behavior that work across many different frameworks (such as Lit, React, Vue, and Svelte) as well as web environments (such as Eleventy, Wordpress, and Ruby on Rails). GM3 Expressive is not available.
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Required features
*GM3 requirements for Web products have not changed since January 2023.*
Planning \& process
-------------------

1. Identify your team's cross-functional owners, including PM, PgM, UX, and Eng
2. Walk through the required features as a cross-functional group
3. Conduct an audit to determine which features apply to your product
4. Complete engineering prerequisites
5. Create a design proposal and set up a [Material UX Review](http://goto.google.com/gdp-ux-review)
6. Create an implementation roadmap with feature release milestones
7. Make it happen
{#isPasted}
Required features
-----------------
##### Color
> [Baseline color scheme](/google-material-3/pages/static/gm3-baseline)  
>
> [User generated dynamic color](/google-material-3/pages/dynamic/user-generated) (select teams)
> The static baseline (blue) scheme is the default color scheme for GM3.{#isPasted}
##### Typography
> [Type scale: styles and tokens](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens)  
>
> [Google Sans Text](/google-material-3/pages/typography/fonts)
##### Components
> [Buttons](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/overview){#isPasted}
> [FAB](/google-material-3/pages/fab/overview) + [Extended FAB](/google-material-3/pages/extended-fab/overview)
> [Dialogs](/google-material-3/pages/dialogs/overview)
> [Chips](/google-material-3/pages/chips/overview)
> [Lists](/google-material-3/pages/lists/overview)
> All GM3 components are available for 1P teams to adopt on the web. 1P teams are required to adopt this subset but are encouraged to adopt all components applicable to their product. Components not required in 2024 might be required in a future GDP release cycle. {#isPasted}

<br />
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Get started with GM3
For product teams, PMs, designers, and developers

## Overview
Using GM3 in your product
-------------------------
Google Material 3 (GM3) is Google's foundational design system. It provides the basis for all product-specific design systems.
* Check with your manager to understand when to use GM3 directly versus a product-specific system
* Always reference GM3 for Google-wide standards and UX updates
* Learn more about design systems at Google from [UI Academy](https://googleacademy.exceedlms.com/student/path/667345-welcome-to-ui-academy){#isPasted}
2024 feature requirements {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------

Requirements and timelines vary for each engineering platform, but are largely unchanged since January 2023. Check each platform for specific requirements:{#isPasted}

* [Android](/google-material-3/pages/android/overview) (new requirement in 2024: user-controlled contrast)
* [iOS](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-overview)
* [Web](/google-material-3/pages/web/overview)
GM3 Expressive {#isPasted}
--------------------------
In September 2024, Material released [GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/), a major update to GM3 which adds several new components, expressive features to existing components, variable font typography support using Google Sans Flex, an Expressive motion scheme, a new shape library, and updates the color scheme to be more vibrant.

[More about GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/)
What's available in GM3
-----------------------
### Using GM3

Learn how GM3 is applied to different platforms, how tokens work, and browse essential design resources like Design Kits.

* [Why use GM3?](/google-material-3/pages/why-gm3)
* [Design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens)
* Platforms: [Android](/google-material-3/pages/android), [iOS](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms), [Web](/google-material-3/pages/web)
* Resources:
  * [GM3 Figma Design Kits](/google-material-3/pages/resources/resources/latest#898a1474-672d-4964-a53b-a3aa9d5f8239)
  * [Material Theme Builder](/google-material-3/pages/resources/resources/latest#88f61c0c-f09f-48f4-bf98-4ea4583e0efa)
  * [Image assets](/google-material-3/pages/resources/resources/latest#f20500db-d209-4011-8391-a7f5ea2a35a7)
  * [Fonts \& icons](/google-material-3/pages/resources/resources/latest#efdbf000-8651-4c99-94d1-aa63f348341a)
  * [Learn about Figma and design at Google](/google-material-3/pages/resources/resources/latest#7651625b-79ed-4ebd-891a-3b828f0e82c7)
### Foundations

UX best practices and essential concepts that apply across Google products:

* [Accessibility](/google-material-3/pages/accessibility/overview)
* [Building for all](/google-material-3/pages/building-for-all)
* [Content design](/google-material-3/pages/overview)
* [Design for generative AI](/google-material-3/pages/ai/overview)
* [Googlewide prefabs](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview)
* [Interaction](/google-material-3/pages/gestures)
* [Layout](/google-material-3/pages/understanding-layout/overview) \& [Adaptive design](/google-material-3/pages/adaptive-design)
* [Usability](/google-material-3/pages/usability)
### Styles

Visual properties of GM3, including:

* [Color](/google-material-3/pages/color/system-overview)
* [Elevation](/google-material-3/pages/elevation/overview)
* [Icons](/google-material-3/pages/icons/overview)
* [Motion](/google-material-3/pages/motion)
* [Shape](/google-material-3/pages/shape/overview)
* [Typography](/google-material-3/pages/typography/overview)
### Components

Basic UI buildings blocks of GM3, including:

* [Buttons](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/overview)
* [Cards](/google-material-3/pages/cards/overview)
* [Chips](/google-material-3/pages/chips/overview)
* [Dialogs](/google-material-3/pages/dialogs/overview)

[See all components](/google-material-3/pages/components)
Support {#isPasted}
-------------------

[Find answers to specific questions or get in touch](/google-material-3/pages/resources/contact-help)
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Designers
Google designers should:

1. [Get set up in Figma](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-get-started/designers#f9be4d5d-686e-4bac-8f96-d140725d4bf3)
2. [Read and apply GM3 specs, guidelines, and tokens](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-get-started/designers#4cefad07-22ac-4081-8d2e-777ec54ff55b)
3. [Ask questions \& share ideas](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-get-started/designers#869d5ac0-5846-4893-9e7d-128a7e298390)
4. [Explore with Material UX](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-get-started/designers#e9bf7089-8e7b-448a-9849-1c96d3a12a43)
5. [Extend the system](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-get-started/designers#b6346ad3-14f6-4aec-87c8-97fa523a881d)
Get set up in Figma
-------------------
Get a [full Figma license](https://newstuff.corp.goog/google/en/Software/Other-Software/Figma-(Full-License)-and-Figjam-(View-or-Full-License)/p/S123123123) if you don't already have one. Check with your manager to understand [how GM3 relates to your product](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-get-started/overview) and if any product-specific design kits should be used with or instead of the GM3 kit.
### Add GM3 Design Kits and Google Symbols to a Figma file {#isPasted}

The [GM3 Android Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7){#isPasted} and [Google Symbols library](https://www.figma.com/design/6iUAbBfL2Taf0QHvgn679m/Google-Symbols-Library?node-id=79-0&p=f&t=q5hIN93dlLZX69HK-0){#isPasted} should be added to all Google Figma files by default, but if you don't see them in your Assets tab, or would like the [Web](https://www.figma.com/design/lcRw8MOK5FMHmNl820IMAo/GM3-Web-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7) or [iOS](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7) kits, here's how to get them:

1. In any corp file, select the "Assets" tab on the top left of the Layers panel{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Select the "Team Library" button (shaped like an open book)
3. In the "Libraries" search field, type "GM3 Design Kit". There are 3 kits:
   1. [GM3 Android Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7){#isPasted}
   {#isPasted}
   2. [GM3 Web Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/lcRw8MOK5FMHmNl820IMAo/GM3-Web-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7){#isPasted}
   {#isPasted}
   3. [GM3 iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7)
4. Click on the switch to the left of the GM3 Design Kit library to activate it
5. All assets are now available directly in your working file

Repeat the steps for the Google Symbols library.

To disable the design kit or symbols library in a file, follow the same directions but toggle the switch off.
Check the [Design Kit changelog](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1RKRyOXaxCgvg9yMs0VkKUYVMVznyc3xypOBkXs4n36g/edit?resourcekey=0-8R0HRTWxGu69pRCmBo2iUA#gid=0){#isPasted} to see the latest design kit updates, additions, and bug fixes. The GM3 design kits stay up-to-date with Material UX guidelines. Submit questions, requests, and bugs at [go/m3-dk-bug](https://b.corp.google.com/issues/new?component=1240212&template=1729813){#isPasted}.
### Inherit GM3 into your system's design kit

Learn how to use and extend the GM3 Design Kits: [go/adopt-gm3-kit](http://go/adopt-gm3-kit)
Read and apply GM3 specs, guidelines, and tokens
------------------------------------------------
Refer to GM3 guidelines, specs, components, and tokens when designing and building Google apps:

* Check the information on this site while designing
* Apply the information to designs using the [GM3 Design Kit](http://go/gm3-design-kit)
* Build the information in code using the platform libraries
Ask questions \& share ideas
----------------------------
Ask [Material Design questions in YAQs](https://yaqs.corp.google.com/eng/t/material-design?sort_by=recently_created&filter_by=show_all). Material has an on-call designer who monitors these channels and answers questions, often within 24 hours.

Share work-in-progress and discuss challenges in the [Material Expressive chat](https://chat.google.com/room/AAAAllmmM9E?cls=7).
Explore solutions \& get feedback from the Material team {#isPasted}
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Get feedback from the Material design team, ask questions, and learn about new features at Material UX reviews.

Sign up at [go/gdp-ux-review](http://goto.google.com/gdp-ux-review)
Extend the system when required {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------------
There will be cases when your product needs to extend the GM3 system, whether in specs, usage, or other ways. Such extensions are acceptable when done deliberately and with good reason (not sacrificing quality or long-term maintenance for convenience).
**Adjust parameters before building new components**{#isPasted}

Flexibility is often baked into component code, so first try adjusting the parameters of existing components before creating something new. For example, it's better to make tweaks to the height and width of existing navigation components rather than build similar-but-different ones from scratch.

Material UX will often endorse thoughtful adjustments.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

Explore solutions \& get feedback from the Material team {#isPasted}

Sign up at [go/gdp-ux-review](http://goto.google.com/gdp-ux-review)
