## Glossary
Adaptive design
---------------

A design approach in which the interface changes based on known user, device, or environmental conditions. Adaptive design in Material includes layout and component adaptations.
App bars {#isPasted}
--------------------

A component that displays information and actions at the top of a screen. [Learn more about app bars](/google-material-3/pages/app-bars/overview)
Banner
------

A component displaying a prominent message and related optional actions.
Button
------

A component helping people initiate actions such as sending an email, sharing a document, or liking a post. [Learn more about buttons](/google-material-3/pages/buttons-overview/)
Bottom sheet
------------

A component containing supplementary content that's anchored to the bottom of the screen.
Card
----

A component containing content and actions that relate information about a subject. [Learn more about cards](/google-material-3/pages/cards/overview/)
Checkbox
--------

A component allowing users to select one or more items from a set. Checkboxes can turn an option on or off.
Chip
----

A component for helping people enter information, make selections, filter content, or trigger actions. Chips can show multiple interactive elements together in the same area, such as a list of selectable movie times, or a series of email contacts. [Learn more about chips](/google-material-3/pages/chips/overview/)
Color: Baseline scheme {#isPasted}
----------------------------------

The **baseline scheme** refers to the group of selected tones that make up the default colors values used for light and dark themes.
Color: Dynamic color {#isPasted}
--------------------------------

A customization feature in which a user-generated color scheme is mapped too an app's color scheme.

Dynamic color is reflected in apps that accept user-generated schemes as inputs for color roles in an app. Dynamic color isn't simply the output of the Material Theme Builder, or an algorithmic color scheme, but is the presence of a changeable color role that renders an app's appearance dynamic in response to user input.
Color: Extended color {#isPasted}
---------------------------------

A color specified (in addition to key colors) in order to fill color roles for custom schemes. Extended color is a category for idiosyncratic color roles and applications, such as brand expression or conventional (semantic) meanings.
Color: Harmonized color {#isPasted}
-----------------------------------

A source color + an extended color = a harmonized color. To harmonize is to derive a new color that's compatible with tonal palettes and custom color schemes. Harmonization takes an extended color hue and a source color hue as the variables to synthesize.
Color: Key color {#isPasted}
----------------------------

A key color is not an extracted color--it's a derivation of the [source color](/google-material-3/pages/glossary/tab-1/latest#1f3b29f3-068a-4caa-b416-0eb2d2c7dee8). Key colors are a concept useful to understanding dynamic color, but are not used in code. The term describes any color that undergoes hue and chroma transformation of a source color. A key color is the basis for a tonal palette.
Color: Scheme {#isPasted}
-------------------------

Any mapping of color roles to specific tones from a tonal palette. Schemes consist of multiple color roles. A **dark scheme** informally describes a group of colors using the tones from a group of palettes that are mapped for a dark theme.

Note: A **light scheme** is not the same as a **light theme** . *See* [*theme*](/google-material-3/pages/glossary/tab-1/latest#ad1cd750-42c1-45ad-8359-2e8348350010)
Color: Source color {#isPasted}
-------------------------------

The single color that's extracted to define all five key colors is called a source color. While it may not be needed in code, it's a useful distinction for understanding that a dynamic color scheme has its root in one initiating color, AKA hue, chroma, and tone. **Key colors** are defined in relationship to the **source color's** *hue*.
Color: Tonal palette {#isPasted}
--------------------------------

A tonal palette comprises a 13-tone range that serves as the basis for mapping tones to specific roles. A tonal palette offers variations in tonality so that a color scheme automatically provides contrast and visual differentiation within any color group, such as primary.
Color: Tone {#isPasted}
-----------------------

A grouping of tones are colors with the same hue and chroma. Informally, tone means degrees of lightness.

**In code** : Tone.of(hex code).get(tone) or Tone.of(hue, chroma).get(tone)

**In design guidance**: "A change in a color's tone can be used to distinguish interaction states or visual interest to component elements."
Color: User-generated schemes {#isPasted}
-----------------------------------------

An aspect of dynamic color that describes the colors derived and applied from an individual user's wallpaper selection or Android preset colors.
Component {#isPasted}
---------------------

A distinct category of related Material Design variants used for a specific function.
Component: Configuration {#isPasted}
------------------------------------

Component options that produce ++minor++ changes in behavior or layout. Usually achieved by editing variant values, not using different APIs.
Component: Element {#isPasted}
------------------------------

Fixed, measurable component parts that contain attributes.
Component: States {#isPasted}
-----------------------------

Visually distinct component stages tied to interaction.
Component: Variant {#isPasted}
------------------------------

Component options that produce ++major++ changes in behavior or layout. Each variant usually has its own API and preset code out of the box.
Condition {#isPasted}
---------------------

A signal that determines when and how an adaptive layout or component should adapt.
Contrast {#isPasted}
--------------------

Difference between colors. For accessibility, contrast refers strictly to the difference in tone. A difference of 40 in tone guarantees a [WCAG](https://www.w3.org/WAI/standards-guidelines/wcag/) contrast ratio ≥ 3.0; a difference of 50 in tone guarantees a contrast ratio ≥ 4.5.
Customization {#isPasted}
-------------------------

A modification made to a UI reflecting an app, OEM, or individual user's visual preferences and brand. Customization can focus on individual UI elements (for example, styling the color of a component or adding optional actions to a template), or it can be implemented globally (for example, applying a set of styles as a theme across the UI).
Dark theme {#isPasted}
----------------------

A dark theme is a low-light UI that displays mostly dark surfaces.
Data table
----------

A component displaying sets of data across rows and columns.
Date picker
-----------

A component that lets users select a date, or a range of dates.
Design attribute {#isPasted}
----------------------------

The style aspect that a design token or hard-coded value applies to, such as color or font.
Design guidelines {#isPasted}
-----------------------------

Guidelines are descriptive written and illustrated docs that demonstrate usage and behavior mainly through examples. They're the long-form discussion of specs that help designers and developers with problem-solving and decision-making.
Design specs {#isPasted}
------------------------

Specs are annotated designs and docs that specify the values and parameters that define a component or a feature's coded capabilities.
Design System Package (DSP) {#isPasted}
---------------------------------------

An open-format folder structure created by Adobe to help teams share design system information across tools. [Learn more about the DSP format](https://github.com/AdobeXD/design-system-package-dsp)
Design tokens {#isPasted}
-------------------------

A design token represents a small, reusable design decision that's part of a design system's visual style. Tokens replace static values with self-explanatory names. [Learn more on the design tokens page](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Design tokens: Context {#isPasted}
----------------------------------

System-level changes that change how all tokens resolve, like dark theme or medium contrast
Design tokens: Role {#isPasted}
-------------------------------

A shortened version of the system token name (for example, **Secondary container color** ; **Headline 1**).
Design tokens: Types {#isPasted}
--------------------------------

* **Reference tokens** : All available tokenized values. For example, **md.ref.palette.secondary200.**
* **Component tokens**: Component tokens define the design attributes of elements in a component, such as the color of a button container.
* **System tokens** : The choices or roles that make the system, from color to typography, elevation, and shape. For example, **md.sys.color.secondary-container.**
Design tokens: Value {#isPasted}
--------------------------------

The information defining a design attribute, either stored in a token or hard-coded.
Dialog
------

A component providing important prompts in a user flow. They can require an action, communicate information, or help users accomplish a task. [Learn more about dialogs](/google-material-3/pages/dialogs/overview/)
Divider
-------

A thin line that groups content in lists and layouts.
Element {#isPasted}
-------------------

The part of the component that a token or value applies to, such as the container or label text.
Extended FAB {#isPasted}
------------------------

A component for helping people take primary actions. They're wider than FABs to accommodate a text label and larger target area. [Learn more about extended FABs](/google-material-3/pages/extended-fab/overview/)
Floating action button (FAB)
----------------------------

A component representing the most important action on a screen. They put key actions within reach. [Learn more about FABs](/google-material-3/pages/fab/overview/)
HCT {#isPasted}
---------------

HCT is an abbreviation of hue, chroma, tone. It's the name of the color space that enables dynamic color. HCT is based on CAM16 **hue** and **chroma** ; the L\* construct for luminance from L\*a\*b\* (CIELAB, 1976) is denoted as T for **tone**.
Image list {#isPasted}
----------------------

A component displaying a collection of images in an organized grid.
Libraries {#isPasted}
---------------------

Developer libraries supported by Material 3 include Android and Jetpack Compose, with in-progress support being developed for Flutter and Web.
List {#isPasted}
----------------

A component comprised of continuous, vertical indexes of text or images.
Material Components {#isPasted}
-------------------------------

Open-source UI elements created to help developers implement Material Design across Android, Flutter, and the web.
Material Design {#isPasted}
---------------------------

Material is an adaptable system of guidelines, components, and tools that support the best practices of user interface design. Backed by open-source code, Material streamlines collaboration between designers and developers, and helps teams quickly build beautiful products.

Material was launched in 2014 and has evolved over the years.

**Material Design 1** (M1): The first generation of Material Design, launched in 2014, is archived and accessible at: material.io/archive/guidelines.{#isPasted}

**Material Design 2** (M2): The second generation of Material Design, launched in 2018, introduced Material Theming and code for Material Components.

**Material Design 3** (M3): The third generation of Material, launched in 2021, includes the Material You features like dynamic color.

* **Material You**: The new Material visual style and set of features embracing the personal and expressive needs of individual users, part of Material Design 3.

**Google Material** (GM2, GM3): Google Material represents a set of styles and decisions based on Material Components and style systems. Google product teams should refer to GM2 and GM3 for product design, rather than the external site (Material.io) which is designed for third-parties.
Material Theming {#isPasted}
----------------------------

The ability to systematically customize Material Design to better reflect your product's brand.

While a scheme represents selections or a subset of a single style group, **theme** describes a set of multiple styles and attributes in combination. Themes adjust global styles to adjust for a given user context or preference, such as low light or high contrast. For example, **dark theme** describes design decisions beyond color since adjustments to elevation and state are also integral to expressing a dark UI effectively.
Material You {#isPasted}
------------------------

*See* [*Material Design*](/google-material-3/pages/glossary/tab-1/latest#cf8f496e-e546-4843-91a5-30480398b6b6)
Menu {#isPasted}
----------------

A component that displays a list of choices on a temporary surface.
Mode {#isPasted}
----------------

Binary setting provided by a system to help people use the device better. Examples: **Focus mode** , **A** **irplane mode** , and **B** **attery Saver mode**.
Navigation bar {#isPasted}
--------------------------

A component offering a persistent and convenient way to switch between primary destinations in an app. [Learn more about navigation bars](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-bar/overview/)

<br />
Navigation drawer {#isPasted}
-----------------------------

A component providing ergonomic access to destinations in an app. [Learn more about navigation drawers](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-drawer/)
Navigation rail {#isPasted}
---------------------------

A component providing access to primary destinations in apps when using tablet and desktop screens. [Learn more about navigation rails](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-rail/overview/)
Orbiter {#isPasted}
-------------------

Floating UI elements that control the content within spatial panels.
Pane {#isPasted}
----------------

The building blocks of a layout. Content and actions are grouped into panes, which adapt the content to best fit the screen.
Progress indicator {#isPasted}
------------------------------

A component displaying an unspecified wait time or the length of a process.
Radio button {#isPasted}
------------------------

A component allowing users to select one option from a set.
Role {#isPasted}
----------------

Short nickname describing the purpose of a design token within a design system. Also known as slots. Examples: **On surface** ; **Body 1**.
Side sheet {#isPasted}
----------------------

A component containing supplementary content that's anchored to the left or right edge of the screen.
Slider {#isPasted}
------------------

A component that allows users to make selections from a range of values.
Snackbar {#isPasted}
--------------------

A component that provides brief messages about app processes at the bottom of the screen.
Spatial {#isPasted}
-------------------

Relates to the placement of UI in space through extended reality (XR).
Style {#isPasted}
-----------------

One or more properties, typically customizable, that define an aspect of a UI's appearance, such as color, typography, or shape.
Switch {#isPasted}
------------------

A component that toggles the state of a single item on or off.
Tab {#isPasted}
---------------

A component used to organize content across different screens, data sets, and other interactions.
Text field {#isPasted}
----------------------

A component that lets users enter and edit text.
Theme {#isPasted}
-----------------

A set of styles, such as color, elevation, and type, that can be applied globally to an app's UI components as a way to consistently adjust aspects of appearance in an app.
Time picker {#isPasted}
-----------------------

A component that helps users select and set a specific time.
Toolbars
--------

A component displaying navigation and key actions at the bottom of mobile screens.
Tooltip {#isPasted}
-------------------

A component that displays informative text when users hover over, focus on, or tap an element.
Widget {#isPasted}
------------------

Widgets are small gadgets or controls for Android applications placed on the home screen. [Learn more about widgets](/google-material-3/pages/accessibility/patterns#6e9515a7-8ac9-4dc9-b809-5bd5413801f6)
XR {#isPasted}
--------------

Extended reality includes UI viewed in virtual reality or in passthrough blended with the physical environment.

## Contact & help
Join the [Material Expressive chat space](https://chat.google.com/room/AAAAllmmM9E?cls=7) to talk about GM3 and receive occasional updates.

Join the [Design System Guild](https://chat.google.com/room/AAAAh85wf20?cls=7) chat space to talk all about making and inheriting design systems.

Have a burning question about GM3? Try this first:
Search GM3
----------

Search the GM3 site using the search bar at the top of the screen. Search across other design systems if you can't find what you need.

Still stuck? Try [a Moma search](https://moma.corp.google.com/).
Ask a question on YAQS
----------------------

Ask a question about GM3 using the #material-design tag.
File a bug or request a feature {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------------

* [Guideline bug](http://go/gm3-bug) -- typo, missing guidance{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Design system bug](https://b.corp.google.com/issues/new?component=740637&template=1346547) -- incorrect color role, incorrect component behavior{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Feature implementation request](https://forms.gle/SFrh7equBBLq7rGx5) -- request a new component implementation or feature{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Accessibility bug](http://goto.google.com/material-a11y-dependencies) - GAR or accessibility issue{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
Get UX feedback {#isPasted}
---------------------------

Share work and get feedback from the Material UX team either in the [Material Expressive](https://chat.google.com/room/AAAAllmmM9E?cls=7) chat or by signing up for a Material UX Review at [go/gdp-ux-review](http://goto.google.com/gdp-ux-review).
Get high-touch help {#isPasted}
-------------------------------

Email [gdp-ux-engagement@google.com](mailto:mailtto:gdp-ux-engagement@google.com) to get help planning your team's adoption of GM3.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQs](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Design tokens
Design tokens are the building blocks of all UI elements. The same tokens are used in designs, tools, and code.

* Tokens point to style values like colors, fonts, and measurements{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Use design tokens instead of hardcoded values
* Each token is named for how or where it's used (for example, **md.fab.container.color** sets the container color for a FAB)
* Even if a token's end value is changed, its name and use remain the same
* Material Design has three classes of tokens: [reference](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/tokens#703bdf18-f18c-42ec-acc0-e35727d22bdc), [system](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/tokens#fecfb17b-869b-498d-9032-93556cd65141), and [component](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/tokens#baa0add3-1001-401d-9fd7-4885c5ca5ff8)
Resources {#isPasted}
---------------------

|                                                                    Type                                                                     |                            Link                            |  Status   |
|                                                                   Design                                                                    | [GM3 Android Design Kit](http://go/gm3-design-kit) (Figma) | Available |
|                                                                   Design                                                                    |
|                                                                   Design                                                                    |
|                                                                   Design                                                                    |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|-----------|
| [GM3 iOS Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7){#isPasted} (Figma) | Available                                                  |
| [GM3 Web Design Kit](https://www.figma.com/design/lcRw8MOK5FMHmNl820IMAo/GM3-Web-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7){#isPasted} (Figma) | Available                                                  |
| [Material Theme Builder Figma plugin](http://go/material-theme-builder-1p)                                                                  | Available                                                  |

{#isPasted}
What's a design token? {#isPasted}
----------------------------------
Design tokens are small, reusable design decisions that make up a design system's visual style. Tokens replace static values with self-explanatory names.{#isPasted}

A design token consists of 2 things:

1. A code-like name, such as **md.ref.palette.secondary200**

2. An associated value, such as **#ECB07E**

The token's value can be one of several things: A color, typeface, measurement, or even another token.
Design tokens meaningfully connect style choices that would otherwise lack a clear relationship.{#isPasted}

For example, if a designer's mock-ups and an engineer's implementation both reference the same token for the "secondary container color," then they can be confident that the same color is being used in both places. This applies even if the hex value assigned to that token gets updated.
Why are tokens important? {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
Tokens make it possible for a design system to have a single source of truth -- a repository where style choices are recorded and changes can be tracked.  

Because tokens are reusable and purpose-driven, they can define system-wide updates to themes and contexts. For example, you can use tokens to systematically apply a high-contrast color palette for improved visibility, or change the typographic scale to ensure that text is legible on a TV screen.  

By using tokens for design and implementation, style updates propagate consistently through an entire product or suite of products. They also help designers and engineers "speak the same language," reducing confusion during handoff from design to implementation.
Deciding if tokens are right for you {#isPasted}
------------------------------------------------
**Tokens will be most helpful if:**{#isPasted}

* You plan to update the design of your product or are building a product from scratch
* Your design system is applied across a suite of products or platforms
* You want to make it easy to maintain or update styles in the future
* You want to get the most out of the Material Design system, including features like dynamic color
**Tokens will be less helpful if:**{#isPasted}

* You have an existing app using hard-coded values that is unlikely to change in the next year or two
* Your product does not have a design system
Tokens and Material {#isPasted}
-------------------------------
In the past, Material styles were communicated through guidelines, design files, tools, and platform-specific component libraries.  

With design tokens, you can now download, customize, and apply Material styles and integrate them across your design and development process.  

Tokens allow decisions to be documented in a platform-agnostic and shareable format.
On this site, you'll see tokens listed in interactive modules.  

These modules let you quickly look up the default baseline value stored by tokens for color, font, font size, font weight, etc. They also show the relationship between a role, its system token, reference token, and stored pre-set value.
You'll also see tokens in the specs tabs of component articles.  

Tokens are first grouped by **state** (enabled, disabled, hover, etc) and then by **element** , which is the part of the component that a token or value applies to, such as the container or label text.  

Columns include:

* **Name**-- The component style aspect that the token applies to, such as color or font
* **Token ID** -- The token defining the component style aspect
* **Description** -- Optional descriptive info
* **Context/value** -- The value stored in the token for a given context
### How to use token modules {#isPasted}

Let's say you need to verify the color role for a filled button's label text.{#isPasted}

Navigate to Common buttons \> Specs, find the token module for filled buttons, and search for the "label text" tokens under elements.

Copy the color token and paste it in code, or compare it to the color role in Figma.
Parts of a token name {#isPasted}
---------------------------------
The parts of a token name are separated by periods and proceed from the most general information ("md") to the most specific ("on-secondary").

1. All token names in a design system start with the system name (such as "md" for Material Design)
2. An abbreviation for the token class: "ref" for reference tokens, "sys" for system tokens, and "comp" for component tokens
3. The token ends with descriptive words communicating the token's role
Classes of tokens {#isPasted}
-----------------------------
There are three classes of tokens in Material:{#isPasted}

1. Reference tokens  
   All available tokens with associated values.  

   {#isPasted}
2. System tokens  
   Decisions and roles that give the design system its character, from color and typography, to elevation and shape.  

3. Component tokens  
   The design properties assigned to elements in a component, such as the color of a button icon.

With three classes of tokens, teams can update design decisions globally or apply a change to a single component.
### Reference tokens {#isPasted}

These tokens make up all of the style options available in a design system.{#isPasted}

They usually point to a static value -- such as a color hex code or font size -- but can also point to other reference tokens. Reference tokens don't change based on context.

By providing a list of options, reference tokens give your team a starting point of approved colors, typography, measurements, etc.

All reference tokens start with the prefix **ref**.
### System tokens {#isPasted}

These are the decisions that systematize the design language for a specific theme or context.

System tokens define the purpose a reference token serves in the UI.

This is where theming occurs. The system token can point to different reference tokens depending on the context, such as a light or dark theme.

Whenever possible, system tokens should point to reference tokens rather than static values.

All system tokens start with the prefix **sys**.
### Component tokens {#isPasted}

These represent the elements required to compose a component, such as containers, label text, icons, states, and their values such as size, shape, color, or elevation.{#isPasted}

Whenever possible, component tokens should point to a system or reference token, and not contain hardcoded values such as hex codes.

Not every stylistic choice of a component will be able to be expressed as a token, but whenever a design choice applies to multiple components of similar intent, a token should be used.

All component tokens start with the prefix **comp**.
Contexts {#isPasted}
--------------------
Tokens can point to different values depending on a set of conditions. These conditions are called contexts and their resulting values are called contextual values.  

Examples of different contexts include: device form factors, dark theme, dense layouts, and right-to-left writing systems.  

You can think of a context as a kind of tag. If a token value is tagged with dark theme then it will override the default token value in a dark theme context.

# Why use GM3?
Build a Google product your users will love

##### GM3 has been shown to produce desirable and accessible products, support a seamless cross-product experience, and increase team velocity.
Make your product desirable and accessible
------------------------------------------
* Add user-coveted features like personalization, expressive components, and new color algorithms
* Make your product accessible-by-default using GM3 styles and components (which exceed GAR 2024 requirements)

{#isPasted}

[**Learn how personalization drives desirability**](http://go/gdp-increased-intent-to-buy)
Tap into the Google ecosystem
-----------------------------
* Build loyalty and user retention by using the same styles, components, and patterns as the top 15 Google apps {#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* The more Google products join this consistent ecosystem, the greater their value to users{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}

[**Learn about the effort to make Google UX expressive, consistent, cohesive, and comfortable**](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/e/2PACX-1vRkopeS04NSyRQMefq0k66Y_khKfQlw-3yaNmyYziyeyQvTB6JlX7nEajMgq-I0W2zXdw99BuXbxxHA/pub?start=true&loop=false&delayms=60000&slide=id.p)
Increase velocity
-----------------
* Don't make your team reinvent the wheel. Get customizable designs and code for experiences across Android, iOS, and the web.
* Use the latest available designs with the **GM3 Figma Design Kits** for [Android](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7), [iOS](https://www.figma.com/design/7JhYjge17BBMKzTtvb0ZFx/GM3-iOS-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7), and [Web](https://www.figma.com/design/lcRw8MOK5FMHmNl820IMAo/GM3-Web-Design-Kit?m=auto&t=ltyzO8OSvz2sKVSf-7)
* Reduce tech debt and maintenance costs by using design tokens in Figma and code

[**Learn how GM3 tokens saved Geo 2,000 SWE hours**](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/e/2PACX-1vRp5yN2LafRHFsT3TyRTm8BttL_d8wBF5wYVXf0Q80E5-u3vORubvl_UBWgoIztJVp960U0cPNlLo7G/pub?start=true&loop=false&delayms=60000){#isPasted}
Learn more
----------
* Email [gdp-ux-engagement@google.com](mailto:mailtto:gdp-ux-engagement@google.com){#isPasted} to get help planning your team's adoption of GM3
* Add yourself to the [Material Expression chat](https://chat.google.com/room/AAAAllmmM9E?cls=7)
* Subscribe to [weekly updates](https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/gm-discuss), and [monthly newsletters](https://groups.google.com/a/google.com/g/material-announce)

<br />

[**Or just get started**](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-get-started/overview)

## Specs
Types
-----

# Google Material 3
GM3 is the latest version of Google's design system

##### **The Material 3 Expressive update has arrived!** New components, exciting motion, variable fonts, and vibrant color schemes, all in one package. [++Check out the GM3 Expressive overview for details++](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/)
##### Google Material 3 (GM3) is your source for foundational UX concepts, styles, components, and Google-wide patterns. You can directly use GM3 designs and code, or use guideline inheritance to extend GM3 into a design system for your PA or product.
Designed for consumer experiences
---------------------------------

GM3 is built for consumer experiences---products like Gmail or Docs that are used by individuals. Expressive and personalization features like dynamic color inspire emotion and give individuals the creative freedom to customize their apps.{#isPasted}

Enterprise teams can use GM3 as a starting point, inheriting style tokens and components to maintain consistency, while extending the design system to meet product-specific needs.
Built for Android, iOS, and web
-------------------------------

GM3 supports products that span multiple platforms, tailoring components and features to meet user expectations. Not all components and features are available on all platforms.

Check the platform pages for specific requirements:

* [Android](/google-material-3/pages/android/overview)
* [iOS](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-overview)
* [Web](/google-material-3/pages/web/overview)
Google Material 3 is created and maintained by the [Google Design Platform](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/google-design-platform/home?utm_source=GM3&utm_medium=homepage-footer) team
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Alt text
Effective alt text describes the meaning and context of images for screen reader users

How to write great alt text
---------------------------
### Overview

Alternative text (alt text) refers to off-screen text that is used by screen reader accessibility software. Screen readers read out visible content such as paragraph and button text, as well as hidden content, including alt text for icons and headings.{#isPasted}

Alt text also displays when an image doesn't load. It tells people what they need to know about an image if they can't see it.
### When to use alt text

If you remove the image from the page and no information is lost, then the image is decorative and doesn't need alt text or a caption.{#isPasted}

An image can be marked decorative using a null alt attribute, such as **alt=""** in HTML, which will hide the content from screen reader users.
### Focus on the meaning of the image {#isPasted}

Describe the context and overall meaning, rather than focusing on the details. For example, in a shopping app, focus on the item for sale rather than the things around it.

Alt text can also help improve SEO, but its primary purpose should be to make sites usable for all.
### Keep it short {#isPasted}

The recommended length for alt text at Google is 125 characters.

If alt text is too long, it may be cut off by some screen readers, which is a poor user experience.
### Don't start alt text with "image of"

Screen readers announce "image" when they come across an image. If your start your alt text with "image of" a screen reader will announce "image, image of."
Context and nearby text
-----------------------
### Context matters {#isPasted}

Use adjacent text to establish the context and word choices for alt text. Adjacent text includes all of the text near an image, such as body text, captions, and headlines. {#isPasted}

Don't repeat the caption in alt text. It's against best practices because the user will hear the same text twice, which is a poor experience.

Be consistent in your word choices. For example, don't use "antique" in a caption and "vintage" in the alt text.
### Alt text is subjective

Alt text is another layer of creative expression and should support the goals and style of its context.{#isPasted}

The same image should have different alt text in different settings. Use the information in the app or article and the caption to decide what to emphasize in the alt text.
### Captions should benefit all users

A caption should be useful to someone who can see the image clearly, as well as a screen reader user. {#isPasted}

Good captions support the image rather than duplicating its information. A well-written caption makes it easier to write alt text.
Types of imagery
----------------
### When to describe the type of image

Occasionally it benefits users to name the type of image. This can include:{#isPasted}

* Chart
* Infographic
* Map
* Graph
* Screenshot
* Headshot
* Diagram
### Charts and graphs {#isPasted}

Alt text is particularly important for visualizations such as charts and graphs.   

Visualizations can either be editorial and meant to support a specific purpose or key takeaway, or more open-ended and used for general data analysis.  

Consider the core purpose of the chart or graph, and what information someone would need to use it.  

Link to the data that generated the chart or graph, if it's available.
When possible, explain the key takeaways and meaning in context, rather than detailing every data point.  

A general formula for chart alt text would be: "Summary of \[data type\] + \[reason for showing the chart\]."
#### Charts for editorial use

Charts with an editorial focus are used to support a key takeaway. This takeaway and the main data points confirming it should be the focus of the alt text. To create the alt text, leverage existing content from the body text, labels, and any text in the graph.  

For example, a chart from a weekly digest showing highlights of physical activity may have a written summary of the total change in step count. This can be used in the alt text.
#### Charts for analysis {#isPasted}

Charts for analysis are meant to be reviewed as a whole, and offer pieces of data to be analyzed. They can be dense, appear alongside related charts, and have many takeaways.   

For charts that have one or two key takeaways, include these insights in the alt text.   

To avoid introducing unintentional bias in charts with many possible takeaways, highlight opportunities for exploration, such as a link to the data's source. Emphasize key data points or mention that there isn't a single takeaway.
#### Use interactive charts {#isPasted}

To improve the screen reader experience, consider using interactive charts instead of static images, especially for complex visualizations that are used for analysis. For interactive charts, a tooltip can show additional detail for specific data points.  

See [Top Tips for Data Accessibility](https://m3.material.io/blog/data-visualization-accessibility) for more ways to make these visualizations accessible.
### Video and motion alt text

As with still images, if the animated information isn't elsewhere on the screen, then it is informative and needs alt text.{#isPasted}

Long-form video content uses video description to narrate the visual elements of a video, so it doesn't need alt text.
Alt text on informative GIFs or motion assets should highlight the important points. Think of a heading or title you might give it, and that's likely your alt text.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Radio button
Radio buttons let people select one option from a set of options

## Overview
* Use radio buttons (not switches) when only one item can be selected from a list{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Label should be scannable
* Selected items are more prominent than unselected items
Availability \& resources {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
What's new {#isPasted}
----------------------
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color

  <br />
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/next-bug)

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
Radio buttons are the recommended way to allow users to make a single selection from a list of options. {#isPasted}

Only one radio button can be selected at a time.
Use radio buttons to:{#isPasted}

* Select a single option from a set
* Expose all available options
Avoid nesting radio buttons or using radio buttons to select multiple options.
### Alternate selection controls

Radio buttons are one of several selection controls, which allow people to make choices such as selecting options or switching settings on or off.{#isPasted}

Switches and checkboxes are alternative selection controls that can be used to change settings or preferences.
Use radio buttons when there are five or fewer options.{#isPasted}

Consider using a drop-down menu instead of radio buttons when it's important to save space on a screen. However, drop-down menus require additional steps for a person, both in the number of clicks and cognitive effort.
Anatomy
-------
### Adjacent label text

Always pair radio buttons with an adjacent label describing what the radio button selects.{#isPasted}

Because only one radio button can be selected at a time, each choice must have its own label.
Placement
---------
Radio buttons are often arranged in stacked layouts.
Radio buttons should be vertically listed and have one option always selected.{#isPasted}

<br />
Behavior
--------
A radio button is successfully selected when a person clicks or taps either the radio button icon or the label.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
Tokens \& specs
---------------

[Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview){#isPasted}
Color {#isPasted}
-----------------

Color values are implemented through design tokens. For design, this means working with color values that correspond with tokens. For implementation, a color value will be a token that references a value. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview/)
### Adjacent text label color

Use the color role **on surface** for adjacent text labels. This remains the same even if interacting with the label or component.
States
------

States are visual representations used to communicate the status of a component or interactive element. [Learn more about interaction states](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states)
[State specs are in the token module above](/google-material-3/pages/radio-buttons/specs/latest#834bf282-e961-4290-8b8d-8e8728f424b9)
Measurements
------------
|    Attribute     | Value |
|    Icon size     | 20dp  |
| State layer size | 40dp  |
|   Target size    | 48dp  |
|------------------|-------|
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Accessibility
Use cases {#isPasted}
---------------------

People should be able to do the following with assistive technology:

* Navigate to a radio button
* Select a radio button
* Get appropriate feedback based on input type
Interaction \& style {#isPasted}
--------------------------------

A radio button can be either selected or unselected. Selecting one radio button deselects any others. A radio group can start with one radio button selected, or none selected.{#isPasted}

Once a radio button is selected, the group can't be deselected. To let people opt out of their selection, either provide a **Not applicable** or **No option** radio button, or provide a separate way to deselect all radio buttons, like **Clear selection**.

People should be able to select either the text label or the radio button to select an option.
### Avoid applying density by default {#isPasted}

Don't apply density to radio buttons by default. This lowers their targets below Material's recommendation of 48x48 CSS pixels. Instead, give people a way to choose a higher density, like selecting a denser layout or changing the theme.{#isPasted}

To ensure this density setting can be easily reverted when it's active, keep all targets to change it at a minimum of 48x48 CSS pixels each.
Initial focus
-------------
When outside the radio group, **Tab** moves focus directly to the selected radio button, or the first one if none are selected. {#isPasted}

**Shift+Tab** instead focuses on the last radio if none are selected.

Use the **arrows** to navigate between options.
Keyboard navigation {#isPasted}
-------------------------------
|        Keys         |                                                            Actions                                                             |
|       **Tab**       |                   Moves focus into the group to the selected radio button, or the first if none are selected                   |
| **Shift** + **Tab** |                   Moves focus into the group to the selected radio button, or the last if none are selected                    |
|       Arrows        | Moves focus and selects the previous or next radio button. Wraps focus and selection between the first and last radio buttons. |
|      **Space**      |                               Selects a focused radio button. If already selected, does nothing.                               |
|---------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|

{#isPasted}
Labeling elements {#isPasted}
-----------------------------
If the UI text is correctly linked to the radio button, assistive tech such as a screenreader will read the UI text, followed by the component's role.

The accessibility label for a group of radio buttons is typically the same as its title. The role is **R** **adio group**.
The accessibility label for an individual radio button is typically the same as its adjacent text label.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## iOS
By default, Material guidance applies to Android and the web. This tab covers adjustments needed for iOS. [Apple's Human Interface Guidelines](https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines) include additional iOS best practices and platform considerations
Not supported on iOS
--------------------
This component is not supported on iOS. Consider using a [list with checkmarks](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/components/Lists/cell-catalog.md?cl=head&polyglot=objective-c#checkmark-cells).
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs){#isPasted}  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Shape
The GM3 shape system includes original shapes, a corner radius scale, and built-in shape morphing

## Shape morph
The Material shape library supports easy transitioning, or morphing, between shapes. Shape morph is leveraged in the standard button group and loading indicator components.
Using shape morph {#isPasted}
-----------------------------
Access to the Material shape library and the shape morph functionality are available through platform-specific APIs.  
{#isPasted}

* For Android, use the [Shapes-Lib API](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/styles/shape.md?cl=head#material-shapes-expressive-update){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* For iOS, use the [Transformable Shape API](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/foundations/styles/Shape/engineering.md#shape-morphing){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Wiz is not currently available{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}

Shape morphing uses the expressive motion scheme by default. This can be switched to the standard motion scheme as needed.
GM3 uses shape morphing in the standard button group and loading indicator components.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview & principles
* Use abstract shapes thoughtfully to add emphasis and decorative flair
* Leverage GM3 shapes for built-in shape morphing
* Rectangular shapes are fully rounded in all corners by default
* Individual corners can be adjusted to create asymmetrical rectangular shapes
Availability \& resources
-------------------------
{#isPasted}

|      Type      |                                                                                      Resource                                                                                       |        Contact         |  Status   |
|----------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| Design         | [Shape library](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit?m=auto&node-id=229457-13765&t=Ks0gtfe55m4RoEfR-1){#isPasted} (Figma)                             | gm-design-kit@         | Available |
| Implementation | [Jetpack Compose](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/styles/shape.md?cl=head#material-shapes-expressive-update) (Shape Library) | material-compose-team@ | Available |
| Implementation | [Android Views](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/g3doc/components/shape.md?cl=head)                                                         | material-android-team@ | Available |
| Implementation | [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/foundations/styles/Shape/index.md?cl=head&polyglot=swiftui)                                                        | material-ios-team@     | Available |
GM3 Expressive update
---------------------
Added 35 **new shapes** and **shape morphing** to the Material Shape Library ([Figma](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit?m=auto&node-id=256804-1594&t=CxEbZZrnJEbP77UD-1)) and Android ([Views](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit?node-id=256804-1594) and [Compose](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/compose/g3doc/styles/shape.md?cl=head#material-shapes-expressive-update)), with 15 shapes made for [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/foundations/styles/Shape/index.md?cl=head&polyglot=swiftui).{#isPasted}

Added new shape principles and a refreshed art direction.

Added corner radii tokens:

* Large increased (20dp)
* Extra large increased (32dp)
* Extra extra large (48dp)
* Updated fully rounded corners to use **full**. Previously, this was defined using 50% of the component size.

[More on GM3 Expressive](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview/f1478e55-a3ea-4cb1-87e1-c3433e2760aa?edit=true)
Shape library
-------------
15 shapes were implemented to work on iOS. More shapes may become available in the future.
Use shapes and text in harmony
------------------------------

Shapes are expressive elements of Material 3 that echo key visual attributes of [Google Sans Flex](/google-material-3/pages/typography/fonts#2310cfc5-7b15-4d0f-b999-9d88c01f6829). {#isPasted}

Use these together for products to feel cohesive and polished.
Morph shapes to connect function and feeling
--------------------------------------------

Shapes should morph to improve understanding and add moments of delight. Use shape morph to better communicate:{#isPasted}

* Interaction states, like when a button is selected
* Actions in progress, like a friend typing, or a page loading
* Changes in the environment, like sound, temperature, or time of day

Think about how shapes could react to different interactions, such as tapping, swiping, scrolling, releasing, and long pressing.
Be bold and dare to embrace tension
-----------------------------------

Tension happens when the shape story changes unexpectedly, such as when contrasting shapes are used. This can be created using both square and rounded shapes, unconventional shapes, and other contrasting elements.{#isPasted}

Material historically focused on rounded shapes. However, using sharp shapes, thereby adding tension, creates more dynamic design, one that's more memorable and expressive.

This tension can be used in many ways, like conveying states, drawing attention to an element, or to improve the visual aesthetic.
Shape is versatile, not semantic
--------------------------------

Avoid making shapes literal or assigning a specific function or meaning to a single shape.{#isPasted}

For example, the loading indicator can be wavy, but the waveform is not a strict symbol of progression. Progress could just as easily be shown using rotating shapes or shape morph.

Plus, waveforms could be used in other places unrelated to progress, like button containers.
Use abstract shapes sparingly
-----------------------------

Be intentional when using shapes in product UI. Don't compromise clarity for the sake of visual design.{#isPasted}

When incorporating diverse shapes, think about how they fit into the overall design and consider how they balance with the entire composition. Ensure that shapes resonate with the product's narrative. Consider the 'why' behind their inclusion and the value they contribute to the overall user experience.

<br />
Emphasize aesthetic moments with shape
--------------------------------------

Get creative when using shape in graphics, for photography cropping, personalized avatar masking, and other non-interactive elements.{#isPasted}

Decorative moments offer the most flexible and creative uses of shape.
Shape can be 2.5D
-----------------

When effectively used, shape and motion can make 2D visuals feel 3D. They provide the illusion of depth and volume, making visuals more eye-catching and natural.
YAQS
----
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)[](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Corner radius scale
Material components use a corner radius scale to define all rectangular shapes, such as buttons, carousels, and dialogs.
Shape tokens {#isPasted}
------------------------

Material has shape corner tokens to define all corners, and corner-value tokens for individual corners. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview){#isPasted}
### Corner radius scale
The Material 3 shape system uses a size-based scale with ten styles. Styles are assigned to components based on the desired amount of roundedness. {#isPasted}

1. None - 0dp
2. Extra small - 4dp
3. Small - 8dp
4. Medium - 12dp
5. Large - 16dp
6. Large increased - 20dp
7. Extra large - 28dp
8. Extra large increased - 32dp
9. Extra extra large - 48dp
10. Full - fully rounded corners

[Apply shape styles using tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens)
Symmetry
--------
Components can have either symmetric or asymmetric corner shapes. Symmetric shapes have the same values for all corners, while asymmetric shapes can have corners with different values.{#isPasted}

Both symmetric and asymmetric shapes use the same 10-step scale.

Asymmetrical shapes are used in GM3 components with closely-grouped items, such as menus and split buttons. These are called **inner corners**.
Customizing shapes {#isPasted}
------------------------------
Generally, products should consistently use the Material 3 shape styles. However, customization is sometimes necessary, and even encouraged, for hero moments or custom components. Shapes can be customized at the **style** or **component** level.
### Style changes {#isPasted}

The corner radius shape style, like **medium**, can be customized to be a different size.

This applies the change to all components mapped to that shape style, unless they have an override.
### Component changes {#isPasted}

The style of a specific component, such as a button, can be changed by customizing which corner radius shape style it maps to.

For example, by default, buttons are mapped to the **full** corner radius shape style. If your product needs a less rounded shape, remap the token to another style in the shape scale, such as **small** or **medium**.
### Adjust for optical roundness

When nesting rounded objects, avoid using the same corner radii for both objects. This can make the corners look unbalanced.{#isPasted}

Instead, adjust the corner radii to be proportional to each other; this is called optical roundness. To calculate optical roundness:

* Outer radius - padding = inner radius
* For example: 48dp - 14dp = 34dp
### Using the shape library {#isPasted}

The Material 3 shape library ([Figma](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit?node-id=256804-1594)) can be used to create more interesting containers. Use the shape library for mostly visual elements. Avoid applying unconventional shapes to text-heavy containers.

Shapes should be used sparingly to provide a stronger emphasis and moments of delight.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Inputs
Inputs are devices that provide interactive control of an app. Common inputs include a mouse, keyboard, or touchpad.

* Design for touch, keyboard, and mouse interactions{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Embrace multiple input methods and gestures within your app
External inputs for devices
---------------------------
People can use external inputs like a mouse, keyboard, or stylus with their phone, tablet, foldable, TV, laptop, or desktop computer. When someone connects an external input to their device, they expect it to behave in familiar and useful ways. Designing for different input methods can make a product more usable and accessible on all screen sizes.
### Common features of external inputs
#### **Mouse**

* Left and right click
* Mouse wheel
* Extra buttons
#### **Trackpad**

* Left and right click
* Gestures
* Haptics
#### **Physical keyboard**

* Replaces virtual keyboard
* Media keys
* Modifier keys
### Input device behaviors {#isPasted}

Depending on the input device, designers and developers can implement behaviors that meet standard conventions and user expectations.  

|           Input device action            |               Anticipated behavior               |
|       Mouse and trackpad movement        |        Show a mouse cursor on the screen         |
|              Primary click               | Treat mouse clicks differently than touch events |
|             Secondary click              |              Activate context menus              |
|                  Hover                   |             Change component states              |
|                Highlight                 |  Allow text to be selected by the mouse cursor   |
| Mouse wheel and trackpad two finger drag |     Scroll list vertically and horizontally      |
|              Trackpad pinch              |             Zoom an element or page              |
|            Physical keyboard             |         Hide and show on screen keyboard         |
|------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
Mouse and cursor interactions
-----------------------------
When an external mouse input device is used, a mouse cursor should be shown, regardless of the device type.   

A mouse may be connected to tablets, laptops, phones, foldables, and more. On some devices, it's possible to use an external input device simultaneously with touch input.
### Primary click
On devices that don't specifically recognize mouse input, the mouse cursor input is mapped to touch input.{#isPasted}

A mouse click should demonstrate the same feedback as touch input. One example of this is showing the ripple for a pressed state.
### Secondary click
#### Context menus

The secondary button of an external input device should activate a context menu. The context menu shows additional options for the object that's clicked. See [menus](/google-material-3/pages/menus/overview) for more usage and guidelines.
### Hover
When using a mouse cursor, help users discover interactive objects by enabling visual changes. When the mouse rests on an interactive element, the hover state is a valuable cue for interaction. See [states](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/applying-states#bf01ead3-12e0-4077-98d1-05927d284c35) for styles and guidelines.  

Hovering with a cursor (or stylus) should also invoke tooltips when applicable. See [tooltips](/google-material-3/pages/tooltips/overview) for guidance.
### Cursors {#isPasted}
Cursors appear when using external input devices like a mouse or trackpad. The cursor can change to communicate more information about interactive elements.
#### Pointer {#isPasted}

By default, external input control should be rendered as a pointer.
#### Hand

The cursor should appear as a hand to indicate links or linked images.
#### Resize arrows {#isPasted}

The cursor should change to resize arrows on the boundaries of resizable elements.
#### I-beam {#isPasted}

The cursor should appear as an I-beam when hovering on text. When manipulating editable text, the following interactions apply:  
{#isPasted}

* Single click places the cursor  
  {#isPasted}
* Double click selects a word  
  {#isPasted}
* Triple click selects a paragraph  
  {#isPasted}
* Single click deselects text and repositions the cursor{#isPasted}
### Text selection

When selecting text using a mouse, trackpad, or stylus:  
{#isPasted}

* Highlight the selected area using a single color  
  {#isPasted}
* Don't show touch controls next to the highlighted area{#isPasted}
### Text selection with touch control {#isPasted}
When interacting using touch, always show touch controls, even if other inputs are connected.  

When using a mouse, trackpad, or stylus, show the I-beam and context menu, even if it's a touch device.
### Stylus input {#isPasted}

When using a stylus, cursors are usually not necessary, unless they communicate tool properties such as brush size or shape.
Mouse wheel and trackpad gestures {#isPasted}
---------------------------------------------
When an external mouse or touchpad is used, the mouse wheel and trackpad gesture allow more actions.
### Vertical scroll

When a cursor is positioned on a list, the mouse wheel and two-finger touchpad gesture should allow vertical scrolling of the list.
### Touch scroll \& mouse text selection

Upon touch and drag gesture, the text area will scroll. With a mouse interaction, dragging in a text area will select the text.
### Horizontal scroll

Mouse users should be able to scroll with a mouse wheel to navigate horizontally scrolling fields. Trackpad users should be able to scroll using a two-finger horizontal gesture.
Physical keyboard
-----------------
When a physical keyboard is connected to a device, either externally or as a built-in laptop keyboard, users should be able to perform any actions that the virtual keyboard provides, and more.
### Show and hide virtual keyboard

A virtual keyboard should appear or hide in response to the presence of a physical keyboard.
### Common keyboard interactions
#### Enter key

People typically expect the **E** **nter** key on a physical keyboard to be enabled by developers to allow a common function like sending a message.
#### Spacebar control

People typically expect the **Spacebar** (or available media keys) to be enabled to play and pause music or video.
#### Tab focus

When keyboard users navigate a page using **Tab** , the focus on interactive items must follow a logical order. On most pages, that means left to right, top to bottom.  

When focused from a keyboard or other input device, the focus state includes a ring-like keyboard focus indicator.
#### Escape key {#isPasted}

People typically expect the **Escape** key on a physical keyboard to dismiss elements, remove focus, or clear selections.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# GM3 Expressive
The Google Material 3 Expressive update builds on the foundations of Material 3 to address user and market demands with fresh app design, cutting-edge technology, and cross-device experiences.

## Applying expressive
Expressive design tactics
-------------------------

Expressive tactics are design techniques that achieve a particular style or feeling and create an impactful user experience. They can be applied with expressive tools, such as components, subsystems, and themes. When applied properly, these tactics greatly improve usability. [See how Expressive can improve usability in an example app](/google-material-3/pages/usability/applying-m-3-expressive)
### Use the tactics to diverge with purpose {#isPasted}

Material 3 is a foundation for product design, not a restriction. Start from what makes your product special, and then build on that to create an experience that differentiates it from similar products.
### Apply expressive tactics {#isPasted}

You're the driver, but you have support! Material has six expressive design tactics for creating impactful hero moments. Consider how each of these tactics can work together in your product.

[Read about how using expressive tactics also improves usability](/google-material-3/pages/usability)
#### **1. Shape: Diversify shapes** {#isPasted}

Combine both classic and abstract shapes to create unique silhouettes or groupings. Use the Material shape library and new corner radii subsystem to mix round and square shapes for tension and visual contrast.
#### **2. Color: Apply rich and nuanced colors** {#isPasted}

Create visual hierarchy with surface tones. To emphasize primary actions, use secondary and tertiary color roles on other actions, and have a strong contrast between the button and the surface.
#### **3. Typography: Compose refined typography** {#isPasted}

Improve hierarchy and direct attention with emphasized text styles. Create editorialized moments that add user delight using the Google Sans Flex variable font axes.
#### **4. Containment: Enhance content with containment** {#isPasted}

Reinforce hierarchy and guide the person's focus and interaction by organizing the page content into logical groupings. Give the most important content, tasks, or actions visual prominence through ample space and the brightest surface mapping.
#### **5. Motion: Infuse fluid and natural motion** {#isPasted}

Make interactions feel alive and spirited through shape morph or surface effects. Apply the motion physics system and custom micro-animations.
#### **6. Flexibility: Leverage component types, configurations, and adaptive behaviors** {#isPasted}

UI should adapt to the user context. Shift components or controls depending on environment to make completing tasks easier. Adapt content to foldable and large screens through custom tweaks or applying canonical layouts.
### Combine tactics to create powerful hierarchy {#isPasted}

Organize the page to guide the viewer's eye to the most important information first, letting less critical details recede in prominence. Apply scale to components, type, or containment to emphasize actions or tasks or draw focus to delightful moments.
### Implement tactics gradually {#isPasted}

Introduce expressive elements gradually and intentionally following the above principles. By starting with tactics like diverse shape and rich color, expressive design can be applied in small changes over time.
Creating hero moments
---------------------
Hero moments are experiences in your product that break from predictable or uniformly applied design ideas. They're meant to make a stand-alone statement, or frame essential information in a fresh, editorial way. Hero moments can be a focusing mechanism. Invest your time in making the most critical interactions sing; these moments are the heart of your product! {#isPasted}

Remember, hero moments are just that --- moments. They're brief, delightful, surprising, and unexpected. Stick to one or two hero moments; too many moments can be overwhelming or distracting. To identify your hero moment, **ask yourself the following questions**.
### Is this interaction emotionally impactful? {#isPasted}

Consider whether a design choice can highlight an emotional reaction or emphasize familiarity with message recipients.
### Is this a key interaction in your product? {#isPasted}

Think about whether a detail or flow can be emphasized for clarity.

Guide the user's attention by making an important button dominant, or emphasizing key information.
### Is this interaction connected to your brand value and differentiation? {#isPasted}

Consider whether there is a metric or summary that is distinct to or especially valuable within your particular product.
> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug){#isPasted}

## Overview
What's expressive?
------------------
GM3 Expressive is an update to the GM3 design system that provides tools and capabilities for apps to create differentiated and delightful user experiences. Building on the foundational system released in 2021 and 2022 with Material You and GM3, the expressive update gives designers tools to craft singular, showcase moments while tailoring to user needs. Launching on Android first, GM3 Expressive will also be on web and iOS.
New expressive tools include: {#isPasted}

* Brighter, more vibrant color scheme options for components
* New [typography themes](/google-material-3/pages/typography/overview/d9ded8e7-d34e-4b9b-b336-46ee78c0ce04?edit=true&toolbar=pages)
* A new [motion physics system](/google-material-3/pages/motion/how-it-works/)
* A new [shape](/google-material-3/pages/shape/overview-principles/8a51f95f-6f36-44db-9a55-96ad8562731a?edit=true&toolbar=pages) library and updated system
* [15 expressive component updates](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/overview#8aa38eff-09bb-4323-ad04-9df00bfb9083)

Note: Product images are inspirational and don't necessarily represent a final product.
Why expressive?
---------------
GM3 Expressive was built in partnership with 1P apps to meet user demand for experiences that are modern, relevant, and distinct. Expressive also allows designers to mirror specific emotions and feelings in the layout and presentation of the interface. Research shows that Google users view their mobile devices and software experiences as an extension of themselves. There is a user expectation for app experiences to reflect individual user preferences for style (color, type, contrast) and behaviors (for example, prioritizing frequently used apps and actions). This demand is especially prevalent with younger generations' dynamic tastes and expectations.
Material User Experience Research has found that expressive design is preferred by all ages when compared to GM3 baseline and the iOS OS (Apple HIG). But expressive design is especially preferred by ages 18-24.
1P apps are testing and realizing the value of expressive first-hand, leveraging the innovative UXR framework. [Gmail tested expressive on their newest feature](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1SJ9ALa5WdIPEyy5Dx0sL4vffvTifGtkdXrs43AzF1BI/edit?resourcekey=0-wQ26YA1Eju-PfjRuEbBftA#slide=id.g2c452736bf9_0_19), smart cards, and found transformative cards are perceived by people as easier, more appealing, more modern, and cooler.
Material UXR has found in eye tracking studies that the expressive tactics help people interact and take action with critical parts of the interface up to 3x faster. Stronger use of color and hierarchy makes it clear which item someone is interacting with and leads to a more calming experience and faster critical user journey completion.
Levels of expressive
--------------------
To help visualize how GM3 design criteria is cumulative, Material developed the GM3 levels of expressive: **foundational** , **excellent** , and **transformative**. {#isPasted}

Each level can only be achieved by meeting the previous level's criteria first.
### Foundational {#isPasted}

**Uses the base GM3 system**

These foundational criteria were established in Bluechip 21 with GM3 mobile and Bluechip 22 with GM3 adaptive.

To be foundational, a product:

* Uses GM3 tokens
* Uses foundational GM3 color, typography, and components available for the platform ([Android](/google-material-3/pages/android/overview){#isPasted}, [iOS](/google-material-3/pages/gm3-apple-platforms/ios-overview), and [web](/google-material-3/pages/web/overview))
* Complies with GM3 best practices and accessibility standards

Tokens make it much easier to adopt expressive features and reach the excellent and transformative levels.
### Excellent {#isPasted}

**Applies GM3 Expressive features**

Excellent criteria add GM3 expressive features to the product. Products are expected to start implementing the excellent criteria.

To be excellent, a product may use:

* The GM3 variable font theme (Google Sans Flex) and emphasized type styles
* Tonal surface colors, and use of vibrant color configurations
* Material shapes and shape morph
* The motion physics system
* New component types and configurations introduced in GM3 Expressive
### Transformative {#isPasted}

**Applies expressive features in creative ways**

Apps can meet and exceed excellent requirements through extensive use of the motion subsystem and by creating product specific hero moments.
What's included in Expressive?
------------------------------
### Components (15) {#isPasted}

These components were updated to move and operate with expressive flourish, making use of bold emphasis, wavy outlines, and sleekly shifting states.

* [App bars](/google-material-3/pages/app-bars/overview)
* [Buttons](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Button groups (Standard \& connected)](/google-material-3/pages/button-groups/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Carousel](/google-material-3/pages/carousel/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Extended FABs](/google-material-3/pages/extended-fab/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [FABs](/google-material-3/pages/fab/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [FAB menu](/google-material-3/pages/fab-menu/overview)
* [Icon buttons](/google-material-3/pages/icon-buttons/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Loading indicators](/google-material-3/pages/loading-indicators/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Navigation bar](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-bar/overview)
* [Navigation rails](/google-material-3/pages/navigation-rail/overview)
* [Progress indicators](/google-material-3/pages/progress-indicators/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Sliders](/google-material-3/pages/sliders/overview/){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Split button](/google-material-3/pages/split-button/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* [Toolbars](/google-material-3/pages/toolbars/overview){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
### Variable fonts {#isPasted}

* Static and variable font themes{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Google Sans Flex
* Emphasized type styles and tokens

Dynamic text styling can reflect different emotional states and construct unique editorial layouts.

Tokens can subtly adjust weight and roundness to add an emphatic flair to hierarchy and specific actions.

[Learn more about variable fonts](/google-material-3/pages/typography/variable-axes)
### Expressive motion system {#isPasted}

Nuanced, buoyant animations add precision and cheerfulness to motion. The new motion physics system includes spring animations and bespoke micro-animations.

[Learn more about motion](/google-material-3/pages/motion/how-it-works/)
**Spatial motion springs**
**Effects motion springs**
### Expanded shape library {#isPasted}

35 shapes allow fluid, changeable silhouettes.

Shape morph functionality allows smooth transitions from one shape to another. This can be dynamic, or as simple as a square changing to a circle.

[Learn more about shapes](/google-material-3/pages/shape/overview)
### Expressive design tactics {#isPasted}

Six design tactics can be applied to color, motion, typography, and more.

Combine these tactics in layered effects to create hero moments, and provide moments of surprise and delight while maintaining core functions.

[Learn more about expressive design tactics](/google-material-3/pages/expressive/applying-expressive/4cd41624-21ba-44fc-84fe-c19235391c53)
Getting started on adoption
---------------------------
### Adoption planning \& resources

GM3 Expressive will be available on Android Views and Jetpack Compose first, and iOS and web later. To know when a component or feature will be released on a specific platform, reference [go/gm3-delivery-schedule](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1UmK6i83ix-Dh4DU1HGY8OyNStPjeD9ciVsuofZHIjbI/edit#gid=0).
