## Research
Research philosophy {#isPasted}
-------------------------------
Expressive design is transforming how people experience Google products. By incorporating elements such as unexpected color and eclectic shapes, we aim to create products that not only function well, but also evoke positive emotions and foster lasting connections. Our research has shown that expressive design can significantly impact [a person's emotions](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1dxQY69kvKiACGC9g1dMIGd4C5eo2KoecOD6F_Wrx0S0/edit?resourcekey=0-F-G58xKqFNCfP2TiHk3iLg#slide=id.g2760eea2977_0_812), increasing surprise, and [engagement](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/122zANBQR9oZes-M7yrw4cK9mNMQjWEqWGoQInbyOCIE/edit?resourcekey=0-1X0v9_v0YQ5QNRvWJ3VxUA#slide=id.g2ac56eda0cb_0_360). These insights have led us to evolve our understanding of what expressive design means in a Google product experience, as well as the research methods we use to evaluate those experiences, delving deeper into the interplay between emotional impact and functional needs.

But every product is different. Your unique users, [CUJs](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/cujhub), and use contexts shape how expressive design will land. That's why we want to learn from you. By sharing your team's research and findings in our central [expressive UXR repository](https://research-archive.googleplex.com/site/expressive-uxr), we can collectively build a deeper understanding of people's reactions to expressive design across product categories.
Measuring "feeling"
-------------------
To measure the impact of expressive design on "feeling" and ensure it complements functionality, we developed a multi-method framework. This framework evolved alongside the product life cycle, from static mocks to interactive prototypes, from side-by-side to A/B experiments, and from predicted probabilities of preference to absolute levels of desirability.

These best practices have helped Google Design Platform to quantify the impact of expressive design on [desirability](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/10mwTcPvgkyQe55MaAHVzIO97QLv5tOzEHWNEbLxjgms/edit#slide=id.g142b70d16ca_0_38) across multiple [geographies and operating systems](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1CgYyPRfRJ9p5wcYz7Qrsj-qea6fm-I3aUrhGQ_g82DA/edit?pli=1#slide=id.g2554d17b746_0_4) and product teams such as [Chat](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1oz2B8Imk2ztDpIPwr0mzriaoBUe3tFiCqnqQUaOPSIo/edit#slide=id.g2769924c929_0_0), [Bard](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1sGN6XsRRyzgadEJFzUdUAVOcL-aPm_bokO6iG1mXG9o/edit?resourcekey=0-7jHNPGFhXqwRHSWs_q4Aaw#slide=id.g1ed79fbf342_0_5), [Gmail](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1SJ9ALa5WdIPEyy5Dx0sL4vffvTifGtkdXrs43AzF1BI/edit?resourcekey=0-wQ26YA1Eju-PfjRuEbBftA#slide=id.g2c452736bf9_0_19), and [Meet](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1v-n8hnA0JvBp0uCbcy750AzTg3_26XydQk9r3FZOp_g/edit?resourcekey=0-F2S4ijfhpEC07NBWvoohXA#slide=id.g2c2b91d9d20_0_0) to prioritize design sprint concepts. For all prior research, check out the [expressive UXR page at the Research Archive](https://research-archive.googleplex.com/site/expressive-uxr).
Research approaches
-------------------
### SxS comparisons

Ideal for early design exploration, SxS comparisons present pairs of UI side-by-side to identify meaningful, salient differences between similar UI mocks, and similar UI elements, to provide direction and confidence throughout the design process.{#isPasted}

* **Style**: Measures key stylistic perceptions, including expressiveness (playful, positive, creative, energetic, and modern).
* **Hierarchy**: Measures balance and perceived findability of primary features.
* **Use**: Measures people's initial usability expectations.
### A/B experiments

Ideal for evaluating more mature designs, this method randomly exposes people to either the current UI design or an expressive alternative, allowing us to measure the intensity of people's perceptions and pinpoint areas for refinement.

* **Subculture**: Part of an autonomous group outside mainstream society.
* **Modern**: Tendency to be different and do things that have not been done before.
* **Iconoclastic**: Tendency to oppose, fight, or combat conventions and social norms.
* **Expressive**: Creativity and originality and ability to break design conventions.

Expressive levels can also be tested, for example excellent vs. transformative.
Research tools {#isPasted}
--------------------------
Beyond the best practices above, Material has ready-to-go tools that you can use to validate the positive impact of expressive design while ensuring functionality for teams with little or no UXR support. Note: This is an evolving space.
### G-Rex

G-Rex (short for Google Rapid Experimentation) is a platform we developed to make evaluating the visual design of your UIs through SxS comparisons as fast and easy as possible. G-Rex quickly creates a comparison between UIs, drawing feedback from a user test group on fundamental questions of style and hierarchy.  

To create your experiment, go to [go/create-g-rex-experiment](https://colab.sandbox.google.com/drive/11dJ6-ofGJ1b22H132iq9FEXfqM52cp5Z?resourcekey=0-JXTTZvpnAtau9J03UD5ipw#sandboxMode=true).
### Expression UXR Kit

The Expression UXR Kit is the best choice for ensuring designs are desirable and usable in A/B experiments where people interact with a prototype. It provides a toolkit and instructions for conducting user research on expressive design.  

Find the kit and resources at   
[go/expression-uxr-kit](https://docs.google.com/document/d/13H_ZzodCL8MkDnf1G1W9bfD_NPLcl15WPQyytrScrPM/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.9nevncl3x3tn)
Share your research
-------------------

When running any research on expression for your product, tag it as "**ExpressiveUXR** " when uploading to the [research archive](https://research-archive.googleplex.com/home), so we can add it to our resources and other teams can benefit from what you learn. Additionally, join our mailing list (expressive-research@) and share updates or questions with the group.
Things to keep in mind
----------------------
### Thoughtful changes

The insights you gain from user research directly depend on the designs you compare. To ensure actionable results, be deliberate in your design variations, ensuring they are distinct yet meaningfully related. For instance:

* **SxS** allows you to evaluate multiple expressive alternatives against the baseline
* **A/B tests** are effective for comparing an expressive experience to the baseline

Regardless of the method you choose, carefully consider the changes you make, as they will directly shape the insights you gather and pinpoint the elements responsible for any shifts in user behavior.
### Perceptions vs. behavior

Survey questions provide insights into people's perceptions, but do not always translate into behavior. Teams should compliment findings from perceptual studies with behavioral data from live experiments to understand the nuanced and sustained impact on people's perception.
Research highlights {#isPasted}
-------------------------------
### Familiarity vs. expression

Most of our studies show that expressive designs are initially perceived as more appealing and desirable but less familiar or clean.   

Prioritizing the right design candidate should be informed by a deeper understanding of the influence of both familiarity and expressivity on the overall, holistic desirability of your product in the live experience of your users, informed by behavioral data.
### Functionality is essential

While embracing expressive elements, core functionality cannot be compromised. For instance, reducing visual clutter in [Maps](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1INjixed_VwJqJVCfykpnlRrHpQn3VjS1S8B9KjxyzFo/edit?resourcekey=0-G3GM4VtMFEFGVWOqK-8Huw#slide=id.p) or maintaining a structured list in [Dialer](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1MoE71UBGXZNZFTN9rTHaT0j7ZdKZIMCb37uAoJV20uU/edit?resourcekey=0-NuDG2CwCzqNj-PyX7anCXw#slide=id.g2c2b91d9d20_0_0) helps to preserve usability. However, there's room for expressive flourishes.

[Read more about how expressive tactics can improve usability](/google-material-3/pages/usability)
### Structure \& hierarchy

Grids and clear visual hierarchy are essential for effective expressive design. Organizing elements such as avatars on a grid (see [Meet](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1v-n8hnA0JvBp0uCbcy750AzTg3_26XydQk9r3FZOp_g/edit?resourcekey=0-F2S4ijfhpEC07NBWvoohXA#slide=id.g2c2b91d9d20_0_0)) and strategically using size and color to highlight key features improve people's experience. Avoid overwhelming people with too many competing visual elements (see [Numberpad](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1MoE71UBGXZNZFTN9rTHaT0j7ZdKZIMCb37uAoJV20uU/edit?resourcekey=0-NuDG2CwCzqNj-PyX7anCXw#slide=id.g2c2b91d9d20_0_0)).
### Check out the expressive UXR page for more research {#isPasted}

[Expressive UXR research archive](https://research-archive.googleplex.com/site/expressive-uxr)
> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug){#isPasted}


# Voice and tone
Google’s voice and tone is friendly, respectful, and focused on the user

## Product names
### Capitalize products, not features {#isPasted}

Capitalize the first letter of each word in product names. Don't capitalize feature names. If you're unsure whether a name should be capitalized, refer to the Google-wide localization glossary at [go/termbrowser](https://localization.google.com/glossary)
### Differentiate user-generated content {#isPasted}

Don't capitalize content created using Google products, such as videos, images, projects, or files. Differentiate between user-generated content and Google's content.

<br />
### Use care when naming products {#isPasted}

Naming can have broad internal and external consequences. Use product marketing's extensive naming resources, including the [naming process overview](https://standards.google/guidelines/brand-standards/naming/naming-process/#naming-process-overview) created by Brand Standards and [go/naming-playbook](http://go/naming-playbook).

<br />

<br />

<br />

<br />
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Gender inclusive language
To learn more about inclusive design, visit [Material's accessibility guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/accessibility/overview). To learn more about inclusive language at Google beyond UX writing, visit [go/preciselanguage-handout](http://go/preciselanguage-handout). For a list of inclusive and exclusive terminology, visit [go/respectful-words](http://go/preciselanguage-handout). For a quick exercise to check inclusive word choices, visit the Center for Equity, Gender \& Leadership's [Inclusive Language Guide](https://www.egalresources.com/inclusive-language-guide).
### Use gender neutral language if pronouns aren't clear {#isPasted}

In English, "they," "them," and "theirs" can be used instead of singular gendered pronouns, like "he" or "she."

Consider using [gender APIs](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/transconsole/g3doc/messages-icu-plurals-gender.md?cl=head) to automate the substitution of "their" with "his" or "her."

<br />
### Don't combine gender options {#isPasted}

Don't combine multiple gendered pronouns in one sentence.

<br />
### Only collect gender info if necessary {#isPasted}

If gender isn't necessary, skip it. Explore ways to convey the same message while omitting gender altogether. If it's required, consider a more inclusive approach.

Only collect gender and biological sex information when it's required. Note that "sex" can be jarring to see, so aim to use it sparingly and accurately.

* Be sure all terms are used correctly
* List options in alphabetical order
* Give context for why it's needed
* Consider explaining who has access to this info
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Tone
### Tell, don't sell

Explain functionality and benefits directly, but avoid trying to sell features with exaggerated language. A UI should feel objective and helpful, not pushy.
### Offer solutions

Address users through proactive, positive language. {#isPasted}

Google tone and voice aims to provide solutions that direct people to additional help. Avoid expressing outcomes or errors in strictly prohibitive or discouraging terms.

When an error occurs, tell people what happened as clearly and directly as possible, without technical jargon. Learn more about writing errors at [go/write-errors](http://go/write-errors).
### Avoid absolutes

Avoid absolute statements like "never" and "always." These can be interpreted as promises. Instead, focus on the present state.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Voice
### Use brand voice with care {#isPasted}

A unique brand voice is central to Google's identity. It strengthens Google's relationship with users, complements Google's distinct visual design, and helps make Google apps more fun. Visit [Google's brand standards site](https://standards.google/guidelines/brand-standards/) to learn more.

Brand voice can be at odds with standard UX writing principles of being concise and focusing on user goals. It may extend word counts, but not without purpose. Wit and elegance characterize the brand, as long as our wordplay is accessible to everyone.
### Be human, be helpful {#isPasted}

Use plain, direct, and natural language. Avoid giving information that isn't helpful or is hard to understand. Avoid using terms like "user" in UI text.
### Surprise and delight {#isPasted}

Google's brand voice often arises from clever word play, while keeping the message accessible to all users.

Use unexpected language to surprise and delight. Maintain an informal tone in ways that align with Google's mission.
### Time it right {#isPasted}

Inspirational messages should appear infrequently and only during unique moments in the flow, like when celebrating an accomplishment or reaching the end of a user journey.

Mid-task messaging may use inspirational language when it won't interrupt the primary task.

<br />
### Localize voice {#isPasted}

Use plain language and work with your localization team to translate Google's brand voice appropriately

* Highlight text where you've used Google's brand voice and explain any figurative language
* Offer alternative, plain-English versions in case there's not an accurate translation
* Provide Oxford American dictionary definitions for any informal words or phrases
* Avoid slang that could be offensive, such as words like "jeez" or "gosh"

Learn more about [localization](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/searchl10n/home)
### Be clear

Words should not be intentionally misspelled. Avoid creating words (such as "Cloud-tastic") and opt for real, dictionary-defined words.{#isPasted}

Glyphs and symbols should not be used to communicate imagery. Use emoji with caution: many emoji don't translate or may communicate different meanings across cultures.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Writing and text
Ensure text is helpful, clear, and resilient to change

## Text resizing
Text resizing
-------------
### Background

People with low vision or those who prefer large text must be able to scale up the size of text in a UI. This adjustment is often performed through a device OS setting or in-app option.

UIs should support a minimum text increase of 200%.
Most components behave the same when text is resized:{#isPasted}

* Text and line height scale up proportionally, multiplied by scale value
* Padding remains constant at 1x the default size
* Spacing between elements in a component remain constant at 1x the default size
When text resizing isn't controlled by the device OS, offer multipliers such as 1.5x or 2x to allow users to increase the text size. Using multipliers to scale text can result in values with decimals, but this approach is more feasible for implementation.

To calculate a font's size using multipliers, take the **default** **font size** (density = 0) and **multiply it by the scale value** .
Components that don't include text, like progress indicators, checkboxes, or radio buttons, aren't affected by text resizing.
### Designing for large type {#isPasted}
Large type is used regularly by people with low vision and those with difficulty processing written words. They tend to increase text size:  
{#isPasted}

* To make it easier to read  
  {#isPasted}
* To limit interruptions and focus on one task  
  {#isPasted}
* To avoid overwhelming their senses{#isPasted}

Use these methods to design a product to handle large type properly.
### Methods

Avoid common text resizing issues by increasing container size, reflowing layout, enabling scrolling, and adding tooltips.
#### **Increase container size** {#isPasted}

Resizing containers can prevent text from overlapping, clipping, or truncating.

Consider how text might reflow in a way that allows the eye to follow the end of one line to the beginning of the following line.

<br />

#### **Reflow the layout** {#isPasted}

Consider reflowing the layout, especially when components grow very long. To accommodate larger text, components can be stacked on top of one another, rather than fixed side-by-side.
#### **Enable content to scroll** {#isPasted}

When long strings of enlarged text don't fit on one screen, consider adding a scrollbar to provide access to more content.

Vertical scrolling is preferable to horizontal. Users should only be asked to scroll in one direction, rather than both vertically and horizontally.
#### **Use touch \& hold tooltips to provide enlarged labels** {#isPasted}

Some components, such as app bars and navigation bars, position text in spaces with stricter space and character limits. In these situations, you can add a tooltip to display enlarged content in the UI.

In this case, the text size in the component remains displayed at 1x while the scaled up text is displayed in a tooltip on touch \& hold.

Tooltips are the best choice for displaying enlarged text in: {#isPasted}

* Top app bar
* Navigation bar
* Navigation rail
* Tabs, when fixed to the top of a screen and don't move off-screen upon scrolling
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Best practices
Accessibility text {#isPasted}
------------------------------

Accessibility text refers to text that is used by screen reader accessibility software, such as Google's TalkBack on Android, Apple's VoiceOver on iOS, and Freedom Scientific's JAWS on desktop. Screen readers read aloud the on-screen text and elements (such as buttons), including both visible and nonvisible alternative text.
### Adjacent text {#isPasted}

To make an image more accessible, the text in and around images should consider presenting key information about the image.
### Captions {#isPasted}

Captions are the text that appear below an image. They explain contextual information about the image to help the reader understand how it relates to the content. Both sighted and screen reader users rely on captions for descriptions of images.
### Embedded text in images {#isPasted}

Screen readers are unable to read text that is embedded in imagery. If there is essential information embedded as text in the image, include the essential information in the [alt text](/google-material-3/pages/alt-text).
### Alternative text (Alt text) {#isPasted}

Alt text helps translate a visual UI into a text-based UI. Alt text is a short label (up to 125 characters) in the code that describes an image for users who are unable to see them. Since alt text is only for images, there is no need to add "image of" or "picture of" to the alt text. A screen reader will read the alt text aloud in place of the image.

Alt text is valuable for sighted users, as well, because alt text appears if an image fails to load. Include targeted keywords to help inform the user about the image. Keywords can also improve search engine optimization (SEO).

[Learn more about writing alt text](/google-material-3/pages/alt-text)
Text color
----------
### Essential and non-essential elements {#isPasted}

Informative images have essential and non-essential elements. Essential information should have a 3:1 minimum color contrast for large text and 4.5:1 for small text.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Text truncation
Text truncation
---------------
Information should always be available to readers, even if text is truncated or wrapped.
### Background {#isPasted}

Increased size of text, increased spacing between text, and translation into longer languages shouldn't result in losing content. This requires designing for text truncation and creating designs flexible enough to accommodate any viewport size or increase in zoom. Some common methods of designing for larger text include text wrapping, increased height or width of components, and truncation with ellipses and hover or link.
### Requirements {#isPasted}

Content, understandability, and functionality must not be lost when users modify their type settings. See the GAR 2024 text spacing criterion for reference. There may be exceptions to these requirements for non-Latin alphabet languages.
### Text wrapping {#isPasted}

* "Wrapped" text extends from one line to another, increasing the height of the text container{#isPasted}
* Text should be wrapped when it's critical, to ensure understandability, or when there's space in the component{#isPasted}
### Height and width of components

* Some components can extend vertically or horizontally for more text{#isPasted}
### Ellipses with hover or link {#isPasted}

* Truncated text can be replaced with an ellipsis if the text is available through a tooltip or link{#isPasted}
* Links can be used when they're contained in the text that's truncated, and when the link displays what's been truncated{#isPasted}
* If there's an ellipsis, but no way to show the truncated text, it doesn't pass GAR 2024{#isPasted}
* Note that this option can add difficulty for some people. See [go/cognitive-persistent](http://go/cognitive-persistent) to learn more about memory and persistence.{#isPasted}
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Accessible design
Accessible design makes products usable for people with all kinds of abilities

## Principles
Accessibility \& Material Design {#isPasted}
--------------------------------------------

**Accessibility by default** is a core design value for Material. Material's accessibility requirements and goals are documented across component pages and guidelines. Understanding your product's accessibility can enhance usability for all users, including those with low vision, blindness, hearing impairments, cognitive impairments, motor impairments, or situational disabilities (such as a broken arm).

Accessibility standards are built into Material components, providing a foundation for inclusive product design. Anticipating a wide range of human experiences and disabilities prevents costly redesigns, reduces tech and design debt, and conserves resources upfront.
Principles for accessible design {#isPasted}
--------------------------------------------

The following principles for accessible design are approaches that can help **anticipate, include, and respond to the needs of individuals**. They're considerations for design, development, collaboration, and co-creation that encourage inclusive and equitable outcomes.
### Honor individuals {#isPasted}

Universal default experiences rarely meet everyone's needs. Introducing customizable features in a default experience allows room for individual adaptation. Honoring the shifting needs of any person can mean giving more freedom to choose things for themselves.

Because a person's experience throughout their day, or year, or life can continue to change, it helps to support varying preferences and choices that allow individuals to address how their changing conditions, individual knowledge, and varying needs are met.
### Learn before, not after {#isPasted}

Before defining solutions, invest time in understanding the needs of users with a wide range of abilities and life experiences. Formal and informal research can open new ways of thinking, reduce biases, and encourage creative ways to make access available, especially to those who fall outside prevailing norms.

Accessible design processes anticipate as many potential product or experience outcomes as possible upfront. When user problems do arise, exploring the unanticipated outcomes can become a research foundation for learning, adapting, and recovering thoughtfully.
### Requirements as a starting point {#isPasted}

The minimum requirements established by[](https://www.w3.org/WAI/standards-guidelines/wcag/) [WCAG](https://www.w3.org/WAI/standards-guidelines/wcag/){#isPasted} and [GAR](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/accessibility-site/product-development/gar) support specific human needs. However, these requirements can produce creative solutions with broad benefits.

History has shown that features originating from responses to specific access needs (dark mode, text-to-speech, speech-to-text) are a result of creative problem-solving within specified constraints. Seeing the requirements as opportunities, rather than constraints, has been shown to lead to solutions that ultimately serve many.
Disclaimer: These materials are for your information only, and do not constitute legal advice. Consider consulting your attorney for advice on any particular issue.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Assistive technology
Assistive technology {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
Assistive technology helps increase, maintain, or improve the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities. People can live more independently by engaging with technology through devices like keyboards, screen readers, and braille displays, as well as tracking input, magnifiers, and voice input.
### Common forms of assistive technologies {#isPasted}
#### **Keyboard**

Hardware or software directional controllers such as keyboards, a D-pad, or trackball allow users to jump from selection to selection in a linear fashion.

#### **Screen readers**

A screen reader is a software program that uses either a braille display or reads text aloud, such as Google's screen reader, TalkBack. People with vision impairments, experiencing difficulty reading, or who temporarily can't read might use a screen reader. Screen readers verbalize visible content and read it aloud. Paragraph and button text, as well as hidden content like alternative text for icons and headings, are identified by the program. Content can be labeled to optimize the experience for those who use screen readers or experience a text-only version of your UI.

#### **Switch input**

Switches scan the items on your screen, highlighting each item in turn, until you make a selection. Switch Access lets you interact with your Android device using one or more switches instead of the touchscreen.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Product icons
Product icons are branded symbols representing individual products and their services

## Designing icons
##### If our icons aren't recognizably Google, users won't associate Google with the products they love. Follow these specs to create an icon that works for your individual product while clearly living within the broader family of Google product icons. {#isPasted}
Design basics
-------------
**Create icons that fit in Google's icon family**

Each of our icons is part of a family of Google product icons, and they must be cohesive when seen as a group. Create unity by applying our core principles of icon design: use our [brand colors](https://standards.google/guidelines/brand-standards/color/palette/#brand-colors){#isPasted}, embrace white space, keep it simple, and use clear metaphors.
**Think beyond ribbon icons, which lack differentiation**  
While we do have some existing icons that are ribbons or four-color lines, overusing them runs the risk of creating an unclear brand experience where multiple icons could be confused for one another. They're particularly hard to differentiate in small scale, where they often appear.

<br />
**Avoid including letters**

The only letter-based icon is the Google Search G. Other product icons should avoid including letters, especially the letter G as it doesn't add anything to the icon and often only complicates the design.
Color
-----
**Use the four Google brand colors**

Color is the most effective tool to visually connect a product icon to Google. Even with only three colors, people quickly recognize us. And one-color product icons work for [subproducts](https://standards.google/guidelines/brand-standards/brand-architecture-framework/flagship-group/#subproducts){#isPasted} under a recognizable [flagship](https://standards.google/guidelines/brand-standards/brand-architecture-framework/flagship-group/#flagship-products).
### Common color challenges
**Colors that clash when touching**

Be mindful when arranging colors. Some combinations can clash or interact in problematic ways. Choose another color combination or add a darker tone or shadow.
**Insufficient contrast between blue and red**

Don't create icons with insufficient contrast between blue and red. Instead, add a top light shadow to separate layers.
**Don't combine red and green**

The combination will vibrate where the two colors intersect. Use a recommended color combination instead.
Grid and keylines
-----------------
Use the grid and keylines in our [downloadable templates](/google-material-3/pages/product-icons/submitting-icons/latest#949d82a5-b515-4a80-bb44-278f3280a29d){#isPasted} to maintain a consistent visual proportion throughout your icons.
Keylines help unify product icons and maintain consistent sizing across different platforms.
Shape
-----

Shape can help convey the concept behind your product. Follow these principles when considering the shape of your icon.
**Keep it simple**  
Simple shapes and silhouettes work better at small sizes and align with our brand personality. By reducing product icons to the simplest elements, we can clarify the core idea and intent of the product.
**Use geometric shapes**

We use geometric forms instead of organic forms for product icons. Geometric forms are simpler, easier to understand at small sizes, and align with the shape of our [brand logo](https://standards.google/guidelines/brand-standards/logos/google-logo/){#isPasted}.
**Tend toward symmetry**

Symmetry is a powerful way to reduce the complexity of product icons and make them feel cohesive and balanced. Symmetrical product icons are more balanced, cohesive, and memorable.
**Use layers sparingly**

We try to keep our icons as simple as possible and stay away from too many layers. But when we do use layers to create depth, we keep it simple. Layers can be used to create dimension or add depth within an icon. But use no more than three layers.
Corners
-------
**Use a 12dp corner radius**

Our keylines have consistent corner radiuses that are key to unifying our product icons. Use a 12px corner radius on the silhouette form of the icon.
Shading
-------
**Be deliberate with shading**

Use top light shadows to help distinguish overlapping layers or increase the separation between colors with little contrast.

## Submitting icons
##### Follow these instructions to properly export your product icon files, submit them for approval, and upload them.
1. [Design](/google-material-3/pages/product-icons/submitting-icons/latest#949d82a5-b515-4a80-bb44-278f3280a29d) the product icon files using the provided templates and checklists
2. [Submit](/google-material-3/pages/product-icons/submitting-icons/latest#9cbd6965-2b4f-4cad-bf68-0260e505fcc4) for design approval and for trademark approval
3. [Upload](/google-material-3/pages/product-icons/submitting-icons/latest#a9603bd6-be22-40d2-a399-2e48aaed408c) your product icon
Templates
---------
Design \& submission process
----------------------------
### Design basics

1. Follow the guidelines \& specs
2. Create your design in Adobe Illustrator (not Sketch or another design tool)
3. Use the downloadable templates above
### Make your icon pixel perfect {#isPasted}

Icons must be "on pixel" to avoid distortion.{#isPasted}

1. Ensure your artboard has the [correct pixel settings](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/7iqhNCoEHz6Yb3P)
   1. Reference point set to top left
   2. Reference point coordinates (X,Y) set to zero
2. Confirm artboard size is correct per final deliverable requirements
3. Use only one artboard per artwork file (not multiple artboards in one file)
4. Confirm that your icon measurements are whole numbers without decimals ([example](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/3K4oaHGsiyny47J){#isPasted})
   1. Select your icon artwork without bounding box
   2. Make sure the top left reference point is selected in the Transform window
   3. Check that the X and Y coordinates, as well as width and height measurements, are integers and do not contain decimals
#### Check your design in Pixel Preview

1. Use "Pixel Preview" in Adobe Illustrator to preview your icon{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Verify that the pixel-perfect artwork sits appropriately, given its shape, on the pixel-perfect icon keyline
### Check your colors {#isPasted}

1. Verify "Document Color Mode" is RGB ([example](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/3mp5v74ZxiEoYnb)){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Confirm colors used in artwork are correct RGB brand colors. If not, then add brand RGB color swatches via .ase --- available in the [swatch libraries section of Brand Standards](https://standards.google/guidelines/brand-standards/color/resources/#swatch-libraries) --- and apply to artwork.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
3. If your icon has neighboring colors that are touching, fix any color bleeding by overlapping the two shapes that create a color border{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
4. Confirm that the overlapping colors follow guidelines{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
### Finalize layers

#### Standard and iOS full-bleed icon

1. Ensure icon artwork is on its own layer{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Ensure all layers are named correctly as seen in this example
3. Ensure layer named "bounding box" is bottommost layer
4. Group all icon shapes
5. Ensure all shapes are unlocked
6. Delete the layers "trim area," "keylines," and "grid" from final file
#### Round icon

1. Ensure icon artwork is on its own layer{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Ensure all layers are named correctly as seen in this example
3. Ensure "BG circle" and "BG edge" layers are present
4. Ensure layer named "bounding box" is bottommost layer
5. Group all icon shapes
6. Ensure all shapes are unlocked
7. Delete the layers "trim area," "keylines," and "grid" from final file
#### Adaptive icon

* Ensure each icon element is on the appropriate layer, either the "foreground" and/or the "background" layer{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Ensure all layers are named correctly as seen in this example
* Group all icon shapes
* Ensure all shapes are unlocked
* Delete the layers "keylines" and "grid" from final file
### Verify bounding box settings

* Make sure bounding box is included (i.e., a square that is the same size as the artboard, with "Fill" and "Stroke" set to "None")
* Make sure the bounding box is on its own layer named "bounding box" and is the bottommost layer ([example](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/75aqSx5Af3xsHwp)){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
### Clean up files {#isPasted}

* Delete all unused color swatches, symbols, and brushes{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Delete all stray points via "Select \> Object \> Stray Points"
* Delete all guides, collapse all layers, then save at 100% view with the artboard sitting at the top left corner of the AI window. This gives the next person who opens the file a better sense of what's in the file.
### Save your .ai and .svg files {#isPasted}

1. Click "File \> Save a Copy..."{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Check "Use Artboards"
1. Set SVG Profile to "SVG 1.1"{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Set Fonts type to "Convert to outline"
3. Select "Embed"
4. Select "Presentation Attributes"
5. Use 2 decimal places
6. Verify that "Responsive" is unchecked
### Name your icon files {#isPasted}

1. Use the naming structure:  
   **{icon_name}_{icon_type}_{size}px.\[ai\|svg\]**   

2. Follow these conventions:

<!-- -->

1. Name your artboard and file the same
2. Icon name cannot be 16 characters long. It must be either longer or shorter.
3. No need to use "google" in the name; all icons are for Google
4. Use all lowercase
5. No spaces; use underscores
6. If your name has more than one word, use underscores to separate them
### Submit your files for approval {#isPasted}
#### **Submit for design approval**

1. Open a ticket at [go/producticon-designapproval](http://go/producticon-designapproval){#isPasted}
2. Submit all file types (Standard, Round, Adaptive, and optionally iOS) at the same time

The Material Design team will review your final product icon files. Plan for a 4-week turnaround time from submission to launch to account for fixing any errors and resubmitting.

* If your files are built and saved correctly, they will be approved. Then, you can submit for trademark approval.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* If the icons don't meet all the requirements listed in this page, you'll be reassigned the ticket to fix and resubmit the files.
* About 66% of product icon submissions are rejected the first time. Plan ahead and follow the instructions closely.
#### **Submit for trademark approval** {#isPasted}

1. Submit your product icon design for trademark approval using [go/tmrequest](https://gutsv3.corp.google.com/#create/new/?core.ticketSectionName=TM%20Search&core.summary=Trademark%20Search%3A%20(1)%20____%3B%20(2)%20____%3B%20etc){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Once the trademark team has approved your product icon, you can continue to the next step.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
### Upload your files

1. Follow the instructions at [go/product-icons-docs](http://go/product-icons-docs).
2. You're all done! Your changes should go live in 2--6 hours.

## Overview
* Product icons are clear, simple, and use the four Google brand colors
* Use the official [grid and keylines](/google-material-3/pages/product-icons/designing-icons#bfcd1b57-c2a1-4af9-8b02-318f19f3c893) to design product icons
* Follow the [submission instructions](/google-material-3/pages/product-icons/submitting-icons/) to get approval for new or updated icons
* Each Google product icon must work within the Google ecosystem so that all of our icons are unified and understandable
Resources
---------
|                                                  |                             Product icons                             |  Google Symbols \& system icons   |
|--------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Get current icons                                | [go/product-icons](http://go/product-icons)                           | [go/icons](http://go/icons)       |
| Submit a new or updated icon for design approval | [go/producticon-designapproval](http://go/producticon-designapproval) | [go/newicons](http://go/newicons) |
| Submit for trademark review                      | [go/tmrequest](http://go/tmrequest)                                   | --                                |
| Upload icons                                     | [go/product-icons-docs](http://go/product-icons-docs)                 | --                                |
| Use iOS Liquid Glass effects                     | [go/liquid-glass-icons](http://go/liquid-glass-icons)                 |                                   |

{#isPasted}
Gradient Google G
-----------------
The newly launched gradient Google G, or "Super G," product icon is currently only for AI-powered experiences in Search. Don't use it for non-Search experiences, and don't apply four color gradients to other logos or word marks. It should remain unique visually to users, differentiated from other product and marketing assets.

If you're in Search and have questions, reach out to marketing at searchmarketing@google.com.
Product icons are... {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
**Cohesive**

A product icon isn't a new logo for a product. It's one icon in a universe of Google icons that must all work together.

<br />

**Designed with the brand colors**

Our core colors are recognizably Google. Using colors from our palette helps tie product icons to the Google brand.

<br />

**Simple and understandable**

The concept of the product should be clear from its icon. Avoid complicated designs or confusing metaphors.
Principles of product icons
---------------------------
Our product icons must be individual enough to represent a particular product, but still unified and cohesive with Google's icon family. Here are some important principles for designing product icons.
### Use the brand colors

Using the core colors from our [brand color palette](https://standards.google/guidelines/brand-standards/color/palette/){#isPasted} ties our product icons to Google and all the positive associations (trust, confidence, reliability) that people have with our brand. There are many ways to apply our colors, ranging from all four core colors with tonal variations down to a single color for subproducts.
### Make the metaphor clear

The concept of the product needs to be easy to recognize -- like the pin from Google Maps. Complicated visuals or hard-to-understand metaphors just confuse users.
### Keep it clear and simple

Keeping things simple aligns with our brand personality. Our product icons shouldn't be too busy, cluttered, or complicated.
### Embrace open space

Our product icons need to appear in lots of different places: launchers, phones, websites, marketing. Using open space ties into our clean brand design and makes our icons easily recognized at a glance.


# Selection
Selection is how people interact with UI elements or choose which items to act on

* Selection is shown through changes to surface color or other visible elements{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* An entire component can be selected, or just certain parts in a component
* Selection can be performed via tap, cursor, keyboard, or voice
Resources
---------
|  Type  |                                        Resource                                        |    Contact     |  Status   |
| Design | [Design Kit (Figma)](https://www.figma.com/file/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit) | gm-design-kit@ | Available |
|--------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------|-----------|

{#isPasted}
Selection indicators
--------------------
Selections are displayed using a check mark icon, a checkbox component, a change in surface color, or a combination.{#isPasted}

Selections are inherited by the following components:

* Cards
* Checkboxes
* Chips
* Data tables
* Icon buttons
* List items
* Menu items
* Pickers
* Radio buttons
* Segmented buttons
* Sliders
* Switch
The following components use an active indicator to represent which item is currently selected:{#isPasted}

* Navigation bar
* Navigation drawer
* Navigation rail
* Tabs

The color and shape of the active indicator varies between components. In these components, only one item should be selected at a time.
Types of selection
------------------
### Touch

On touch devices, select items using:{#isPasted}

* Long press touch or two-finger touch
* Selection shortcut, if available, such as tapping an avatar
### Entering and exiting selection mode

To select an item and enter selection mode, long press the item or use a shortcut, such as tapping the item's avatar. To select additional items, tap each of them.{#isPasted}

To exit a selection mode, tap each selected item until they're unselected, or tap an action on the toolbar.
### Larger selections

To select multiple items simultaneously, long press and drag across items. Don't use this gesture combination if it is already in use to pick up and move items, like cards.
### Click

On desktop, checkboxes are always visible when selection is the primary activity. When selection is secondary, checkboxes (or other indicators) are displayed:{#isPasted}

* As a single checkbox for that item on hover
* For all items after one item is selected

To make a selection, hover over an item to reveal a checkbox. The checkbox can then be clicked.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# States
States show the interaction status of a component or UI element

## Overview
* States have two visual indicators to ensure accessibility{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* States can be combined, such as selection and hover
* Apply states consistently across components
Resources
---------
|  Type  |                                        Resource                                        |    Contact     |  Status   |
| Design | [Design Kit (Figma)](https://www.figma.com/file/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit) | gm-design-kit@ | Available |
|--------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------|-----------|

{#isPasted}
Types of states
---------------
1. [Enabled](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/applying-states/latest#79d4c7b3-bd64-49ba-90f1-3eeb62f1b328)An enabled state communicates an interactive component or element.{#isPasted}

2. [Disabled](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/applying-states/latest#78347ef4-cf14-4037-95fa-f2f4b654f785)  
A disabled state communicates an inoperable component or element.

<br />
3. [Hover](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/applying-states/latest#bf01ead3-12e0-4077-98d1-05927d284c35)  
A hover state communicates when a user has placed a cursor above an interactive element.{#isPasted}

4. [Focused](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/applying-states/latest#bfc1624f-6bcc-4306-b0c1-425e2d8a1bf9)  
A focused state communicates when a user has highlighted an element, using an input method such as a keyboard or voice.

<br />
5. [Pressed](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/applying-states/latest#9e111dbb-45fd-4f39-8414-af31dfee340c)  
A pressed state communicates a user tap.{#isPasted}

6. [Dragged](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/applying-states/latest#198c29c7-771e-4264-91e9-70c32b8902ec){#isPasted}  
A dragged state communicates when a user presses and moves an element.

<br />
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Applying states
Enabled
-------
An enabled state communicates an interactive component or element. Enabled states use the default styling for each interactive component.
Disabled
--------
A disabled state communicates when a component or element isn't interactive. This state is visually communicated through color changes and reduced elevation.{#isPasted}

**Disabled states don't need to meet Material's contrast requirements.**
Disabled states **are** inherited by action, selection, and input components:{#isPasted}

Buttons{#isPasted}

* Cards
* Checkboxes
* Chips
* List items
* Radio buttons
* Switches
* Text fields
Disabled states **aren't** inherited by communication, containment, navigation, and some actions components: {#isPasted}

* App bars
* Badges
* Dialogs
* Floating action buttons (FABs){#isPasted}
* Menus
* Navigation bar, drawer, and rail
* Sheets
* Tabs
* Tooltips
### Behavior {#isPasted}

Disabled components can't be focused, dragged, or pressed, and they don't change state when tapped or hovered over.
There can be any number of disabled states in a layout.
Hover {#isPasted}
-----------------
Hover states are initiated by the user pausing over an interactive element using a cursor.{#isPasted}

The lower-emphasis surface overlay for hover states can be applied to the entire component, elements within a component, or as a circular shape over part of the component.
Hover states **are** inherited by action, selection, and input components:{#isPasted}

* Buttons
* Cards
* Checkbox
* Chips
* Date and time pickers
* List items
* Slider
* Switch
* Text fields
Hover states **aren't** inherited by communication, containment, or navigation components: {#isPasted}

* App bars
* Badges
* Dialogs
* Menus
* Navigation bar, drawer, and rail
* Sheets
* Tabs
### Behavior

Hover states are initiated by the user pausing over an interactive element using a cursor.
Hover states can be combined with focused, activated, selected, or pressed states.
There can only be one hover state at a time in a layout.
Focused {#isPasted}
-------------------
A focused state communicates when a user has highlighted an element using a keyboard or voice. Focus states apply to all interactive components.{#isPasted}

The higher-emphasis surface overlay for focused states can be applied to the entire component, elements within a component, or as a circular shape over part of the component.
Focus states **are** inherited by action, selection, and input components:{#isPasted}

* Buttons
* Cards
* Checkbox
* Chips
* Date and time pickers
* List items
* Selection controls
* Text fields
Focus states **aren't** inherited by most communication, containment, or navigation components:{#isPasted}

* App bars
* Badges
* Banner
* Card
* Dialogs
* Navigation bar, drawer, and rail
* Sheets
### Keyboard focus indicator

Many people use the **Tab** key or other shortcut to navigate the interactive elements of a web page, like links, buttons, and chips.{#isPasted}

When an element is tabbed to, it appears in its focused state with a ring-like **keyboard focus indicator**. This indicator helps web users know where they are on the page.

While focused, an element can be acted on with the keyboard.
### Behavior

Focus states are initiated by the user by pressing the **Tab**key on the keyboard (or equivalent).{#isPasted}

Focus states can be represented in combination with hover, activated, or selected states.
There can only be one focus state at a time in a layout.
Pressed {#isPasted}
-------------------
A pressed state communicates a user-initiated tap or click via cursor, keyboard, or voice input. This state applies to all interactive components.{#isPasted}

Pressed states trigger a change in composition and should be high-emphasis.

A ripple overlay signifies a pressed state. It can be applied to an entire component or elements within a component, or as a circular shape over part of the component.
Some components, such as buttons or cards, can inherit elevation to signify a pressed state.
Pressed states **are** inherited by action, selection, and some containment components: {#isPasted}

* Buttons
* Cards  
  {#isPasted}
* Checkbox  
* Chips  
* List items  
* Text fields
Pressed states **aren't** inherited by communication, navigation, or some containment components: {#isPasted}

* App bars
* Badges
* Bottom navigation
* Dialogs
* Menus
* Sheets
* Tabs
### Behavior {#isPasted}

Pressed states are initiated by user keyboard or voice input on an interactive element.
Pressed states can be combined with hovered, focused, activated, or selected states.
There may only be a single pressed state at a time in a layout.
Dragged
-------
A dragged state occurs when a user presses and moves an element or component. Dragged states should be low emphasis, to avoid distracting users from their task.{#isPasted}

Dragged states use a lower emphasis overlay. It can be applied to the entire component or to elements within a component.

Some components, such as list items, chips, or cards, can inherit elevation to signify a dragged state.
Dragged states **are** inherited by some containment and selection components: {#isPasted}

* Cards
* Chips
* List items
* Sliders
Dragged states **aren't** inherited by action, communication, navigation, or some containment components: {#isPasted}

* App bars
* Badges
* Buttons
* Dialogs
* Menus
* Navigation bar, drawer, and rail
### Behavior {#isPasted}

Dragged states are initiated when users touch and hold elements, using an input method such as a tap or click.
There may only be a single dragged state at a time within a layout.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## State layers
State layers {#isPasted}
------------------------
A state layer is a semi-transparent covering on an element that indicates its state. State layers provide a systematic approach to visualizing states by using opacity. A layer can be applied to an entire element or in a circular shape and only one state layer can be applied at a given time.
To transition from an enabled style to a stateful style requires the addition of a state layer.

The state layer is an overlay with a fixed opacity for each state and uses the same color as the content.

For example, if the enabled style uses the **secondary container** color for the container and **on secondary container** for content, the state layer will be an overlay using the **on secondary container** color.

If the enabled style uses the **surface** color for the container and the **primary** color role for content, then the state layer will be an overlay using the **primary** color.
The size of state layers is 40dp while the interactive target size is 48dp.
### On colors {#isPasted}

By default, a component's state layer color is derived from the color of its content, either the color of an icon or label text if no icon is present.

An [on color](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles#9690b320-2feb-40af-9080-7dacad21aa91) is a color role used by the content. Each container color has its own corresponding on color.   

For example, if a container color is **secondary container** , the content will use the **on secondary container** color role.
### State layer tokens \& values

The state layer uses a fixed percentage for the opacity for each state. A state layer uses the color used by content (usually the [on color](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles#9690b320-2feb-40af-9080-7dacad21aa91)) and the percentage opacity for its respective state.
### Activated states {#isPasted}

Unlike hover, focus, pressed, and dragged states that use state layers, the container and content of activated components change color directly.

Activated components use the **secondary container** color for the component container and change the content color to **on secondary container**.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## How to adopt
Planning \& process
-------------------

1. Identify your team's cross-functional owners, including PM, PgM, UX, and Eng
2. Walk through the adoption criteria as a cross-functional group
3. Conduct an audit to determine which features apply to your product
4. Complete engineering prerequisites
5. Create a design proposal and set up a [Material UX Review](http://goto.google.com/gdp-ux-review)
6. Create an implementation roadmap with feature release milestones
7. Make it happen
Actionable migrations
---------------------

1P engineering teams should fully migrate their code and GMDC component libraries to G3. This migration updates code and components with new features like the foundational GM3 color mappings and accessibility updates. Developer documentation and guidance for this migration are available at [go/gm3-compose](http://go/gm3-compose) and [go/gm3-views](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/g3doc/material-next-home.md?cl=head){#isPasted}. This migration is not equivalent to adopting GM3. Component redesigns will be released and required annually.
Foundational features {#isPasted}
---------------------------------
star  
Note:  
The typography and motion features were updated for GM3 Expressive. Foundational features are the basic criteria for creating a product using GM3.
### Color
[Baseline scheme](/google-material-3/pages/static/gm3-baseline)  

[User dynamic color](/google-material-3/pages/dynamic/choosing-a-source)  

[User-controlled contrast](/google-material-3/pages/color/how-the-system-works#a360c176-b99e-4804-8034-9884833009c8)
### Typography {#isPasted}
GM3 type scale:[](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens){#isPasted}

* [Static font theme](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens) (Google Sans \& [Google Sans Text](/google-material-3/pages/typography/fonts#414cc759-0992-47a4-891d-2ab0575c9cfd){#isPasted})
* [Baseline type styles](/google-material-3/pages/typography/type-scale-tokens)
### Motion
No current criteria. The easing and duration system is no longer required.{#isPasted}

Consider adopting the recommended [motion physics system](/google-material-3/pages/motion/how-it-works/).
### Components
