# Elevation
Elevation is the distance between two surfaces on the z-axis

## Applying elevation
**Material 3's elevation system is deliberately limited to just a handful of levels. This creative constraint means you need to make thoughtful decisions about your UI's elevation story.**
Depicting elevation
-------------------
Elevation can be depicted using shadows or other visual cues, such as surface fills with a tone difference or scrims.
To successfully depict elevation, a surface must show:{#isPasted}

* Surface edges, contrasting the surface from its surroundings
* Overlap with other surfaces, either at rest or in motion
* Distance from other surfaces
### Tonal difference
Tonal difference between surfaces helps to express the tactile quality of Material surfaces. They show where one surface ends and another begins by separating different parts of a UI into identifiable components. For example, the edges of an app bar show that it is separate from a grid list, communicating to the user that the grid list scrolls independently of the app bar.

By default, Material 3's surfaces use tonal difference to indicate separation. Other methods can be used to indicate edges, such as:{#isPasted}

* Giving surfaces a drop shadow
* Placing a scrim behind a surface
For interactive components, edges must create sufficient contrast between surfaces (by meeting or exceeding accessible contrast ratios) for them to be seen as separate from one another.
### Surface color roles and elevation
You can pick from a range of surface and surface container color roles. These roles are not tied to elevation, and provide flexibility for defining containment areas.  

Any overlapping containment areas or components should have different color roles in order to visually communicate separation.  

[Guideline on surface color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles#90a5e0bf-896c-4b5c-ade2-73d10688bac4)
### *Deprecated* -- Surface tint overlays {#isPasted}

Before [introducing the surface color roles](https://material.io/blog/tone-based-surface-color-m3) in March 2023, surface elevation was communicated through tint overlays. Material Design no longer supports those overlays, and they should not be used in combination with the new surface color roles.{#isPasted}

For products that haven't yet migrated to the new surface color roles, here is reference information on the now-deprecated tint overlays:

*Surfaces at elevation levels +1 to +5 are tinted via color overlays based on the primary color, such as app bars or menus. The addition of a grade from +1 to +5 introduces tonal variation to the surface baseline.*
Shadows
-------
Shadows can express the degree of elevation between surfaces in ways that other techniques cannot.{#isPasted}

Both a shadow's size and amount of softness or diffusion express the degree of distance between two surfaces. For example, a surface with a shadow that is small and sharp indicates a surface's close proximity to the surface behind it. Larger, softer shadows express more distance.
When it comes to applying shadows, less is more. The fewer levels in your UI, the more power they have to direct attention and action.
### When to use visible shadows
#### **Protect elements** {#isPasted}

When a background is patterned or visually busy, the hairline style might not provide sufficient protection. In these cases, use elevation to separate and emphasize elements such as cards, chips, or buttons, as in the image below.
#### **Encourage interaction** {#isPasted}

Elements can temporarily lift on focus, selection, or another kind of interaction, like swipe. A raised element can also lower when a higher element appears
Scrims
------
A scrim can bring focus to specific elements by increasing the visual contrast of a large layered surface. Use the scrim beneath elements like modals and expanded navigation menus.

Scrims use the scrim color role at an opacity of 32%.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Tokens
Tokens
------
Elevation levels can be implemented with tokens. Surface tint color is deprecated --- use elevation level tokens (0--5) instead. [Learn more about design tokens](/google-material-3/pages/design-tokens/overview)
Component elevation
-------------------
Most components have a default elevation. Component elevation is only used to determine where the component sits in relation to other components, including when hovered or focused (which usually raises elevation by one level). Elevation has no shadow or value of its own by default.
| Resting level |                                                                                                                                                            Component                                                                                                                                                            | DP Height |
|       5       |                                                                                                                                                 (not assigned as resting level)                                                                                                                                                 |   12dp    |
|       4       |                                                                                                                                                 (not assigned as resting level)                                                                                                                                                 |    8dp    |
|       3       |                                                                                                                    Date pickers Dialogs (modal) Extended FAB FAB FAB Menu (close button) Search Time pickers                                                                                                                    |    6dp    |
|       2       |                                                                                                                             App bar (scrolled){#isPasted} Menu Navigation bar Rich tooltip Toolbar                                                                                                                              |    3dp    |
|       1       |                                                                                             Banner{#isPasted} Bottom sheet (modal) Button (elevated) Card (elevated) Chips (elevated) Navigation drawer (modal) Side sheet (modal)                                                                                              |    1dp    |
|       0       | App Bar (not scrolled){#isPasted} Buttons (filled, tonal, outlined) Button groups Cards (filled, outlined) Carousel Chips Dialog (full-screen) Extended FAB (in navigation rail) FAB (in navigation rail) FAB Menu (list items) Icon buttons List Navigation rail Segmented button Side sheet (docked) Slider Split button Tabs |    0dp    |
|---------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------|
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
* Elevation is applied to all surfaces and components
* Tokens codify the distance on the z-axis to ensure components appear consistently relative to each other
* Tokens have no shadows or color; each platform determines the specific shadows and values to use at each elevation level
* Elevation can be shown as tonal surface colors or shadows
* Avoid changing the default elevation of Material 3 components
* Stick to using a small amount of elevation levels
**Elevation is measured as the distance between components along the z-axis in density-independent pixels (dps).**
Resources
---------
{#isPasted}

|                |                                                             Resource                                                             |         Contact          |  Status   |
|----------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| Design         | [Design Kit (Figma)](https://www.figma.com/design/66FLhHgKhumes3j72fexFp/GM3-Design-Kit?node-id=0-1)                             | gm-design-kit@           | Available |
| Implementation | [Android Views](https://developer.android.com/reference/com/google/android/material/elevation/ElevationOverlayProvider)          | material-android-team@   | Available |
| Implementation | [Flutter](https://go/gm3-flutter#elevation)                                                                                      | material-flutter-team@   | Available |
| Implementation | [iOS](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/ux/material/system/ios/g3doc/foundations/styles/Elevation/index.md?cl=head&polyglot=swiftui) | material-iOS-team@       | Available |
| Implementation | [Jetpack Compose](https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/compose/designsystems/material3#elevation)                            | material-compose-team@   | Available |
| Implementation | [MWC](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/third_party/javascript/material/web/g3doc/docs/components/elevation.md?cl=head)              | material-web-components@ | Available |
| Implementation | [Wiz](https://go/gm3-wiz-tokens#how-to-use-tokens-in-a-wiz-product)                                                              | material-web-team@       | Available |
Differences from GM2
--------------------
* Shadows: Instead of applying shadows by default to all levels, use shadows only when required to create additional protection against a background or to encourage interaction
* Color: New color mappings and compatibility with dynamic color
* Levels: Elevation is now described in terms of levels
All surfaces and components have elevation values
-------------------------------------------------
Surfaces at different elevations do the following:{#isPasted}

1. Allow surfaces to move in front of and behind other surfaces, such as content scrolling behind app bars
2. Reflect spatial relationships, such as how a FAB's shadow indicates it is separate from a card collection
3. Focus attention on the highest elevation, such as a dialog temporarily appearing in front of other surfaces
### Resting elevation (default)
All components have a default resting elevation. Avoid changing the default elevation of Material components.
### Changing elevation
Components should change elevation in response to system events or user interaction, like hovering. This elevation change should be consistent across all similar elements.

For example, hovering a FAB temporarily increases the elevation by 1 level, from level 3 to level 4. All Material buttons increase elevation by 1 level when hovered.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Transitions
Transitions help guide people as they navigate an app

## Applying transitions
star  
Note:  
GM3 transitions use the legacy easing and duration system. They'll eventually be updated to use the motion physics system.
What makes a good transition?
-----------------------------

Well-designed transitions should have these characteristics:
### Follows accessibility settings {#isPasted}

Most platforms have a reduced animation setting to help users with a sensitivity to motion. If that setting is on, transitions should:

* Use subtle fades instead of intense sliding or scaling animations
* Disable decorative effects like parallax or shape morphing
### Consistent {#isPasted}

Consistently applying the right type of transition helps make apps feel cohesive and predictable to use.
### Stable layouts {#isPasted}

Use skeleton loaders so that UI elements are coherent and stable during a transition. Avoid content shifting positions or instantly popping in as it loads. It can be distracting and frustrating to use.
### No jarring jump cuts {#isPasted}

Jump cuts should generally be avoided as a default setting since they can be disorienting. Instantly transitioning from one screen to the next offers no clues to help a user orient themselves.

If pure efficiency is a top priority, like opening a menu in a productivity app, a jump cut may be preferred.
### Coherent spatial model {#isPasted}

Transitions are used to establish a coherent spatial model. This helps users understand the physical layout of an app.
### Unified direction {#isPasted}

A transition should have a unified direction of movement. Elements are grouped and move along a primary axis instead of moving in independent directions. Only important elements like hero images remain persistent throughout the transition. This helps guide a users focus.
### Clean fades {#isPasted}

Fully fade out content before fading new content in. This avoids the overlap of partially transparent elements resulting in distracting and messy frames.

If a cross fade needs to occur, keep it quick and hide it during the fastest part of the transition.
Don't slowly fade components on top of other content as they enter or exit. This creates distracting cross faded frames. If a fade is needed, like with a Dialog entering in the middle of the screen, the fade should use a short duration to hide that part of the transition.
### Simple style {#isPasted}

Transitions are not receptive to highly stylized motion. They're frequent, often occupy large portions of the screen, and are primarily meant to help users accomplish a task.
Choosing a transition pattern {#isPasted}
-----------------------------------------

Consider the following to choose the right transition for a given use case:
### Container transform {#isPasted}
This pattern is highly effective at creating a relationship between elements. It's also the most dramatic pattern in terms of style and should be reserved for the right context. Consider using it for:{#isPasted}

1. Hero moments that should be expressive
2. Shallow hierarchies where you expand an element for more detail then collapse it
3. Creating a seamless connection between elements

**Read the research for the benefits of container transform** **[here](https://material.io/blog/motion-research-container-transform).**
Use a container transform transition for hero moments rather than a forward and backward transition.
### Forward and backward {#isPasted}
Both Android and iOS should use platform defaults for forward and backward navigation. It's easy to implement and stays current as platforms update. They have a simple motion style suitable for such a common transition.
### Lateral {#isPasted}
Lateral transitions are used to browse peer content that's part of the same set, like navigating between tabs in a media library. By sliding content horizontally, it hints at being able to swipe the content area to navigate between peers.
Don't use a Lateral transition for navigating hierarchical screens. Sliding content the full width of the screen is excessive for a high frequency transition. It also implies an equal peer relationship which isn't accurate to the hierarchy of the screens.
### Top level {#isPasted}
When tapping a navigation bar, rail or drawer, a quick fade is used to transition to a new destination. Top level destinations aren't necessarily related, so the motion intentionally does not create a connection between screens.{#isPasted}

A lateral transition pattern is not recommended for this type of navigation. It implies you can swipe between top level destinations which conflicts with other components like carousels or swipe-able list items.
### Enter and Exit {#isPasted}
This transition pattern is used to introduce a component in context of the screen's main UI. It can be modal, like a dialog requiring a user to take action. Or it can allow for simultaneously using both regions of the UI, like a standard bottom sheet over a map.{#isPasted}

Don't use this pattern for navigating hierarchical screens. Sliding content the full height of the screen is excessive and it creates an unclear relationship between screens.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Specs
warning  
Caution:  
The Direct tool is not actively maintained, causing issues with these specs. The spec values are still available for reference, but the animations won't play. These specs are for the legacy easing and duration system --- not the motion physics system.
Container transform {#isPasted}
-------------------------------

**Commonly used with:**Cards, lists, image galleries, search boxes, FABs, and chips
Forward and backward {#isPasted}
--------------------------------

With the fade through version, the start screen fades out and then the end screen fades in. This creates a cleaner style, but it requires knowing the background color to achieve. In cases where it's not feasible to know the background color, a cross fade version can be used. The cross fade version doesn't require knowing the background color making it easier to implement.

**Commonly used with** **:**Lists, cards, buttons, links
Top level {#isPasted}
---------------------

**Commonly used with:**Navigation bar, navigation rail, and navigation drawer
Enter and exit {#isPasted}
--------------------------

#### Within screen bounds {#isPasted}

**Commonly used with:** FABs, dialogs, menus, snackbars, time pickers and tooltips
#### Beyond screen bounds {#isPasted}

**Commonly used with:**App bars, banners, navigation bar, navigation rail, navigation drawer, and sheets
Skeleton loader {#isPasted}
---------------------------

## Implementation
Container transform {#isPasted}
-------------------------------

**Commonly used with:** Cards, Lists, Image galleries, Search boxes, Sheets, FABs, and Chips.
Use a MaterialContainerTransform Transition from the [Material Android library](https://github.com/material-components/material-components-android/blob/master/docs/theming/Motion.md#container-transform) to create shared element transitions between Activities, Fragments, or Views. {#isPasted}

In the example, an item in a RecyclerView is given a transitionName that is shared by the root ViewGroup in the destination Fragment. The destination Fragment sets its sharedElementEnterTransition to a MaterialContainerTransform in onCreate, which handles transforming and swapping the two Views. For a working example, see the sample app, [Reply](https://github.com/material-components/material-components-android-examples/blob/develop/Reply/app/src/main/java/com/materialstudies/reply/ui/email/EmailFragment.kt).
Forward and Backward {#isPasted}
--------------------------------

**Commonly used with** : Lists, Cards, Buttons, Links
When activity transitions are enabled in your app, the default transition is activated between the entering and existing activities. This is the simplest way to implement this transition.{#isPasted}

If your app isn't built with activities, use the Shared x-axis transition from the [Material motion library](https://github.com/material-components/material-components-android/blob/master/docs/theming/Motion.md#shared-axis). It supports activities, fragments and views.
### iOS {#isPasted}

Use the default transition supplied by [UINavigationController](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/uikit/uinavigationcontroller).
Lateral
-------

**Commonly used with:** Tabs, carousels, and image galleries
### Tabs

Use the Material Tab component [here](https://material.io/components/tabs/android).
### Carousels

(Hidden until carousel component launches)
Top level
---------

**Commonly used with:** Navigation bar, Navigation rail, and Navigation drawer
Use the Fade through transition from the [Material motion library](https://github.com/material-components/material-components-android/blob/master/docs/theming/Motion.md#fade-through) for Android. It supports activities, fragments and views.
Enter and exit within screen bounds
-----------------------------------

**Commonly used with:** FABs, dialogs, menus, snackbars, time pickers and tooltips
### FAB
### Dialog
### Menu
### Snackbar
Enter and exit beyond screen bounds
-----------------------------------

**Commonly used with:** App bars, banners, navigation bar, navigation drawer, and sheets
### App bar
### Navigation Bar
### Navigation drawer
### Bottom sheets

Use the Material Bottom sheet component [here](https://material.io/components/sheets-bottom/android#using-bottom-sheets){#isPasted}.
Skeleton loaders
----------------
(Hidden until we have an implementation solution)

## Transition patterns
star  
Note:  
GM3 transitions use the legacy easing and duration system. They'll eventually be updated to use the motion physics system.
Transitions are short animations that connect individual elements or full-screen views of an app. They are fundamental to a great user experience because they help users understand how an app works. Well-designed transitions makes an experience feel high quality and expressive. They should be the top priority for a strong motion implementation.{#isPasted}

These are six common transition patterns:

1. [Container transform](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/transition-patterns/latest#f54970a2-3841-4a67-9488-aeb44abd8e69)
2. [Forward and backward](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/transition-patterns/latest#87b30c4c-3350-4daf-8150-4045916e7d02)
3. [Lateral](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/transition-patterns/latest#6d722921-5814-4f40-ace7-9c350854484b)
4. [Top level](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/transition-patterns/latest#9da2ddb2-fd7d-4ab2-bdcc-fe903422a191)
5. [Enter and exit](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/transition-patterns/latest#23b34725-e5af-451f-b4c2-d52f6691bb7d)
6. [Skeleton loaders](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/transition-patterns/latest#6e61fc43-bf53-4ad7-87a1-2f7d3747a929)
Container transform {#isPasted}
-------------------------------

This pattern is used to seamlessly transform an element to show more detail, like a Card expanding into a details page.

* **Commonly used with:** Cards, lists, image galleries, search boxes, sheets, FABs, and chips
* **Read more:** [UX Research](https://material.io/blog/motion-research-container-transform), [Guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/applying-transitions#b5d8e72d-df6d-47a0-b486-2c85d7d97e35), [Android implementation](https://github.com/material-components/material-components-android/blob/master/docs/theming/Motion.md#container-transform)
Persistent elements are used to seamlessly connect the start and end state of the transition. The most common persistent element is a container, which is a shape used to represent an enclosed area. It can also be an important element, like a hero image. Of all transition patterns, this one creates the strongest relationship between elements. It's also perceived to be the most expressive.
#### Between full-screen views {#isPasted}
#### Within a screen {#isPasted}
Forward and backward {#isPasted}
--------------------------------

This pattern is used for navigating between screens at consecutive levels of hierarchy, like navigating from an inbox to a message thread.

* **Commonly used with**: Lists, cards, buttons, links
* **Read more:** [Guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/applying-transitions#0a8bce3f-40f4-42a3-86d5-a22e5b27fb12), [](https://github.com/material-components/material-components-android/blob/master/docs/theming/Motion.md#shared-axis) [Android implementation](https://github.com/material-components/material-components-android/blob/master/docs/theming/Motion.md#shared-axis){#isPasted}
A horizontal sliding motion indicates moving forward or backward between screens. Android and iOS have different default styles:{#isPasted}

**1. Android** uses a fade as screens slide. This reduces the amount of motion, since the screens don't have to slide the full width of the device.

**2. iOS** uses a parallax effect, meaning the background slides slower than the foreground. This also reduces the amount of motion.
Lateral {#isPasted}
-------------------

This pattern is used for navigating between peer content at the same level of hierarchy, like swiping between tabs of a content library.

* **Commonly used with:**Tabs, carousels, and image galleries
* **Read more:** [Guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/applying-transitions#1e18ed4d-3174-4085-b7ed-1746674bb82f)
Lateral transitions use a sliding motion similar to a forward and backward pattern, but it does not use a fade or parallax effect. Instead elements are grouped and slide in unison, creating a strong peer relationship. This also hints at being able to gesturally swipe elements to navigate.
Top level {#isPasted}
---------------------

This pattern is used to navigate between top-level destinations of an app, like tapping a destination in a Navigation bar.

* **Commonly used with:** Navigation bar, navigation rail, and navigation drawer
* **Read more:** [Guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/applying-transitions#048ea44b-1325-4e36-a1c9-8a29fe0c20ea), [Android implementation](https://github.com/material-components/material-components-android/blob/master/docs/theming/Motion.md#fade-through)
The exiting screen quickly fades out and then the entering screen fades in. Since the content of top level destinations isn't necessarily related, the motion intentionally does not use grouping or persistent elements to create a strong relationship between screens.
Enter and exit {#isPasted}
--------------------------

This pattern is used to introduce or remove a component on the screen. Components can enter and exit within the screen bounds, like a dialog appearing over an app. They can also enter and exit by crossing the screen bounds, like a navigation drawer or bottom sheet that slides on and off screen.
### Within screen bounds {#isPasted}

* **Commonly used with:** FABs, dialogs, menus, snackbars, time pickers and tooltips
* **Read more:** [Guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/applying-transitions#48b39db4-98b4-422a-9bb3-9350463aaef0)[](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/specs#40ea3880-65ad-4a3a-a5e7-fab7c8cf3fbd){#isPasted}
**Android** components expand and collapse along the x or y axis as they enter and exit. Scale and z-axis motion is avoided since they imply elevation change, which doesn't match GM3's reduced elevation model.{#isPasted}

**iOS** components uniformly scale as they enter and fade out to exit.
The direction a component enters is informed by their location on screen, expanding away from the device edge. A menu at the top of the screen expands downwards, and a snackbar at the bottom of the screen expands upwards.
### Beyond screen bounds {#isPasted}

* **Commonly used with:**App bars, banners, navigation bar, navigation rail, navigation drawer, and sheets
* **Read more:** [Guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/applying-transitions#48b39db4-98b4-422a-9bb3-9350463aaef0)[](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/specs#06576451-0af2-4f72-a4ac-257c7564f6a5)
**Android** components expand and collapse along the x or y axis as they slide on and off screen. This emphasizes their shape, making an otherwise simple transition more expressive.{#isPasted}

**iOS** components slide on and off screen without changing shape.
Components like a side sheet can also enter and exit at the same elevation as the main content. Coplanar sheets shrink the available area for content.
Components can enter and exit from beyond the screen bounds based on a scroll gesture. This allows for more screen space to browse.
The location components enter and exit help establish a coherent spatial model of an app:{#isPasted}

1. A **notification** enters from the top indicating the notification drawer can also be pulled down from the top
2. A **nav drawer** enters from the left helping users understand where it's located when it's off screen
3. A **bottom sheet** and the keyboard enters from the bottom of the screen. This is a sensible default location for sheets to enter since the bottom of the screen is easiest to reach.
Skeleton loaders {#isPasted}
----------------------------

This pattern is used to transition from a temporary loading state to a fully loaded UI.{#isPasted}

*

  #### **Read more:** [Guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/transitions/applying-transitions#93de2382-b8e0-4bf2-8a74-329ce246ce3a) {#isPasted}
Skeleton loaders are UI abstractions that hint at where content will appear once it's loaded. They're used in combination with other transitions to reduce perceived latency and stabilize layouts as content loads.{#isPasted}

<br />
Skeleton loaders have a subtle pulsing animation to indicate indeterminate progress. It starts at the top left of the screen and moves down to the bottom right.{#isPasted}

Once content is loaded, it quickly fades in on top of the skeleton loader.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Color roles
There are 26 standard color roles organized into six groups: primary, secondary, tertiary, error, surface, and outline

What are color roles?
---------------------

Color roles are like the "numbers" in a paint-by-number canvas. They're the connective tissue between elements of the UI and what color goes where.

* **Color roles are mapped to Material Components**   
  You'll use these color roles whether you're using the static baseline scheme or dynamic color. If your product contains custom components, they'll need to be properly mapped to this set of color roles.
* **Color roles ensure accessibility**   
  The color system is built on accessible color pairings. These color pairs provide an accessible minimum 3:1 contrast.
* **Color roles are tokenized**  
  Roles are implemented in design and code through tokens. A design token represents a small, reusable design decision that's part of a design system's visual style.
General concepts
----------------

Here are helpful-to-know words you'll see in the names of color roles:

* **Surface**-- A role used for backgrounds and large, low-emphasis areas of the screen.
* **Primary, Secondary, Tertiary** -- Accent color roles used to emphasize or de-emphasize foreground elements.
* **Container** -- Roles used as a fill color for foreground elements like buttons. They should not be used for text or icons.
* **On** -- Roles starting with this term indicate a color for text or icons *on top* of its paired parent color. For example, **on primary** is used for text and icons against the **primary** fill color.
* **Variant** -- Roles ending with this term offer a lower emphasis alternative to its non-variant pair. For example, **outline variant** is a less emphasized version of the **outline**color.
### Pairing and layering colors

To ensure accessible visual contrast in your app, apply colors only in the intended pairs or layering orders described in the following sections.

Combining colors improperly may break contrast necessary for visual accessibility, particularly when colors are adjusted through dynamic color features such as user-controlled contrast.
Accent color roles
------------------
Accent color roles include primary, secondary, and tertiary. Assign them to elements in the UI based on importance and needed emphasis. Use caution when changing color roles for visual effect.
* **Primary roles** are for important actions and elements needing the most emphasis, like a FAB to start a new message.  

  {#isPasted}
* **Secondary roles** are for elements that don't need immediate attention and don't need emphasis, like the selected state of a navigation icon or a dismissive button.  

  {#isPasted}
* **Tertiary roles**are for smaller elements that need special emphasis but don't require immediate attention, such as a badge or notification.{#isPasted}
Primary
-------
Use primary roles for the most prominent components across the UI, such as the FAB, high-emphasis buttons, and active states.
* **Primary:** High-emphasis fills, texts, and icons against surface  

* **On primary:** Text and icons against primary  

* **Primary container:** Standout fill color against surface, for key components like FAB  

* **On primary container:**Text and icons against primary container
{#isPasted}
Secondary
---------
Use secondary roles for less prominent components in the UI such as filter chips.
There are four secondary roles:{#isPasted}

* **Secondary:** Less prominent fills, text, and icons against surface  

* **On secondary:** Text and icons against secondary  

* **Secondary container:** Less prominent fill color against surface, for recessive components like tonal buttons  

* **On secondary container:** Text and icons against secondary container
Tertiary
--------
Use tertiary roles for contrasting accents that balance primary and secondary colors or bring heightened attention to an element such as an input field.
There are four tertiary roles:

* **Tertiary:** Complementary fills, text, and icons against surface  

* **On tertiary:** Text and icons against tertiary  

* **Tertiary container:** Complementary container color against surface, for components like input fields  

* **On tertiary container:**Text and icons against tertiary container
The tertiary color roles can be applied at the designer's discretion. They're intended to support broader color expression.
Error
-----
Use error roles to communicate error states, such as an incorrect password entered into a text field.
There are four error roles:

* **Error:** Attention-grabbing color against surface for fills, icons, and text, indicating urgency  

* **On error:** Text and icons against error  

* **Error container:** Attention-grabbing fill color against surface  

* **On error container:** Text and icons against error container

Error is an example of a static color (it doesn't change even in dynamic color schemes). Error color roles are made static by default with any dynamic color scheme. They still adapt to light and dark theme.
Surface
-------
Use surface roles for more neutral backgrounds, and container colors for components like cards, sheets, and dialogs.
There are three surface roles:

* **Surface:** Default color for backgrounds  

* **On surface:** Text and icons against any **surface** or **surface container** color  

* **On surface variant:** Lower-emphasis color for text and icons against any **surface** or **surface container** color
There are also five surface container roles named based on their level of emphasis:

* **Surface container lowest:** Lowest-emphasis container color  

* **Surface container low:** Low-emphasis container color  

* **Surface container:** Default container color  

* **Surface container high:** High-emphasis container color  

* **Surface container highest:** Highest-emphasis container color

{#isPasted}

**Surface container** is the default role, but the others are especially helpful for creating hierarchy and nested containers in [layouts for expanded screens](/google-material-3/pages/applying-layout/expanded){#isPasted}.
The most common combination of surface roles uses **surface** for a background area and **surface container** for a navigation area.

Text and icons typically use **on surface** and**on surface variant**on all types of surfaces.
All color mappings -- but especially surface colors -- should remain the same for layout regions across window size classes. For example, the body area will use the **surface** color and the navigation area will use the **surface container** color on both mobile and tablet.
Depending on necessary hierarchy, feature area, and design logic, you can use [add-on surface container colors](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles#ba786092-5e05-4582-9295-c3a8f916eedf) in larger window class sizes as long as colors are consistently applied.
By default, neutral-colored components such as navigation bars, menus, or dialogs are mapped to specific surface container roles, but these [roles can be remapped](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/apply-colors/latest#149a3fa7-e126-43f6-9010-ac5a8f5c7741) by makers to suit user needs.
### Inverse colors {#isPasted}

Inverse roles are applied selectively to components to achieve colors that are the reverse of those in the surrounding UI, creating a contrasting effect.

* **Inverse surface:** Background fills for elements which contrast against surface  

  {#isPasted}
* **Inverse on surface:** Text and icons against inverse surface  

* **I** **nverse primary:** Actionable elements, such as text buttons, against inverse surface
### *Deprecated* -- Surface tint overlays {#isPasted}

Before [introducing the surface color roles](https://material.io/blog/tone-based-surface-color-m3) in March 2023, surface elevation was communicated through tint overlays. Material Design no longer supports those overlays, and they should not be used in combination with the new surface color roles.
For products that haven't yet migrated to the new surface color roles, here is reference information on the now-deprecated tint overlays: {#isPasted}

* *Surface 1: At elevation +1 components with surface color containers receive a surface-tint color overlay with****5% opacity*** *.*
* *Surface 2:* *At elevation +2 components with surface color containers receive a surface-tint color overlay with****8% opacity*** *.*
* *Surface 3:* *At elevation +3 components with surface color containers receive a surface-tint color overlay with****11% opacity*** *.*
* *Surface 4:* *At elevation +4 components with surface color containers receive a surface-tint color overlay with****12% opacity*** *.*
* *Surface 5:* *At elevation +5 components with surface color containers receive a surface-tint color overlay with****14% opacity*** *.*

*Surface 4 and 5 are used only for elevated FAB states. At rest, a FAB is Surface 3. Upon hover, it becomes Surface 4; when pressed it becomes Surface 5.*
Outline
-------
There are two outline colors to be used against a surface:

* **Outline:** Important boundaries, such as a text field outline  

* **Outline variant:** Decorative elements, such as dividers, and when other elements provide 4.5:1 contrast
Add-on color roles
------------------
Most products won't need to use these add-on color roles. However, some products require the greater flexibility and control that add-on roles provide. If you aren't sure whether your product should use the add-on roles, it probably shouldn't and you can ignore them.
### Fixed accent colors {#isPasted}

**Primary fixed, secondary fixed,** and **t** **ertiary fixed** are fill colors used against surface. These colors maintain the same tone in light and dark themes, as opposed to regular container colors, which change in tone between these themes. The fixed color role may be used instead of the equivalent container role in situations where such fixed behavior is desired.

The **primary fixed dim, secondary fixed dim,** and**tertiary fixed dim** roles provide a stronger, more emphasized tone relative to the equivalent fixed color. They may be used where a deeper color but the same fixed behavior is desired.
### On fixed accent colors

**On fixed** colors are used for text and icons which sit on top of the corresponding Fixed color. For example, **on** **primary fixed** is used for text and icons against the **primary fixed**color. The same usage applies for the equivalent secondary and tertiary colors.{#isPasted}

<br />

**On fixed variant** colors are used for text and icons needing lower emphasis against the corresponding fixed color. For example, **on primary fixed variant** is used for low emphasis text and icons against the **primary fixed**color. The same usage applies for the equivalent secondary and tertiary colors.
### Bright and dim surface roles {#isPasted}

There are two add-on surface roles:

* **Surface dim:** Dimmest surface color in light and dark themes
* **Surface bright:** Brightest surface color in light and dark themes
While the default **surface** color automatically inverts between light and dark themes (it's a light color in light theme and it flips to a dark color in dark theme), the **surface bright** and **surface dim** colors invert in a slightly different way. More precisely, they keep their relative brightness across both light and dark theme.

For example, in an interface using the default **surface** role, the mapped area is the brightest in light theme and the dimmest in dark theme. In an interface using the **surface bright** role, the mapped area is the brightest in both light and dark theme.
### Generative AI color roles

There are six [custom color roles](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/define-new-colors#89baf21a-12ba-40fb-a9ae-950812a8a334) you can add to your system to use for AI elements:

1. AI main
2. AI analog
3. AI analog variant
4. AI complement
5. AI complement variant
6. AI muted

These six new colors are derived from a product's existing primary color. The AI colors are an additional palette used only for Generative AI UI elements. The AI colors are made to be eye-catching without distracting from existing layouts. [Learn more about generative AI colors](/google-material-3/pages/ai/color-gradient)
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Notifications
Notifications help people see information they need or want at the right time

## Security
If you think your product might need security notifications, which are commonly used to notify users about logins, changed passwords, and compromised accounts, get in touch with the [Account Notifier](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/userhub/what-we-offer/privacy-and-security/security-notifications?authuser=0) team for a full security notification flow.
### Reassure that security is taken seriously {#isPasted}

Sound human, but convey Google's respect for users' security. Avoid jokes, puns, or a casual tone.
### Convey risk for critical notifications {#isPasted}

If an account may be compromised, help users take action quickly. Use terms like: critical, risk, urgent, unrecognized, suspicious, immediately, and take action. See [research](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1BLVWg61pc82Xf_UH-wvjaYNRKuQBPuWVG-Tl8-1-oNk/edit?usp=sharing) about new device notifications.
### Be informative for non-critical notifications {#isPasted}

When a security-related action took place, but Google has no indication it wasn't done by the user, reassure the user that Google has their back. Help someone quickly understand the purpose of the notification without needing to click on it, and don't alarm them.
### Advise confidently {#isPasted}

People look to Google to advise them how to stay safe. It's ok to [recommend next steps](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lZNyIyK_vsZ3YrkFySvABx4PT_5V_UoHtkyywCHuQMY/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.4oqhgjo9iw3z).
### Make risks tangible {#isPasted}

Help people understand the consequences of what's happened. People tend to underestimate the risk to them personally and to [ignore abstract warning](https://storage.googleapis.com/pub-tools-public-publication-data/pdf/43265.pdf)[s](https://storage.googleapis.com/pub-tools-public-publication-data/pdf/43265.pdf).
### Avoid technical or unfamiliar terms {#isPasted}

People aren't familiar with Google terminology, and may [ignore notifications they don't understand](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1dqJiCwcRkBCze-Oq2uc2M6V0pdYJVnvKlngZ2wp3l0g/edit?resourcekey=0-cViJTraBp1L2hrYsiZHDaQ#slide=id.gd64d939ac5_0_377). This is especially problematic when hackers take sensitive actions. Avoid rarely-used feature names, setting names, or device models the user might not recognize.
### Attribute activity to the right actor {#isPasted}

Taking responsibility for users' actions or communicating the action was theirs without knowing for sure can cause misunderstandings and may have legal implications. It's OK to use passive voice to maintain accuracy.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## General
Notifications should:{#isPasted}

* Be about the user, not the product{#isPasted}
* Be precise, timely, actionable, contextual, and relevant{#isPasted}
* Give users easy controls to opt out{#isPasted}
* Not be used to send unsolicited ads{#isPasted}

Your notification is just one of many the user has to process. See the [Google Notifications prefab](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview/tab-1#e4f99402-61f1-474a-ad01-aff0a16f2ec5) and [more notification writing guidance](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1YrtFEWPN_FBdWPw5zwCPwa1BiKiVNUQqBC1b5Ztbl_U/edit?pli=1&tab=t.0).
### Creating notifications at Google {#isPasted}

Wherever possible, notifications should be implemented using the Google Notifications Platform (GNP), which can send notifications on all platforms, including in-app, push, SMS, web, desktop, and more. This ensures that all notifications are managed and sent using a single service, and that notifications are high-quality, trustworthy, and easy for internal teams to setup, manage, and track. See the [prefab](/google-material-3/pages/prefabs-overview#e4f99402-61f1-474a-ad01-aff0a16f2ec5) to get started designing and implementing a notification or [learn about the team](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/gant-platforms/gnp?pli=1).
### Put important information at the top {#isPasted}

Put the most important info at the beginning and make it easy to understand. People skim rather than read, often in an F shape. When it makes sense, try to move the most critical information to the front of sentences, rather than the end, where it may get overlooked or truncated.
### Tell users what they can do {#isPasted}

If you're prompting someone to take action, make that clear. CTAs should be concise, specific, and actionable. If you know what motivates people to take action, add it.   

If your notification includes buttons, see guidance for [buttons, links \& chips](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1lUqKkXXGx-E9jbdv3rzcnsuL5oMQUlPbSgzguRP3rxM/edit?resourcekey=0-Ej-OwjcPebUmzvYxXbIZJw#heading=h.4z1dtx8hi48v).
### Make it relevant and personal {#isPasted}

A notification shouldn't be sent to everyone. The more you broaden the audience and the message for a notification, the more you risk being irrelevant. Consider user segments, in-app behavior, and personalized information to make sure notifications go to people who will benefit most from them.
### Avoid dynamic text {#isPasted}

Dynamic text are words that are implemented by engineering to change based on the user or context, like adding a user's campaign name to headline or "good afternoon" when users log in at certain times.  

Try not to use dynamic text in notifications. It often breaks character limits, especially in headlines when translated. Text that gets truncated in the headline will not expand, even in expandable notifications. If you must use dynamic text in the headline, try to pair it with no more than one additional word. Create a backup notification that fits the character count when your primary notification won't.
### Mind your characters {#isPasted}

Stay within these suggested character counts so text doesn't get cut off:{#isPasted}

* Title: \<29 chars
* Collapsed body: \<40 chars
* Expanded body: \<80 chars; start with collapsed body and add to it
* Button: 1-2 buttons (1-2 words each)

<br />

There's more room for text on later versions of Android, but these limits are still recommended to prevent truncation on smaller devices.
### SMS messages {#isPasted}

SMS helps users get messages when they might not have access to their Google Account. It's used for important or urgent communication only.   

SMS breaks into multiple messages after a certain number of characters, resulting in increased costs. To avoid this, stick to the following character limits:{#isPasted}

* Latin languages: \<160 characters
* Non-latin languages: \<134 characters

<br />

If translation is needed, let translators know about the character limits in the message description.
### Emoji with caution {#isPasted}

Use emoji sparingly. Many don't translate and aren't universally understood across cultures. [Experiments](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1GJrr5k-XASZ0CKmg-Pu1SlRAjo-o5hSRpz46b2Zq6CY/edit#slide=id.g45d8f21999_0_213) show that face and hand gesture emoji perform better than generic emoji because they tell a better story. Emoji should enhance content, not replace it.  

Since it's not clear to users whether emoji mean we're sympathizing with them or attempting to project our feelings onto them, don't use emoji to accentuate bad news. In experiments, there was a strong negative reaction to negative emoji, such as frowning face, anguished face, and weary face.  

Gen Z adds another cultural nuance to emoji by [inventing new meanings](https://www.dictionary.com/e/gen-z-explains-emoji-to-millennials/) that go beyond the official or literal [definitions](https://emojipedia.org/).
### Don't overdo delight {#isPasted}

What seems funny or cute may not come across that way. When vying for limited user attention, be useful. Don't focus on delight: delicately added and tested polish can better support Google's brand.
### Be day-specific {#isPasted}

Use the days of the week. Don't use "today," "tomorrow," "tonight," or similar words. About 20% of users don't see notifications on the day they're sent, so "tomorrow" might be read when it's "today" for the user.  

An exception is when a notification auto-dismisses at a specific timestamp.
### Don't interrupt the flow {#isPasted}

If you have notifications or emails related to the onboarding process, make sure they don't trigger during onboarding.
### Don't name the product (again) {#isPasted}

An app's name or logo is already included in a notification's design. Use the limited space for other information.
### Opting in and out {#isPasted}

Give users a way to opt-out of notifications in context. If you don't, they'll have to dig into settings and may get frustrated. If you do offer opt-outs or opt-ins, make it clear what benefit the user is getting or losing.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug){#isPasted}


# Global writing
Writing for a global audience makes content work better for everyone

## Word choice
### Use global examples \& explain local references

References to local places, holidays, and companies won't always make sense to global audiences.
If it doesn't make sense to use a global example, explain the reference in the message description so the translator can substitute a locale-specific example. Some instances where local references should be called out include:

* Locations
* Names (common first names and nicknames)
* Currencies
* Temperatures
* Date formats
* Providers (internet and cable)
### Use short, simple sentences

Break text into shorter sentences. Use bullets or separate content into sections with headings. {#isPasted}

Other languages average at 1.5 times longer than English, so text that's short may be long when translated. Use [go/longest](http://go/longest) to see what your string could look like in other languages.
### Avoid abbreviations

Abbreviations don't translate well and can be confusing out of context. Spell things out whenever possible.{#isPasted}

However, common abbreviations for time are acceptable. Learn more about time abbreviations and best practices at [go/time-guidelines](http://go/time-guidelines).
### Clarify pronouns

Using pronouns, like "it," can get tricky when translators are working with small, unconnected strings of text and when nouns have genders in many languages. Repeat the noun, or clarify the noun in a message description.
### Clarify "this" and "that"

Don't start a sentence with "this" or "that" unless it's immediately followed by the noun. When the noun is unclear, the sentence is more difficult to translate.
### Avoid idiomatic, colloquial, and polite expressions

Idiomatic or colloquial phrases can be confusing if the meaning isn't clear. If you use them, clarify the purpose and context of the phrase to help the translator choose an appropriate alternative. See advice for [writing message descriptions](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/searchl10n/message-descriptions?authuser=0).{#isPasted}

Avoid polite expressions, such as "Please," "Sorry," and "Thank you," especially in error messages. However, "please" may be used when asking the user to do something inconvenient.
### Reduce technical jargon

Technical terms don't always translate. Descriptions should be simple, and in some cases literal, to avoid confusion when translating.
### Clarify ambiguities

Some words have multiple meanings. For example, "traffic," "filter," and "change" can be used as either nouns or verbs. Avoid using both meanings of the word in the same string or body of text. If a word has the potential to be confusing, provide as much context as possible in the message description so the translation will be accurate.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
### Global writing

Global writing is the practice of writing inclusively for many cultures and optimizing for localization. This makes writing easier to read, understand, and translate. Writing for localization is necessary when a product will be translated, but can also be a helpful lens when writing for anyone.
### Localization process

Although the localization process can differ slightly between teams, generally it involves UX or engineering preparing and submitting a document through [go/localize](http://go/localize) with final strings, message descriptions for translators, and screenshots with context. Questions from translators may come up through Buganizer or your localization project manager. Learn more about the [localization process](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/searchl10n/the-l10n-process).
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Writing for trust
It’s easier for users to trust something they understand

Users value great tech, but perceptions of brand integrity and [benevolence](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/benevolent-by-design?usp=sharing) matter just as much. In addition to foundational insights for content design, this curated collection points to resources and required companion UX policies from [Privacy UX](https://carbon.googleplex.com/privacy-ux-guidebook/pages/welcome), such as guidelines for [Notice \& Consent](https://carbon.googleplex.com/privacy-ux-guidebook/pages/notice-consent-ux).
### Start with the users' point of view

Empower user agency by providing information to make informed decisions, including tradeoffs or costs, and by clearly explaining the impact of settings and [choices](https://carbon.googleplex.com/privacy-ux-guidebook/pages/notice-consent-ux). To identify user needs, start with people's thoughts, sentiments, and questions from research.
* Leverage the power of "you." Use "you" to describe user actions, and phrases like "you can," "your info," and "your" to help users recognize agency and ownership.
* Approach privacy as a journey. Consider reminding users of choices they made, and make it easy to access settings to manage their data and information.
* Use research to confirm areas for improvement in the UX. Be cautious not to introduce doubt, uncertainty, or alarm where it doesn't exist.
* When you must use legal or technical terms, add a definition to help people learn.
### Use voice and tone to respect user context

When users feel respected, they're more likely to engage. A misplaced upsell or overly playful line may lead to negative results when users expect a more formal tone. In practice, avoid what could be perceived as pushing users towards a specific outcome, and be cautious with sales or marketing language. Leaning into [Google's voice](/google-material-3/pages/voice-and-tone/voice) and tone also helps build trust through familiarity, while inconsistencies can result in uncertainty and hesitation.
* In sensitive situations, consider leading with a question and asking for permission to set the tone. People appreciate being asked rather than directed.
* Trust users to make decisions that benefit them. Research shows that pushing or selling can erode user trust over time.
* Consider the user's country or region to determine the right tone and information needs. Avoid a one-size-fits-all casual approach.
### Set expectations and add genuine value

Convey a meaningful fair value exchange. Users should feel confident there's a genuine benefit in return.
* Watch out for absolutes (like "never" and "always") and subjective terms (like "better" and "best"). Ensure promises have the proof to back them up.
* Give users an idea of changes they'll experience or see, especially in moments of choice.
* Balance trust in the system and help users understand their role in keeping their info secure.
### Reduce cognitive load with clarity and visuals to complement the content

Clarity and comprehension can be more important than being concise. For instance, words like "safe," "private," and "secure" can mean different things to different people. When there are multiple ways to interpret a word, describe and provide examples of what a word means in context with limitations, tradeoffs, and benefits users need to know.

Policy and legal language doesn't have to mean long paragraphs of text. Consider how you can improve comprehension with the use of visuals and consistent icons instead of words alone.
* Relatable examples make it easier for users to see value by providing direction to guide thinking. Don't place the burden on users to guess.
* Inconsistencies in language or how a feature is presented can confuse users and introduce issues later on.
* Use visuals and icons to complement clear, plain language. A recommendation you'll also find in many laws and regulations, such as [GDPR](https://gdpr-info.eu/recitals/no-58/).
### Policy guidance

This is not an exhaustive list, but common guidelines for UX that accompany Google policies, such as [privacy](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/company/teams/security-privacy/policies/privacy/index.md?cl=head#policies). In addition to using these resources, work with your legal team and product area [Privacy Working Group](http://go/pwg) (PWG) to finalize the UX.
|              Topic              |                                                                                                                     Overview                                                                                                                     |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          Resources                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          |
|---------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|

# Content design
Writing and information design for better user experiences

* UI text should be clear to anyone{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Follow [Google's voice](/google-material-3/pages/voice-and-tone/voice/latest#b4981579-7eec-4b62-8513-487adde62f80) -- friendly, respectful, and focused on the user
* Use [Associated Press](https://www.apstylebook.com/) (AP) style unless noted otherwise
Resources
---------
| **Type** |                                                               **Resource**                                                                |
|----------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Writing  | [go/uxwriting](http://go/uxwriting)                                                                                                       |
| Writing  | [go/uxwords](http://go/uxwords)                                                                                                           |
| Writing  | [Writing guidelines for products and verticals](https://sites.google.com/corp/google.com/uxwriting-classic/guidelines/products-verticals) |
| Writing  | [AP Stylebook](https://www.apstylebook.com/)                                                                                              |
What's new
----------
* New [global writing](/google-material-3/pages/global-writing/overview/) guidelines{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Updated guidelines on [gender-inclusive language](/google-material-3/pages/voice-and-tone/gender-inclusive-language/){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Updated guidelines on [first-person pronouns](/google-material-3/pages/style-guide/word-choice/latest#9a1f0da6-c2a8-4658-87ef-a9c456f7fcff)
* Updated guidelines on [product naming](/google-material-3/pages/voice-and-tone/product-names/)
* Improved content, examples, illustrations, and organization{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
### Coming soon

Upcoming releases will include:{#isPasted}

* Notice and consent
* Updated guidance on writing for components
Designing content
-----------------
Material's writing standards reflect Google's brand guidelines and contributions of subject-matter experts throughout the company, incorporating the expertise of content designers across 15 product areas. The aim of these standards is to scale a consistent voice and experience for all users and guide anyone working on the information architecture, language, navigation, tone, voice, strategy, or communication style of a Google product.

Thank you to our Google-wide contributors and the UXW Style Committee. Get in touch to contribute or influence the future of Google's writing standards at [go/gm3-writing-bug](http://go/gm3-writing-bug).
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQs](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Style guide
UI text should be understandable by anyone, anywhere

## Word choice
Word lists {#isPasted}
----------------------

The Google UX word list ([go/uxwords](http://go/uxwords)) has guidance on how to spell, capitalize, hyphenate, and use hundreds of words in products. Word lists for specific products are available at [go/product-wordlinks](http://go/product-wordlinks). The inclusive word list ([go/respectful-words](http://go/respectful-words)) includes disrespectful words to avoid and respectful alternatives.{#isPasted}

Google word choice and style generally follows the [Associated Press (AP) style guidelines](https://www.apstylebook.com/).
Pronouns {#isPasted}
--------------------

### Use second person pronouns ("you")

Use the second-person pronouns "you" and "your" to help the user to feel like the UI is talking to them and referring to their actions
#### Don't combine first and second person

Avoid mixing "me" or "my" with "you" or "your." It can cause confusion to see both forms of address in the same context. [Learn more about pronoun usage](/google-material-3/pages/voice-and-tone/gender-inclusive-language/latest#0255d5af-2297-4803-ac51-4b4e2895dfe0)
#### Use caution with "I" and "we"

When written on behalf of a large, global company like Google, "we" or "I" may come across as robotic or disconcerting. {#isPasted}

Focus on the user's point of view, rather than Google's, and consider if it's possible to rewrite a phrase without "we."
Some legal texts may merit an exception: "I" or "my" (the first person) emphasizes ownership in agreements or acknowledgments. For example, "I agree to the terms of service."  

Don't personify Google or the product unless it is part of the brand, such as the tone of a defined character, or represents when a person at Google takes action, such as reviewing an appeal or responding to a suggestion.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## UX writing best practices
### Explain consequences

Emphasize the results of the user's potential action in neutral, direct language. Avoid cautions or warnings that might sound alarming, intimidating, or condescending. Focus instead on communicating the consequences of a function.
### Use scannable words and formats

People scan UI text in search of the most meaningful content to them. Help by using specific titles and headings that clearly describe a topic. When users are skimming or hurrying through an action, this organization helps them avoid mistakes and unintentional actions.
### Use sentence case

Unless otherwise specified, use sentence-style capitalization, where only the first letter of the first word in a sentence or phrase is capitalized. All text, including titles, headings, labels, menu items, navigation components, app bars, and buttons should use sentence-style capitalization.{#isPasted}

[Products and branded terms](/google-material-3/pages/voice-and-tone/product-names/latest#2ca11723-75dd-47b2-a6be-e3875ac28bb3) may also be capitalized.
### Use abbreviations sparingly

Spell out words whenever possible. Shortened forms of words can be difficult for people to understand and screen readers to read. Avoid Latin abbreviations in UI text such as e.g. or etc. Instead, use full phrases like "for example," or "and more."{#isPasted}

Use [go/abbreviations](http://go/abbreviations) for more specific guidance on abbreviations.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Grammar and punctuation
This style guide is specific to English-language UX writing. Google generally follows [Associated Press (AP) style](https://www.apstylebook.com/).
### Skip periods and unnecessary punctuation {#isPasted}

To help readers scan text, avoid using periods and other unnecessary punctuation.

Avoid using periods to end single sentences, particularly in:{#isPasted}

* Labels
* Tooltip text
* Bulleted lists
* Dialog body text
* Hyperlinked text

Use periods on:

* Multiple sentences
* Long or complex sentences, if it suits the context
### Use contractions {#isPasted}

Contractions can make a sentence easier to understand and scan.

However, sometimes "do not" can give more emphasis than "don't" when caution is needed.
### Use serial commas {#isPasted}

Use the serial (or Oxford) comma, except before an ampersand.

Always place commas inside quotation marks.
### Use commas for numbers between 1,000 and 1 million {#isPasted}

Use commas for numbers over 1,000. Don't use commas when identifying something, such as a:{#isPasted}

* Street address{#isPasted}
* Radio frequency{#isPasted}
* Year{#isPasted}

For numbers over 1 million, comma use depends on context. "Million" can be abbreviated with with "M" and the value can be rounded when the intent is to give people a sense of the volume, rather than the exact numbers.
### Skip colons in headings

For headings on lists of items, do not use colons. For lists within body text, use a colon.
### Use exclamation points sparingly {#isPasted}

Exclamation points can come across as shouting or overly friendly. Some exceptions include greetings or congratulatory messages.
### Use ellipses sparingly {#isPasted}

<br />

Use ellipses to indicate an action in progress or incomplete text. Truncated text may appear with ellipses, but check with your engineering partners before implementing, as this often happens automatically.  

Don't add a space before ellipses. Omit ellipses from menu items or buttons that open a dialog or start a process.
### Use parentheses to define terms {#isPasted}

Parentheses can be used to define acronyms or jargon or when referencing a source. They shouldn't be used when adding a side note or an afterthought of a sentence.
### Skip ampersands in body text {#isPasted}

The "\&" symbol can be used instead of "and" in headlines, column headers, table headers, navigation labels, and buttons. However, when there's room, spelling out "and" can improve readability and make scanning easier.

"And" should be spelled out in sentences and paragraphs, before the final item in a 3+ item list, and in email subject lines.
### Use dashes with caution {#isPasted}

Dashes and hyphens can interrupt a sentence and lead to a fragmented experience, so they should be used with caution. There are three kinds of dashes:

* Em dash: ---
* En dash: --
* Hyphen: -

Em dashes are best avoided in UX writing, as they indicate a break in the flow of a sentence that could be simplified using a comma, period, or new sentence.

Use an en dash without spaces to indicate a range, such as 9 AM--Noon. To learn more about writing about time, visit [go/time-guidelines](http://go/time-guidelines).
### Use hyphens with care {#isPasted}

Hyphens can help readers better understand how words relate to each other by binding closely related words. They can also be used to represent negative numbers, such as -100. Spaces should never be used surrounding hyphens.

Refer to the [Associated Press (AP) style guidelines](https://www.apstylebook.com/) if you are unsure whether an adjective or noun phrase needs a hyphen.
|                                                           **Rule**                                                            |                                                   **Examples**                                                   |                                                                                                                                                                                                     **Why**                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Hyphenate adjective phrases**                                                                                               | Case-by-case basisBest-in-class performanceOnce-in-a-lifetime opportunity                                        | When multiple words are used together as an adjective, they should be hyphenated                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
| **Hyphenate adjective phrases**                                                                                               | Cell phone numberChocolate chip cookie                                                                           | However, proper nouns and common, easily understood adjective phrases don't need to be, such as "cell phone number" or "chocolate chip cookie"                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
| **Hyphenate noun phrases**A noun phrase is two or more words acting as a noun. These phrases are hyphenated in certain cases: | Sign-offDrive-throughGo-ahead                                                                                    | Hyphenate a noun phrase if it contains a verb followed by an adverb                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |
| **Hyphenate noun phrases**A noun phrase is two or more words acting as a noun. These phrases are hyphenated in certain cases: | Higher-upMost-read                                                                                               | Hyphenate an adjective phrase that is functioning as a noun                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     |
| **Hyphenate noun phrases**A noun phrase is two or more words acting as a noun. These phrases are hyphenated in certain cases: | Jack-of-all-tradesStick-in-the-mud                                                                               | Some noun phrases, especially long or complicated ones, are always hyphenated                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| **Don't hyphenate verb phrases**A verb phrase is two or more words acting as a verb. These should not be hyphenated.          | Look out for falling rocks{#isPasted} Please drive in and drop off your car Check in to the room when you arrive | Don't hyphenate a verb followed by an adverb or preposition if it's functioning as a verb phrase. For example, "check in" would not need a hyphen when used as a verb, such as "check in to the room," rather than as a noun, like "the next check-in." Also, note that since "in" is a part of the verb, it can't be combined with "to" to form "into," since check into doesn't mean the same as check in to. |
### Use italics sparingly {#isPasted}

Italics typically aren't easy to read. When emphasizing text, use bold weight instead.{#isPasted}

However, italics can provide unique emphasis when applied to a single word or phrase, like a name or title.  

[Learn more about font selection and accessibility](http://go/cognitive-fonts)
### Don't use caps blocks {#isPasted}

Avoid using caps blocks altogether; they're not accessible.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQs](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# AI color &amp; gradient
Colors and gradients are the most recognizable parts of generative AI features

Color and gradient show Google AI in action. When users see a UI element with a moving gradient, they should immediately recognize it as an AI-powered experience. However, not all colors and gradients are equally effective. This guidance ensures your product remains legible and cohesive. AI color is based on the Gemini branding ecosystem, and extends the [GM3 color system](/google-material-3/pages/color/system-overview). {#isPasted}

For Gemini branded products, use the Gemini color palette available at [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook). For non-Gemini branded products consider the following:

* If your product doesn't use dynamic color, use Gen AI baseline colors. These are created from a subset of Gemini colors and can be used for non-Gemini branded AI products.
* If your product uses dynamic color, map your AI features using the AI dynamic color roles
Gemini ecosystem
----------------
### Gemini color palette

The Gemini core brand palette and gradient are made up of colors found in the spectrum between Google's brand colors: Google Blue and Red. See the latest at [go/geminiplaybook](http://go/geminiplaybook).
Google AI
---------
### Baseline AI colors
There are six color values used for AI elements:{#isPasted}

1. #217BFE (AI main)
2. #078EFB (AI analog)
3. #AC87EB (AI complement)
4. #D7E6FF AI main variant)
5. #C7E4FF (AI analog variant)
6. #DCE2FF (AI complement variant)
### Usage {#isPasted}
Use AI colors and moving gradients to show AI thinking and generating, like the active loading state after a user has initiated an AI action, but before the UI presents the result.  

The generative AI color palette uses a subset of colors from the Gemini branding ecosystem, and extends your product's existing color palette.  

The AI colors don't replace any of your palette's existing colors (secondary, tertiary, etc). Rather, they create an additional palette used only for AI UI elements. The AI colors are precisely calculated to be eye-catching without distracting from existing layouts.  

Don't use baseline AI colors for icons outside of pre-approved use cases, such as the Gemini product icon.
Dynamic color for AI
--------------------
GM3 uses dynamic color to create a color palette from on-screen content using a **seed color**. Products using Google AI can leverage the following colors in these palettes to generate complementary AI gradients:{#isPasted}

1. Primary{#isPasted}
2. Primary T80{#isPasted}
3. Tertiary T80{#isPasted}
4. Primary container{#isPasted}
5. Secondary container{#isPasted}
6. Tertiary container{#isPasted}
Applying AI colors {#isPasted}
------------------------------
### Use lighter tones for surfaces behind text {#isPasted}

For gradients that appear behind text, such as button fills or cards, use either of the two combinations of **AI main variant, AI analog variant** , and **AI complement variant** shown below. This helps ensure legibility.{#isPasted}

Always confirm that your combination of text color and AI colors meets the 3:1 contrast minimum.
### Use darker tones for outlines {#isPasted}
