For gradients that appear in elements that would benefit from increased chroma, such as outlines, use AI main, AI analog, AI complement variant, and surface in the order shown here. This provides a stronger visual cue.
### Combining and mixing colors {#isPasted}

AI colors should always be used in combination as a gradient, never alone. In outlines, the ordering should always use a mix of: 1. **AI main** to 2. **AI analog** to 3. **AI complement variant** to 4. **Surface** to 5. **AI analog** to 6. **AI main** . For fills, gradients can be comprised of mixes of **AI main variant, AI analog variant** , and **AI complement variant**.
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://goto.google.com/gm3-bug)


# Choosing a scheme
Static color schemes emphasize brand and uniformity, while dynamic schemes emphasize content or user settings to make products feel more personal

A **color scheme** describes all of a product's colors, color roles, and color relationships across light and dark themes.

There are two kinds of color schemes in Material:

1. Static
2. Dynamic
Static color {#isPasted}
------------------------
Working with static color will be the most like other color workflows you may have used. Static colors won't ever change based on user input or in-app content.

Material provides a static GM3 baseline color scheme including default color assignments and mappings. Each color has been selected by a designer to align with colors in GM2 as much as possible.
**What you get:**

✓ Accessible colors  
✓ Pre-made baseline color scheme  
✓ Colors that won't break a GM2 app  
✓ Ability to easily update to dynamic color in the future
**What you *don't* get:**

✗ Personalized colors  
✗ Colors that change based on user's wallpaper or in-app content  
✗ User-controlled contrast settings
**Products using baseline** (as of Oct 2023)

* Maps
* Search App (Android)
* Home
* Play Store
* Primitives
### **Use static (baseline) color if...** {#isPasted}

* You're not ready to implement dynamic color (though it'll be easy to switch when you are)
* Your product is migrating from GM2 and you want to get GM3 features without breaking your app
* Your product is for enterprise users who wouldn't benefit from personalized color or user-controlled contrast settings
* Your product is built for iOS

<br />

**Choosing baseline?** [**Start designing with the baseline colors**](/google-material-3/pages/static/gm3-baseline)
Dynamic color
-------------

Dynamic color will automatically create an accessible color scheme based on a specific source color.

Because the UI could end up with any number of different source colors, it's best to initially design it using the baseline color scheme so you can ensure the right color roles are mapped to the right components in your product. You'll use the Material Theme Builder to see how your UI mocks look across a range of source colors and adjust it as-needed.
**What you get:**

✓ Accessible colors  
✓ Personalized colors that change based on a user's wallpaper or in-app content  
✓ Ability to use advanced customizations like chroma fidelity to alter the dynamic color output  
✓ User-controlled contrast settings
**What you *don't* get:**

✗ Exact same UI colors across all devices
**Products using dynamic color** (as of Oct 2023)

* Gmail
* Photos
* Keep
* Docs / Slides / Sheets
* Meet
* Assistant
* Translate
### **Use dynamic color if** {#isPasted}

* You want your product to showcase personalization
* You want the colors to change base on a user's wallpaper or in-app content
* You want your product to offer user-controlled contrast settings
* You aren't sure if you'll need to also use a mix of dynamic and static colors (you can customize your color scheme to include static colors as your work progresses)

<br />

**Choosing dynamic color? Next** [**pick a dynamic color source**](/google-material-3/pages/dynamic/choosing-a-source)
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Advanced customizations
Apply, define, or adjust colors to create a fine-tuned, unique color experience

## Apply colors
You can apply colors in places or ways that aren't provided by default.
Combine multiple color schemes {#isPasted}
------------------------------------------
Use multiple color schemes in the same app experience, such as a baseline scheme combined with a dynamic content-based scheme.
### Why

If your app features content-rich moments, such as a media player, it can enhance a user's experience by applying local color based on that content.

### How

* Start from a [baseline](/google-material-3/pages/static/gm3-baseline) or [user-generated dynamic](/google-material-3/pages/dynamic/user-generated) scheme to create a consistent color foundation in your app.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* On top of this foundation, [map content-based color roles to contained spaces](/google-material-3/pages/dynamic/content-based) to emphasize or celebrate content. For example, a music app might derive color from a specific album's artwork to build upon the personal connection to a music library.

### Best practices

* Consider where content exists in the UI and where content-based color can enhance a person's experience. Your existing app structure can suggest contained areas for content-based color to live.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* **Build hierarchy \& direct attention:** When many types of information and actions share a screen, use content-based color to add hierarchy and draw attention to the content.
* **Link and associate content on a screen:** In lists and collections of repeated items that benefit from differentiation, content-based color can help associate related elements. This helps people quickly distinguish and pair related information, such as a list item and its associated action.
* **Immerse users in content color:** Full-screen content-based color moments can orient users within a content-driven experience, such as a media control or a purchase flow.
* **Pair content-based color with its source content:** Keep the source for content-based color visible on a screen using the content color. This way users are shown where a content-based color originates. Avoid applying content-based color in spaces where the content itself isn't visible.
* **Limit the number of color source types per screen:** Limit a screen to two color schemes from different source types. Too many color schemes on the same screen may lead to confusion and visual disarray. For example, a baseline or user-generated color scheme can be combined with one type of on-screen content (such as album art).
* **Don't replace semantic colors:** Use caution when applying content-based color in places where a semantic color or conventional color meaning is important for usability. For example, a common red error message or a common green positive action shouldn't be replaced with dynamic content-based color because it may interfere with someone's understanding.
Map or remap colors on UI elements {#isPasted}
----------------------------------------------
You can change a component's default color mapping, or apply colors to your own custom components.
### Why

You want to map colors to a custom-built component or change a Material Component's default color mapping to improve its function (such as visual contrast) or style.

### How

Choose an appropriate color role based on how the color is used (see [color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles)) and how well the role supports your intended design expression.

#### Design

* In Figma, select the component or element you want to remap so that you see its colors in the Design panel on the right of the screen
* To remap a color, hover on the color row in the Design panel and select the **Style** icon (four dots). This opens a selection dialog.
* Search for your theme name to see the available color roles
* Select the color role that most closely matches that color's use case in the component. For example, the background color of a component could be replaced with the **surface** color role and the color for text or icons could be **on surface** . See [Color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles) for more information on what color to use where.
* Select **Use style** to apply that color to the selected objects
* Repeat until all colors in the component have been replaced with color roles from your scheme

### Best practices

* Make sure to use color roles that support Material's contrast requirements for the component. Any color roles starting with "on-" are guaranteed to have sufficient contrast with the corresponding color role. Other color role pairs may not meet the 4.5:1 (small text) and 3:1 (large text) Material contrast requirements.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* If you're applying a dynamic scheme, test how the color on the component appears under different themes (such as light and dark; red, yellow, green and blue) to ensure it looks as desired in dynamic color
* Always apply color roles rather than static values or tonal palette values, as these colors will break with light and dark themes, contrast control, and other features. If the color in a role does not meet your needs, you can define new colors or adjust existing colors.
Apply dynamic color to images {#isPasted}
-----------------------------------------
You can adjust colors on images and illustrations so that they match dynamic color schemes.
### Why

If you have images or illustrations in your app and you are using a dynamic scheme, you should adjust colors in those assets to appear harmonious in dynamic color. Static assets within a dynamically themed app can appear incongruous and low quality.

### How

#### Vector assets

* If your image is vector based, such as an SVG, you can use [Lottie](http://go/lottie) to assign color roles or tonal palette colors to elements inside the image.
* Use a combination of primary, secondary, tertiary, and neutral colors for an expressive distribution of color in the image that portrays depth. Accent (primary, secondary, and tertiary) colors stand out and can be applied to foreground elements, while neutral (surface) colors recede and should be used for background elements.
Skin and hair tones should not be mapped to color roles, as these colors can change and appear inappropriate in dynamic color. For such colors, apply static values.
If you apply tonal palette values or static values rather than color roles, you must specify values for both light and dark themes for the image to respond to those themes. You may define new colors to have Material automatically handle light and dark themes.
#### Bitmap assets

* For bitmap images, you can use [image harmonization](http://go/material-image-harmonization). This feature adjusts the hues of all colors in an image to either match or harmonize with the primary color in the dynamic color scheme.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Use image harmonization when an overall color change is desired for bitmap assets (which do not contain elements that can be individually assigned color roles) such as GIFs, PNGs, TIFFs, and JPEGs. For example, image harmonization could be applied to a JPEG photo but cannot be applied to vector assets composed of discrete shapes, such as an SVG.
* Image harmonization applies uniformly to the entire asset. To control individual elements and their colors within an image, use color roles with vector assets, such as an SVG.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Overview
From changing a component's default color mapping to creating additional color roles, advanced customizations fall within one of three general actions: applying, defining, or adjusting colors.
Apply colors
------------

You can apply colors in places or ways that aren't provided by default.

[Combine multiple color schemes](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/apply-colors/latest#a32c91f2-f775-426f-9122-6f63a48ac7c9)  
Use multiple color schemes in the same app experience, such as a baseline scheme combined with a dynamic content-based scheme.

[Map or remap colors onto UI elements](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/apply-colors/latest#149a3fa7-e126-43f6-9010-ac5a8f5c7741)  
Change a component's default color mapping, or apply colors to your own custom components.

[Apply dynamic color to images](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/apply-colors#9e909459-8970-4a63-99cf-657499f9b05a){#isPasted}  
Change the colors in images and illustrations to match dynamic color schemes.
Define new colors
-----------------

You can add colors to your color scheme, extending the default color roles.

[Define static colors](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/define-new-colors/latest#78c883a7-05e8-4f9f-929c-45ca26991d4e) (formerly known as custom colors)  
Add colors to a dynamic color scheme that stay static, which are useful for semantic colors.

[Define custom color roles](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/define-new-colors/latest#89baf21a-12ba-40fb-a9ae-950812a8a334)  
Create new color roles to use alongside the 26+ standard color roles.
Adjust existing colors
----------------------

You can control the color algorithm's output to adjust the appearance of dynamic colors.

[Define your own baseline scheme](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/adjust-existing-colors/latest#2df8c69f-55d6-464a-8221-cad27fbfeca8)  
You can input colors to define your own baseline scheme.

[Define your own dynamic scheme](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/adjust-existing-colors/latest#b78ed6b4-1769-4410-9cea-98a27ff9fa34)  
You can define color algorithm rules to produce your own custom dynamic scheme.

[Use color fidelity](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/adjust-existing-colors/latest#62778ca1-c8ea-453e-bef2-fabd82086e14)  
You can apply color fidelity to make scheme colors better match your input colors.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Define new colors
You can add colors to your scheme to extend the color roles provided by Material out of the box.
Define static colors {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
*Formerly known as custom colors*

You can define additional colors in your scheme that stay static even when other colors dynamically change. When you input a desired reference color, Material will return four derived color roles that align with the design of existing roles in the color scheme.
### Why {#isPasted}

You may need to apply static colors in your app for brand expression or to communicate semantic meaning, like a green success state. By defining these colors using the Material system, they'll work with existing Material colors and support features like dynamic color and user-controlled contrast.

### How

Use the Material Theme Builder to input a custom color. Material will return four color roles derived from that reference color. The main color, on-main color, container color, and on-container color all follow the conventions of the accent colors in the main scheme, and can be applied to your UI according to the same relationships. See [++map or remap colors on UI elements++](https://carbon.googleplex.com/m3/pages/advanced/apply-colors#d15f5373-c03b-4282-a309-db569975d395) for more information.

### Best practices

* If the colors provided back from your input color appear differently than expected, you can enable or disable color fidelity. [Color fidelity](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/adjust-existing-colors/latest#62778ca1-c8ea-453e-bef2-fabd82086e14) is a feature that adjusts colors' tones to match that of your input color.
* Material provides the red Error color out of the box as an example of a static color, so you do not need to define your own static color for a semantic red color.
* If you are using static colors in a dynamic scheme, you can choose to [harmonize your static colors](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/adjust-existing-colors/latest#4a368184-de7b-4271-aa30-392473a11ad5) to the scheme's primary color. This will shift your static colors' hues slightly warmer or cooler for a more harmonious overall appearance, while retaining the semantic meaning associated with the colors' hue range.

<br />
Define custom color roles {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
You can define custom color roles in addition to those already existing in the color scheme. By defining these roles the same way Material does (specifying a reference palette, starting tones, and contrast requirements), these roles can achieve colors more specific to your needs while working seamlessly with features such as user-controlled contrast.
### Why {#isPasted}

You may need to define your own custom color roles if the scheme's existing colors or additional static colors don't meet your product's needs. In particular, you should create them within the Material system to respect dynamic colors and unlock other features like user-controlled contrast.

### How

Abstract your new color into a color role by specifying the following criteria:

* **Palettes and reference tones:** For each color role, you must assign its value from a Material palette (primary, secondary, tertiary, neutral, neutralVariant, error) and a reference tone (for example: primary70, primary80, primary90...) for both light and dark themes.
* **Color pairings:** You must specify any visual relationships in your design, such as color pairs that are used together as foreground and background, or which should retain a tone delta between them (difference in lightness or darkness).
* **Contrast:** Confirm that custom foreground and background color pairings meet [Material's contrast minimums](/google-material-3/pages/designing/color-contrast).
* See a [sample design doc](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1LoiiDBMH7u6nhspIlpTszs6sBbFImTYjIKBzq9R8QGY/edit?usp=sharing) for more examples.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}

Once the above criteria are known, you can define the new color roles in your own dynamic color object. For each color role, you may then call Material Color Utilities (MCU) to generate the color value dynamically, according to different conditions such as user theming or contrast level.

To test these colors in UI, you may wish to contribute to Material's color design prototype playground [Monet Gallery](http://go/monetgallery). [Contact Material Xplat team](mailto:material-xplat-team@google.com) for more information.

### Best practices

Defining custom color roles should be considered only if you cannot achieve your desired colors with other Material color solutions.

<br />
Harmonize colors
----------------

In dynamic schemes, you can automatically adjust the hue of static colors so they look better alongside the scheme's primary color.
You can harmonize custom colors so that they automatically adjust to look good alongside dynamic colors. This ensures visual balance and maintains accessible contrast when a custom color appears in combination with any dynamic colors.
### Why {#isPasted}

Harmonize your custom colors if you intend to use them alongside dynamic colors. You can also use color harmonization to achieve more color control and customization for elements within a dynamic color environment.
### How {#isPasted}

Use the [Material Theme Builder](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1020820278641129628/Material-Theme-Builder-1P)'s color harmonization feature to see how custom colors will look when harmonized.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Adjust existing colors
You can control the color algorithm's output to adjust the appearance of colors within the roles provided by default.
Define your own baseline scheme {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------------

You can input colors to define your own baseline scheme.
### Why {#isPasted}

You may want to define your own baseline scheme so your app's colors stay static (ie. does not change with dynamic color), such as to reflect your brand colors. By providing your own custom input colors for the primary, secondary, tertiary, and neutral colors in the scheme, Material will provide back the scheme's regular color roles with values derived from your reference colors.
### How

Using the Material Theme Builder, input your own colors for primary, secondary, tertiary, neutral, and neutral variant. The Theme Builder will generate a color scheme with values based on your inputs, and the given color roles can be used in the same manner as those from any other Material scheme.

### Best practices

* Conventionally, primary and tertiary colors are the most visually prominent in the scheme, with tertiary appearing complementary to primary by changing its hue. Secondary, neutral variant, and neutral colors match primary in hue but are progressively less chromatic in that order. Input your colors into the appropriate category to maintain similar relationships as designed by Material, and ensure expected and visually pleasing results when those colors are mapped to components.
* If the colors provided back from your input color appear differently than expected, you can enable or disable [color fidelity](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/adjust-existing-colors/latest#62778ca1-c8ea-453e-bef2-fabd82086e14). Color fidelity is a feature that adjusts colors' tones to match that of your input color.
* If the 26+ standard color roles do not meet your needs, you may need to [define custom color roles](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/define-new-colors/latest#89baf21a-12ba-40fb-a9ae-950812a8a334).
Define your own dynamic scheme {#isPasted}
------------------------------------------

You can define color algorithm rules to produce your own dynamic scheme.
### Why

Control the appearance of your app's colors while respecting dynamic color. For example, you may want your app to match the user's wallpaper theme, but appear more vibrant than the default dynamic theme colors.
### How {#isPasted}

* Material generates the color scheme by following hue and chroma values specified for each group of colors (primary, secondary, tertiary, neutral, and neutral variant). For more information, see [how the system works](/google-material-3/pages/color/how-the-system-works). To adjust the appearance of these colors and produce your own dynamic scheme, you must provide your own hue and chroma values for each of these color groups.
* Once these values are known, you may define your own scheme variant and call Material Color Utilities (MCU) to dynamically generate the scheme and provide color values for each role in the scheme.
* To test these colors in UI, you may wish to contribute to Material's color design prototype playground [Monet Gallery](http://go/monetgallery). Contact the [Material Xplat team](mailto:material-xplat-team@google.com) for more information.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}

### Best practices

* Defining custom color roles should be considered only if you cannot achieve your desired colors with other Material color solutions.
* If the colors provided back from your input color appear differently than intended, you can enable or disable [color fidelity](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/adjust-existing-colors/latest#62778ca1-c8ea-453e-bef2-fabd82086e14). Color fidelity is a feature that adjusts colors' tones to match that of your input color.
* If the color roles provided by Material out of the box do not meet your needs, you may need to [define custom color roles](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/define-new-colors/latest#89baf21a-12ba-40fb-a9ae-950812a8a334) for greater control over their appearance.
Use color fidelity {#isPasted}
------------------------------

You can apply color fidelity to make scheme colors better match your input colors.
### Why

Material scheme colors are mapped to tones (lightness or darkness) to achieve visually accessible color pairings with sufficient contrast between foreground and background elements. In some cases, these tones can prevent colors from appearing as intended, such as when a color is too light to appear vibrant. Color fidelity is a feature that adjusts tones in these cases to produce the intended visual results without harming visual contrast.

<br />
### How {#isPasted}

* In the Material Theme Builder, you can toggle the "match color" option on your input color to enable or disable fidelity. By default, fidelity is enabled when you use Theme Builder to [create a custom baseline scheme](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/adjust-existing-colors/latest#2df8c69f-55d6-464a-8221-cad27fbfeca8) or [define static colors](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations/define-new-colors/latest#78c883a7-05e8-4f9f-929c-45ca26991d4e).
* In code, you can flag color roles in your scheme with a boolean which will enable or disable fidelity for those colors.

### Best practices

* When producing a custom baseline scheme or defining static colors, you may wish to toggle fidelity on and off to determine which setting better suits your desired design.
* Because color fidelity adjusts tones (lightness or darkness of colors), to ensure accessible contrast, remember to pair appropriate [colors roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles) together, such as a background color with its corresponding foreground "on" color.
Harmonize colors
----------------

In dynamic schemes, you can automatically adjust the hue of static colors so they look better alongside the scheme's primary color.
### Why

Static colors may visually clash with a scheme's dynamically changing colors. To improve visual harmony, Material provides an optional 'harmonize' function that slightly adjusts static colors to look better in dynamic schemes.{#isPasted}

Colors that are closer in hue appear more pleasing together than colors with hues farther apart. Based on this principle, harmonization adjusts the hue of static colors, making them closer to the hue of the scheme's primary color.
To preserve the semantic meaning of static colors (such as a red to communicate errors), harmonization limits the amount that a color's hue can change. Harmonized colors will become warmer or cooler in hue without appearing like another type of color.
### How

* In the Material Theme Builder, you can toggle harmonization on and off within the overflow menu for each static color you have added to the scheme.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* In code, use the 'Blend' function from [Material Color Utilities](https://github.com/material-foundation/material-color-utilities) to harmonize colors

### Best practices

* Harmonization will adjust a static color differently depending on the scheme's primary color, so check the results under a variety of schemes to see the range of how they can appear in dynamic color.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Don't harmonize colors whose appearance should stay absolutely consistent, such as brand colors.
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# AI components
Use AI components to apply AI features. AI components are based off Material components.

## Chips
There are two types of AI chips:{#isPasted}

1. AI assist chips
2. AI suggestion chips (fka prompt chips)
No more than three AI chips should be used together in a single screen.
AI assist chips {#isPasted}
---------------------------
### Usage

Use AI assist chips for initiating AI-powered actions like editing a photo or generating text
### Specs {#isPasted}

AI assist chips have the same specs and behaviors as [GM3 assist chips](/google-material-3/pages/chips/guidelines/e1abe09b-bd39-46f3-9756-f4d1dbb77ec9#9bd93b07-ccfb-48bf-b392-69cd91650d80) except they:

1. Use an AI system icon  
   to communicate that the action is AI-powered
2. Have labels that are required to work in tandem with the AI system icon   
   and should describe the task. When generating, the label should change to illustrate this action using labels such as 'Generating'
3. When in the generating state, the stroke of the chip is removed
4. Use AI colors and gradients  
   for the generating state
AI assist chips can have different container heights on mobile and desktop{#isPasted}

1. Mobile - 32dp height
2. Desktop - 36dp height
AI assist chips have the ability to contain longer strings and can wrap up to three lines in height. AI system icons should align to the top of the chip and text should be formatted to be left aligned. The top and bottom padding should also increase to 8dp.
Use caution when displaying images in AI assist chips or using them to indicate actionable terms in inline text.{#isPasted}

These are emerging patterns that don't yet have established best practices.
AI suggestion chips {#isPasted}
-------------------------------
### Usage {#isPasted}

Use AI suggestion chips to suggest AI prompts in a chat or conversation flow.
### Specs {#isPasted}

AI suggestion chips have the same specs and behaviors as [GM3 suggestion chips](/google-material-3/pages/chips/guidelines/e1abe09b-bd39-46f3-9756-f4d1dbb77ec9#87b15688-d749-463f-ab3b-bc217bf761e5) except they:

1. Use the AI suggestion icon
2. Have a descriptive label that should always follow the AI suggestion icon
3. Have a chat-bubble-like asymmetrical corner style
AI suggestion chips can have different container heights on desktop and mobile.{#isPasted}

1. Mobile - 32dp height
2. Desktop - 36dp height
AI suggestion chips have a unique asymmetrical shape. The top trailing corner has a 4dp radius and all other corners keep a half-height radius of 16dp on mobile and 18dp on desktop.
AI suggestion chips can have a high-emphasis color mapping to better distinguish them from AI assist chips in groupings.

## Buttons
There are two types of AI buttons:{#isPasted}

1. AI outline buttons
2. AI icon buttons
Use AI system icons in buttons that start a generative AI process. These icons communicate that a generative AI action is about to take place.
**Chips aren't buttons**   

Chips and buttons are similar in that they both provide visual cues to prompt users to take actions and make selections. However, there are important distinctions.  

Multiple chips should appear together in a group, whereas there should be no more than 3 buttons in a single arrangement.
AI outlined buttons
-------------------
### Usage {#isPasted}

Use AI outlined buttons for initiating generative AI actions like **generate image** or **generate summary**.
### Specs {#isPasted}

AI outlined buttons have the same specs and behaviors as [GM3 outlined buttons](/google-material-3/pages/common-buttons/guidelines#c082404f-eb54-45a8-a9a8-0e5471a05c07) except they:  
{#isPasted}

1. Use an AI system icon to communicate that the action is powered by AI
2. Use AI colors and gradients for the generating state
3. Do not have an outline during the generation state
All AI gradients live in code and should only be applied to buttons as the moving generating state. AI color roles and gradients should never to be applied to a button in static form.{#isPasted}

Gradients rotate in a counter-clockwise direction and should never be static
In their generation state, buttons must still pass accessibility and GAR requirements.{#isPasted}

1. Text and icon must meet 4.5:1 contrast with the background gradient. Using **primary** for the icon and label against the AI color gradient meets this requirement
2. Label text must communicate what is happening in the generation state, such as **Generating**.  

Ensure accessibility by using the primary color role for icons and text, and changing the label to communicate what is happening in the generation state
Once the generation process is complete, the button can:{#isPasted}

1. Return to the default enabled state
2. Change to an enabled state with a new label to **Generate more**
3. Change to a disabled state if the user is unable to take further action
AI icon button
--------------
### Usage {#isPasted}

Only use AI icon buttons:{#isPasted}

1. In or next to a text field for a send action
2. Within an action group, such as the set of icon buttons in a top app bar

Don't use an AI icon button in any other case. Instead, use an AI outline button or AI FAB to start the generating process.
### Specs

AI icon buttons have the same specs and behaviors as [GM3 icon buttons](/google-material-3/pages/icon-buttons/overview) except they use an AI system icon rather than a standard system icon.
There are two ways to show the generation state for AI icon buttons:{#isPasted}

1. In the outline of the adjacent text fieldWhen the AI icon button is contained within a text field or outside of it, the text field can have a 2dp animated stroke inside.
2. Behind a group of actionsWhen the AI icon button is within a group of actions, the animated gradient is applied behind the AI icon button, similar to a hover state.  

The AI icon button should not have an animated background when used in combination with a AI text input field. It only has an animated background when it's a standalone icon within a group of action icons.
There are two ways to show the generation state for AI icon buttons:{#isPasted}

1. In the outline of the adjacent text field
2. In the icon button container

<br />

When grouped with a text field, the generation state of an AI icon button appears as a 2dp animated gradient around the text field outline

When placed in an action group, the generation state of an AI icon button appears as an animated gradient filling the icon button container
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug){#isPasted}


# AI writing
Be transparent, explain, empower, and educate people when writing for AI features

These are high level, general guidelines for writing about and for AI at Google, and should be used alongside the AI word list ([go/aiwords](http://go/aiwords)). Individual products will have their own audiences and apply their own use cases. AI technology at Google is evolving rapidly, so these guidelines are a work in progress and will continue to change over time.
Be transparent
--------------
Use plain, familiar language to highlight the "presence" of AI and describe its capabilities and limits, intended and unintended uses, and potential flaws. Consider what [terms and phrases](http://go/aiwords) will best reinforce these messages and use those terms in-product and in promotional materials. Keep the [identified harms](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1g4sKiuY_FGuqQK7hJXONtzM5vXhYcZFmu8sx9OtFLBQ/edit#slide=id.g14886503b22_0_179) in mind, including privacy, misrepresentation, and stereotyping.
### Set expectations and communicate risk {#isPasted}

Help users understand when AI generated the content. Talk to your legal partners about disclaimer language, and avoid suggesting that AI-generated content is accurate or authoritative.
### Avoid personifying AI outside a chat interface {#isPasted}

"I" should only be used in a chat window space, such as a response. Don't use first person pronouns in the UI, including tooltips, help text, or subheadings.
### Plan for pitfalls {#isPasted}

Understand the types of errors users might encounter and have a plan for resolving them. Consider how much context a user or AI product may have.
### Avoid overpromising {#isPasted}

Clearly explain what people can do with AI features without overpromising capabilities.  

AI technology is evolving, and may get things wrong. Don't imply the technology knows or is capable of everything, and don't condescend.
### Use care when naming products {#isPasted}

Be careful with AI, GenAI, LLM, and other AI-related terms. Just because a product or feature uses AI doesn't mean it needs to be in the name. Be cautious with generic terms, like "smart," "magic," or "intelligent." Work with your product marketing team when a name is needed. Review the [Gemini Playbook naming guidance](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1eTWCkU6ds75E7Gp8p0oO7trQXCeMoLFbNJSUEGcnH0A/edit#slide=id.g1f4e1bc49ba_195_2289) and file relevant naming requests at [go/namingrequest](http://go/namingrequest).
### Focus on the benefit, not the AI technology {#isPasted}

Explain the user benefit, not what the tool can do or how it's made. Be specific about when AI is being used. Consider that Google AI encompasses more than just Gemini or generative AI. Follow the [Gemini playbook](http://go/geminiplaybook) for guidance.
Explain and guide in context {#isPasted}
----------------------------------------
AI technology is new and evolving. Users need guidance to understand what they're using and how to use it effectively. Keep it simple and be specific.
### Focus on action {#isPasted}

Give people a starting place that's easy to find and understand, like example prompts. Help the user learn by doing, and educate throughout the experience. Focus on what the user can do with the product.
### Show don't tell

AI products, experiences, interactions are not all the same. Outputs can be multimodal, such as text, images, video, or code. Users may have different techniques or intentions when using AI.
Empower and educate users {#isPasted}
-------------------------------------
AI technology is evolving and may get things wrong. Teach people that AI technology (particularly generative AI) may be better for drafting and ideating than finding facts. Bear in mind the risks of hallucinations and inaccuracies, and let that inform your language and design.
### Help users, don't replace them {#isPasted}

Make it clear what users can or can't do or influence to help build trust and alleviate anxiety. Human-AI collaboration is a partnership. AI should offer insights and suggestions under the user's direct guidance.
### Cite sources when it makes sense {#isPasted}

Provide affordances for users to find the source when they want to. Determine the level of need for exact attributions based on the context.


# Color resources
Use color tools and resources to quickly create and apply color palettes

Tools
-----
### Material Theme Builder (1P)

Material Theme Builder (MTB) is a Figma plugin that allows makers to emulate the color extraction process for dynamic color and create custom tonal schemes. {#isPasted}

MTB creates a full tonal scheme, contrast checking, and tokens.

[go/gm3-MTB](http://go/gm3-MTB)
### Monet Gallery

Monet Gallery can be utilized as a testing ground for UX and Eng to work on color-related updates. It contains applets that are prototypes for new features around color.{#isPasted}

[go/monetgallery](http://go/monetgallery)
### Swatchie

Swatchie is a tool and companion library for assigning GM3 roles to Lottie illustrations. This tool solves the problem of Lottie animations being constrained to a static color palette that is generated when the file is created.{#isPasted}

[go/swatchie](http://go/swatchie)
### Image harmonization

Bitmap images can be harmonize to match a dynamic color scheme. This especially applies to images and illustrations that sit on surfaces that will dynamically change.

[go/material-image-harmonization](http://go/material-image-harmonization)
### Google-approved Figma plugins

(internal-only)

* [Find and Replace Colors](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/806266638862897503) --- Quick \& easy solution for finding all colors and replacing them
* [Convert GM2 Colors to GM3 Colors](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1048722454716783034) --- Automatically convert mocks using styles from the old Google Material (GM2) to the new Google Material (GM3) library
* [paletteSwap](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/780955056558128750) --- Updates multiple color styles allowing the designer to switch between different color themes
Tutorials and codelabs
----------------------
**(Internal-only)**{#isPasted}

* [GM3 Colors Figma Kit](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/17zuL5hui6BQwDzdPp9VEYo01NsMoLubLBh-rA0n2FEI/edit#slide=id.g22f32fb84ef_0_0) --- TL;DR deck explaining the color roles and how they appear in Figma
**(External-friendly)**

* [Visualizing dynamic color in your app](https://codelabs.developers.google.com/visualize-dynamic-color?hl=en#0) --- Learn how to apply user-generated color to your app
* [Designing with accessible colors](https://codelabs.developers.google.com/color-contrast-accessibility?hl=en#0) --- Learn how color relates to accessibility and contrast guidelines
* [Customizing Material color](https://codelabs.developers.google.com/customizing-material-color?hl=en#0) --- Learn how to create a custom theme with the Material Theme Builder and apply it to design mockups in Figma
* [Build a Material color scheme](https://www.figma.com/community/file/1248805263844976008/Build-a-Material-color-scheme) --- Make a M3 color scheme with the color roles used in Material Components
Google brand colors
-------------------
Baseline \& static palettes
---------------------------
*** ** * ** ***

Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# Dynamic color schemes
Dynamic color can change a color palette to match user settings, like wallpapers, or in-product content

## User-generated source
**User-generated color** comes from an Android user's personal wallpaper. The wallpaper is digitally analyzed, a single color is selected as the source color, and tones are chosen and assigned to each color role.

**With a dynamic user-generated color scheme, end-users see**{#isPasted}

* Their apps and system UI change to a color pulled from their device wallpaper
* A product that looks personalized
Design with user-generated color
--------------------------------
Because the final colors will be dynamically generated on each user's personal device, **it's more important to understand and apply the system's color roles in your designs** rather than use a particular hex value.

The easiest way to do this is by using a color scheme generated by the Material Theme Builder (not the GM3 baseline scheme) and focus on applying the color roles to your designs instead of particular hex values. Then you can use the Material Theme Builder to test how well the design functions across potential user-generated color options.
### Use Material color roles in new design files {#isPasted}

**First, get the color roles by creating a new theme with the Material Theme Builder**{#isPasted}

1. Open your Figma file. Select the **Resources** button in the Figma toolbar ([screenshot](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/5koToFuumF9Nkkj))
2. Find the [Material Theme Builder 1P plugin](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1020820278641129628/Material-Theme-Builder-1P) and select **Run**. This will open a plugin dialog showing the default color scheme, including Core colors and Extended colors.
3. Open the plugin's **Settings** (gear icon at lower right of dialog) and select the checkboxes for both **New theme color diagram** and **Generate State Layers** . This will create a handy visualization of your branded color scheme and also generate state layers essential for designing interactions. [Learn more about state layers](/google-material-3/pages/interaction-states/state-layers)
4. Navigate out of settings
5. Open the Current Theme dropdown at the top of the dialog and select **+ ADD NEW THEME**
6. Give your theme a short name (this name will become the prefix of your color roles in Figma)
7. Select **ADD THEME**
8. With **Custom** selected, select **Primary**. This opens a dialog prompting you to select a custom source color.
9. Enter the Hex value for a color and hit **Apply**. This will generate a full color scheme.
10. With the Current Theme dropdown at the top of the dialog, select your theme{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
11. Select the frames or components in your file and then hit **Swap** in the bottom right of the dialog. This will automatically update the colors for any GM3 Design Kit components.

{#isPasted}

<br />

**Alternatively,**

1. Copy your preferred scheme's color diagram from [go/material-theme-builder-1p](http://go/material-theme-builder-1p){#isPasted} (don't use GM3 baseline since it has special tweaks to align it with GM2) and paste it into your file (this makes the color roles available in the Design panel on the right of the screen as part of your local styles)
2. Apply the scheme colors to custom components and UI elements by hovering on the element's color property in the Design panel on the right of the screen and selecting the **Style** icon (four dots). This opens a selection dialog.
3. Search for the scheme name (such as "GM3 green") to see its color roles
4. Select the color role that most closely matches the use case and intent (see [Color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles) for more information on what color to use where)
5. Repeat until all custom elements are using the scheme's color roles

<br />

### Apply color roles to an existing file or GM3 Design Kit components

**First, get the color roles into your file**

1. Copy your preferred scheme's color diagram from [go/material-theme-builder-1p](http://go/material-theme-builder-1p) and paste it into your file (this makes the color roles available in the Design panel on the right of the screen as part of your local styles){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}

**Swap colors in GM3 Design Kit components for your preferred colors**

1. Select the **Resources** button in the Figma toolbar. ([screenshot](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/5koToFuumF9Nkkj))
2. Find the [Material Theme Builder 1P plugin](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1020820278641129628/Material-Theme-Builder-1P) and select **Run**. This will open a plugin dialog showing the default color scheme, including Core colors and Extended colors.
3. In the Current Theme dropdown at the top of the dialog, select your preferred non-blue scheme (such as GM3 green)
4. Select the frames or GM3 Design Kit components in your file that need a color update and then hit **Swap** in the bottom right of the dialog. This will automatically update their colors from baseline colors to your selected scheme colors

**Then, update** **any remaining non-color-role-based colors**

1. Manually change any hex values or non-color-role-based color styles by selecting all and looking through the Selection colors in the Design panel on the right of the screen ([screenshot](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/BeqKaCnMMoGgbqB)){#isPasted}
2. Any colors that don't start with your selected scheme name (like GM3 green) need to be replaced with a corresponding color role
3. Hover on a non-color-role-based color row in the Design panel and select the **Style** icon (four dots). This opens a selection dialog.
4. Search for your scheme name to see the color roles
5. Select the color-role-based color that most closely matches that color's use case (see [Color roles](/google-material-3/pages/color-roles/roles) for more information on what color to use where) and select **Use style** to apply it to the selected objects
6. Repeat until all non-color-role-based colors in the file have been replaced with color roles

<br />

### Try out how your designs will look with dynamic color {#isPasted}

1. Select the **Resources** button in the Figma toolbar. ([screenshot](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/5koToFuumF9Nkkj)){#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
2. Find the [Material Theme Builder 1P plugin](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1020820278641129628/Material-Theme-Builder-1P) and select **Run**. This will open a plugin dialog showing the default color scheme, including Core colors and Extended colors.
3. Select Dynamic ([screenshot](https://screenshot.googleplex.com/Bn7rfcRzqtcedEk))
4. Add an image, or select the **Shuffle** icon to get a random source color
5. Select the frames or components in your file that you want to see with dynamic color and then hit **Swap** in the bottom right of the dialog. This will automatically update their colors to the user-generated dynamic colors.
6. Repeat with a range of colors to get a sense of how your product will appear across different users' devices

{#isPasted}

<br />

**Need to make adjustments to the scheme? Check out [Advanced customizations](/google-material-3/pages/advanced-customizations)**
Develop with user-generated color
---------------------------------
* [Android -- Color docs](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/java/com/google/android/libraries/material/g3doc/components/color.md?cl=head#gm3-dynamic-colors)
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Content-based source
**Content-based color** comes from in-app content, such as an album thumbnail image, logo, or video preview. {#isPasted}

Like user-generated color, the image is digitally analyzed through quantization, a single color selected as the source color, and tones are chosen and assigned to each color role.

**With a dynamic content-based color scheme, end-users see**{#isPasted}

* The product (and possibly system UI) change to a color corresponding to on-screen imagery
* A product that looks "smart"
Design with content-based color
-------------------------------
* Apply content color in apps and areas where it can enhance brand identity and convey the GM3 spirit of personalization.
* For example, a music app might derive color from a specific album's artwork to build upon the personal connection to a music library. For a news feed, content-based color may be applied from a given publication to differentiate brands and help users navigate the platform.
* In-app content provides a color source for any content-based color scheme, and can be applied within a specified area of an app, such as a set of components or a particular screen.
* Get started: Use the color tokens and roles built into the Theme Builder. Check that the preferred scheme is selected when using [Material Theme Builder](https://www.figma.com/community/plugin/1020820278641129628/Material-Theme-Builder-1P) to create color schemes from images.
* Explore advanced customizations
Develop with content-based color {#isPasted}
--------------------------------------------
* Android -- [Content-based dynamic color](https://g3doc.corp.google.com/third_party/java_src/android_libs/material_components/g3doc/theming/Color.md?cl=head#content-based-dynamic-colors)
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQs](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)

## Choosing a source
There are two ways your product can get a source color:

1. **User-generated color** from a user's wallpaper
2. **Content-based color** from in-app content like a music album or book cover

Learn [how dynamic color works](/google-material-3/pages/color/how-the-system-works/latest#b719142f-f9cb-4c5d-83b6-6cd0196c3add)
Both types of dynamic color are accessible and personalized, so deciding which type to use is based on what's most important in the product: content or user preference.
User-generated color {#isPasted}
--------------------------------
Choose a user-generated color source if:

* Your users would benefit from a personalized experience that's tested well
* You want your product to showcase the latest and greatest Material features

[**Get started with user-generated color**](/google-material-3/pages/dynamic/user-generated)
Content-based color {#isPasted}
-------------------------------
Choose a content-based color source if:

* Content is front-and-center in your product
* Your team can do a bit of advanced customization
* Content-based color would support usability of specific features like media players
* Content-based color is best used for contained screen elements adjacent to the source image, though the source image is not always visible.

[**Get started with content-based color**](/google-material-3/pages/dynamic/content-based)
Multiple color sources {#isPasted}
----------------------------------
Choose to use multiple color sources if:

* Your product requirements meet multiple criteria above
* You don't mind doing a bit of advanced customization

[**Get started with user-generated color**](/google-material-3/pages/dynamic/user-generated)**before customizing**
*** ** * ** ***

> Ask a question: [YAQS](http://go/gm3-yaqs)  
> File a bug: [go/gm3-bug](http://go/gm3-bug)


# All components
Use these components to create the complete text-to-speech experience

star  
Note:  
Text-to-speech components are owned and maintained by the [Speech team](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/7688290715). These components may differ slightly from GM3 components and patterns.
Availability (All components)
-----------------------------
|      Type      |                                                      Resource                                                      |   Status    |
|----------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|
| Design         | [TTS Figma sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/file/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library) | Available   |
| Implementation | Android                                                                                                            | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Compose                                                                                                            | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Web                                                                                                                | Unavailable |
Content highlighting
--------------------

Make it easier to follow along with text being spoken aloud at the phrase, sentence, or word level.
Display settings
----------------

Adjust text settings to make it easier to read.
Long-form player
----------------

View and control playback of long-form content.
Voice selection
---------------

Quickly browse, preview, download, and select the voice that reads text.
Voice speed
-----------

Control voice speed to be slower or faster.


# Voice names &amp; attributes
Text-to-speech voices are abstract sounds. Use the chosen, standardized names, or create your own with our naming criteria.

## Dispreferred approaches
Dispreferred names
------------------
Our team explored the following naming conventions, but realized that they don't improve accessibility for users or comply with product inclusion guidelines.
{#isPasted}

|                          **Themes explored**                          |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     **Reasons for discarding**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
|-----------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| ##### Real names Examples: April, Jeffrey                             | Real names imply gender and sometimes race - biases we want to avoid. Real names can be perceived as referring to additional personas which conflict with the persona the product already has (i.e. am I talking to my Google Assistant or to "Adrian"?).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| ##### Colors Examples: Red, Orange                                    | This approach doesn't i18n well due to different cultural conventions for color names (see source: [Languages don't all have the same number of terms for colors](https://theconversation.com/languages-dont-all-have-the-same-number-of-terms-for-colors-scientists-have-a-new-theory-why-84117)). You will quickly run out of well-known words (most languages have 11 main color words, but that includes white/black which have racial stereotypes). Many colors already have established meanings (e.g., red = angry or stop in many Western cultures or life/celebration in Chinese culture). |
| ##### Emojis Examples: 🌟, 🎓                                         | Research showed emoji names were divisive and did not help users choose a voice (see [January 2023 Voice Discovery \& Selection Research report](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1kOsYAxCCSE72B00Qew99qVLNeIxN_4ofjiDAJfHG_-g/edit#slide=id.gad5270cb4a_0_37)).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| ##### Adjectives Examples: Bright, Calm                               | The name needs to be a noun or it will be awkward to refer to. Adjectives should only be used for personality attributes.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| ##### Adjectives + nature nouns Examples: Warm Beach, Strong Mountain | Adjective/noun combinations can conjure stereotypes, i.e. comforting images for female voices and images associated with strength for male voices (see [March 2023 Voice Discovery \& Selection Research Report](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1qtCBRc-acgXKXWze-AzHhY3ajsxPLyRJlfk5MqWRS-U/edit?resourcekey=0-I7DbimjBVdOwkd8M8tB4fg#slide=id.g203d7419400_0_257).                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| ##### Locale Examples: Sydney Starlight, London Moonlight             | There may not be a one-to-one match between the language/locale combo and the way the TTS voice speaks. This may pigeonhole or stereotype speakers from a region.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| ##### Professions Examples: Professor, Optometrist                    | These may imply socioeconomic class and doesn't always evoke a specific style of speech.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| ##### Animals Examples: Horse, Deer                                   | Animals have stronger associated cultural meanings than other nature names and are more likely to have offensive connotations.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
1. Source: [Languages don't all have the same number of terms for colors](https://theconversation.com/languages-dont-all-have-the-same-number-of-terms-for-colors-scientists-have-a-new-theory-why-84117).
2. Source: [Research showed emoji names were divisive and did not help users choose a voice](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1kOsYAxCCSE72B00Qew99qVLNeIxN_4ofjiDAJfHG_-g/edit#slide=id.gad5270cb4a_0_37).
3. Source: [Adjective/noun combinations can conjure stereotypes, i.e. comforting images for female voices and images associated with strength for male voices](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1qtCBRc-acgXKXWze-AzHhY3ajsxPLyRJlfk5MqWRS-U/edit?resourcekey=0-I7DbimjBVdOwkd8M8tB4fg#slide=id.g203d7419400_0_257).
{#isPasted}
### Avoid using redundant labels

Pitch is an accurate description for vocal tone. Avoid using similar terms, like liveliness, energy, or range of variation (male voices have larger pitch ranges than female voices). These repeat the information that the average pitch label provides.
### Avoid irrelevant information and stereotypes {#isPasted}

For example, "Mom of 2 from Florida" doesn't add any new information about what the voice will sound like, so it doesn't help the user make a choice. It also reinforces stereotypes that make parenthood a woman's primary identity, and misrepresents what people from Florida sound like.

Long, multi-word descriptors can also clutter the UI.

## Best practices
star  
Note:  
Text-to-speech guidance is owned and maintained by the [Speech team](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/7688290715). Reach out to them to talk about incorporating the TTS framework in your product.
##### TTS voices are abstract sounds. Names and descriptions help users differentiate between voices and choose which one they want. The same names and descriptions should be used in all Google products for consistency across products, languages, and locales. {#isPasted}

##### View the full list of recommended names in our [naming repository](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Uv4Lsj7oT7KDquGCt6H0vTUn_4ODfZzC6-nZ4mF9jiw/edit?resourcekey=0--ikWdUaZusfNj5sN_9IoEw#gid=1260052636){#isPasted}.
Names
-----
Our approach
------------
The TTS team went through a comprehensive and exhaustive approach in ensuring inclusivity of the names. Asa a result, the naming practices below have been approved by the Trust \& Safety and Product Inclusion teams. Names that deviate from the guidelines noted on this page need to be cleared with the TTS team so we can guide you through the Trust \& Safety and inclusion process.
For consultation on voice naming, please contact **Leland Rechis (lrechis@).**
Best practices
--------------
|                            **Guideline**                            |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  **Description**                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| ##### Simple nature names                                           | Nature names such as River, Ruby, and Sunset. The full list is in our [naming repository](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Uv4Lsj7oT7KDquGCt6H0vTUn_4ODfZzC6-nZ4mF9jiw/edit?resourcekey=0--ikWdUaZusfNj5sN_9IoEw#gid=1260052636). Scaling is easy with nature names since they are nearly infinite. <br /> Nature words are less culturally specific than most other name options, so they lend themselves well to internationalization. Also, nature words are common and easily legible. Finally, nature names support the product brand because they have minimal controversy and low risk of being conflated with the product persona. |
| ##### Consider internationalization                                 | Each voice's name should stay the same in all contexts. The English-speaking voice called "Field" should always be called by its English name, even if the user is viewing it with a non-English system language. No two voices should have the same name, even if they are in separate locales. If there is a voice called "Field" in US English, there should NOT be a voice called "Field" in UK English. Names in one language don't have to restrict name choices in another. If there is a voice called "Field" (en-US), there could be a completely different voice called "Campo" \["Field"\] (it-IT).                                    |
| ##### Leverage abstraction to avoid bias                            | Even though users will assume gender, race, and other characteristics for TTS voices, Google takes an inclusive approach that avoids shaping these assumptions with stereotypical names and descriptions. That means the nature names and descriptions should be generic enough to work equally well for any TTS voice. Trying to be more specific could lead to bias or stereotyping.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |
| ##### Ensure alignment with established branding and/or persona     | Use names that reflect the Google brand. Virtually all products, including Assistant, have defined personas or brand attributes. Don't add anything that conflicts with the existing definitions, i.e. nicknames, additional personification ("mother of two from Florida"), or new descriptors ("fun-loving and giggly").                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| ##### Prioritize accessibility                                      | To keep the cognitive load light, ensure the names are simple. Be concise.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        |
| ##### Optimize for scalability and internationalization {#isPasted} | Voice names will be viewed in different locales, so prioritize linguistic and cultural universals.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                |
Voice attributes {#isPasted}
-----------------------------

To describe TTS voices objectively, focus on attributes that can be directly measured from the acoustic signal.
Attributes, such as the personality or pitch of a voice, help provide more detailed information that help users understand the characteristics of a voice.{#isPasted}

[View the full prioritized list of attributes](/google-material-3/pages/voice-selection/overview/36e92185-fc67-400f-b2ea-f6a22f943d44?edit=true)
### Use "pitch" instead of "gender"

Typical male voices have a lower average pitch than typical female voices, but this is not always the case. "Pitch" is more neutral.
### Only use accents for locales

Examples: "British accent", "Australian accent"

Avoid using "accent" to describe regional ("southern") or cultural ("African American Vernacular English, AAVE") accents.
### Describing personality
View the full list of recommended names and personalities in our [naming repository](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Uv4Lsj7oT7KDquGCt6H0vTUn_4ODfZzC6-nZ4mF9jiw/edit?resourcekey=0--ikWdUaZusfNj5sN_9IoEw#gid=1260052636). Start by using the following common voice adjectives:{#isPasted}

* Calm
* Warm
* Serene
* Gentle
* Bright
* Breezy
* Relaxed
* Soothing
* Chill
* Peaceful
#### Use brief personality adjectives

Use one-word descriptions that give a sense of what the voice "feels" like. You can be more creative with these since interpretation of a voice's personality is subjective.
#### Reuse personality adjectives {#isPasted}

Personality adjectives don't need to be unique. Multiple voices can have the same label. In fact, our research shows that the most popular personality adjective for a voice is "calm", so use it frequently.
#### Avoid excessive repetition

Google typically casts for a very specific sound in its TTS voices, so many of them will have similar adjectives. In those cases, you can use synonyms to avoid excessive repetition, like "serene" instead of "calm".


# Voice speed
Defines the tempo of the TTS voice so that users can read and/or listen to content at their desired pace

## Overview
star  
Note:  
Text-to-speech components are owned and maintained by the [Speech team](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/7688290715). These components may differ slightly from GM3 components and patterns.
* Two components: Voice speed controls and menu
* Use the controls on mobile, or alongside a [longform mini player](/google-material-3/pages/longform-player/overview/8eab9ad5-9cb6-4ddf-83d6-8f6c084c90cd?edit=true)
* Use the menu when accessing from the longform player{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
Availability
------------
|                |                                                                                       Resource                                                                                        |   Status    |
|----------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|
| Design         | [TTS Figma sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/file/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library?type=design&node-id=1485%3A121643&mode=design&t=MyG0XL5gYJTy1Q91-1) | Available   |
| Implementation | Android                                                                                                                                                                               | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Jetpack Compose                                                                                                                                                                       | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Wiz                                                                                                                                                                                   | Unavailable |

{#isPasted}

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------

Users should be able to:{#isPasted}

* Navigate to, and interact with voice speed controls{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Navigate to, open, and close a menu
* Navigate between and select menu items
Interaction \& style
--------------------
**Voice speed controls** should take focus when they're opened. When selected, screen readers should announce the new speed.
The **voice speed menu** behaves like regular menus. Ensure that the icon updates to reflect the current speed.
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
|      Key      |                  Action                   |
|---------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Space / Enter | Select an icon button, select a menu item |
| Arrows        | Navigate between the menu items           |
| Tab           | Navigate between interactive elements     |
| Esc           | Closes the menu                           |
Labeling elements {#isPasted}
-----------------------------
When labelling the increase and decrease buttons, indicate what the new speed will be, like "Increase voice speed to 1.2x."
Voice speed menus have the same accessibility patterns as GM3 menus.

Use labels and roles to accurately describe the purpose of the buttons and menus.

## Specs
Anatomy
-------
### Voice speed controls
### Voice speed menu
Color
-----
### Voice speed controls
### Voice speed menu
Measurements
------------

## Guidelines
Usage
-----
The voice speed setting can be adjusted using controls or a menu. Speeds are calculated in relation to the 1x default system speed. For example, 4x is four times faster than 1x.{#isPasted}

Use the controls in compact window sizes, like mobile.

Use the menu in larger window sizes, like desktop.
Based on research, the voice increments should be: 0.5x, 0.8x, 1x, 1.2x, 1.5x, 2x, 3x, 4x. Avoid hiding these speed options. More speeds can be added.{#isPasted}

The default speed should be 1x. However, system level settings should override the default.

For example, if someone is using assistive technology, settings, or extensions that change their preferred TTS speed at a system level, their default speed should be the nearest available equivalent instead of 1x.

<br />
### Voice speed controls usage {#isPasted}
Once the user reaches the highest or lowest value on the voice controls, disable the button.{#isPasted}

The current speed should be represented by an icon. Decimal speed icons without the "x" can be used to save space ("0.5" rather than "0.5x"). Some speed icons are available in [Google Symbols](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?icon.query=speed). All speed icons can be found in the [TTS sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/design/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library?node-id=1260-14134&t=AN3cjVcAxr96GJXM-4).
The controls can float above the UI, or can be in a container, like a bottom sheet. Adjust the container color to match the surface.{#isPasted}

When floating, voice controls should have a drop shadow, or have enough contrast to distinguish it from the background.
### Voice speed menu usage {#isPasted}
Any changes to the voice speed should be reflected in the component that opened the menu, like an icon button.{#isPasted}

Some speed icons are available in [Google Symbols](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?icon.query=speed){#isPasted}. All speed icons can be found in the [TTS sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/design/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library?node-id=1260-14134&t=AN3cjVcAxr96GJXM-4).
Research
--------
The speeds and specific UI controls are optimized to address common user needs, verified by user research. Read the [Reading Capabilities sticker sheet document](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1TdLLv8hcO4AeknFeTNuovNIomZIS0-QveKSQjNIbuYo/edit#slide=id.g21c8f967b16_0_218) for more insights.


# Voice selection
This research-backed guidance highlights which identifying elements to include for each voice, making it easy to scan the full list of voices and choose an enjoyable one.

## Specs
Anatomy
-------
### Voice selection bottom sheet
### Voice selection dialog
### Voice selection menu
Color
-----
Consult the [TTS sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/design/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library?node-id=593-38435) for the latest color roles.
Measurements
------------

## Overview
star  
Note:  
Text-to-speech components are owned and maintained by the [Speech team](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/7688290715). These components may differ slightly from GM3 components and patterns.
* Three components: bottom sheet, dialog, and menu{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Required elements: Voice name, personality description, audio playback.{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* Shows all voice options, playback, and identifiers in a consistent, scannable way{#isPasted}
{#isPasted}
* The menu can open from a variety of components, including icon buttons and text fields
Availability
------------
|                |                                                                                                Resource                                                                                                |   Status    |
|----------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|
| Design         | [TTS Figma sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/file/ZOonX6AaugqXStOig32zB7/TTS-Listening-Experiences%3A-Top-Journeys-%26-Solutions?type=design&node-id=2464%3A51074&mode=design&t=qLEZ6LHjITZUleIY-1) | Available   |
| Implementation | Android                                                                                                                                                                                                | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Compose                                                                                                                                                                                                | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Web                                                                                                                                                                                                    | Unavailable |

{#isPasted}

## Guidelines
##### There are three components for showing the voice selection options: **bottom sheet** , **dialog** , and **menu** .
