Products can create custom voice selection experiences as long as they include the [required elements](/google-material-3/pages/voice-selection/guidelines#9db4706c-5243-49da-b152-f66dcbcca1de).
Usage
-----
The **bottom sheet** voice selection component has limited space for extra actions.{#isPasted}

It should only be used in compact window sizes.
The **dialog** voice selection component has more space for extra functionality, like searching.

It's the most flexible, and can be used in any window size.
The **menu** voice selection component can be opened from an interactive element like an icon button.{#isPasted}

It should only be used in large window sizes, like desktop.
The attributes can be customized into many different formats. As long as playback, voice name, and personality are included, the voice selection experience can be turned into many things.
Anatomy
-------
The voice selection framework has required and optional elements to meet accessibility needs. This applies to all voice selection components and custom designs.
Required elements
-----------------
### Audio {#isPasted}

Listening to the audio is best way for people to know what the voice sounds like. Align it to the trailing edge for easy access, and so the selection can be on the leading edge.
### Name

Voice names are unique identifiers that helps people differentiate between voices. View the full list of recommended names and their personalities in our [naming repository](https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1Uv4Lsj7oT7KDquGCt6H0vTUn_4ODfZzC6-nZ4mF9jiw/edit?resourcekey=0--ikWdUaZusfNj5sN_9IoEw#gid=1260052636).{#isPasted}

[Best practices for naming voices](/google-material-3/pages/voice-names-attributes/best-practices/)
### Personality

Personality helps people identify the tone and feeling of a voice.
In research on what attributes make a voice likeable, users ranked personality as the most important. The same personality label can apply to multiple voices. "Calm" was a particularly appealing term. During casting, Google aims to recruit voice actors with calm voices, so virtually all the TTS voices can be described that way. To avoid excessive repetition, synonyms can also be used (ex: "serene").
Optional elements
-----------------
### Accent (optional)

Accent labels should only refer to locales other than the user's default. To avoid label repetition, consider organizing voices by locale-specific headers. In research, accent was important to users but not to the degree that audio, personality, and pitch were.
### Color (optional)

Each voice name is paired with a thematically appropriate color to visually anchor the user. Since each voice's color is consistent across experiences, it adds an extra way for users to quickly recognize specific voices in different products.{#isPasted}

For scalability and accessibility (i.e. color blindness), color should never be the primary or only way to identify a voice.

The color should meet WCAG accessibility requirements for contrast (4.5:1).
### Download (optional)

Voices can be downloadable for products that need to work on-device or offline.
### Pitch (optional)

Pitch is a specific, scientifically measurable alternative to using a gender label. In research, users ranked pitch as important for choosing a voice, but not as much as audio or personality.
### Search (optional)

Searching can be used in dialogs or other larger-screen products to more effectively find the correct voice. It's most useful with large libraries of voices across different languages.
Behavior
--------
### Downloading

When people download a set of voices, te download button should turn into a circular progress indicator with the label "Downloading". {#isPasted}

Once the voices are downloaded, the label and progress indicator should disappear.

For larger, longer processes, consider including the download progress as well.
### Scrolling

Longer dialogs and menus should have a persistent scroll bar. {#isPasted}

Avoid putting scrolling in bottom sheets when possible.
### Searching

Searching should automatically filter down the results in the list to the user's query.
### Voice preview

An audio sample should play when selecting the voice playback button. The icon should change to a pause icon. When the audio ends, the button changes back to a play icon.

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------

Users should be able to do the following with assistive technology:{#isPasted}

* Navigate to, open, and close a bottom sheet, dialog, or menu
* Navigate between and select items
* Navigate between all interactive elements
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
Labeling elements
-----------------
All interactive elements should be labelled based on their action, like "Search by language", "Play voice preview", "Pause voice preview". {#isPasted}

The colors are decorative, and don't require a label.


# Longform player
Users should be able to listen to articles at their own pace. The longform player gives users the controls they need to customize the pace of the TTS experience.

## Overview
star  
Note:  
Text-to-speech components are owned and maintained by the [Speech team](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/7688290715). These components may differ slightly from GM3 components and patterns.
There are three types of longform players: {#isPasted}

1. Dynamic player
2. Mini player
3. Web player

The design patterns for longform players prioritize consistency and user needs. They're designed to look and feel similar to reduce cognitive load.
Availability
------------
|                |                                                                                       Resource                                                                                       |   Status    |
|----------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|
| Design         | [TTS Figma sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/file/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library?type=design&node-id=1059%3A34099&mode=design&t=jZAvTOkZdEDdJT0P-1) | Available   |
| Implementation | Android                                                                                                                                                                              | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Compose                                                                                                                                                                              | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Web                                                                                                                                                                                  | Unavailable |

{#isPasted}

## Guidelines
Usage
-----

There are three different types of longform players:{#isPasted}

* **Dynamic player:** A detailed player with additional visible controls and text, ideal for dedicated reading and listening experiences.
* **Mini player** **:** A floating player with limited controls that users can place anywhere the screen.
* **Web player:** A compact player that has the most common playback settings always visible. It was designed based on user research and accessibility best practices.
### Dynamic player

Use the dynamic player to show more content at once, like descriptions, images, or more actions. The amount of content can be customized to fit user needs.  

It's best used for very long content with multiple sections, like books or detailed articles, so people can easily track their playback progress.  

The back and forward buttons can be customized based on the type of media being player.
The most basic form of this player was built and optimized for efficiency and meeting core user needs, based on user research. Avoid modifying the core layout.  

The voice speed is the most common voice control. According to research, users change voice speed more often than any other setting.  

The forward, backward, play, and pause buttons should be accessible at all times.  

To keep the player easy to use, all other controls should be available in the settings menu.
The dynamic player can show images or the text currently being spoken out loud.
### Mini player {#isPasted}

The mini player can float above all other content. Since it doesn't have back and forward controls, it shouldn't be the only kind of longform player. Instead, it should be an option to open from one of the other longform players.{#isPasted}

It's optimized for web, but can also be used on mobile.

Keep the close button far from the play and pause button to support users with dexterity challenges.

The mini player can show additional text and images, like the dynamic player.
The dynamic player can be customized to fit your product's color scheme. Android Read Aloud has customized the color scheme.
Placement
---------
Dynamic and mini players should be placed at the bottom of the screen by default so they're always accessible.   

In products with minimal UI, longform players can be dismissed or hidden with actions that remove UI, like tapping or swiping.
### Web player
The web player resembles an app bar. It can show many TTS settings at once. When space runs out for more settings, use a menu button to store any settings that no longer fit in the bar.{#isPasted}

When content is playing, the web player should reveal the back and forward buttons.

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------

Users should be able to:{#isPasted}

* Navigate to the long-form player using assistive technology
* Navigate between the customization options like voice speed or other settings
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
Use **tab** to navigation between all interactive elements in a longform player, and **Space/Enter** to select buttons.
Labeling elements
-----------------
Label the buttons in the longform player according to [icon button accessibility guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/icon-buttons/accessibility).{#isPasted}

In the label, include the type of media being played and paused, like "pause song."

## Specs
Anatomy
-------
### Dynamic player
### Mini-player
### Web player
Color
-----

Refer to the [TTS sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/design/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library?node-id=593-38438) for the latest GM3 color roles.
Measurements
------------


# Display settings
These display settings make it easier to follow along with spoken text and include the following: font face and size, line height, letter spacing, and color theme.

## Specs
Anatomy
-------
star  
Note:  
GM3 menus have one leading icon by default. Some TTS display setting menus have two leading icons.
Most display settings are shown in menus. They can open from any interactive component, like a text field or icon button. Go to the [guidelines](/google-material-3/pages/display-settings/guidelines/) for information on which icons to use in the menus.
The font size setting is a custom component. It's most similar to a segmented button.

It can be floating with a drop shadow, or placed in-line with other buttons.
Color
-----

All display settings support light and dark schemes. For display settings that use menus, use the [GM3 menu color roles](/google-material-3/pages/menus/specs#a4518e0e-f645-4904-9a1f-c33077bccac9).

The custom icons for color settings should represent the same colors that the setting will create.
Measurements
------------

Display settings in menus have the default [GM3 menu measurements](/google-material-3/pages/menus/specs/latest#a0f63e65-0bd3-4ff7-b307-b1693dc671ac). {#isPasted}

The font size controls has custom measurements:

## Guidelines
##### The TTS display settings change how spoken text is rendered. Group multiple display settings close together in the UI.
Place all display settings near each other, like in a bar or overflow menu.{#isPasted}

The [TTS web longform player](/google-material-3/pages/longform-player/overview/) incorporates all the settings into a single toolbar:

1. [Voice speed controls](/google-material-3/pages/voice-speed/overview/)
2. [Voice selection menu](/google-material-3/pages/voice-selection/overview/)
3. [Content highlighting button](/google-material-3/pages/content-highlighting/overview/)
4. Font size controls
5. Font selection menu
6. Color theme menu
7. Line height menu
8. Letter spacing menu
Usage
-----
### Color theme menu {#isPasted}

Use this to change how the background and text color render in the product.

Include light and dark theme by default. The other recommended color theme options are yellow and blue. The menu item icons should represent the background and text colors that will be shown.

Use the icon:

* [Palette](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google+Symbols:palette:FILL@1;wght@400;GRAD@0;opsz@24&icon.platform=web&icon.query=color)
Rationale: The [Speakup report](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1xEBxO74-OyacSMnhpC-6noUGUbYELV5K/view?resourcekey=0-wr5_szV_3eQm4HmESYKzkg){#isPasted}, followed by a focus group conversation, led us to the conclusion that people with learning disabilities prefer the yellow and blue options most. Including these colors should cover most needs.
### Font selection menu

Use this to show all available font options in a text-to-speech reader.{#isPasted}

* Open the menu from a text field or other component.
* The default font should be Poppins. Poppins will be updated to [Google Sans Flex](https://fonts.corp.google.com/specimen/Google+Sans+Flex) once it's ready for production.{#isPasted}
* All font faces were chosen for the best accessible experience, and have been validated through user research.{#isPasted}
* Download recommended fonts: [Poppins](https://fonts.google.com/specimen/Poppins), [Comic Neue](https://fonts.google.com/specimen/Comic+Neue?query=Comic+Neue), [Lexend Deca](https://fonts.google.com/specimen/Lexend+Deca), [EB Garamond](https://fonts.google.com/specimen/EB+Garamond), [Stix Two Text](https://fonts.google.com/specimen/STIX+Two+Text)
### Font size controls {#isPasted}

Use this to increase or decrease the size by 1pt. Set the default font size to be 18pt for desktop, or 20pt for mobile.

Use the icons:

* [Text increase icon](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google+Symbols:text_increase:FILL@0;wght@400;GRAD@0;opsz@24&icon.platform=web&icon.query=text+increase)
* [Text decrease icon](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google+Symbols:text_decrease:FILL@0;wght@400;GRAD@0;opsz@24&icon.platform=web&icon.query=text+decrease)
Rationale: Font size is something that [users change](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1My7CQ31dRqhcO0yxrnr3EfMR4h_gSDxqskA_GoRhLmc/edit#slide=id.g19f2d98a12b_0_654){#isPasted} often because it is contextually driven. For example, people with higher fatigue may prefer a larger font, while those wanting to see lots text at a time may prefer a smaller font.
The font size controls can float above the UI, or can be in a component like a bottom sheet. Adjust the container color to match the surface or component.{#isPasted}

When floating, the container should have a drop shadow to distinguish it from the background.
### Letter spacing menu {#isPasted}

Use this to change spacing between letters.

There should be 3 letter spacing options:

* Standard: normal (default selection)
* Wide: .2rem
* Very wide: .4rem

Use the icons:

* [Format letter spacing 2](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google+Symbols:format_letter_spacing_2:FILL@0;wght@400;GRAD@0;opsz@24&icon.platform=web&icon.query=format+letter+spacing) (button){#isPasted}
* [Format letter spacing standard](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google+Symbols:format_letter_spacing_standard:FILL@0;wght@400;GRAD@0;opsz@24&icon.platform=web&icon.query=format+letter+spacing){#isPasted}
* [Format letter spacing wide](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google+Symbols:format_letter_spacing_wide:FILL@0;wght@400;GRAD@0;opsz@24&icon.platform=web&icon.query=format+letter+spacing){#isPasted}
* [Format letter spacing wider](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google+Symbols:format_letter_spacing_wider:FILL@0;wght@400;GRAD@0;opsz@24&icon.platform=web&icon.query=format+letter+spacing){#isPasted}
Rationale: [UXR conducted by Read Anything Chrome](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1My7CQ31dRqhcO0yxrnr3EfMR4h_gSDxqskA_GoRhLmc/edit#slide=id.g19f2d98a12b_0_806){#isPasted} informed that participants desired wide letter spacing, but didn't want it to be too wide.
### Line height menu {#isPasted}

Use this to change line heights to appear closer or further apart.

There should be 3 line height options:

* Standard: 1.15
* Loose: 1.5 (default selection)
* Very loose: 2

Use the icons:

* [Format line spacing](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google+Symbols:format_line_spacing:FILL@1;wght@400;GRAD@0;opsz@24&icon.platform=web&icon.query=spacing) (button)
* The menu item icons aren't available in Google Symbols. Use the icons in the [TTS Figma sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/file/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library?type=design&node-id=1260-14320&mode=design&t=KwnVpQAQuLckVgJz-4).{#isPasted}
Rationale: [UXR conducted by Read Anything Chrome](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1My7CQ31dRqhcO0yxrnr3EfMR4h_gSDxqskA_GoRhLmc/edit#slide=id.g19f2d98a12b_0_746){#isPasted} informed that participants preferred loose line height. Dyslexia friendly style guide also states that larger line spacing improves readability.
Behavior
--------
### Selection

Display setting menus should use a check as the leading icon to shows which item the user has selected.

## Overview
star  
Note:  
Text-to-speech components are owned and maintained by the [Speech team](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/7688290715). These components may differ slightly from GM3 components and patterns.
Display settings are used to control font face, font size, color, and spacing. They should appear together.

* Use menus to show all available options for each setting
* Avoid changing the options in the menus
* These menus can open from text fields, icon buttons, or other custom components
* The font size buttons should be next to each other
Availability
------------
|                |                                                                                      Resource                                                                                       |   Status    |
|----------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|
| Design         | [TTS Figma sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/file/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library?type=design&node-id=1123%3A8159&mode=design&t=MyG0XL5gYJTy1Q91-1) | Available   |
| Implementation | Android                                                                                                                                                                             | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Compose                                                                                                                                                                             | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Web                                                                                                                                                                                 | Unavailable |

{#isPasted}

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------

Users should be able to do the following with assistive technology:{#isPasted}

* Navigate to, open, and close a menu
* Navigate between and select menu items
* Interact with floating controls
Keyboard navigation
-------------------
|      Keys      |                Actions                |
|----------------|---------------------------------------|
| Tab            | Navigate between interactive elements |
| Arrows         | Navigate between menu items           |
| Space or Enter | Activate a button; select a menu item |
Labeling elements
-----------------
Use accessibility labels to describe what's changing when activating the font size controls, like "decrease font size to 15pt."
Use accessibility labels to describe the menu or controls that are opening.


# Content highlighting
Make it easier to follow along with text being spoken aloud at the phrase, sentence, or word level.

## Accessibility
Use cases
---------

Users should be able to:{#isPasted}

* Turn content highlighting on and off
* Read along with the current words being spoken aloud
Interaction and style
---------------------
Content highlighting needs to meet color contrast ratios of 3:1 with the background and 4.5:1 with the text. There are no further accessibility requirements.

## Guidelines
Content highlight patterns
--------------------------

There are three ways to highlight content as a TTS voice reads it aloud: by **phrase** , **word** , or **sentence**.
### By phrase (recommended)

Groups of words that make sense together are highlighted as they are read aloud.{#isPasted}

In research, most users preferred this approach. Its implementation is WIP; contact the Chrome Read Aloud engineer for the latest updates.

This pattern is recommended at all speeds.
### By word

As the content is read aloud, one word is highlighted at a time. This pattern is used by most read aloud tools, including Reading mode and Lookout.{#isPasted}

Use at regular speed, but avoid highlighting by word at 2x speed or higher. This can be too "jumpy" and creates discomfort for users. Instead, switch to highlighting by phrase or by sentence.
There are two versions:{#isPasted}

* The word's background is highlighted in color.
* The text itself is in bold and in a different color.

If bolding the text, make sure it uses a primary color to make it more noticeable.
### By sentence (not recommended)

The entire sentence is highlighted when it's read aloud. {#isPasted}

This is not recommended at regular speeds, since sentences can be very long, but is acceptable at 2x speed or higher.

## Overview
star  
Note:  
Text-to-speech components are owned and maintained by the [Speech team](https://moma.corp.google.com/team/7688290715). These components may differ slightly from GM3 components and patterns.
Text is highlighted as the TTS voice reads it aloud. This helps users know where they are at while reading or listening to the content. {#isPasted}

This setting should be toggled on and off with an icon button.

Users can set this to highlight phrases, sentences, or individual words at a time.
Availability
------------
|                |                                                                                       Resource                                                                                       |   Status    |
|----------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------|
| Design         | [TTS Figma sticker sheet](https://www.figma.com/file/hKXKaQPAfPyNwerkp4ZrDk/TTS-voice-framework-component-library?type=design&node-id=1263%3A20314&mode=design&t=jZAvTOkZdEDdJT0P-1) | Available   |
| Implementation | Android                                                                                                                                                                              | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Jetpack Compose                                                                                                                                                                      | Unavailable |
| Implementation | Web                                                                                                                                                                                  | Unavailable |

{#isPasted}

## Specs
Anatomy
-------
Use a toggle icon button to turn highlighting on and off.{#isPasted}

Use the following icons from [go/icons](http://go/icons):

1. [Ink highlighter](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google%20Symbols%3Aink_highlighter%3AFILL%400%3Bwght%40400%3BGRAD%400%3Bopsz%4024) (unselected)
2. [Ink highlighter move](https://fonts.corp.google.com/icons?selected=Google%20Symbols%3Aink_highlighter_move%3AFILL%400%3Bwght%40400%3BGRAD%400%3Bopsz%4024) (selected)
Color
-----
Use the following GM3 color roles to ensure accessibility:

1. Background: **Surface**
2. Spoken text: **On surface variant**
3. Highlight: **Primary container**
4. Unspoken text: **On surface**

If customizing the color scheme, make sure the chosen highlight colors meet the minimum accessible color contrast ratios:

* Highlight with text: 4.5:1
* Highlight with background: 3:1
{#isPasted}
When changing the text instead of using a highlight, increase the font weight or grade, and change the color.

1. Text being spoken: **Primary**
### Custom examples
These are examples of how Chrome reader has customized the color scheme.
